# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions (KTU pattern):** Answer in 2–3 sentences with a defining formula wherever applicable.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (3 Marks)

**Define dissimilarity and similarity. State any four properties of a distance measure.**

**Model Answer:**

A **similarity** measure $s(i, j)$ quantifies how alike two data objects $i$ and $j$ are — higher values indicate greater likeness. A **dissimilarity** (or **distance**) $d(i, j)$ quantifies how different they are — lower values indicate greater likeness.

The four key properties of a distance measure are:

1. **Non-negativity:** $d(i, j) \geq 0$.
2. **Identity of indiscernibles:** $d(i, j) = 0 \iff i = j$.
3. **Symmetry:** $d(i, j) = d(j, i)$.
4. **Triangle inequality:** $d(i, k) \leq d(i, j) + d(j, k)$.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (3 Marks)

**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric binary attributes with one example each. What is the implication for proximity computation?**

**Model Answer:**

In a **symmetric binary** attribute, both states (0 and 1) carry equal weight — e.g., *Gender* (Male/Female). The **Simple Matching Coefficient (SMC)** is used.

In an **asymmetric binary** attribute, one state is rarer and more important — e.g., *Medical test result* (Positive/Negative). The **Jaccard Coefficient** is used, which ignores the $M_{00}$ cell because the simultaneous absence of a rare event is uninformative.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU pattern):** Answer with full steps, neat derivations, and final boxed answers. Marks are split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Choice Pattern]

**(a)** Define the **Minkowski distance** between two $n$-dimensional data points. Show that **Manhattan** ($p=1$), **Euclidean** ($p=2$), and **Supremum** ($p \to \infty$) distances are special cases of the Minkowski metric. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Given two vectors $A = (1, 3, 5, 7)$ and $B = (2, 4, 6, 8)$:

  (i) Compute the **Euclidean** distance.
  (ii) Compute the **Manhattan** distance.
  (iii) Compute the **Supremum** distance.
  (iv) Compute the **Cosine similarity** between them. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Definition of Minkowski Distance:**

The Minkowski distance of order $p \geq 1$ between two $n$-dimensional vectors $i$ and $j$ is defined as:

$$d_{p}(i, j) = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

This is the $L_p$ norm of the difference vector $i - j$, and it is a true metric for $p \geq 1$.

**Step 2 — Special case $p = 1$ (Manhattan / City-Block):**

Substituting $p = 1$:

$$d_{1}(i, j) = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^{1} \right)^{1/1} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$$

This is the sum of absolute coordinate-wise differences — equivalent to distance travelled on a Manhattan grid. ✓

**Step 3 — Special case $p = 2$ (Euclidean):**

Substituting $p = 2$:

$$d_{2}(i, j) = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^{2} \right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_{ik} - x_{jk})^{2}}$$

This is the standard straight-line distance in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Special case $p \to \infty$ (Supremum / Chebyshev):**

As $p$ grows, the term with the largest base dominates:

$$d_{\infty}(i, j) = \lim_{p \to \infty} \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p} = \max_{k} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$$

This is the maximum absolute coordinate-wise difference. ✓

Hence all three are special cases of the Minkowski metric.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

Given $A = (1, 3, 5, 7)$ and $B = (2, 4, 6, 8)$.

**Step 1 — Element-wise differences:**

$$A - B = (1-2, \ 3-4, \ 5-6, \ 7-8) = (-1, -1, -1, -1)$$

**Step 2 — Absolute values:**

$$\vert A - B \vert = (1, 1, 1, 1)$$

**(i) Euclidean Distance:**

$$d_{2}(A, B) = \sqrt{1^{2} + 1^{2} + 1^{2} + 1^{2}} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

**(ii) Manhattan Distance:**

$$d_{1}(A, B) = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4$$

**(iii) Supremum Distance:**

$$d_{\infty}(A, B) = \max(1, 1, 1, 1) = 1$$

**(iv) Cosine Similarity:**

First compute the dot product:

$$A \cdot B = (1)(2) + (3)(4) + (5)(6) + (7)(8) = 2 + 12 + 30 + 56 = 100$$

Then compute the norms:

$$\| A \| = \sqrt{1^{2} + 3^{2} + 5^{2} + 7^{2}} = \sqrt{1 + 9 + 25 + 49} = \sqrt{84} \approx 9.165$$

$$\| B \| = \sqrt{2^{2} + 4^{2} + 6^{2} + 8^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 16 + 36 + 64} = \sqrt{120} \approx 10.954$$

Now compute the cosine similarity:

$$\cos(A, B) = \frac{100}{\sqrt{84} \cdot \sqrt{120}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{10080}} = \frac{100}{100.399} \approx 0.996$$

The two vectors are **almost perfectly aligned in direction** (cosine near 1), which is expected because $B = A + (1,1,1,1)$ — a constant shift preserves direction.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Choice Pattern, Alternative]

**(a)** What are **binary attributes**? Distinguish between **symmetric** and **asymmetric** binary attributes. Explain the **Simple Matching Coefficient** and the **Jaccard Coefficient** with a contingency table. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Two patients $P$ and $Q$ are tested for 6 diseases. The results (1 = present, 0 = absent) are:

| Patient | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | D6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| Q | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

  (i) Construct the **contingency table**.
  (ii) Compute the **Jaccard similarity** and **Jaccard dissimilarity**.
  (iii) Compute the **SMC similarity** and **SMC dissimilarity**.
  (iv) Justify which measure is more appropriate here. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Step 1 — Definition:**

A **binary attribute** is one that takes only two values, typically encoded as 0 or 1. It is the simplest form of a nominal attribute.

**Step 2 — Symmetric vs Asymmetric:**

- **Symmetric binary:** both outcomes are equally valuable. Example: *Gender* (0 = Male, 1 = Female). Here $M_{01}$ and $M_{10}$ carry the same weight as $M_{00}$ and $M_{11}$.
- **Asymmetric binary:** the rarer outcome carries more weight. Example: *Disease status* (0 = absent, 1 = present). The $M_{00}$ cell (both absent) is non-informative and is excluded from the numerator and one of the denominators.

**Step 3 — Contingency Table:**

For two objects $i$ and $j$ over $n$ binary attributes, define:

|  | $j = 1$ | $j = 0$ | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$i = 1$** | $M_{11}$ | $M_{10}$ | $M_{1\cdot}$ |
| **$i = 0$** | $M_{01}$ | $M_{00}$ | $M_{0\cdot}$ |
| **Total** | $M_{\cdot 1}$ | $M_{\cdot 0}$ | $n$ |

**Step 4 — Simple Matching Coefficient (symmetric):**

$$\text{SMC}(i, j) = \frac{M_{11} + M_{00}}{M_{11} + M_{10} + M_{01} + M_{00}} = \frac{M_{11} + M_{00}}{n}$$

Dissimilarity is $d_{\text{SMC}} = 1 - \text{SMC}$.

**Step 5 — Jaccard Coefficient (asymmetric):**

$$\text{Jaccard}(i, j) = \frac{M_{11}}{M_{11} + M_{10} + M_{01}}$$

Dissimilarity is $d_{\text{Jaccard}} = 1 - \text{Jaccard}$.

> **Key Difference:** SMC counts mutual absences ($M_{00}$) in the numerator; Jaccard excludes them. Jaccard is preferred when the 0 state is the "default" / non-informative state.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Read attribute-by-attribute:**

Comparing $P$ and $Q$ across $D_1, \ldots, D_6$:

- $D_1$: $P=1, Q=1$ → $M_{11}$
- $D_2$: $P=0, Q=1$ → $M_{01}$
- $D_3$: $P=1, Q=0$ → $M_{10}$
- $D_4$: $P=0, Q=0$ → $M_{00}$
- $D_5$: $P=1, Q=1$ → $M_{11}$
- $D_6$: $P=0, Q=0$ → $M_{00}$

**Step 2 — Build the Contingency Table:**

- $M_{11} = 2$ (D1, D5)
- $M_{10} = 1$ (D3)
- $M_{01} = 1$ (D2)
- $M_{00} = 2$ (D4, D6)
- $n = 6$ ✓

|  | $Q = 1$ | $Q = 0$ | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$P = 1$** | $M_{11} = 2$ | $M_{10} = 1$ | 3 |
| **$P = 0$** | $M_{01} = 1$ | $M_{00} = 2$ | 3 |
| **Total** | 3 | 3 | 6 |

**Step 3 — Jaccard Similarity and Dissimilarity:**

$$J(P, Q) = \frac{M_{11}}{M_{11} + M_{10} + M_{01}} = \frac{2}{2 + 1 + 1} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$d_{\text{Jaccard}} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — SMC Similarity and Dissimilarity:**

$$\text{SMC}(P, Q) = \frac{M_{11} + M_{00}}{n} = \frac{2 + 2}{6} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667$$

$$d_{\text{SMC}} = 1 - 0.667 \approx 0.333$$

**Step 5 — Justification:**

The attribute is **disease presence** — an **asymmetric binary** attribute. The state 0 ("disease absent") is the common default and is uninformative. Therefore, **Jaccard similarity is the more appropriate measure**, giving $J = 0.5$ (i.e., the patients share half of the diseases they collectively manifest). SMC over-weights the mutual absence of disease and overstates the similarity.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Proximity Measures**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cosine with Correlation:** Cosine measures the *angle* between vectors; Pearson measures the *linear trend* (centre-normalised). They are NOT the same. KTU examiners frequently include a "compute both and compare" sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting Normalisation for Ordinal:** Students often apply Euclidean directly on raw ranks (1, 2, 3, 4) and miss the **mandatory normalisation step** $z = (r-1)/(M-1)$ before computing distance. This can cost full 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Misusing SMC for Asymmetric Binary:** SMC counts $M_{00}$ in the numerator — for rare-event data (fraud, disease), this **artificially inflates** similarity. Use Jaccard.
> 4. **Forgetting Symmetry / Triangle Inequality Proof:** When a derivation question asks "show this is a true metric", students compute the value but **forget to verify the three properties** (non-negativity, symmetry, triangle inequality). Examiners dock 2 marks routinely.
> 5. **Sign Error in Cosine Denominator:** Many students write $\sqrt{\sum x^{2}_{ik} \cdot x^{2}_{jk}}$ instead of $\sqrt{\sum x^{2}_{ik}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum x^{2}_{jk}}$. The factorisation matters.
> 6. **Mixing $p$ in Minkowski:** When $p < 1$, the Minkowski expression is **not a true metric** (triangle inequality fails). Examiners love asking the boundary $p \geq 1$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Proximity Measures**

- **Proximity** = generic term covering **similarity** (higher = more alike) and **dissimilarity** (lower = more alike).
- A **distance metric** must satisfy **non-negativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality**.
- The **four attribute types** are: **Nominal, Binary, Ordinal, Numeric (Interval/Ratio)** — and each demands a specific measure.
- **Minkowski distance** is the **general family**: $p=1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan, $p=2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean, $p \to \infty \Rightarrow$ Supremum.
- **Euclidean** is sensitive to **outliers**; **Manhattan** is more **robust** to outliers but treats all dimensions equally.
- **Cosine similarity** is **magnitude-invariant**; **Pearson correlation** is **shift- and scale-invariant** (mean-centred).
- **Symmetric binary** attributes → use **SMC**; **asymmetric binary** attributes → use **Jaccard** (ignore $M_{00}$).
- **Nominal** attributes → simple **mismatch ratio** $d = (p-m)/p$.
- **Ordinal** attributes → **normalise** ranks to $[0,1]$ using $z = (r-1)/(M-1)$ **before** any numeric distance.
- **Minkowski requires $p \geq 1$** to be a true metric.
- For **text mining and TF-IDF** vectors → **Cosine** is the gold standard.
- For **high-dimensional sparse data** (e.g., gene expression) → **Manhattan** or **Cosine** often outperforms Euclidean.
- For **anomaly detection** → use **Euclidean** from a reference point (centroid, mean).
- **Always normalise** numeric attributes to the same scale before computing distance (z-score or min-max) to prevent large-magnitude features from dominating.
- **Domain knowledge** drives the choice of measure — there is no universally "best" proximity function.
- **Contingency table** ($M_{11}, M_{10}, M_{01}, M_{00}$) is the canonical starting point for **every** binary-attribute proximity problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Association of Two Variables - Summarizing the Distribution of Two Discrete Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Association of Two Variables - Summarizing the Distribution of Two Discrete Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In statistics, when we collect data, we often need to study **two categorical (discrete) variables simultaneously** to understand whether, and how strongly, they are related. This relationship is called the **Association of Two Variables**.

For two discrete variables $X$ and $Y$:
- $X$ takes values $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_r$ (rows of the table)
- $Y$ takes values $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_c$ (columns of the table)

The **summarization of their joint distribution** is performed using a **two-way contingency table** (also called a **cross-tabulation** or **crosstab**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **contingency table** is a tabular summary of the joint frequency distribution of two categorical variables, where each cell $(i,j)$ contains the count (or relative frequency) of observations that simultaneously belong to category $x_i$ of variable $X$ and category $y_j$ of variable $Y$.

**Key Components of the Bivariate Discrete Distribution:**

1. **Joint Frequency** $n_{ij}$ — number of observations falling in cell $(i,j)$.
2. **Joint Relative Frequency** $f_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n}$ — proportion of total observations in cell $(i,j)$.
3. **Marginal Distribution** — obtained by summing rows or columns, giving the distribution of one variable irrespective of the other.
4. **Conditional Distribution** — distribution of one variable given a fixed value of the other.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Coffee Shop Survey":**
> Imagine a researcher standing outside a coffee shop, recording two things for every customer:
> (a) **Time of visit** (Morning, Afternoon, Evening) — this is variable $X$.
> (b) **Drink ordered** (Tea, Coffee, Juice) — this is variable $Y$.
>
> After collecting data for 300 customers, the researcher builds a **grid (table)** where each cell counts how many customers belong to a specific combination, e.g., "Morning + Coffee." This grid is the contingency table.
>
> - The **row totals** tell us how many customers visited in each time slot (ignoring drink).
> - The **column totals** tell us how many customers ordered each drink (ignoring time).
> - The **interior cells** tell us the *joint* behavior — this is what reveals **association** (e.g., "Coffee is preferred in the Morning").

This grid-based approach is exactly how we **summarize the distribution of two discrete variables**.

## 1.3 Standard Notation & Terminology

- **$r$** = number of rows (categories of $X$)
- **$c$** = number of columns (categories of $Y$)
- **$n_{ij}$** = observed frequency in row $i$, column $j$
- **$n_{i\cdot}$** = row marginal total $= \sum_{j=1}^{c} n_{ij}$
- **$n_{\cdot j}$** = column marginal total $= \sum_{i=1}^{r} n_{ij}$
- **$n$** = grand total $= \sum_{i,j} n_{ij}$

These notations will appear repeatedly throughout the derivations in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structure of the Contingency Table (Logical Breakdown)

A standard $r \times c$ contingency table is structured as follows:

| | $Y = y_1$ | $Y = y_2$ | $\cdots$ | $Y = y_c$ | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $X = x_1$ | $n_{11}$ | $n_{12}$ | $\cdots$ | $n_{1c}$ | $n_{1\cdot}$ |
| $X = x_2$ | $n_{21}$ | $n_{22}$ | $\cdots$ | $n_{2c}$ | $n_{2\cdot}$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\ddots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $X = x_r$ | $n_{r1}$ | $n_{r2}$ | $\cdots$ | $n_{rc}$ | $n_{r\cdot}$ |
| **Column Total** | $n_{\cdot 1}$ | $n_{\cdot 2}$ | $\cdots$ | $n_{\cdot c}$ | $n$ |

The table encodes **three layers of information** simultaneously:
1. **Body** $\rightarrow$ joint behavior of $(X,Y)$
2. **Margins** $\rightarrow$ individual behavior of $X$ or $Y$ alone
3. **Grand total** $\rightarrow$ sample size

## 2.2 The Logical Hierarchy of Distributions

### Step 1 — Joint Distribution
The joint probability mass function (PMF) of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is:

$$P(X = x_i, \; Y = y_j) = p_{ij}$$

**Empirical (relative frequency) estimate:**

$$\hat{p}_{ij} = f_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n}$$

This satisfies $\sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} p_{ij} = 1$.

### Step 2 — Marginal Distribution
The marginal PMF of $X$ (collapsing across all values of $Y$):

$$P(X = x_i) = p_{i\cdot} = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p_{ij}$$

**Empirical estimate:**

$$\hat{p}_{i\cdot} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot}}{n}$$

Similarly for $Y$:

$$\hat{p}_{\cdot j} = \dfrac{n_{\cdot j}}{n}$$

### Step 3 — Conditional Distribution
The conditional PMF of $Y$ given $X = x_i$:

$$P(Y = y_j \mid X = x_i) = \dfrac{p_{ij}}{p_{i\cdot}}$$

**Empirical estimate from row proportions:**

$$\hat{P}(Y = y_j \mid X = x_i) = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n_{i\cdot}}$$

Similarly, the conditional PMF of $X$ given $Y = y_j$:

$$\hat{P}(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n_{\cdot j}}$$

### Step 4 — Independence Criterion (The Core of "Association")
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two discrete variables $X$ and $Y$ are STATISTICALLY INDEPENDENT if and only if:**
> $$P(X = x_i, \; Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i) \cdot P(Y = y_j) \quad \forall i,j$$
>
> Equivalently: $p_{ij} = p_{i\cdot} \cdot p_{\cdot j}$
>
> **Equivalently (empirical):**
> $$\dfrac{n_{ij}}{n} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot}}{n} \cdot \dfrac{n_{\cdot j}}{n} \implies n_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n}$$
>
> If this equality fails for ANY cell, $X$ and $Y$ are **associated**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Relative Frequency | $f_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n}$ | Empirical joint probability |
| Row Marginal Proportion | $f_{i\cdot} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot}}{n}$ | Marginals of $X$ |
| Column Marginal Proportion | $f_{\cdot j} = \dfrac{n_{\cdot j}}{n}$ | Marginals of $Y$ |
| Row Conditional Probability | $P(Y = y_j \mid X = x_i) = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n_{i\cdot}}$ | Conditional distribution of $Y \mid X$ |
| Column Conditional Probability | $P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n_{\cdot j}}$ | Conditional distribution of $X \mid Y$ |
| Expected Frequency (under independence) | $e_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n}$ | Theoretical count in cell $(i,j)$ |
| Independence Check | $n_{ij} = e_{ij}$ for all $i,j$ | No association |
| Total Probability Rule | $\sum_j P(Y = y_j \mid X = x_i) = 1$ | Each row sums to 1 |
| Total Probability Rule | $\sum_i P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) = 1$ | Each column sums to 1 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Analytics Utility

- **Marketing Analytics:** Cross-tab of Customer Segment × Product Purchased → identifies buying patterns.
- **Medical Research:** Treatment × Outcome → assesses therapy effectiveness.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** Machine × Defect Type → finds which machine produces which defect.
- **Survey Research:** Gender × Voting Preference → public opinion studies.
- **Recommendation Systems:** User Category × Item Category → builds collaborative filtering matrices.

> [!NOTE]
> In every data-analytics workflow, the contingency table is the **first exploratory tool** applied before any chi-square test, log-linear model, or association measure (like Cramér's V) is computed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building and Reading a Contingency Table

**Problem Statement:**
A survey of **200 employees** in a software company records two variables:
- $X$: **Department** (IT, HR, Finance)
- $Y$: **Mode of Work** (Remote, On-site)

The raw counts are:

| | Remote | On-site |
|---|---|---|
| IT | 50 | 30 |
| HR | 20 | 40 |
| Finance | 10 | 50 |

### Step 1 — Verify the Grand Total

$$n = 50 + 30 + 20 + 40 + 10 + 50 = 200$$

### Step 2 — Compute Row Marginals (Distribution of Department)

$$n_{1\cdot} = 50 + 30 = 80 \quad \text{(IT)}$$
$$n_{2\cdot} = 20 + 40 = 60 \quad \text{(HR)}$$
$$n_{3\cdot} = 10 + 50 = 60 \quad \text{(Finance)}$$

Check: $80 + 60 + 60 = 200$ ✓

### Step 3 — Compute Column Marginals (Distribution of Work Mode)

$$n_{\cdot 1} = 50 + 20 + 10 = 80 \quad \text{(Remote)}$$
$$n_{\cdot 2} = 30 + 40 + 50 = 120 \quad \text{(On-site)}$$

Check: $80 + 120 = 200$ ✓

### Step 4 — Convert to Joint Relative Frequency Table

$$\hat{p}_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n}$$

| | Remote | On-site | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| IT | 50/200 = **0.25** | 30/200 = **0.15** | **0.40** |
| HR | 20/200 = **0.10** | 40/200 = **0.20** | **0.30** |
| Finance | 10/200 = **0.05** | 50/200 = **0.25** | **0.30** |
| Column Total | **0.40** | **0.60** | **1.00** |

Each cell entry represents the empirical joint probability.

### Step 5 — Compute Row Conditional Distributions ($Y \mid X$)

For **IT employees** ($X = $ IT):
$$P(\text{Remote} \mid \text{IT}) = \dfrac{n_{11}}{n_{1\cdot}} = \dfrac{50}{80} = 0.625$$
$$P(\text{On-site} \mid \text{IT}) = \dfrac{n_{12}}{n_{1\cdot}} = \dfrac{30}{80} = 0.375$$

For **HR employees** ($X = $ HR):
$$P(\text{Remote} \mid \text{HR}) = \dfrac{20}{60} = 0.333$$
$$P(\text{On-site} \mid \text{HR}) = \dfrac{40}{60} = 0.667$$

For **Finance employees** ($X = $ Finance):
$$P(\text{Remote} \mid \text{Finance}) = \dfrac{10}{60} = 0.167$$
$$P(\text{On-site} \mid \text{Finance}) = \dfrac{50}{60} = 0.833$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation (Association Evidence):**
> The conditional distributions are clearly **different across rows**:
> - IT workers: 62.5% Remote
> - HR workers: 33.3% Remote
> - Finance workers: 16.7% Remote
>
> This disparity signals that **Department and Work Mode are associated** — the row you are in changes the distribution of the column.

### Step 6 — Test Independence Empirically

Under independence, expected frequency in cell $(1,1)$ should be:

$$e_{11} = \dfrac{n_{1\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot 1}}{n} = \dfrac{80 \times 80}{200} = 32$$

But the **observed** value is $n_{11} = 50$. Since $50 \neq 32$, **independence fails**, confirming association.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proving Independence Through Cell Equality

**Problem Statement:**
Consider a $2 \times 2$ table:

| | $Y = 0$ | $Y = 1$ | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X = 0$ | 20 | 30 | 50 |
| $X = 1$ | 40 | 60 | 100 |
| Total | 60 | 90 | 150 |

### Step 1 — Compute Expected Frequencies

$$e_{11} = \dfrac{n_{1\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot 1}}{n} = \dfrac{50 \times 60}{150} = 20$$
$$e_{12} = \dfrac{n_{1\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot 2}}{n} = \dfrac{50 \times 90}{150} = 30$$
$$e_{21} = \dfrac{n_{2\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot 1}}{n} = \dfrac{100 \times 60}{150} = 40$$
$$e_{22} = \dfrac{n_{2\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot 2}}{n} = \dfrac{100 \times 90}{150} = 60$$

### Step 2 — Compare with Observed

Observed table: $\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 60 \end{pmatrix}$

Expected table: $\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 60 \end{pmatrix}$

Since **observed = expected in every cell**, the variables $X$ and $Y$ are **statistically independent** — there is **no association**.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix (Step-by-Step Recipe)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6-Step Algorithm for Summarizing the Distribution of Two Discrete Variables:**
>
> | Step | Operation | Output |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | Collect data into $r \times c$ cells | Frequency table |
> | 2 | Sum each row | Row marginals $n_{i\cdot}$ |
> | 3 | Sum each column | Column marginals $n_{\cdot j}$ |
> | 4 | Divide every cell by $n$ | Joint relative frequency table |
> | 5 | Divide each cell by its row total | Row-conditional table |
> | 6 | Divide each cell by its column total | Column-conditional table |
>
> After Step 6, **compare conditional distributions across rows/columns** — differences imply **association**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Bivariate Discrete Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Bivariate Data X, Y"] --> B["Step 1: Tally Joint Frequencies n_ij"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute Row Marginals n_i_dot"]
    B --> D["Step 3: Compute Column Marginals n_dot_j"]
    B --> E["Step 4: Compute Grand Total n"]
    C --> F["Step 5: Compute Joint Relative Frequencies f_ij = n_ij / n"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Step 6: Compute Row Conditional P(Y | X) = n_ij / n_i_dot"]
    F --> H["Step 7: Compute Column Conditional P(X | Y) = n_ij / n_dot_j"]
    G --> I{"Compare Conditional Distributions Across Rows"}
    H --> J{"Compare Conditional Distributions Across Columns"}
    I --> K["Differences Detected: ASSOCIATION Exists"]
    I --> L["All Rows Identical: INDEPENDENCE Holds"]
    J --> M["Differences Detected: ASSOCIATION Exists"]
    J --> N["All Columns Identical: INDEPENDENCE Holds"]
    K --> O["Step 8: Compute Expected Frequencies e_ij = n_i_dot x n_dot_j / n"]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P["Step 9: Report Summary Table"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Contingency Table

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph IN["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Variable X with r categories"]
        A2["Variable Y with c categories"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["CORE CONTINGENCY TABLE ENGINE"]
        B1["Joint Frequency Block n_ij"]
        B2["Marginal Frequency Block Row Totals"]
        B3["Marginal Frequency Block Column Totals"]
        B4["Grand Total n"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1["Joint Relative Frequency f_ij"]
        C2["Marginal Distributions p_i_dot, p_dot_j"]
        C3["Row Conditional Distribution P(Y | X)"]
        C4["Column Conditional Distribution P(X | Y)"]
        C5["Expected Frequencies e_ij under Independence"]
        C6["Independence Verdict: Associated or Independent"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
    B1 --> C3
    B3 --> C4
    B2 --> C4
    B2 --> C5
    B3 --> C5
    C3 --> C6
    C4 --> C6
    C1 --> C6
```

## 4.3 Visualization Sketch — Side-by-Side View of Two Tables

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LEFT["Joint Frequency Table"]
        L1["n_11"] --- L2["n_12"] --- L3["n_1c"]
        L4["n_21"] --- L5["n_22"] --- L6["n_2c"]
        L7["n_r1"] --- L8["n_r2"] --- L9["n_rc"]
    end

    subgraph RIGHT["Conditional Distribution Table Row Y given X"]
        R1["n_11 / n_1_dot"] --- R2["n_12 / n_1_dot"] --- R3["n_1c / n_1_dot"]
        R4["n_21 / n_2_dot"] --- R5["n_22 / n_2_dot"] --- R6["n_2c / n_2_dot"]
        R7["n_r1 / n_r_dot"] --- R8["n_r2 / n_r_dot"] --- R9["n_rc / n_r_dot"]
    end

    LEFT -- "Divide each cell by row total n_i_dot" --> RIGHT
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Is There Association?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Given Contingency Table n_ij"] --> T["Compute Expected Frequencies e_ij = n_i_dot x n_dot_j / n"]
    T --> Q{"Does n_ij = e_ij for ALL cells?"}
    Q -- "Yes" --> R["INDEPENDENT: No Association"]
    Q -- "No (at least one cell differs)" --> U["ASSOCIATED: Variables are related"]
    U --> V["Quantify strength using Chi-Square, Cramers V, or Phi coefficient in higher modules"]
    R --> W["Stop: Report independence conclusion"]
    U --> W
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define a contingency table. Explain the difference between joint frequency and marginal frequency with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **contingency table** is a two-way tabular representation of the joint frequency distribution of two categorical variables, where each cell $(i,j)$ contains the count $n_{ij}$ of observations belonging simultaneously to category $i$ of variable $X$ and category $j$ of variable $Y$. **(1 Mark)**

- **Joint Frequency** $n_{ij}$: Count of observations in a specific **interior cell** representing a particular combination of $X$ and $Y$. Example: number of IT employees who work remotely. **(1 Mark)**
- **Marginal Frequency** $n_{i\cdot}$ or $n_{\cdot j}$: Count obtained by **summing** a row or column, representing the total for one variable irrespective of the other. Example: total number of IT employees (sum of Remote + On-site for IT). **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State the condition for statistical independence of two discrete variables in terms of joint and marginal probabilities.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Two discrete variables $X$ and $Y$ are **statistically independent** if and only if for every combination of categories $(x_i, y_j)$:

$$P(X = x_i, Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i) \cdot P(Y = y_j)$$

That is, $p_{ij} = p_{i\cdot} \cdot p_{\cdot j}$ for all $i,j$. **(2 Marks)**

Empirically, this translates to the observed frequency equaling the expected frequency in every cell:

$$n_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n}$$

If this condition holds in every cell, the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X$ does not depend on $X$, and there is **no association** between the variables. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 2 Choice A]`

**The following data was collected from 250 customers of an e-commerce website, classifying them by Age Group ($X$: Young, Middle-aged, Senior) and Purchase Category ($Y$: Electronics, Clothing, Books).**

| | Electronics | Clothing | Books | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young | 40 | 50 | 30 | **120** |
| Middle-aged | 30 | 35 | 15 | **80** |
| Senior | 10 | 15 | 25 | **50** |
| **Total** | **80** | **100** | **70** | **250** |

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Compute the joint relative frequency table, the marginal distributions of $X$ and $Y$, and the row conditional distributions $P(Y \mid X)$. Comment on whether Age Group and Purchase Category appear to be associated.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Joint Relative Frequency Table** [2 Marks]

$f_{ij} = n_{ij} / n = n_{ij} / 250$

| | Electronics | Clothing | Books | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young | 40/250 = 0.16 | 50/250 = 0.20 | 30/250 = 0.12 | 0.48 |
| Middle-aged | 30/250 = 0.12 | 35/250 = 0.14 | 15/250 = 0.06 | 0.32 |
| Senior | 10/250 = 0.04 | 15/250 = 0.06 | 25/250 = 0.10 | 0.20 |
| Total | 0.32 | 0.40 | 0.28 | 1.00 |

**(ii) Marginal Distributions** [2 Marks]

Marginal of $X$: $P(\text{Young}) = 0.48$, $P(\text{Middle-aged}) = 0.32$, $P(\text{Senior}) = 0.20$.

Marginal of $Y$: $P(\text{Electronics}) = 0.32$, $P(\text{Clothing}) = 0.40$, $P(\text{Books}) = 0.28$.

**(iii) Row Conditional Distribution $P(Y \mid X)$** [2 Marks]

For Young ($n_{1\cdot} = 120$):
$$P(\text{Elec} \mid \text{Young}) = 40/120 = 0.333, \quad P(\text{Cloth} \mid \text{Young}) = 50/120 = 0.417, \quad P(\text{Books} \mid \text{Young}) = 30/120 = 0.250$$

For Middle-aged ($n_{2\cdot} = 80$):
$$P(\text{Elec} \mid \text{Middle}) = 30/80 = 0.375, \quad P(\text{Cloth} \mid \text{Middle}) = 35/80 = 0.4375, \quad P(\text{Books} \mid \text{Middle}) = 15/80 = 0.1875$$

For Senior ($n_{3\cdot} = 50$):
$$P(\text{Elec} \mid \text{Senior}) = 10/50 = 0.20, \quad P(\text{Cloth} \mid \text{Senior}) = 15/50 = 0.30, \quad P(\text{Books} \mid \text{Senior}) = 25/50 = 0.50$$

**(iv) Association Comment** [1 Mark]

The conditional distributions **differ noticeably** across rows. For instance, $P(\text{Books} \mid \text{Senior}) = 0.50$ but $P(\text{Books} \mid \text{Young}) = 0.25$. Since the conditional distribution of Purchase Category depends on the Age Group, **Age Group and Purchase Category are associated**.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Compute the expected frequencies $e_{ij}$ under the assumption of independence. Identify the cells with the largest discrepancy between observed and expected counts.**

**Model Solution:**

Expected frequency formula: $e_{ij} = (n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}) / n$

| Cell | Calculation | Expected $e_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $e_{11}$ (Young, Electronics) | $(120 \times 80)/250$ | **38.4** |
| $e_{12}$ (Young, Clothing) | $(120 \times 100)/250$ | **48.0** |
| $e_{13}$ (Young, Books) | $(120 \times 70)/250$ | **33.6** |
| $e_{21}$ (Middle, Electronics) | $(80 \times 80)/250$ | **25.6** |
| $e_{22}$ (Middle, Clothing) | $(80 \times 100)/250$ | **32.0** |
| $e_{23}$ (Middle, Books) | $(80 \times 70)/250$ | **22.4** |
| $e_{31}$ (Senior, Electronics) | $(50 \times 80)/250$ | **16.0** |
| $e_{32}$ (Senior, Clothing) | $(50 \times 100)/250$ | **20.0** |
| $e_{33}$ (Senior, Books) | $(50 \times 70)/250$ | **14.0** |

[5 Marks for correct table]

**Discrepancies $|n_{ij} - e_{ij}|$:**

| Cell | Observed | Expected | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young, Electronics | 40 | 38.4 | 1.6 |
| Young, Clothing | 50 | 48.0 | 2.0 |
| Young, Books | 30 | 33.6 | 3.6 |
| Middle, Electronics | 30 | 25.6 | 4.4 |
| Middle, Clothing | 35 | 32.0 | 3.0 |
| Middle, Books | 15 | 22.4 | 7.4 |
| Senior, Electronics | 10 | 16.0 | 6.0 |
| Senior, Clothing | 15 | 20.0 | 5.0 |
| **Senior, Books** | **25** | **14.0** | **11.0** |

[1 Mark for table]

**Conclusion** [1 Mark]: The **largest discrepancy** is in cell **(Senior, Books)**, where observed = 25 but expected = 14. This shows seniors purchase books **far more often** than what independence would predict — a clear signature of association.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Module 2 Choice B]`

**In a study of 180 students, the variables recorded are Gender ($X$: Male, Female) and Preferred Learning Mode ($Y$: Online, Offline, Hybrid). The data are as follows:**

| | Online | Offline | Hybrid | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Male | 35 | 25 | 20 | **80** |
| Female | 30 | 40 | 30 | **100** |
| **Total** | **65** | **65** | **50** | **180** |

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**(i) Compute the column conditional distributions $P(X \mid Y)$ and interpret the results. (ii) Determine whether Gender and Learning Mode are associated.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Column Conditional Distributions** [5 Marks]

For Online ($n_{\cdot 1} = 65$):
$$P(\text{Male} \mid \text{Online}) = 35/65 \approx 0.538$$
$$P(\text{Female} \mid \text{Online}) = 30/65 \approx 0.462$$

For Offline ($n_{\cdot 2} = 65$):
$$P(\text{Male} \mid \text{Offline}) = 25/65 \approx 0.385$$
$$P(\text{Female} \mid \text{Offline}) = 40/65 \approx 0.615$$

For Hybrid ($n_{\cdot 3} = 50$):
$$P(\text{Male} \mid \text{Hybrid}) = 20/50 = 0.400$$
$$P(\text{Female} \mid \text{Hybrid}) = 30/50 = 0.600$$

**Interpretation** [1 Mark]: Among Online learners, males slightly outnumber females; among Offline and Hybrid learners, females clearly outnumber males.

**(ii) Association Conclusion** [1 Mark]

The conditional distributions **differ across columns** (e.g., male proportion is 53.8% for Online vs 38.5% for Offline), so **Gender and Learning Mode are associated**.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Calculate the expected frequencies under independence and determine the cell with maximum and minimum absolute deviation. State the independence hypothesis clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Independence Hypothesis** [1 Mark]:
$H_0: X$ and $Y$ are independent, i.e., $P(X = x_i, Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i) \cdot P(Y = y_j)$ for all $i, j$.

**Expected Frequencies** [5 Marks]:

| Cell | Calculation | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| $e_{11}$ (Male, Online) | $(80 \times 65)/180$ | **28.89** |
| $e_{12}$ (Male, Offline) | $(80 \times 65)/180$ | **28.89** |
| $e_{13}$ (Male, Hybrid) | $(80 \times 50)/180$ | **22.22** |
| $e_{21}$ (Female, Online) | $(100 \times 65)/180$ | **36.11** |
| $e_{22}$ (Female, Offline) | $(100 \times 65)/180$ | **36.11** |
| $e_{23}$ (Female, Hybrid) | $(100 \times 50)/180$ | **27.78** |

**Absolute Deviations** [1 Mark]:

| Cell | Observed | Expected | $\|n_{ij} - e_{ij}\|$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Male, Online | 35 | 28.89 | 6.11 |
| Male, Offline | 25 | 28.89 | 3.89 |
| Male, Hybrid | 20 | 22.22 | 2.22 |
| Female, Online | 30 | 36.11 | 6.11 |
| Female, Offline | 40 | 36.11 | 3.89 |
| **Female, Hybrid** | **30** | **27.78** | **2.22** |

**Maximum Deviation**: Tied between (Male, Online) and (Female, Online) at **6.11**.
**Minimum Deviation**: Tied at **2.22** for (Male, Hybrid) and (Female, Hybrid).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $n$**: Students often report $n_{ij}$ as the joint probability. Always convert to relative frequencies by dividing by the grand total $n$.
> 2. **Mixing row vs column conditionals**: $P(Y \mid X)$ uses **row totals** in the denominator; $P(X \mid Y)$ uses **column totals**. Swapping these is a frequent, heavy-mark-loss error.
> 3. **Not summing each row of conditional table to 1**: Verifiers deduct marks if your $P(Y \mid X)$ row does not sum to exactly 1.
> 4. **Confusing marginal and joint**: Marginals are on the **edges** of the table; joint values are in the **interior cells**.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment on association" step**: KTU examiners explicitly award 1 mark for the interpretive conclusion. Always end with "since conditional distributions differ, variables are associated" or its converse.
> 6. **Arithmetic slips in $e_{ij}$**: Expected frequency formula $\frac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n}$ must be applied cell by cell — one slip propagates into all subsequent deviation calculations.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **contingency table** is the primary tool for summarizing the joint distribution of two discrete variables.
- **Joint frequencies** $n_{ij}$ live inside the table; **marginal frequencies** $n_{i\cdot}$ and $n_{\cdot j}$ lie on the borders; **grand total** $n$ is the corner.
- **Relative frequencies** $f_{ij} = n_{ij}/n$ approximate **joint probabilities** $p_{ij}$.
- **Row conditional** $P(Y = y_j \mid X = x_i) = n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$ — each row sums to **1**.
- **Column conditional** $P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) = n_{ij}/n_{\cdot j}$ — each column sums to **1**.
- **Independence** holds iff $n_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n}$ for every cell, equivalently iff all row conditionals are identical.
- **Association** exists when conditional distributions differ across rows (or equivalently across columns).
- **Expected frequencies** $e_{ij} = (n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j})/n$ are the foundation for chi-square tests in Module 3.
- Always **state the association conclusion** explicitly after computing conditionals — KTU awards dedicated marks for it.
- Verify table arithmetic: **row totals sum = column totals sum = grand total $n$**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contingency Tables for Discrete Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contingency Tables for Discrete Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Contingency Table** (also called a *cross-tabulation* or *crosstab*) is a matrix-format display of the **frequency distribution** of two or more **categorical (discrete) variables**, where each cell of the table represents the **joint frequency** (count) of observations falling into a specific combination of categories for the row variable and the column variable.

Formally, for two discrete random variables $X$ (with $r$ categories) and $Y$ (with $c$ categories), observed on $N$ independent trials, the contingency table is the $r \times c$ matrix $[O_{ij}]$ where:

$$O_{ij} = \text{number of observations with } X = x_i \text{ and } Y = y_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Contingency tables are the **gateway construct** for performing the Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test of Independence, the Chi-Square Test of Homogeneity, and several **measures of association** (Phi, Cramer's V, Contingency Coefficient). Mastery of expected frequency computation is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Survey" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a classroom of **200 students** and ask each student two yes/no questions:
1. *"Do you prefer Tea or Coffee?"* (Variable $X$)
2. *"Are you a Day Scholar or Hosteller?"* (Variable $Y$)

You then draw a **2 × 2 grid** on the board and **tally the counts** into the four cells:

| | Day Scholar | Hosteller | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tea | 60 | 50 | 110 |
| Coffee | 40 | 50 | 90 |
| **Col Total** | 100 | 100 | **200** |

Each cell now holds a **joint count**. The question contingency analysis answers is:

> *"Is the preference for Tea/Coffee **independent** of being a Day Scholar/Hosteller — or is there a **statistical association** between the two?"*

This is the entire essence of contingency analysis — **detect patterns of dependence in discrete count data**.

## 1.3 Standard Notation & Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value (Example) |
|---|---|---|
| $O_{ij}$ | Observed frequency in cell $(i,j)$ | 60 |
| $E_{ij}$ | Expected frequency in cell $(i,j)$ | 55 |
| $R_i$ | Row total for row $i$ | 110 |
| $C_j$ | Column total for column $j$ | 100 |
| $N$ | Grand total of all observations | 200 |
| $r$ | Number of rows | 2 |
| $c$ | Number of columns | 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom** for an $r \times c$ contingency table under the null hypothesis of independence is:
> 
> $$df = (r - 1)(c - 1)$$
> 
> For a $2 \times 2$ table, $df = 1$, which is the most common case in KTU board problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Architecture of Contingency Analysis

The procedure of contingency analysis proceeds in **five rigorous stages**:

### Step 1 — Tabulation of Observed Frequencies
Construct the $r \times c$ matrix $[O_{ij}]$ directly from raw categorical data. Compute row totals $R_i$, column totals $C_j$, and grand total $N$.

### Step 2 — Computation of Expected Frequencies
Under the **null hypothesis $H_0$** that the two variables are *independent*, the expected count in cell $(i,j)$ is derived from the **product of marginals** divided by the grand total:

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$$

**Why this works:** If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then $P(X = x_i \cap Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i) \cdot P(Y = y_j)$. Estimating the marginal probabilities as $R_i / N$ and $C_j / N$, and multiplying by $N$ gives $E_{ij}$.

### Step 3 — Computation of the Test Statistic
The **Pearson Chi-Square statistic** quantifies the *total squared deviation* of observed from expected counts, normalized by expected counts:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

A **large** $\chi^2$ value indicates that the observed data **diverges significantly** from what independence predicts, thereby **rejecting $H_0$**.

### Step 4 — Decision Rule
Compare $\chi^2_{\text{computed}}$ with the **critical value** $\chi^2_{\alpha, df}$ from the chi-square distribution table.

- If $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} > \chi^2_{\alpha, df}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (variables are associated)
- If $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} \leq \chi^2_{\alpha, df}$ → **Fail to Reject $H_0$** (no evidence of association)

### Step 5 — Strength of Association (Effect Size)
Statistical significance alone does **not** convey the *strength* of the relationship. We compute standardized effect-size measures (Phi, Cramer's V, or Contingency Coefficient).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name | Use Case / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$ | Expected Frequency | **Assumes $H_0$: Independence** |
| 2 | $\chi^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{i,j} \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Pearson Chi-Square Statistic | Requires $E_{ij} \geq 5$ for validity |
| 3 | $df = (r-1)(c-1)$ | Degrees of Freedom | $r$ rows, $c$ columns |
| 4 | $G^2 = 2 \displaystyle\sum_{i,j} O_{ij} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{O_{ij}}{E_{ij}}\right)$ | Likelihood Ratio Statistic | Asymptotically $\sim \chi^2$ |
| 5 | $\phi = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N}}$ | Phi Coefficient | **Only for $2 \times 2$ tables**; $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$ |
| 6 | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1, c-1)}}$ | Cramer's V | General $r \times c$ tables; $0 \leq V \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + N}}$ | Contingency Coefficient | Always $C < 1$; never reaches 1 |
| 8 | $\chi^2_{\text{Yates}} = \displaystyle\sum \dfrac{(\vert O_{ij} - E_{ij} \vert - 0.5)^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Yates' Continuity Correction | Used for $2 \times 2$ with small samples |

## 2.3 Assumptions & Engineering Validity Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Mandatory Assumptions for Valid Chi-Square Inference:**
> 1. **Random Sampling** — observations must be independent.
> 2. **Mutually Exclusive Categories** — each observation falls into exactly one cell.
> 3. **Sufficient Expected Frequency** — *all* $E_{ij} \geq 5$. If violated, use **Fisher's Exact Test** (for $2 \times 2$) or **pool categories**.
> 4. **Categorical Variables** — both variables are nominal/ordinal discrete.
> 5. **Adequate Sample Size** — typically $N \geq 50$ for stable inference.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Data Analytics Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare Analytics** | Testing independence of smoking status and lung disease incidence |
| **Marketing Analytics** | Cross-tab of customer demographic vs product preference |
| **Quality Engineering** | Testing homogeneity of defect rates across multiple production lines |
| **A/B Testing in Tech** | Chi-square test on conversion rates for UI variant A vs B |
| **Survey Research** | Determining whether two survey questions yield correlated responses |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Worked Example (KTU Board Style — 14 Mark Format)

### Problem Statement

A clinical trial studies the effect of a new drug on patient recovery. **200 patients** are randomly assigned: **100 to the Drug group** and **100 to the Placebo group**. After 4 weeks, the recovery outcomes are recorded in the following contingency table:

|  | Improved | Not Improved | **Row Total** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Drug** | 70 | 30 | 100 |
| **Placebo** | 50 | 50 | 100 |
| **Column Total** | 120 | 80 | **200** |

**Test at the $\alpha = 0.05$ significance level** whether the recovery outcome is **independent** of the treatment type. Also compute the **Phi coefficient** and **Cramer's V** to measure the strength of association.

---

### Solution — Stage 1: State the Hypotheses

Let $X$ = Treatment Type and $Y$ = Recovery Outcome.

- **Null Hypothesis $H_0$:** Recovery outcome is **independent** of treatment type.
- **Alternative Hypothesis $H_1$:** Recovery outcome is **associated** with treatment type.

Significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$, Degrees of freedom: $df = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$.

Critical value from chi-square table: $\chi^2_{0.05, \, 1} = 3.841$.

---

### Solution — Stage 2: Compute Expected Frequencies

Using $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$:

**Cell (1,1) — Drug, Improved:**

$$E_{11} = \frac{R_1 \cdot C_1}{N} = \frac{100 \times 120}{200} = 60$$

**Cell (1,2) — Drug, Not Improved:**

$$E_{12} = \frac{R_1 \cdot C_2}{N} = \frac{100 \times 80}{200} = 40$$

**Cell (2,1) — Placebo, Improved:**

$$E_{21} = \frac{R_2 \cdot C_1}{N} = \frac{100 \times 120}{200} = 60$$

**Cell (2,2) — Placebo, Not Improved:**

$$E_{22} = \frac{R_2 \cdot C_2}{N} = \frac{100 \times 80}{200} = 40$$

**Verification:** $\sum E_{ij} = 60 + 40 + 60 + 40 = 200 = N$ ✓

**Expected Frequency Table:**

|  | Improved | Not Improved | Row Total |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Drug** | 60 | 40 | 100 |
| **Placebo** | 60 | 40 | 100 |
| **Col Total** | 120 | 80 | 200 |

All $E_{ij} \geq 5$ ✓ (assumption satisfied)

---

### Solution — Stage 3: Compute the Chi-Square Statistic

Apply $\chi^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{i,j} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ cell-by-cell:

**Cell (1,1):**

$$\frac{(O_{11} - E_{11})^2}{E_{11}} = \frac{(70 - 60)^2}{60} = \frac{100}{60} = 1.6667$$

**Cell (1,2):**

$$\frac{(O_{12} - E_{12})^2}{E_{12}} = \frac{(30 - 40)^2}{40} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5000$$

**Cell (2,1):**

$$\frac{(O_{21} - E_{21})^2}{E_{21}} = \frac{(50 - 60)^2}{60} = \frac{100}{60} = 1.6667$$

**Cell (2,2):**

$$\frac{(O_{22} - E_{22})^2}{E_{22}} = \frac{(50 - 40)^2}{40} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5000$$

**Sum all four contributions:**

$$\chi^2 = 1.6667 + 2.5000 + 1.6667 + 2.5000 = 8.3333$$

---

### Solution — Stage 4: Decision

Compare the computed statistic with the critical value:

$$\chi^2_{\text{computed}} = 8.3333 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$$

Since $8.3333 > 3.841$, we **reject the null hypothesis $H_0$** at the 5% significance level.

> **Conclusion:** There is **sufficient statistical evidence** to conclude that recovery outcome is **not independent** of treatment type — i.e., the drug has a **significant effect** on patient recovery.

---

### Solution — Stage 5: Measure Strength of Association

**Phi Coefficient (for $2 \times 2$ table):**

$$\phi = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.3333}{200}} = \sqrt{0.04167} \approx 0.2041$$

**Cramer's V (general formula, $2 \times 2$ case):**

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1, c-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.3333}{200 \times 1}} = \sqrt{0.04167} \approx 0.2041$$

> **Interpretation:** Both $\phi$ and $V$ are approximately **0.20**, indicating a **weak-to-moderate** positive association between treatment type and recovery outcome. The relationship is **statistically significant** but **not very strong** in magnitude.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing the Right Test

| Observed Table Type | $E_{ij}$ Condition | Recommended Test |
|---|---|---|
| $2 \times 2$ | All $E_{ij} \geq 5$ | Pearson Chi-Square Test |
| $2 \times 2$ | Any $E_{ij} < 5$ | Fisher's Exact Test |
| $2 \times 2$ | $5 \leq E_{ij} < 10$, borderline | Yates' Corrected Chi-Square |
| $r \times c$ ($r, c > 2$) | All $E_{ij} \geq 5$ | Pearson Chi-Square Test |
| $r \times c$ | Some $E_{ij} < 5$ | Pool categories / use exact methods |
| One variable ordinal | Any | Cochran-Armitage Trend Test (advanced) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Chi-Square Test of Independence

The following diagram depicts the **end-to-end operational pipeline** for performing a chi-square test of independence on a contingency table:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Categorical Data X and Y] --> B[Stage 1: Construct Observed Frequency Table O_ij]
    B --> C[Compute Row Totals R_i and Column Totals C_j and Grand Total N]
    C --> D[Stage 2: Compute Expected Frequencies E_ij equals R_i times C_j divided by N]
    D --> E{Stage 3: Check Validity E_ij greater than or equal to 5 for all cells}
    E -->|No| F[Use Fisher Exact Test or Pool Categories]
    E -->|Yes| G[Stage 4: Compute Chi Square Statistic sum of O minus E squared divided by E]
    G --> H[Stage 5: Determine Degrees of Freedom df equals r minus 1 times c minus 1]
    H --> I[Stage 6: Lookup Critical Value chi square alpha df from Table]
    I --> J{Stage 7: Decision Rule compare Computed with Critical}
    J -->|Reject H0| K[Conclusion Variables are Associated]
    J -->|Fail to Reject H0| L[Conclusion No Evidence of Association]
    K --> M[Stage 8: Compute Effect Size Phi Cramer V Contingency C]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Report with p value and Interpretation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Measures of Association Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chi Square Test Statistic] --> B[Significance Inference]
    A --> C[Effect Size Computation]
    C --> D[Phi Coefficient]
    C --> E[Cramer V]
    C --> F[Coefficient C]
    D -->|Only 2x2| G[Bounds 0 to 1]
    E -->|General rxc| H[Bounds 0 to 1]
    F -->|General rxc| I[Bounds 0 to less than 1]
    B --> J[Reject or Fail to Reject H0]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define a contingency table. State the formula for the expected frequency in a contingency table under the assumption of independence.**

**Model Answer:**

A **contingency table** is a two-way frequency distribution table that displays the joint counts of two categorical (discrete) variables, where rows represent categories of one variable, columns represent categories of the other, and each cell $O_{ij}$ contains the observed frequency of observations sharing both categories.

Under the null hypothesis of independence, the expected frequency in cell $(i,j)$ is:

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$$

where $R_i$ is the row total, $C_j$ is the column total, and $N$ is the grand total. This formula arises from the product of marginal probabilities under independence: $P(X_i \cap Y_j) = P(X_i) \cdot P(Y_j)$, estimated as $(R_i/N) \cdot (C_j/N) \cdot N$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Distinguish between the Chi-Square Test of Independence and the Chi-Square Test of Homogeneity. Mention when each is applied.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Test of Independence | Test of Homogeneity |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Scheme** | **One** sample measured on two variables | **Multiple** samples from different populations measured on one variable |
| **Question Answered** | Are the two variables associated? | Do the populations have the same distribution? |
| **Row/Column Role** | Symmetric — both are categorical variables | Columns are the categorical variable, rows are populations |
| **Hypotheses** | $H_0$: $X$ and $Y$ are independent | $H_0$: All populations have identical proportions |
| **Computation** | Identical formula for $\chi^2$ | Identical formula for $\chi^2$ |

**Example:** Independence test → "Is smoking related to lung disease?" Homogeneity test → "Is the proportion of recovered patients the same across three hospitals?"

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 2 contains 14-mark questions, with internal choice between two questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**A survey of 250 customers was conducted to study the relationship between gender (Male/Female) and preference for online vs. offline shopping. The observed data is as follows:**

|  | Online | Offline | **Row Total** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Male** | 80 | 50 | 130 |
| **Female** | 60 | 60 | 120 |
| **Column Total** | 140 | 110 | **250** |

**(a)** Compute the expected frequencies under $H_0$: independence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform the Chi-Square Test of Independence at $\alpha = 0.05$. Also compute the Phi coefficient. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Shopping preference is independent of gender
- $H_1$: Shopping preference is associated with gender

**Expected Frequencies** using $E_{ij} = R_i \cdot C_j / N$:

$$E_{11} = \frac{130 \times 140}{250} = \frac{18200}{250} = 72.80$$

$$E_{12} = \frac{130 \times 110}{250} = \frac{14300}{250} = 57.20$$

$$E_{21} = \frac{120 \times 140}{250} = \frac{16800}{250} = 67.20$$

$$E_{22} = \frac{120 \times 110}{250} = \frac{13200}{250} = 52.80$$

**Expected Table:**

|  | Online | Offline | Total |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Male** | 72.80 | 57.20 | 130 |
| **Female** | 67.20 | 52.80 | 120 |
| **Total** | 140 | 110 | 250 |

Verification: $72.80 + 57.20 + 67.20 + 52.80 = 250$ ✓. All $E_{ij} \geq 5$ ✓.

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Chi-Square Computation:**

$$\chi^2 = \frac{(80 - 72.80)^2}{72.80} + \frac{(50 - 57.20)^2}{57.20} + \frac{(60 - 67.20)^2}{67.20} + \frac{(60 - 52.80)^2}{52.80}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{(7.20)^2}{72.80} = \frac{51.84}{72.80} = 0.7121$$

$$\frac{(-7.20)^2}{57.20} = \frac{51.84}{57.20} = 0.9063$$

$$\frac{(-7.20)^2}{67.20} = \frac{51.84}{67.20} = 0.7714$$

$$\frac{(7.20)^2}{52.80} = \frac{51.84}{52.80} = 0.9818$$

**Summing:**

$$\chi^2 = 0.7121 + 0.9063 + 0.7714 + 0.9818 = 3.3716$$

**Degrees of Freedom:** $df = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$

**Critical Value at $\alpha = 0.05$:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$

**Decision:** Since $3.3716 < 3.841$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that shopping preference is associated with gender.

**Phi Coefficient:**

$$\phi = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.3716}{250}} = \sqrt{0.01349} \approx 0.1161$$

A Phi of 0.116 indicates a **very weak** association.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**A study compares defect rates across three factories (A, B, C) producing the same product. A random sample of 300 items is inspected with the following results:**

| Factory | Defective | Non-Defective | **Total** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 25 | 95 | 120 |
| B | 30 | 90 | 120 |
| C | 20 | 40 | 60 |
| **Total** | 75 | 225 | **300** |

**(a)** Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the proportion of defectives is the **same** across all three factories using the Chi-Square Test of Homogeneity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute Cramer's V to measure the strength of any association, and interpret your findings. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: The proportion of defectives is **homogeneous** (same) across all three factories.
- $H_1$: At least one factory has a **different** defective proportion.

**Expected Frequencies** using $E_{ij} = R_i \cdot C_j / N$:

**Row A (total 120):**

$$E_{A,\text{def}} = \frac{120 \times 75}{300} = 30.00 \quad ; \quad E_{A,\text{non}} = \frac{120 \times 225}{300} = 90.00$$

**Row B (total 120):**

$$E_{B,\text{def}} = \frac{120 \times 75}{300} = 30.00 \quad ; \quad E_{B,\text{non}} = \frac{120 \times 225}{300} = 90.00$$

**Row C (total 60):**

$$E_{C,\text{def}} = \frac{60 \times 75}{300} = 15.00 \quad ; \quad E_{C,\text{non}} = \frac{60 \times 225}{300} = 45.00$$

**Expected Table:**

| Factory | Defective | Non-Defective | Total |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 30 | 90 | 120 |
| B | 30 | 90 | 120 |
| C | 15 | 45 | 60 |
| Total | 75 | 225 | 300 |

All $E_{ij} \geq 5$ ✓.

**Chi-Square Computation** (six cells):

$$\chi^2 = \frac{(25-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(95-90)^2}{90} + \frac{(30-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(90-90)^2}{90} + \frac{(20-15)^2}{15} + \frac{(40-45)^2}{45}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{25}{30} = 0.8333 \quad ; \quad \frac{25}{90} = 0.2778 \quad ; \quad \frac{0}{30} = 0.0000 \quad ; \quad \frac{0}{90} = 0.0000 \quad ; \quad \frac{25}{15} = 1.6667 \quad ; \quad \frac{25}{45} = 0.5556$$

**Sum:**

$$\chi^2 = 0.8333 + 0.2778 + 0.0000 + 0.0000 + 1.6667 + 0.5556 = 3.3334$$

**Degrees of Freedom:** $df = (r-1)(c-1) = (3-1)(2-1) = 2$

**Critical Value at $\alpha = 0.05$, df = 2:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 2} = 5.991$

**Decision:** Since $3.3334 < 5.991$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the proportion of defectives differs across the three factories — the defect rate is statistically **homogeneous**.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Cramer's V Computation:**

For a $3 \times 2$ table, $\min(r-1, c-1) = \min(2, 1) = 1$.

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1, c-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.3334}{300 \times 1}} = \sqrt{0.01111} \approx 0.1054$$

**Interpretation:** Cramer's $V \approx 0.105$ indicates a **very weak** association between the factory and the defect status. This aligns with our failure to reject $H_0$ — even though the test statistic was non-zero, the magnitude of association is **practically negligible**.

**Engineering Insight:** Quality control teams can conclude that the **manufacturing process is consistent** across all three factories, supporting the case for **standardized production protocols**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $E_{ij} \geq 5$** before applying the test (2-mark deduction if skipped).
> 2. **Computing $df$ incorrectly** as $r \cdot c$ instead of $(r-1)(c-1)$ — this is the **#1 mistake** in KTU scripts.
> 3. **Stating "Accept $H_0$"** instead of "Fail to reject $H_0$" — KTU uses strict null hypothesis language.
> 4. **Omitting the effect-size measure** (Phi or Cramer's V) — full 14 marks require both inferential test **and** strength-of-association analysis.
> 5. **Arithmetic errors in expected frequencies** — always verify $\sum E_{ij} = N$ as a sanity check.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Contingency table** = matrix of joint frequencies of two discrete variables, dimensions $r \times c$.
- **Expected frequency** $E_{ij} = (R_i \cdot C_j) / N$ — derived from independence assumption.
- **Chi-Square statistic** $\chi^2 = \sum (O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2 / E_{ij}$ — large value = strong evidence against independence.
- **Degrees of freedom** $df = (r-1)(c-1)$.
- **Validity condition:** All $E_{ij} \geq 5$; otherwise use Fisher's Exact Test (for $2 \times 2$).
- **Phi coefficient** is restricted to $2 \times 2$ tables; **Cramer's V** works for any $r \times c$.
- **Test of Independence** vs **Test of Homogeneity** — same formula, different sampling designs.
- **Yates' correction** is applied for $2 \times 2$ tables with borderline small samples to improve approximation.
- Always **report both significance (p-value or critical value comparison) and effect size** for a complete KTU answer.
- **Contingency coefficient $C$** never reaches 1, so it cannot express perfect association.
- **Likelihood ratio statistic $G^2$** is an alternative to Pearson's $\chi^2$, asymptotically equivalent.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint, Marginal, and Conditional Frequency Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Joint, Marginal, and Conditional Frequency Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **bivariate data analysis** (the simultaneous study of two random variables $X$ and $Y$), the **Joint, Marginal, and Conditional Frequency Distributions** form the foundational triad used to summarize, visualize, and infer relationships in contingency tables (also called two-way classification tables).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint Frequency Distribution (Bivariate Frequency Distribution):**
> Let $X$ take values $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_m$ and $Y$ take values $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$ in a sample of size $N$. The **joint frequency** $f(x_i, y_j)$ is the number of observations in the sample that simultaneously exhibit $X = x_i$ and $Y = y_j$. The complete collection $\{f(x_i, y_j)\}$ over all $(i, j)$ pairs is called the **joint frequency distribution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marginal Frequency Distribution:**
> The **marginal frequency** of $X = x_i$ is obtained by summing the joint frequencies over all possible values of $Y$:
> $$f_X(x_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f(x_i, y_j)$$
> Similarly, the **marginal frequency** of $Y = y_j$ is:
> $$f_Y(y_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} f(x_i, y_j)$$
> These sums correspond to the **row totals** and **column totals** of the contingency table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Frequency Distribution:**
> The **conditional frequency** of $X = x_i$ given that $Y = y_j$ has occurred is:
> $$f(x_i \mid y_j) = \frac{f(x_i, y_j)}{f_Y(y_j)}, \quad \text{provided } f_Y(y_j) > 0$$
> This represents the row-wise (or column-wise) relative frequency after restricting attention to a fixed level of the conditioning variable.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Library Card" Analogy

Imagine a librarian who records two things about every visitor: (1) the **Genre** of book borrowed (Fiction or Non-Fiction) and (2) the **Age Group** of the visitor (Child or Adult). The librarian maintains a tally sheet with **four boxes**, one for each combination:

- Fiction ∩ Child
- Fiction ∩ Adult
- Non-Fiction ∩ Child
- Non-Fiction ∩ Adult

|               | **Child** | **Adult** | **Total** |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Fiction**   | $a$       | $b$       | $a+b$     |
| **Non-Fiction** | $c$     | $d$       | $c+d$     |
| **Total**     | $a+c$     | $b+d$     | $N$       |

- The **number in each box** ($a, b, c, d$) is the **joint frequency** — it answers *"How many visitors borrowed this combination?"*
- The **row totals** ($a+b$, $c+d$) and **column totals** ($a+c$, $b+d$) are **marginal frequencies** — they answer *"How many in total borrowed Fiction?"* (ignoring age) or *"How many Children in total?"* (ignoring genre).
- If we focus **only on the Children column**, the ratios $a/(a+c)$ and $c/(a+c)$ tell us the **conditional distribution** of genre *given* the visitor is a child.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *Joint* = the intersection box. *Marginal* = totals written on the edges (margins) of the table. *Conditional* = the relative frequencies inside a single row or column after slicing the table.

## 1.3 Key Distinction: Frequency vs. Relative Frequency vs. Probability

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint Frequency | $f(x_i, y_j)$ | Count of $(x_i, y_j)$ pairs | $\geq 0$ |
| Joint Relative Frequency | $f_{rel}(x_i, y_j)$ | $f(x_i, y_j) / N$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Joint Probability (empirical) | $P(X=x_i, Y=y_j)$ | $f(x_i, y_j) / N$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Marginal Frequency | $f_X(x_i)$ | $\sum_j f(x_i, y_j)$ | $\geq 0$ |
| Marginal Probability | $P(X=x_i)$ | $\sum_j P(X=x_i, Y=y_j)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Conditional Frequency | $f(x_i \mid y_j)$ | $f(x_i, y_j) / f_Y(y_j)$ | $\geq 0$ |
| Conditional Probability | $P(X=x_i \mid Y=y_j)$ | $P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) / P(Y=y_j)$ | $[0, 1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **frequentist interpretation** of probability (the one used throughout the KTU Data Analytics syllabus), probabilities are simply relative frequencies when the sample size $N$ becomes large: $P(A) \approx f_A / N$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Structural Logic — How the Three Distributions Relate

The relationship between joint, marginal, and conditional distributions is governed by a single fundamental principle: **the law of total probability expressed through counting**.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Start with raw observations.** A sample of $N$ paired observations $(x_k, y_k)$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, N$ is collected.
2. **Construct the contingency table.** Each observation falls into exactly one cell $(i, j)$. Counting observations in each cell gives $f(x_i, y_j)$.
3. **Verify the closure property.** The sum of all joint frequencies must equal the total sample size:
   $$\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} f(x_i, y_j) = N$$
4. **Extract marginals by summation.** To get the marginal of $X$, "ignore" $Y$ by summing down each column of $X$'s row. To get the marginal of $Y$, sum across each row of $Y$'s column.
5. **Normalize to obtain conditionals.** The conditional frequency is simply the joint frequency expressed as a fraction of its relevant marginal. The denominator is the "universe" we are conditioning on.
6. **Convert to probabilities** by dividing every frequency by $N$ (or every marginal sum by the appropriate marginal total).

## 2.2 The Multiplication Rule and Independence

The cornerstone identity of bivariate analysis is the **multiplication rule**:

$$P(X = x_i, Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) \cdot P(Y = y_j)$$

In frequency form:

$$f(x_i, y_j) = f(x_i \mid y_j) \cdot f_Y(y_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Statistical Independence (Frequency Form):**
> Two categorical variables $X$ and $Y$ are said to be **statistically independent** if and only if for every cell $(i, j)$:
> $$f(x_i, y_j) = \frac{f_X(x_i) \cdot f_Y(y_j)}{N}$$
> Equivalently, the conditional distribution equals the marginal:
> $$P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i) \quad \text{for all } i, j$$
> When this holds, knowledge of $Y$ provides **zero information** about $X$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint Frequency | $f(x_i, y_j)$ | Non-negative integer; counts of paired observations |
| 2 | Joint Relative Frequency | $f_{rel}(x_i, y_j) = f(x_i, y_j) / N$ | Sums to $1$ over all cells |
| 3 | Marginal Frequency of $X$ | $f_X(x_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f(x_i, y_j)$ | Row totals; $\sum_i f_X(x_i) = N$ |
| 4 | Marginal Frequency of $Y$ | $f_Y(y_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} f(x_i, y_j)$ | Column totals; $\sum_j f_Y(y_j) = N$ |
| 5 | Marginal Probability of $X$ | $P(X=x_i) = f_X(x_i) / N$ | In $[0, 1]$; sums to $1$ |
| 6 | Marginal Probability of $Y$ | $P(Y=y_j) = f_Y(y_j) / N$ | In $[0, 1]$; sums to $1$ |
| 7 | Conditional Frequency | $f(x_i \mid y_j) = f(x_i, y_j) / f_Y(y_j)$ | Defined only if $f_Y(y_j) > 0$ |
| 8 | Conditional Probability | $P(X=x_i \mid Y=y_j) = f(x_i, y_j) / f_Y(y_j)$ | In $[0, 1]$; sums to $1$ over $i$ for fixed $j$ |
| 9 | Multiplication Rule | $P(X \cap Y) = P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)$ | Foundation of independence testing |
| 10 | Independence Condition | $P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = P(X=x_i) \cdot P(Y=y_j)$ | Must hold for **every** cell |
| 11 | Expected Frequency under $H_0$ (Independence) | $E_{ij} = f_X(x_i) \cdot f_Y(y_j) / N$ | Used in $\chi^2$ test of independence |
| 12 | Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum_{i,j} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | $O_{ij} = f(x_i, y_j)$ observed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Joint/marginal/conditional frequency analysis is the workhorse of **A/B testing** in product analytics, **quality control** (defect type vs. production line), **medical diagnostics** (symptom vs. disease), **risk modeling** in finance, and **sensor fusion** in IoT systems.

## 2.4 Why This Matters — The "Why" Behind the Theory

- **Marginal distributions** tell us about each variable in isolation — useful for descriptive statistics.
- **Conditional distributions** tell us how one variable behaves *given* a fixed value of another — this is the heart of **predictive modeling** and **causal inference**.
- **Joint distributions** tell us how the two variables *co-vary* — essential for **covariance, correlation, and multivariate modeling** (PCA, regression, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> A KTU examiner will frequently ask: *"Are $X$ and $Y$ independent?"* The simplest test is to verify whether the product of marginals (divided by $N$) equals the observed joint frequency. If even one cell violates this, the variables are dependent.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 A Complete Worked Example: Study Habit vs. Exam Result

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A data analytics instructor collects data on **50 B.Tech students** classifying them by **Study Habit** ($X$: Low, Medium, High) and **Exam Result** ($Y$: Pass, Fail). The observed counts are summarized in the table below.

### Step 1 — Construct the Contingency Table (Joint Frequencies)

| Study Habit ($X$) \ Exam Result ($Y$) | Pass ($y_1$) | Fail ($y_2$) | Row Total $f_X(x_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low** ($x_1$) | $8$ | $12$ | $20$ |
| **Medium** ($x_2$) | $15$ | $5$ | $20$ |
| **High** ($x_3$) | $7$ | $3$ | $10$ |
| **Column Total $f_Y(y_j)$** | $30$ | $20$ | $N = 50$ |

The 9 interior cells are the **joint frequencies** $f(x_i, y_j)$. The 5 totals (3 row + 2 column) are the **marginal frequencies**.

### Step 2 — Derive the Joint Probability Distribution

Divide every joint frequency by $N = 50$:

$$
P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = \frac{f(x_i, y_j)}{N}
$$

Computing each cell:

$$
P(\text{Low}, \text{Pass}) = \frac{8}{50} = 0.16
$$

$$
P(\text{Low}, \text{Fail}) = \frac{12}{50} = 0.24
$$

$$
P(\text{Medium}, \text{Pass}) = \frac{15}{50} = 0.30
$$

$$
P(\text{Medium}, \text{Fail}) = \frac{5}{50} = 0.10
$$

$$
P(\text{High}, \text{Pass}) = \frac{7}{50} = 0.14
$$

$$
P(\text{High}, \text{Fail}) = \frac{3}{50} = 0.06
$$

**Verification of the closure property:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3}\sum_{j=1}^{2} P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = 0.16 + 0.24 + 0.30 + 0.10 + 0.14 + 0.06 = 1.00 \;\checkmark
$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Marginal Probability Distributions

**Marginal of $X$** (row sums):

$$
P(X=\text{Low}) = 0.16 + 0.24 = 0.40
$$

$$
P(X=\text{Medium}) = 0.30 + 0.10 = 0.40
$$

$$
P(X=\text{High}) = 0.14 + 0.06 = 0.20
$$

**Marginal of $Y$** (column sums):

$$
P(Y=\text{Pass}) = 0.16 + 0.30 + 0.14 = 0.60
$$

$$
P(Y=\text{Fail}) = 0.24 + 0.10 + 0.06 = 0.40
$$

**Verification:** $0.40 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓ and $0.60 + 0.40 = 1.00$ ✓

### Step 4 — Derive the Conditional Probability Distribution of $X$ Given $Y$

Using the formula $P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) = P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) / P(Y=y_j)$:

**Conditional on $Y =$ Pass** (universe of 30 passers, $P(Y=\text{Pass}) = 0.60$):

$$
P(X=\text{Low} \mid Y=\text{Pass}) = \frac{0.16}{0.60} = \frac{8}{30} \approx 0.2667
$$

$$
P(X=\text{Medium} \mid Y=\text{Pass}) = \frac{0.30}{0.60} = \frac{15}{30} = 0.5000
$$

$$
P(X=\text{High} \mid Y=\text{Pass}) = \frac{0.14}{0.60} = \frac{7}{30} \approx 0.2333
$$

**Verification:** $0.2667 + 0.5000 + 0.2333 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Conditional on $Y =$ Fail** (universe of 20 failers, $P(Y=\text{Fail}) = 0.40$):

$$
P(X=\text{Low} \mid Y=\text{Fail}) = \frac{0.24}{0.40} = \frac{12}{20} = 0.6000
$$

$$
P(X=\text{Medium} \mid Y=\text{Fail}) = \frac{0.10}{0.40} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.2500
$$

$$
P(X=\text{High} \mid Y=\text{Fail}) = \frac{0.06}{0.40} = \frac{3}{20} = 0.1500
$$

**Verification:** $0.60 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

### Step 5 — Test for Independence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hypothesis Test (qualitative, no $\chi^2$ computed here):** $X$ and $Y$ are independent if and only if $P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = P(X=x_i) \cdot P(Y=y_j)$ for **every** cell.

Check the cell $(x_1, y_1) = (\text{Low}, \text{Pass})$:

$$
P(X=\text{Low}) \cdot P(Y=\text{Pass}) = 0.40 \times 0.60 = 0.24
$$

$$
P(X=\text{Low}, Y=\text{Pass}) = 0.16
$$

Since $0.16 \neq 0.24$, **the variables are NOT independent**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Knowing that a student passed the exam **changes** the distribution of study habits. Among passers, Medium study is most common (50%), while among failers, Low study is most common (60%). This is the data-analytic signature of an association between study habit and exam result.

### Step 6 — Expected Frequencies under the Independence Assumption

Under $H_0$ (independence), the expected joint frequency in cell $(i, j)$ is:

$$
E_{ij} = \frac{f_X(x_i) \cdot f_Y(y_j)}{N}
$$

Compute for each cell:

$$
E_{11} = \frac{20 \times 30}{50} = 12.0 \quad (\text{observed } O_{11} = 8)
$$

$$
E_{12} = \frac{20 \times 20}{50} = 8.0 \quad (\text{observed } O_{12} = 12)
$$

$$
E_{21} = \frac{20 \times 30}{50} = 12.0 \quad (\text{observed } O_{21} = 15)
$$

$$
E_{22} = \frac{20 \times 20}{50} = 8.0 \quad (\text{observed } O_{22} = 5)
$$

$$
E_{31} = \frac{10 \times 30}{50} = 6.0 \quad (\text{observed } O_{31} = 7)
$$

$$
E_{32} = \frac{10 \times 20}{50} = 4.0 \quad (\text{observed } O_{32} = 3)
$$

> [!TIP]
> When the observed and expected counts diverge significantly, we have strong evidence against independence. The formal $\chi^2$ statistic is the sum of $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2 / E_{ij}$ across all 6 cells.

## 3.2 The "Master" Conditional Probability Identity — Derived from Scratch

We now derive the foundational relationship $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ from first principles using frequencies.

**Starting point:** By definition of conditional relative frequency:

$$
f(A \mid B) = \frac{f(A \cap B)}{f(B)}
$$

Multiply both sides by $f(B)$:

$$
f(A \mid B) \cdot f(B) = f(A \cap B)
$$

Now divide both sides by $N^2$ (recall that relative frequency = frequency / $N$):

$$
\frac{f(A \mid B)}{N} \cdot \frac{f(B)}{N} = \frac{f(A \cap B)}{N^2}
$$

But note that $f(A \mid B)/N = P(A \mid B)$, $f(B)/N = P(B)$, and $f(A \cap B)/N^2 = P(A \cap B) / N$. To obtain the cleaner probability form, we proceed as:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

This is the **multiplication rule** and is the single most-used identity in bivariate analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check with our data:** $P(\text{Low} \cap \text{Pass}) = 0.16$, $P(\text{Low} \mid \text{Pass}) = 0.2667$, $P(\text{Pass}) = 0.60$.
> $$P(\text{Low} \mid \text{Pass}) \cdot P(\text{Pass}) = 0.2667 \times 0.60 = 0.16 = P(\text{Low} \cap \text{Pass}) \;\checkmark$$
