# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the terms (i) Joint Frequency Distribution, (ii) Marginal Frequency Distribution, and (iii) Conditional Frequency Distribution. How are they related to each other?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **(i) Joint Frequency Distribution:** For two variables $X$ (with values $x_1, \dots, x_m$) and $Y$ (with values $y_1, \dots, y_n$), the joint frequency $f(x_i, y_j)$ counts the number of observations in the sample that simultaneously satisfy $X = x_i$ and $Y = y_j$. The collection of all $m \times n$ such counts is the joint frequency distribution, typically arranged in a contingency table. **[1 Mark]**

> **(ii) Marginal Frequency Distribution:** The marginal frequency of $X = x_i$ is obtained by summing the joint frequencies across all values of $Y$ for that row: $f_X(x_i) = \sum_j f(x_i, y_j)$. These are the **row totals** (or **column totals** for $Y$) of the contingency table. They describe the distribution of one variable while ignoring the other. **[1 Mark]**

> **(iii) Conditional Frequency Distribution:** The conditional frequency $f(x_i \mid y_j) = f(x_i, y_j) / f_Y(y_j)$ describes the distribution of $X$ restricted to the sub-sample where $Y = y_j$. It is the relative frequency computed **within a single column** (or row) of the table. **[1 Mark]**

**Relationship:** The joint distribution is the most granular; the marginal is obtained by summing the joint over one variable; the conditional is obtained by dividing the joint by the relevant marginal.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and prove the multiplication rule of probability in terms of frequencies. What does it imply if two variables are statistically independent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** For any two events $A$ and $B$ with $f(B) > 0$, the multiplication rule in frequency form is:
$$f(A \cap B) = f(A \mid B) \cdot f(B)$$
In probability form:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

**Proof:** By the definition of conditional frequency, $f(A \mid B) = f(A \cap B) / f(B)$. Multiplying both sides by $f(B)$ yields the multiplication rule. **[2 Marks]**

**Independence:** Two variables $X$ and $Y$ are statistically independent if and only if $P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i)$ for every cell, which is equivalent to $P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = P(X=x_i) \cdot P(Y=y_j)$. In this case, knowledge of $Y$ provides no information about $X$. **[1 Mark]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **conditional frequency** with **joint frequency**. Remember: the denominator of a conditional frequency is a **marginal frequency**, not $N$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to verify that conditional probabilities sum to **1** within each fixed level of the conditioning variable.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Declaring variables "independent" based on a single cell check. Independence must hold for **all cells** simultaneously.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up row and column marginals in 2D tables. Always read the axis labels carefully.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting to write units (the values are dimensionless probabilities in $[0, 1]$).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

> A survey of **100 engineering students** classifies them by **Branch** ($X$: CSE, ECE, ME) and **Internship Status** ($Y$: Placed, Not Placed). The observed data is given below:
>
> | Branch \ Status | Placed ($y_1$) | Not Placed ($y_2$) | Total |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **CSE** | 30 | 10 | 40 |
> | **ECE** | 20 | 15 | 35 |
> | **ME** | 10 | 15 | 25 |
> | **Total** | 60 | 40 | 100 |
>
> **(a)** Construct the **joint probability distribution** and the **marginal probability distributions** of $X$ and $Y$. Verify all closure properties. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Find the **conditional distribution** of Branch given Placement Status. Using the result, test whether Branch and Placement Status are **statistically independent**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Joint and Marginal Probability Distributions [7 Marks]**

Divide every cell by $N = 100$ to obtain the joint probability distribution:

$$
P(X=\text{CSE}, Y=\text{Placed}) = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30
$$

$$
P(X=\text{CSE}, Y=\text{Not Placed}) = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ECE}, Y=\text{Placed}) = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ECE}, Y=\text{Not Placed}) = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ME}, Y=\text{Placed}) = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ME}, Y=\text{Not Placed}) = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15
$$

**Verification of joint closure:** $0.30 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓ **[1 Mark for joint table]**

**Marginal of $X$ (row sums):**

$$
P(X=\text{CSE}) = 0.30 + 0.10 = 0.40
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ECE}) = 0.20 + 0.15 = 0.35
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ME}) = 0.10 + 0.15 = 0.25
$$

**Verification:** $0.40 + 0.35 + 0.25 = 1.00$ ✓ **[2 Marks for marginal of X]**

**Marginal of $Y$ (column sums):**

$$
P(Y=\text{Placed}) = 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.60
$$

$$
P(Y=\text{Not Placed}) = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.15 = 0.40
$$

**Verification:** $0.60 + 0.40 = 1.00$ ✓ **[2 Marks for marginal of Y]**

**Joint Probability Table Summary:**

| Branch \ Status | Placed | Not Placed | $P(X=x_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSE | 0.30 | 0.10 | 0.40 |
| ECE | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.35 |
| ME | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.25 |
| $P(Y=y_j)$ | 0.60 | 0.40 | 1.00 |

**[2 Marks for the consolidated table and full verification]**

---

**Part (b) — Conditional Distribution and Independence Test [7 Marks]**

**Conditional distribution of $X$ given $Y =$ Placed** (use $P(Y=\text{Placed}) = 0.60$ as the universe):

$$
P(X=\text{CSE} \mid Y=\text{Placed}) = \frac{0.30}{0.60} = 0.500
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ECE} \mid Y=\text{Placed}) = \frac{0.20}{0.60} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ME} \mid Y=\text{Placed}) = \frac{0.10}{0.60} \approx 0.167
$$

**Verification:** $0.500 + 0.333 + 0.167 = 1.000$ ✓ **[2 Marks]**

**Conditional distribution of $X$ given $Y =$ Not Placed** (use $P(Y=\text{Not Placed}) = 0.40$):

$$
P(X=\text{CSE} \mid Y=\text{Not Placed}) = \frac{0.10}{0.40} = 0.250
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ECE} \mid Y=\text{Not Placed}) = \frac{0.15}{0.40} = 0.375
$$

$$
P(X=\text{ME} \mid Y=\text{Not Placed}) = \frac{0.15}{0.40} = 0.375
$$

**Verification:** $0.250 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.000$ ✓ **[2 Marks]**

**Independence Test [3 Marks]:**

Compare the two conditional distributions with the marginal of $X = (0.40, 0.35, 0.25)$:

| Branch | Marginal $P(X)$ | $P(X \mid Y=\text{Placed})$ | $P(X \mid Y=\text{Not Placed})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSE | 0.40 | 0.500 | 0.250 |
| ECE | 0.35 | 0.333 | 0.375 |
| ME | 0.25 | 0.167 | 0.375 |

The conditional distributions **differ substantially** from the marginal distribution. For instance, $P(X=\text{CSE}) = 0.40$, but $P(X=\text{CSE} \mid Y=\text{Placed}) = 0.50 \neq 0.40$.

Equivalently, check the cell $(X=\text{CSE}, Y=\text{Placed})$:
$$P(X=\text{CSE}) \cdot P(Y=\text{Placed}) = 0.40 \times 0.60 = 0.24 \neq 0.30 = P(X=\text{CSE}, Y=\text{Placed})$$

**Conclusion:** The variables Branch ($X$) and Placement Status ($Y$) are **NOT statistically independent**. Knowing the placement status materially changes the distribution of branches.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**

> **(a)** Define **(i)** Joint Frequency Distribution, **(ii)** Marginal Frequency Distribution, **(iii)** Conditional Frequency Distribution, and **(iv)** Joint Probability Distribution. For a sample of $N$ observations on variables $X$ and $Y$, derive the relationship:
> $$P(X = x_i \cap Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j) \cdot P(Y = y_j)$$
> from first principles using frequency counts. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** In a clinical trial of **200 patients**, the following contingency table classifies them by **Treatment Type** ($X$: Drug A, Drug B, Placebo) and **Recovery Status** ($Y$: Recovered, Not Recovered):
>
> | Treatment \ Recovery | Recovered | Not Recovered | Total |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Drug A** | 60 | 20 | 80 |
> | **Drug B** | 50 | 30 | 80 |
> | **Placebo** | 20 | 20 | 40 |
> | **Total** | 130 | 70 | 200 |
>
> Compute the **(i)** marginal probability distributions, **(ii)** the conditional distribution of Recovery given Treatment, and **(iii)** state with justification whether Treatment and Recovery are independent. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation [7 Marks]**

**(i) Joint Frequency Distribution:** The collection $\{f(x_i, y_j)\}$ for all combinations of $X = x_i$ and $Y = y_j$ in a sample of size $N$. Each $f(x_i, y_j)$ is a non-negative integer count, and $\sum_{i,j} f(x_i, y_j) = N$. **[1 Mark]**

**(ii) Marginal Frequency Distribution:** The collection of row totals $f_X(x_i) = \sum_j f(x_i, y_j)$ (for $X$) or column totals $f_Y(y_j) = \sum_i f(x_i, y_j)$ (for $Y$). Each marginal is itself a valid univariate frequency distribution. **[1 Mark]**

**(iii) Conditional Frequency Distribution:** For a fixed $y_j$ with $f_Y(y_j) > 0$, the conditional frequency is $f(x_i \mid y_j) = f(x_i, y_j) / f_Y(y_j)$. The set $\{f(x_i \mid y_j)\}_i$ forms the conditional distribution of $X$ given $Y = y_j$. **[1 Mark]**

**(iv) Joint Probability Distribution:** The probabilities $P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = f(x_i, y_j)/N$ assigned to each $(x_i, y_j)$ pair. These are non-negative, dimensionless, and sum to 1. **[1 Mark]**

**Derivation [3 Marks]:**

Start with the definition of conditional relative frequency:

$$
\text{(Step 1)} \quad f(x_i \mid y_j) \;=\; \frac{f(x_i, y_j)}{f_Y(y_j)}, \quad f_Y(y_j) > 0
$$

Multiply both sides by $f_Y(y_j)$:

$$
\text{(Step 2)} \quad f(x_i \mid y_j) \cdot f_Y(y_j) \;=\; f(x_i, y_j)
$$

Now convert each frequency to a probability by dividing the entire equation by $N$. Note that dividing a joint frequency by $N$ gives a joint probability, and dividing a conditional frequency by 1 (since it is already a ratio) keeps it intact only after the marginal is also normalized:

$$
\text{(Step 3)} \quad \frac{f(x_i, y_j)}{N} \;=\; \frac{f(x_i \mid y_j) \cdot f_Y(y_j)}{N} \;=\; f(x_i \mid y_j) \cdot \frac{f_Y(y_j)}{N}
$$

Recognize that $f(x_i, y_j)/N = P(X=x_i, Y=y_j)$, $f(x_i \mid y_j) = P(X=x_i \mid Y=y_j)$, and $f_Y(y_j)/N = P(Y=y_j)$:

$$
\text{(Step 4)} \quad P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) \;=\; P(X=x_i \mid Y=y_j) \cdot P(Y=y_j) \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

**Part (b) — Worked Computation [7 Marks]**

**(i) Marginal Probability Distributions [2 Marks]:**

**Marginal of $X$ (Treatment):**

$$
P(X=\text{Drug A}) = \frac{80}{200} = 0.40
$$

$$
P(X=\text{Drug B}) = \frac{80}{200} = 0.40
$$

$$
P(X=\text{Placebo}) = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20
$$

**Marginal of $Y$ (Recovery):**

$$
P(Y=\text{Recovered}) = \frac{130}{200} = 0.65
$$

$$
P(Y=\text{Not Recovered}) = \frac{70}{200} = 0.35
$$

**(ii) Conditional Distribution of $Y$ given $X$ [3 Marks]:**

**Given $X =$ Drug A** (universe probability $P(X=\text{Drug A}) = 0.40$):

$$
P(Y=\text{Recovered} \mid X=\text{Drug A}) = \frac{60/200}{0.40} = \frac{0.30}{0.40} = 0.75
$$

$$
P(Y=\text{Not Recovered} \mid X=\text{Drug A}) = \frac{0.10}{0.40} = 0.25
$$

**Given $X =$ Drug B** ($P(X=\text{Drug B}) = 0.40$):

$$
P(Y=\text{Recovered} \mid X=\text{Drug B}) = \frac{0.25}{0.40} = 0.625
$$

$$
P(Y=\text{Not Recovered} \mid X=\text{Drug B}) = \frac{0.15}{0.40} = 0.375
$$

**Given $X =$ Placebo** ($P(X=\text{Placebo}) = 0.20$):

$$
P(Y=\text{Recovered} \mid X=\text{Placebo}) = \frac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.50
$$

$$
P(Y=\text{Not Recovered} \mid X=\text{Placebo}) = \frac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.50
$$

**Verification:** Each pair sums to 1.00. ✓

**(iii) Independence Test [2 Marks]:**

If Treatment and Recovery were independent, the conditional distribution of $Y$ would be the **same** for every level of $X$. But we observe:
- $P(Y=\text{Recovered} \mid X=\text{Drug A}) = 0.75$
- $P(Y=\text{Recovered} \mid X=\text{Drug B}) = 0.625$
- $P(Y=\text{Recovered} \mid X=\text{Placebo}) = 0.50$

These are **distinct** values, so Treatment and Recovery are **NOT independent**. Equivalently, the cell check fails:

$$
P(X=\text{Drug A}) \cdot P(Y=\text{Recovered}) = 0.40 \times 0.65 = 0.26 \neq 0.30 = P(X=\text{Drug A}, Y=\text{Recovered})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** There is a clear association between Treatment type and Recovery. Drug A has the highest recovery rate (75%), Drug B intermediate (62.5%), and Placebo the lowest (50%) — exactly the pattern expected in a randomized controlled trial.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- **Joint Frequency $f(x_i, y_j)$:** The count in the interior cell of a contingency table. The **starting point** of all bivariate analysis.
- **Marginal Frequency:** Always a **row total** (for $X$) or **column total** (for $Y$). Sum of marginals = $N$.
- **Conditional Frequency:** $f(x_i \mid y_j) = f(x_i, y_j) / f_Y(y_j)$. The denominator is the **relevant marginal**, not $N$.
- **Relative Frequency vs. Probability:** Divide by $N$ to convert frequencies to probabilities. Probabilities are dimensionless and lie in $[0, 1]$.
- **Closure Properties to Always Verify:**
  1. $\sum_i \sum_j P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = 1$
  2. $\sum_i P(X=x_i) = 1$ and $\sum_j P(Y=y_j) = 1$
  3. $\sum_i P(X=x_i \mid Y=y_j) = 1$ for each fixed $j$
  4. $\sum_j P(Y=y_j \mid X=x_i) = 1$ for each fixed $i$
- **Multiplication Rule:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$. The single most-tested identity.
- **Independence Test:** Verify $P(X=x_i, Y=y_j) = P(X=x_i) \cdot P(Y=y_j)$ for **every** cell. A single failure is enough to reject independence.
- **Expected Frequency under Independence:** $E_{ij} = f_X(x_i) \cdot f_Y(y_j) / N$. Used in the $\chi^2$ test of independence (covered in Module 3 of the KTU syllabus).
- **Engineering Applications:** A/B testing, quality control, medical diagnostics, recommender systems, anomaly detection in sensor networks, customer segmentation, fraud detection.
- **Common Pitfall:** Do NOT compute $f(x_i \mid y_j)$ by dividing by $N$ — that gives the **joint probability**, not the conditional probability. The denominator must be the marginal of the conditioning variable.
- **Notation Convention:** $P(X = x_i \mid Y = y_j)$ is read as "probability of $X$ equaling $x_i$ **given that** $Y$ equals $y_j$." The bar `|` is the conditioning symbol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Joint → Marginal via row/column sums → Conditional via division by the appropriate marginal → Probability via division by $N$.* Master this pipeline, and you master the topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphical Representation of Two Nominal or Ordinal Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Graphical Representation of Two Nominal or Ordinal Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bivariate Categorical Visualization** is the graphical technique used to display the joint distribution of **two qualitative variables** (nominal and/or ordinal) using a single composite plot, enabling visual detection of association, independence, and pattern of variation between the categorical dimensions.

**Nominal Variable** — A categorical variable whose levels have *no natural order* (e.g., Gender: Male, Female; Blood Group: A, B, AB, O; Marital Status).

**Ordinal Variable** — A categorical variable whose levels possess a *meaningful ranking* but the gaps between ranks are not numerically uniform (e.g., Education: Primary < Secondary < Graduate < Postgraduate; Rating: Poor < Fair < Good < Excellent).

When we pair one nominal/ordinal variable with another, the result is a **2-way contingency table** that must be visualized using charts designed specifically for categorical (non-numeric) data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Traditional numerical plots like scatter plots and line graphs are *inappropriate* for two categorical variables because there is no continuous axis to plot. KTU examiners frequently test whether the student can **justify chart choice** based on variable type.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Survey" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and want to know: *"Does the customer's **Gender** influence their **Dessert Preference**?"* You survey 200 diners and jot down two labels per person. Now you have a *pile of data with two categories per row*. How do you "see" the answer?

- You could write a long list — useless for spotting patterns.
- You could compute an average — impossible, since "Chocolate" and "Gender" are not numbers.
- You need a **picture that places both categories side-by-side** so the eye can instantly compare bars, blocks, or shaded cells.

That picture is exactly what bivariate categorical visualization provides.

### 1.3 Classification of Charts for Two Categorical Variables

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU-recognized chart families for two nominal/ordinal variables are:
>
> 1. **Grouped (Clustered) Bar Chart**
> 2. **Stacked Bar Chart** (absolute frequencies)
> 3. **100% Stacked Bar Chart** (relative frequencies)
> 4. **Mosaic Plot**
> 5. **Spine Plot** (Mosaic's smoothed cousin)
> 6. **Heatmap (Categorical)**
> 7. **Cleveland Dot Plot**
> 8. **Population Pyramid** (special case for two ordinal variables)
> 9. **Frequency Polygon (Bivariate)**
> 10. **Side-by-Side Pie Chart** (least preferred, rarely used)

**Standard metrics used throughout:** *Frequency* $n_{ij}$, *Relative Frequency* $f_{ij} = n_{ij}/n$, *Row Percentage*, *Column Percentage*, *Total Percentage* — all in **bold** when used as headings in the board exam answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Concepts — The Contingency Table

Suppose we collect data on two categorical variables $X$ (with $r$ levels) and $Y$ (with $c$ levels). The natural first step is a **contingency table** (cross-tabulation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Table Layout: } & X \text{ on rows, } Y \text{ on columns} \\
& \text{Cell } (i,j) \text{ contains } n_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2 The Four Layered Quantities (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Joint Frequency** $n_{ij}$: Number of observations falling in row $i$ AND column $j$ simultaneously.

**Step 2 — Row Marginal Frequency** $n_{i\cdot}$: Sum across a single row (frequency of $X = i$ regardless of $Y$).

**Step 3 — Column Marginal Frequency** $n_{\cdot j}$: Sum down a single column (frequency of $Y = j$ regardless of $X$).

**Step 4 — Grand Total** $n$: Sum of all cells.

**Step 5 — Conversion to Relative Scale (The Crucial Step for Plots)**:
- Joint relative frequency: $f_{ij} = n_{ij}/n$
- Row conditional: $f_{j \vert i} = n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$
- Column conditional: $f_{i \vert j} = n_{ij}/n_{\cdot j}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners explicitly award marks for distinguishing between **row %** and **column %**. Stacked bars use row %; grouped bars use counts; mosaic plots use joint relative frequency.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Range / Units** | **Used In Chart** |
|-------------|------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Joint Frequency | $n_{ij}$ | Integer $\geq 0$ | Grouped Bar |
| Grand Total | $n = \sum_i \sum_j n_{ij}$ | Integer | All |
| Row Marginal | $n_{i\cdot} = \sum_j n_{ij}$ | Integer | Stacked Bar |
| Column Marginal | $n_{\cdot j} = \sum_i n_{ij}$ | Integer | Spine Plot |
| Joint Relative | $f_{ij} = n_{ij} / n$ | $[0,1]$ | Mosaic Plot |
| Row % | $(n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}) \times 100$ | $[0, 100]$ % | 100% Stacked Bar |
| Column % | $(n_{ij}/n_{\cdot j}) \times 100$ | $[0, 100]$ % | Comparative Bars |
| Cell % | $(n_{ij}/n) \times 100$ | $[0, 100]$ % | Heatmap |
| Conditional Prob | $P(X=i \vert Y=j) = n_{ij}/n_{\cdot j}$ | $[0,1]$ | Spine Plot |
| Chi-Square | $\chi^2 = \sum_i \sum_j (O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2/E_{ij}$ | $\geq 0$ | Independence Test |

> [!NOTE]
> The **vertical bar symbol** $\vert$ is used here strictly for "conditional" probability notation — not for absolute value — to keep mathematical meaning intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Engineering:** Defect Type vs. Shift (Day/Night) — **stacked bar** identifies which shift causes which defect.
- **Medical Analytics:** Treatment Outcome vs. Age Group — **mosaic plot** reveals treatment efficacy across strata.
- **Marketing:** Product Preference vs. Region — **grouped bar chart** exposes regional buying patterns.
- **Survey Research:** Education Level vs. Voting Intent — **100% stacked bar** standardizes for unequal group sizes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Restaurant Dessert Survey

**Problem:** A restaurant surveys **200 diners** about their **Gender** (Male, Female) and **Dessert Preference** (Chocolate, Vanilla, Fruit). The raw data is tallied into the following contingency table.

| Gender \ Dessert | Chocolate | Vanilla | Fruit | **Row Total** |
|------------------|-----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| Male             | 50        | 30      | 20    | **100**       |
| Female           | 40        | 50      | 10    | **100**       |
| **Column Total** | **90**    | **80**  | **30** | **200**     |

We will derive all required quantities and justify the choice of chart for every representation.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Marginal Frequencies

**Row 1 Marginal (Male Total):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{1\cdot} &= n_{11} + n_{12} + n_{13} \\
&= 50 + 30 + 20 \\
&= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 2 Marginal (Female Total):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{2\cdot} &= n_{21} + n_{22} + n_{23} \\
&= 40 + 50 + 10 \\
&= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Column 1 Marginal (Chocolate Total):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\cdot 1} &= n_{11} + n_{21} \\
&= 50 + 40 \\
&= 90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Column 2 Marginal (Vanilla Total):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\cdot 2} &= n_{12} + n_{22} \\
&= 30 + 50 \\
&= 80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Column 3 Marginal (Fruit Total):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\cdot 3} &= n_{13} + n_{23} \\
&= 20 + 10 \\
&= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Grand Total (consistency check):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= n_{1\cdot} + n_{2\cdot} = 100 + 100 = 200 \\
\text{Also, } n &= n_{\cdot 1} + n_{\cdot 2} + n_{\cdot 3} = 90 + 80 + 30 = 200 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Joint Relative Frequencies

**Cell (Male, Chocolate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{11} &= \frac{n_{11}}{n} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 = 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell (Male, Vanilla):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{12} &= \frac{n_{12}}{n} = \frac{30}{200} = 0.15 = 15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell (Male, Fruit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{13} &= \frac{n_{13}}{n} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10 = 10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell (Female, Chocolate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{21} &= \frac{n_{21}}{n} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell (Female, Vanilla):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{22} &= \frac{n_{22}}{n} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 = 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell (Female, Fruit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{23} &= \frac{n_{23}}{n} = \frac{10}{200} = 0.05 = 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $0.25 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.05 = 1.00$ — **constraint satisfied**.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation of Row Percentages (100% Stacked Bar Source)

Row percentage = (cell count) / (row total) × 100

**For Males:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chocolate \%} &= \frac{50}{100} \times 100 = 50\% \\
\text{Vanilla \%} &= \frac{30}{100} \times 100 = 30\% \\
\text{Fruit \%} &= \frac{20}{100} \times 100 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Females:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chocolate \%} &= \frac{40}{100} \times 100 = 40\% \\
\text{Vanilla \%} &= \frac{50}{100} \times 100 = 50\% \\
\text{Fruit \%} &= \frac{10}{100} \times 100 = 10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each row must sum to 100%. Males: $50 + 30 + 20 = 100$ — **valid**. Females: $40 + 50 + 10 = 100$ — **valid**. This row % table is the **direct input** for a 100% Stacked Bar Chart.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation of Column Percentages

Column percentage = (cell count) / (column total) × 100

**For Chocolate column (total = 90):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Male \%} &= \frac{50}{90} \times 100 \approx 55.56\% \\
\text{Female \%} &= \frac{40}{90} \times 100 \approx 44.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Vanilla column (total = 80):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Male \%} &= \frac{30}{80} \times 100 = 37.50\% \\
\text{Female \%} &= \frac{50}{80} \times 100 = 62.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Fruit column (total = 30):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Male \%} &= \frac{20}{30} \times 100 \approx 66.67\% \\
\text{Female \%} &= \frac{10}{30} \times 100 \approx 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each column sums to 100% (e.g., $55.56 + 44.44 = 100$ — **valid**). Column percentages are best for answering *"Given the dessert, what is the gender split?"* — a **column-conditional** perspective.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Chart Selection Logic

| **Research Question** | **Recommended Chart** | **Derived Quantity Used** |
|----------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Compare absolute counts across groups | Grouped Bar Chart | $n_{ij}$ |
| Compare distribution *within* each group | 100% Stacked Bar | Row % |
| Show both proportion AND count | Mosaic Plot | $f_{ij}$ + $n_{i\cdot}$ |
| Many categories, need density | Heatmap | $f_{ij}$ |
| Compare ranks (Ordinal vs Ordinal) | Cleveland Dot Plot | $n_{ij}$ |
| Ordinal vs Nominal (e.g., Age Group vs Gender) | Population Pyramid | $n_{ij}$ |
| Test independence statistically | Chi-Square test | $\chi^2$ statistic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Chart for Two Categorical Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Categorical Variables X and Y] --> B{Is one variable ORDINAL?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is the other NOMINAL?}
    B -- No --> D{Both NOMINAL?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Population Pyramid OR Spine Plot]
    C -- No --> F[Dot Plot OR Heatmap with ordered rows]
    D -- Yes --> G{What is the research goal?}
    G -- H1 --> H[Compare absolute counts: Grouped Bar Chart]
    G -- H2 --> I[Compare proportions within group: 100 percent Stacked Bar]
    G -- H3 --> J[Show both count and proportion: Mosaic Plot]
    G -- H4 --> K[Many categories: Heatmap]
    H --> L[Use raw n_ij]
    I --> M[Use Row Percentage]
    J --> N[Use f_ij and n_i dot]
    K --> O[Use f_ij]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bivariate Categorical Visualization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Categorical Data: two columns] --> tally[Tally into Contingency Table]
    tally --> matrix[n_ij Matrix r x c]
    matrix --> derive1[Derive Row Marginals n_i dot]
    matrix --> derive2[Derive Column Marginals n_dot j]
    matrix --> derive3[Derive Grand Total n]
    derive1 --> pct1[Compute Row Percentage]
    derive2 --> pct2[Compute Column Percentage]
    derive3 --> rel[Compute Joint Relative f_ij]
    pct1 --> stack[100 percent Stacked Bar Input]
    pct2 --> group[Grouped Bar Chart Input]
    rel --> mosaic[Mosaic Plot Input]
    stack --> plot[Render Plot]
    group --> plot
    mosaic --> plot
    plot --> insight[Visual Insight: Association / Independence]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Grouped Bar Chart Layout (for board exam drawing)

```
Frequency
   |
80 |       ##  ##
   |       ##  ##  ##
60 |   ##  ##  ##  ##
   |   ##  ##  ##  ##
40 |   ##  ##  ##  ##  ##
   |   ##  ##  ##  ##  ##  ##
20 |   ##  ##  ##  ##  ##  ##  ##
   |   ##  ##  ##  ##  ##  ##  ##
 0 |___Male_____Female______________
      Choc  Van  Frt   Choc  Van  Frt
        (Cluster 1)    (Cluster 2)
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — 100% Stacked Bar Chart Layout

```
 100% |----------------|--------|
      |                |        |
  80% |     ######     |  ######| ##### Chocolate
      |     ######     |  ######| ######
  60% |######         |########|        Vanilla
      |######         |########|########
  40% |######  ###### |########| #####  Fruit
      |######  ###### |########| ######
  20% |######  ###### |########|######
      |######  ###### |########|######
   0% |___________________________|
            MALE         FEMALE
      | 50% | 30% |20% | 40% |50% |10% |
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define a stacked bar chart and mention one key difference between an absolute stacked bar chart and a 100% stacked bar chart.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stacked bar chart** is a graphical representation in which two categorical variables are shown with one variable along one axis and the second variable broken into colored segments stacked on top of each other within each bar. The length of each segment represents the frequency $n_{ij}$ of the corresponding sub-category.

**Key Difference:**
- **Absolute Stacked Bar:** Total bar height = row total $n_{i\cdot}$ (varies between bars).
- **100% Stacked Bar:** Total bar height is forced to 100%; segment lengths represent row percentages $(n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}) \times 100$.

The 100% version is preferred when row totals differ significantly and the analyst wishes to compare *internal composition*.

*(Marks: 1 — definition, 1 — difference, 1 — practical implication)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is a scatter plot unsuitable for visualizing two nominal variables? Justify in two points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. A scatter plot requires both variables to be **numerical (continuous)** so that each $(x, y)$ point has a meaningful coordinate on a 2-D plane. Nominal categories like "Male/Female" or "Red/Blue/Green" have **no numerical magnitude or order**, so plotting them on a numeric axis is mathematically undefined.

2. Even if dummy codes (0/1) are assigned, the resulting scatter plot **conveys no ordinal or interval information** and clusters of points on (0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1) provide no insight about the joint distribution. Charts like **bar charts, mosaic plots, and heatmaps** are designed to encode frequencies through *bar length* or *color intensity* — both of which work directly with categorical data.

*(Marks: 1 — first point, 1 — second point, 1 — naming correct alternative)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

The following data is collected from **300 employees** of a software company, classified by **Department** (IT, HR, Finance) and **Job Satisfaction Level** (Low, Medium, High).

| Department \ Satisfaction | Low | Medium | High | **Total** |
|--------------------------|-----|--------|------|-----------|
| IT                       | 30  | 50     | 70   | **150**   |
| HR                       | 25  | 20     | 15   | **60**    |
| Finance                  | 20  | 40     | 30   | **90**    |
| **Total**                | **75** | **110** | **115** | **300** |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Construct a 100% stacked bar chart in tabular form by computing row percentages for each department. Comment on the satisfaction pattern across departments.*

**Model Solution:**

Row percentages are computed as $(n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}) \times 100$.

**IT Department (Row Total = 150):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low \%} &= \frac{30}{150} \times 100 = 20.00\% \\
\text{Medium \%} &= \frac{50}{150} \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
\text{High \%} &= \frac{70}{150} \times 100 = 46.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**HR Department (Row Total = 60):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low \%} &= \frac{25}{60} \times 100 = 41.67\% \\
\text{Medium \%} &= \frac{20}{60} \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
\text{High \%} &= \frac{15}{60} \times 100 = 25.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Finance Department (Row Total = 90):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low \%} &= \frac{20}{90} \times 100 = 22.22\% \\
\text{Medium \%} &= \frac{40}{90} \times 100 = 44.44\% \\
\text{High \%} &= \frac{30}{90} \times 100 = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting 100% Stacked Bar Table:**

| Department | Low % | Medium % | High % | Total % |
|------------|-------|----------|--------|---------|
| IT         | 20.00 | 33.33    | 46.67  | 100.00  |
| HR         | 41.67 | 33.33    | 25.00  | 100.00  |
| Finance    | 22.22 | 44.44    | 33.33  | 100.00  |

**Comment on Pattern (1 mark):**
- IT has the **highest proportion of "High" satisfaction (46.67%)**.
- HR has the **highest proportion of "Low" satisfaction (41.67%)** — a clear alert for HR management.
- Finance is skewed toward **Medium satisfaction (44.44%)**.

*(Marks: 5 — calculations, 1 — table, 1 — interpretation)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Compute the joint relative frequencies $f_{ij}$ and describe how a mosaic plot would represent this data. Draw a rough ASCII schematic of the mosaic plot.*

**Model Solution:**

Joint relative frequency $f_{ij} = n_{ij}/n$ where $n = 300$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{11} &= 30/300 = 0.1000 \\
f_{12} &= 50/300 = 0.1667 \\
f_{13} &= 70/300 = 0.2333 \\
f_{21} &= 25/300 = 0.0833 \\
f_{22} &= 20/300 = 0.0667 \\
f_{23} &= 15/300 = 0.0500 \\
f_{31} &= 20/300 = 0.0667 \\
f_{32} &= 40/300 = 0.1333 \\
f_{33} &= 30/300 = 0.1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum Check:** $0.1000 + 0.1667 + 0.2333 + 0.0833 + 0.0667 + 0.0500 + 0.0667 + 0.1333 + 0.1000 = 1.0000$ — **valid**.

**Mosaic Plot Description (3 marks):**
A mosaic plot is a 2-D rectangular grid where:
- The **width of each column** is proportional to $n_{\cdot j}$ (column marginal).
- The **height of each row segment within a column** is proportional to the row-conditional proportion $n_{ij}/n_{\cdot j}$.
- The **area of each cell** equals the joint relative frequency $f_{ij}$.

**ASCII Schematic (2 marks):**

```
   |<---n.1=75--->|<---n.2=110--->|<---n.3=115--->|
   |  (25% wide)  |   (36.7% wide)|  (38.3% wide) |
   +--------------+---------------+---------------+
IT |  Low 0.1000  | Med 0.1667    | High 0.2333   | <- widest, tallest
   +--------------+---------------+---------------+
HR |  Low 0.0833  | Med 0.0667    | High 0.0500   | <- narrowest
   +--------------+---------------+---------------+
Fin|  Low 0.0667  | Med 0.1333    | High 0.1000   |
   +--------------+---------------+---------------+
   Total area = 1.00 (=100% of 300 employees)
```

*(Marks: 3 — frequency calc, 3 — description, 2 — schematic, 2 — interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

A hospital records the **Blood Group** (A, B, AB, O) and **Disease Status** (Diseased, Healthy) of **400 patients** as follows:

| Blood Group \ Status | Diseased | Healthy | **Total** |
|---------------------|----------|---------|-----------|
| A                   | 40       | 80      | **120**   |
| B                   | 35       | 65      | **100**   |
| AB                  | 15       | 25      | **40**    |
| O                   | 50       | 90      | **140**   |
| **Total**           | **140**  | **260** | **400**   |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Draw a grouped (clustered) bar chart representation of the data and explain why a grouped bar chart is preferable to a side-by-side pie chart for this case.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify counts** (already given in the table).

**Step 2 — Construct the grouped bar chart** with **Blood Group** on the X-axis (4 groups: A, B, AB, O) and **Frequency** on the Y-axis. Within each group, place two adjacent bars: one for *Diseased* and one for *Healthy*.

**Grouped Bar Chart Table:**

| Blood Group | Diseased | Healthy |
|-------------|----------|---------|
| A           | 40       | 80      |
| B           | 35       | 65      |
| AB          | 15       | 25      |
| O           | 50       | 90      |

**ASCII Schematic (2 marks):**

```
Frequency
   |
 90 |              ##
   |   ##         ##         ##
 80 |   ##  ##     ##  ##     ##  ##
   |   ##  ##     ##  ##     ##  ##      ##
 65 |   ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##
   |   ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##
 50 |## ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##
   |## ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##
 40 |## ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##  ##
   |## ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##  ##
 35 |## ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##  ##
 25 |## ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##  ##  ##
 15 |## ##  ##  ## ##  ##  ##  ##  ##   ##  ##  ##
   |_________________________________________________
       A        B         AB         O
       ## Diseased   ## Healthy
```

**Why Grouped Bar > Side-by-Side Pie Chart (3 marks):**
1. **Direct length comparison:** The human eye compares bar lengths more accurately than pie-slice angles. A difference of 10 units is instantly visible in bars but hard to discern in slices.
2. **More categories handled cleanly:** A grouped bar chart supports 4+ blood groups × 2 statuses = 8 bars clearly. A side-by-side pie arrangement would require 8 pies — visually overwhelming and statistically meaningless since pie charts are designed for **single-variable part-to-whole** relationships.
3. **Preserves absolute frequency:** Pie charts only show *proportions within one variable*, losing the absolute count. A grouped bar preserves $n_{ij}$ directly on the Y-axis.

*(Marks: 2 — table, 2 — schematic, 3 — justification)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Compute the column percentages and use them to perform a chi-square test of independence between Blood Group and Disease Status. State the hypothesis and conclusion at 5% significance level. (Use $\chi^2_{0.05, 3} = 7.815$)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses (1 mark):**
- $H_0$: Blood Group and Disease Status are independent.
- $H_1$: Blood Group and Disease Status are associated.

**Step 2 — Column Percentages (2 marks):**

For *Diseased* column (total = 140):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A \%} &= \frac{40}{140} \times 100 = 28.57\% \\
\text{B \%} &= \frac{35}{140} \times 100 = 25.00\% \\
\text{AB \%} &= \frac{15}{140} \times 100 = 10.71\% \\
\text{O \%} &= \frac{50}{140} \times 100 = 35.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For *Healthy* column (total = 260):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A \%} &= \frac{80}{260} \times 100 = 30.77\% \\
\text{B \%} &= \frac{65}{260} \times 100 = 25.00\% \\
\text{AB \%} &= \frac{25}{260} \times 100 = 9.62\% \\
\text{O \%} &= \frac{90}{260} \times 100 = 34.62\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expected Frequencies (2 marks):** $E_{ij} = (n_{i\cdot} \times n_{\cdot j})/n$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= \frac{120 \times 140}{400} = 42.00 \\
E_{12} &= \frac{120 \times 260}{400} = 78.00 \\
E_{21} &= \frac{100 \times 140}{400} = 35.00 \\
E_{22} &= \frac{100 \times 260}{400} = 65.00 \\
E_{31} &= \frac{40 \times 140}{400} = 14.00 \\
E_{32} &= \frac{40 \times 260}{400} = 26.00 \\
E_{41} &= \frac{140 \times 140}{400} = 49.00 \\
E_{42} &= \frac{140 \times 260}{400} = 91.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Chi-Square Statistic (1.5 marks):**

$$
\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \frac{(40-42)^2}{42} + \frac{(80-78)^2}{78} + \frac{(35-35)^2}{35} + \frac{(65-65)^2}{65} \\
&\quad + \frac{(15-14)^2}{14} + \frac{(25-26)^2}{26} + \frac{(50-49)^2}{49} + \frac{(90-91)^2}{91} \\
&= \frac{4}{42} + \frac{4}{78} + 0 + 0 + \frac{1}{14} + \frac{1}{26} + \frac{1}{49} + \frac{1}{91} \\
&\approx 0.0952 + 0.0513 + 0 + 0 + 0.0714 + 0.0385 + 0.0204 + 0.0110 \\
&\approx 0.2878
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision (0.5 mark):**
- Degrees of freedom $df = (r-1)(c-1) = (4-1)(2-1) = 3$.
- Critical value $\chi^2_{0.05, 3} = 7.815$.
- Computed $\chi^2 = 0.288 < 7.815$.

**Conclusion:** Fail to reject $H_0$. At 5% significance level, Blood Group and Disease Status are **statistically independent** in this sample.

*(Marks: 1 — hypothesis, 2 — column %, 2 — expected, 1.5 — chi-square, 0.5 — decision)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bivariate Categorical Plot Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing row and column percentages** — Examiners deduct 2 marks if you compute row % but label it column % or vice versa. Always state explicitly: *"Row % of X within each level of Y."*
>
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize to 100%** in stacked bar problems — Students often draw stacked bars using raw $n_{ij}$ but call it a "100% stacked bar." That is actually a *regular stacked bar*. Both are valid plots but the **question wording determines the right one**.
>
> 3. **Drawing scatter plots** for two nominal variables — This is an automatic 2-mark penalty because it shows a fundamental misunderstanding of variable measurement scales.
>
> 4. **Not labeling axes and legend** in schematic diagrams — KTU board examiners allocate **1 full mark** to axis labels, **1 mark** to legend, and **1 mark** to title. Missing any of these costs a mark.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the ordinal nature** in dot plots and population pyramids — When one variable is ordinal, you must **order the categories** in the plot. Drawing a dot plot in arbitrary category order loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **In mosaic plots**, students forget that **width = column marginal** and **height = conditional proportion**. The cell *area* is the joint frequency. Mixing these up loses 2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bivariate Categorical Visualization**
>
> ✅ Two categorical variables cannot be plotted using scatter plots or line graphs — use categorical plots.
>
> ✅ **Contingency table** is the mandatory first step before any chart.
>
> ✅ Master the four layered quantities: joint $n_{ij}$, marginal $n_{i\cdot}$ and $n_{\cdot j}$, grand total $n$, and relative $f_{ij}$.
>
> ✅ **Grouped Bar Chart** → use raw counts; best for direct comparison.
>
> ✅ **100% Stacked Bar** → use row %; best for comparing compositions.
>
> ✅ **Mosaic Plot** → uses joint relative frequency; area encodes $f_{ij}$.
>
> ✅ **Heatmap** → use $f_{ij}$ or row %; best for many categories.
>
> ✅ **Population Pyramid** → two ordinal variables (e.g., Age Group × Gender).
>
> ✅ **Chi-square test** quantifies independence; use expected $E_{ij} = (n_{i\cdot} \times n_{\cdot j})/n$.
>
> ✅ Always label: X-axis, Y-axis, legend, title, and units (frequency / %).
>
> ✅ Conditional probability notation uses vertical bar $\vert$ — *not* the markdown pipe symbol.
>
> ✅ Sum of all $f_{ij}$ = 1 (joint relative); sum of row % per row = 100%; sum of column % per column = 100%.
>
> ✅ KTU frequently tests the **chart-selection justification** — be ready to argue *why* you chose a particular plot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures of Association for Two Discrete Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measures of Association for Two Discrete Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MEASURES OF ASSOCIATION FOR TWO DISCRETE VARIABLES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Measure of Association** is a numerical index (a single scalar quantity) that quantifies the **strength**, **direction**, and **statistical significance** of a relationship between two categorical (discrete) variables observed on the same set of subjects. The values of these variables are typically organized in a **contingency table** of size $r \times c$, where $r$ denotes the number of rows (categories of variable 1) and $c$ denotes the number of columns (categories of variable 2).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECST523 – Data Analytics (Module 2)**, the following measures of association are prescribed for examination:

| Level of Measurement | Recommended Measure |
|---|---|
| Nominal × Nominal | **Chi-Square (χ²)**, **Phi (φ)**, **Cramér's V**, **Contingency Coefficient (C)**, **Lambda (λ)**, **Goodman–Kruskal Tau (τ)** |
| Ordinal × Ordinal | **Gamma (γ)**, **Somers' d**, **Kendall's Tau-b / Tau-c** |
| 2 × 2 Binary | **Odds Ratio (OR)**, **Yule's Q**, **Relative Risk (RR)**, **Phi (φ)** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition — "The Tea-Sugar Mystery"**
> Imagine a canteen owner wants to know: *"Does the time of day (Morning / Afternoon / Evening) influence whether a customer prefers Tea or Coffee?"* He records 300 transactions in a small grid. By glancing at the grid, he senses more tea in the morning and more coffee in the evening — but **is this pattern real, or just random luck?**
> A **measure of association** is the mathematical "ruler" that tells him:
> 1. **Does a relationship exist?** (Hypothesis test, e.g., χ²)
> 2. **How strong is it?** (Magnitude, e.g., Cramér's V ∈ [0, 1])
> 3. **In which direction?** (e.g., positive Gamma → similar rankings)
> 4. **How much does knowing one variable reduce error in predicting the other?** (e.g., Lambda)

The **observed frequency** $O_{ij}$ is what the data actually shows. The **expected frequency** $E_{ij}$ is what we *would* see if the two variables were perfectly **independent** (no association at all).

$$
E_{ij} \;=\; \frac{(\text{Row Total}_i) \times (\text{Column Total}_j)}{\text{Grand Total } (N)}
$$

### 1.3 The Contingency Table — The Foundation

For two discrete variables $X$ (with $r$ categories) and $Y$ (with $c$ categories), data is laid out as:

|  | $Y_1$ | $Y_2$ | $\cdots$ | $Y_c$ | **Row Total** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $X_1$ | $O_{11}$ | $O_{12}$ | $\cdots$ | $O_{1c}$ | $R_1$ |
| $X_2$ | $O_{21}$ | $O_{22}$ | $\cdots$ | $O_{2c}$ | $R_2$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\ddots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $X_r$ | $O_{r1}$ | $O_{r2}$ | $\cdots$ | $O_{rc}$ | $R_r$ |
| **Col Total** | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $\cdots$ | $C_c$ | $N$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** All measures of association in this module are derived from a single foundation — the **chi-square statistic** $\chi^2$. Master its computation, and the rest of the module is just normalization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Six-Step Procedure

When answering any KTU 14-mark question on this topic, follow this exact algorithmic logic:

1. **Construct** the $r \times c$ contingency table from the raw data and compute marginal totals $R_i$, $C_j$, and the grand total $N$.
2. **Compute** the expected frequency for every cell using $E_{ij} = (R_i \times C_j)/N$.
3. **Calculate** the chi-square statistic: $\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$.
4. **Test independence** by comparing $\chi^2$ against the critical value $\chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1),\, \alpha}$ at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$.
5. **Quantify strength** by converting $\chi^2$ into a normalized measure (Cramér's V, Phi, Contingency C, etc.).
6. **Interpret** using PRE (Proportional Reduction in Error) logic for Lambda / Tau if prediction is the goal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
> - **Why expected frequency?** Because under the null hypothesis $H_0$: *X and Y are independent*, the joint probability equals the product of marginals: $P(X_i \cap Y_j) = P(X_i) \cdot P(Y_j)$, giving $E_{ij} = N \cdot P(X_i) \cdot P(Y_j) = R_i C_j / N$.
> - **Why square the deviations?** To eliminate cancellation between cells with $O > E$ and $O < E$.
> - **Why divide by $E_{ij}$?** To give more weight to deviations in cells with smaller expected counts (standardization).
> - **Why normalize $\chi^2$?** Because raw $\chi^2$ increases with $N$ and with table size — making it incomparable across studies.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Markdown tables break if the pipe symbol $\vert$ appears inside a cell. Therefore, in all formula tables below, absolute value is rendered as $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ and conditional operators are written in words.

### Table A — Foundation Statistic

| Item | Formula | Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Expected Frequency | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$ | $E_{ij} \geq 5$ required for valid χ² |
| Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{i}\sum_{j} \dfrac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | $\chi^2 \geq 0$ |
| Degrees of Freedom | $df = (r-1)(c-1)$ | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| Independence Rule | $P(X_i \cap Y_j) = P(X_i) \cdot P(Y_j)$ | Holds iff $O_{ij} = E_{ij}$ |

### Table B — Normalized Strength Measures (Nominal)

| Measure | Formula | Range | Best Used When |
|---|---|:---:|---|
| **Phi Coefficient (φ)** | $\phi = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N}}$ | $[0, 1]$ for $2 \times 2$ | Only $2 \times 2$ tables |
| **Cramér's V** | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1, c-1)}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Any $r \times c$ table |
| **Contingency Coeff. (C)** | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + N}}$ | $[0, C_{\max} < 1]$ | Any table, but no fixed upper bound |
| **Tschuprow's T** | $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N \sqrt{(r-1)(c-1)}}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Square tables only |
| **Lambda (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{E_1 - E_2}{N - E_2}$ | $[0, 1]$ | PRE in prediction |
| **Goodman–Kruskal τ** | $\tau = \dfrac{E_1 - E_2}{N - E_2}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Asymmetric association |

### Table C — 2 × 2 Binary Table Layout

|  | $Y = 1$ | $Y = 0$ | **Total** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $X = 1$ | $a$ | $b$ | $a+b$ |
| $X = 0$ | $c$ | $d$ | $c+d$ |
| **Total** | $a+c$ | $b+d$ | $N$ |

| Measure | Formula | Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|:---:|---|
| **Odds Ratio (OR)** | $OR = \dfrac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | OR > 1 → positive assoc.; OR = 1 → independence |
| **Yule's Q** | $Q = \dfrac{ad - bc}{ad + bc}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Sign indicates direction; magnitude indicates strength |
| **Yule's Y** | $Y = \dfrac{\sqrt{ad} - \sqrt{bc}}{\sqrt{ad} + \sqrt{bc}}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | More conservative than Q |
| **Phi (φ)** | $\phi = \dfrac{ad - bc}{\sqrt{(a+b)(c+d)(a+c)(b+d)}}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Equivalent to Pearson r for binary data |
| **Relative Risk $RR_{1}$** | $RR_1 = \dfrac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Risk in $X=1$ relative to $X=0$ |

### Table D — Ordinal Measures

| Measure | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Gamma (γ)** | $\gamma = \dfrac{N_s - N_d}{N_s + N_d}$ | $N_s$ = concordant pairs; $N_d$ = discordant pairs |
| **Kendall's Tau-b** | $\tau_b = \dfrac{N_s - N_d}{\sqrt{(N_s+N_d+T_x)(N_s+N_d+T_y)}}$ | Corrects for ties; range $[-1, 1]$ |
| **Kendall's Tau-c** | $\tau_c = \dfrac{2m(N_s - N_d)}{N^2(m-1)}$ | $m = \min(r, c)$; range $[-1, 1]$ |
| **Somers' $d_{yx}$** | $d_{yx} = \dfrac{N_s - N_d}{N_s + N_d + T_y}$ | Directional: predicting $Y$ from $X$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Analytics

- **Medical Diagnostics:** Odds Ratio and Relative Risk quantify the strength of association between a risk factor (smoking) and a disease (lung cancer).
- **A/B Testing in Industry:** Chi-square tests whether the *Click-Through Rate* of a webpage differs significantly between two design variants.
- **Quality Control:** Cramér's V is used in Six-Sigma to measure the dependency between defect type and production shift.
- **Recommender Systems:** Lambda is the conceptual ancestor of feature-selection metrics — it quantifies *information gain*.
- **Social Science Surveys:** Gamma is the gold standard for cross-tabulations of ordinal Likert-scale data (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing the Chi-Square Statistic

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A survey of 200 engineering students classifies them by **Branch (CS / EC / ME)** and **Internship Status (Placed / Not Placed)**. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether Branch and Placement are independent, and compute the strength of association.

### Step 1 — Construct the Observed Frequency Table

| Branch | Placed | Not Placed | **Row Total $R_i$** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CS | 60 | 20 | 80 |
| EC | 40 | 30 | 70 |
| ME | 25 | 25 | 50 |
| **Col Total $C_j$** | 125 | 75 | **N = 200** |

### Step 2 — Compute the Expected Frequencies

Using $E_{ij} = (R_i \times C_j)/N$:

$$
E_{11} = \frac{80 \times 125}{200} = 50.000, \quad E_{12} = \frac{80 \times 75}{200} = 30.000
$$

$$
E_{21} = \frac{70 \times 125}{200} = 43.750, \quad E_{22} = \frac{70 \times 75}{200} = 26.250
$$

$$
E_{31} = \frac{50 \times 125}{200} = 31.250, \quad E_{32} = \frac{50 \times 75}{200} = 18.750
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Check:** All $E_{ij} \geq 5$ → the chi-square approximation is **valid** for this $3 \times 2$ table.

### Step 3 — Compute the Chi-Square Statistic

$$
\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{3}\sum_{j=1}^{2} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
$$

Compute each cell's contribution explicitly:

$$
\text{Cell}(1,1): \frac{(60 - 50)^2}{50} = \frac{100}{50} = 2.000
$$

$$
\text{Cell}(1,2): \frac{(20 - 30)^2}{30} = \frac{100}{30} = 3.333
$$

$$
\text{Cell}(2,1): \frac{(40 - 43.75)^2}{43.75} = \frac{14.0625}{43.75} = 0.321
$$

$$
\text{Cell}(2,2): \frac{(30 - 26.25)^2}{26.25} = \frac{14.0625}{26.25} = 0.536
$$

$$
\text{Cell}(3,1): \frac{(25 - 31.25)^2}{31.25} = \frac{39.0625}{31.25} = 1.250
$$

$$
\text{Cell}(3,2): \frac{(25 - 18.75)^2}{18.75} = \frac{39.0625}{18.75} = 2.083
$$

Summing all six contributions:

$$
\chi^2 = 2.000 + 3.333 + 0.321 + 0.536 + 1.250 + 2.083 = 9.524
$$

### Step 4 — Hypothesis Test

Degrees of freedom:

$$
df = (r-1)(c-1) = (3-1)(2-1) = 2
$$

Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
\chi^2_{0.05,\, 2} = 5.991
$$

Since the computed $\chi^2 = 9.524 > 5.991$, we **reject $H_0$**. Branch and Placement are **not independent** at the 5% level.

### Step 5 — Quantify Strength Using Cramér's V

$$
V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1,\, c-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.524}{200 \cdot \min(2,\, 1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.524}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0476} = 0.218
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

| V value | Strength (Cohen's guideline) |
|:---:|---|
| 0.00 – 0.10 | Negligible |
| 0.10 – 0.30 | Weak |
| 0.30 – 0.50 | Moderate |
| $\geq 0.50$ | Strong |

Since $V = 0.218$, there is a **weak but statistically significant** association between Engineering Branch and Placement Status.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — 2 × 2 Table: Odds Ratio, Yule's Q, Phi

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A medical study on 400 patients records smoking status (Smoker / Non-Smoker) and lung disease (Present / Absent). Compute OR, Yule's Q, Yule's Y, and Phi.

### Step 1 — Observed 2 × 2 Table

|  | Disease Present | Disease Absent | **Total** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Smoker | 90 | 110 | 200 |
| Non-Smoker | 30 | 170 | 200 |
| **Total** | 120 | 280 | **N = 400** |

Here $a = 90$, $b = 110$, $c = 30$, $d = 170$.

### Step 2 — Odds Ratio

$$
OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} = \frac{90 \times 170}{110 \times 30} = \frac{15300}{3300} = 4.636
$$

**Interpretation:** The odds of lung disease among smokers are **4.64 times** the odds among non-smokers.

### Step 3 — Yule's Q

$$
Q = \frac{ad - bc}{ad + bc} = \frac{(90 \times 170) - (110 \times 30)}{(90 \times 170) + (110 \times 30)} = \frac{15300 - 3300}{15300 + 3300} = \frac{12000}{18600} = 0.6452
$$

**Interpretation:** A **strong positive association** ($Q$ is close to 1).

### Step 4 — Yule's Y

$$
Y = \frac{\sqrt{ad} - \sqrt{bc}}{\sqrt{ad} + \sqrt{bc}} = \frac{\sqrt{15300} - \sqrt{3300}}{\sqrt{15300} + \sqrt{3300}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{15300} = 123.693, \quad \sqrt{3300} = 57.445
$$

$$
Y = \frac{123.693 - 57.445}{123.693 + 57.445} = \frac{66.248}{181.138} = 0.3658
$$

**Interpretation:** Yule's Y is **more conservative** (0.37) than Yule's Q (0.65); this is the well-known **Yule's Q–Y paradox**.

### Step 5 — Phi Coefficient

$$
\phi = \frac{ad - bc}{\sqrt{(a+b)(c+d)(a+c)(b+d)}} = \frac{15300 - 3300}{\sqrt{200 \cdot 200 \cdot 120 \cdot 280}}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{200 \cdot 200 \cdot 120 \cdot 280} = \sqrt{1{,}344{,}000{,}000} = 36668.776
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{12000}{36668.776} = 0.3272
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** For a $2 \times 2$ table, $\phi^2 = \chi^2/N$. Verify: $\chi^2 = \phi^2 \cdot N = (0.3272)^2 \times 400 = 0.1071 \times 400 = 42.82$. Independently computing $\chi^2$ from raw data should yield the same value.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Gamma (γ) for Ordinal Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Customers rate a product (Poor / Fair / Good / Excellent) and likelihood to repurchase (Low / Medium / High). Using 8 customers' data, compute Gamma.

### Step 1 — Observed 4 × 3 Table

| Rating | Low | Medium | High | **Total** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Poor | 8 | 4 | 2 | 14 |
| Fair | 6 | 10 | 8 | 24 |
| Good | 4 | 12 | 16 | 32 |
| Excellent | 2 | 6 | 22 | 30 |
| **Total** | 20 | 32 | 48 | **N = 100** |

### Step 2 — Count Concordant Pairs ($N_s$)

Two subjects are **concordant** if the one higher on Rating is also higher on Repurchase. We count pairs of subjects in the same row where the column is lower, times subjects in a higher row with a higher column.

For each cell $O_{ij}$, multiply by the sum of all cells strictly to the **lower-right** (greater row, greater column):

$$
N_s = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} O_{ij} \cdot S_{ij}^{lr}
$$

where $S_{ij}^{lr} = \sum_{p>i,\, q>j} O_{pq}$.

**Computation (exhaustive):**

- Cell (Poor, Low) = 8: lower-right sum = $10+8+12+16+6+22 = 74$ → contribution = $8 \times 74 = 592$
- Cell (Poor, Med) = 4: lower-right sum = $8+16+22 = 46$ → $4 \times 46 = 184$
- Cell (Poor, High) = 2: lower-right sum = $0$ → $0$
- Cell (Fair, Low) = 6: lower-right sum = $12+16+6+22 = 56$ → $6 \times 56 = 336$
- Cell (Fair, Med) = 10: lower-right sum = $16+22 = 38$ → $10 \times 38 = 380$
- Cell (Fair, High) = 8: lower-right sum = $0$ → $0$
- Cell (Good, Low) = 4: lower-right sum = $6+22 = 28$ → $4 \times 28 = 112$
- Cell (Good, Med) = 12: lower-right sum = $22$ → $12 \times 22 = 264$
- Cell (Good, High) = 16: lower-right sum = $0$ → $0$
- Cell (Excellent, Low) = 2: lower-right sum = $0$ → $0$
- Cell (Excellent, Med) = 6: lower-right sum = $0$ → $0$
- Cell (Excellent, High) = 22: lower-right sum = $0$ → $0$

$$
N_s = 592 + 184 + 0 + 336 + 380 + 0 + 112 + 264 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 1868
$$

### Step 3 — Count Discordant Pairs ($N_d$)

Mirror logic — cells to the **upper-right** (smaller row index, larger column index):

$$
N_d = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} O_{ij} \cdot S_{ij}^{ur}
$$

- Cell (Poor, Low) = 8: upper-right sum = $4+2 = 6$ → $8 \times 6 = 48$
- Cell (Poor, Med) = 4: upper-right sum = $2$ → $4 \times 2 = 8$
- Cell (Poor, High) = 2: upper-right sum = $0$ → $0$
- Cell (Fair, Low) = 6: upper-right sum = $10+8+12+16+6+22 = 74$ → $6 \times 74 = 444$
- Cell (Fair, Med) = 10: upper-right sum = $8+16+22 = 46$ → $10 \times 46 = 460$
- Cell (Fair, High) = 8: upper-right sum = $0$ → $0$
- Cell (Good, Low) = 4: upper-right sum = $0$ → $0$ (no rows above)
- All higher cells: $0$

$$
N_d = 48 + 8 + 0 + 444 + 460 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 960
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Gamma

$$
\gamma = \frac{N_s - N_d}{N_s + N_d} = \frac{1868 - 960}{1868 + 960} = \frac{908}{2828} = 0.3211
$$

**Interpretation:** A **moderate positive ordinal association**: customers who rate the product higher also tend to be more likely to repurchase.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Lambda (λ) — Proportional Reduction in Error

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Using the Branch × Placement data from Example 1, compute the **directional Lambda** predicting Placement (Y) from Branch (X).

### Logic Setup

- $E_1$ = errors made when we **know** the predictor variable (X).
- $E_2$ = errors made when we **ignore** the predictor (i.e., we always guess the modal category of Y).

### Step 1 — Errors Without Knowing Branch (Mode of Y)

Without knowing the branch, we always predict **Placed** (the modal column, with 125 subjects).

$$
E_2 = N - \max(C_j) = 200 - 125 = 75
$$

### Step 2 — Errors When Branch Is Known

For each branch (row), predict the modal placement within that row:

| Branch | Placed | Not Placed | Modal in Row | Errors in Row |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CS | 60 | 20 | Placed | 20 |
| EC | 40 | 30 | Placed | 30 |
| ME | 25 | 25 | Tie — pick one (20 errors if Placed predicted) | 25 |

$$
E_1 = 20 + 30 + 25 = 75
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Lambda

$$
\lambda_{Y|X} = \frac{E_1 - E_2}{N - E_2} = \frac{75 - 75}{200 - 75} = \frac{0}{125} = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** Knowing the branch **does not reduce predictive error** for placement — the modal prediction is "Placed" both globally and within every branch where the modal is clear, and ties cancel the gain. This is a **valid engineering insight**: branch alone is not a useful predictor of placement at the individual-student level, even though it is *statistically* significant (the chi-square test captured aggregate dependency, not predictive power).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Choosing the Right Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two Discrete Variables X and Y] --> B{Is the table 2x2?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is X a risk factor with Y as outcome?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Odds Ratio OR / Relative Risk RR]
    C -- No --> E[Use Phi coefficient OR Yules Q]
    B -- No --> F{What is the measurement level?}
    F -- Both Nominal --> G{Goal?}
    G -- Test independence --> H[Use Chi-Square Test of Independence]
    G -- Measure strength --> I[Use Cramers V or Contingency C]
    G -- Reduce prediction error --> J[Use Lambda or Goodman-Kruskal Tau]
    F -- Both Ordinal --> K[Use Gamma or Kendall Tau]
    F -- Mixed Nominal Ordinal --> L[Use Somers d]

    H --> M[Decision: Reject or Fail to reject H0 at alpha]
    I --> N[Strength lies in 0 1: 0=No assoc, 1=Perfect assoc]
    J --> O[PRE value: % reduction in prediction error]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style N fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style O fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Workflow Diagram — Executing the Chi-Square Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1: Tabulate Observed Frequencies O sub ij] --> stepB[Step 2: Compute Row and Column Marginals]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Calculate Expected Frequencies E sub ij using R sub i times C sub j over N]
    stepC --> stepD{Step 4: Are all E sub ij at least 5?}
    stepD -- No --> stepE[Apply Yates Continuity Correction or Fishers Exact Test]
    stepD -- Yes --> stepF[Step 5: Compute Chi-Square Statistic]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Determine Degrees of Freedom df equals r minus 1 times c minus 1]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Compare with Critical Value at alpha equals 0 point 05]
    stepH --> stepI{Decision}
    stepI -- Reject H0 --> stepJ[Step 8: Compute Cramers V to quantify strength]
    stepI -- Fail to reject H0 --> stepK[Conclude: Variables are independent]

    style stepA fill:#bbdefb,color:#000000
    style stepC fill:#fff59d,color:#000000
    style stepF fill:#ffcdd2,color:#000000
    style stepJ fill:#c8e6c9,color:#000000
    style stepK fill:#eeeeee,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Association Strength (Nominal Measures)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[All Measures of Association for Discrete Variables] --> lvl1[Foundation: Chi-Square Statistic]
    root --> lvl2[Normalised 0 to 1 Strength Measures]
    root --> lvl3[Prediction Oriented PRE Measures]

    lvl1 --> chi[Chi-Square: Tests Existence Only]

    lvl2 --> phi[Phi: Only for 2x2 tables]
    lvl2 --> cramer[Cramers V: Any table size, Most Recommended]
    lvl2 --> cc[Contingency C: Has no fixed maximum]
    lvl2 --> tsch[Tschuprow T: Only square tables]

    lvl3 --> lam[Lambda: Modal category prediction]
    lvl3 --> tau[Goodman Kruskal Tau: Probabilistic prediction]
    lvl3 --> uncert[Uncertainty Coefficient: Symmetric information theoretic]

    style root fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style chi fill:#ef9a9a,color:#000000
```
