# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Define *Data Preprocessing*. List any four major tasks involved in data preprocessing.

**Model Answer:**
*Data preprocessing* is the process of transforming raw, real-world data into a clean, consistent, and analysis-ready format suitable for data mining or machine learning. It is a critical phase of the KDD process that consumes 60–80% of the total project effort.

**Four Major Tasks:**
1. **Data Cleaning** — Handling missing values, noise, and outliers.
2. **Data Integration** — Merging data from multiple heterogeneous sources.
3. **Data Reduction** — Reducing data volume (dimensionality, numerosity, or compression).
4. **Data Transformation** — Normalizing and aggregating data into suitable forms (e.g., min-max, z-score).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q:** What is meant by *data discretization*? Differentiate between **equal-width binning** and **equal-frequency binning**.

**Model Answer:**
*Data discretization* is the process of transforming a continuous attribute into a categorical (discrete) one by partitioning its range into intervals.

| Feature | Equal-Width Binning | Equal-Frequency Binning |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Divides the data range into $N$ intervals of equal width. | Divides the data into $N$ groups, each having the same number of values. |
| Width | $\text{Width} = \frac{\max - \min}{N}$ | Variable width; uniform frequency |
| Sensitivity | Highly sensitive to outliers | Robust to outliers |
| Example | $[0,10), [10,20), [20,30]$ | Each bin contains 33 values |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Data Cleaning Focus

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** The following dataset represents monthly temperatures (in °C) recorded across 12 months:
$$\{15, 18, 19, 22, 25, 28, 31, 200, 32, 30, 27, 21\}$$
The value **200** is suspected to be a data-entry error. Apply the **IQR method** to detect and handle this outlier. Also perform **equal-width binning** into 4 bins and apply **bin-mean smoothing** on the cleaned data. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the various **missing value handling techniques** with suitable examples. When would you prefer **regression imputation** over **mean imputation**? **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) IQR + Binning Solution

**Step 1 — Sort the data**
$$\{15, 18, 19, 21, 22, 25, 27, 28, 30, 31, 32, 200\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Quartiles** (n = 12)
- $Q_1$ position $= \frac{n+1}{4} = 3.25$ → interpolate between 3rd and 4th values: $\frac{19 + 21 \times 0.25}{1}$ ... simplified → $Q_1 \approx 20.0$
- $Q_3$ position $= \frac{3(n+1)}{4} = 9.75$ → $Q_3 \approx 30.75$

**Step 3 — Compute IQR**
$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 30.75 - 20.0 = 10.75$$

**Step 4 — Compute Fences**
- Upper Fence $= 30.75 + 1.5 \times 10.75 = 46.875$
- Lower Fence $= 20.0 - 1.5 \times 10.75 = 3.875$

**Conclusion:** Since $200 > 46.875$, the value **200 is an outlier**. Remove it.

**Cleaned data:** $\{15, 18, 19, 21, 22, 25, 27, 28, 30, 31, 32\}$ (n = 11)

**Step 5 — Equal-Width Binning into 4 Bins**
$$\text{Width} = \frac{32 - 15}{4} = 4.25$$

| Bin | Range | Values | Mean |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $[15.00, 19.25)$ | $15, 18, 19$ | $17.33$ |
| 2 | $[19.25, 23.50)$ | $21, 22$ | $21.50$ |
| 3 | $[23.50, 27.75)$ | $25, 27$ | $26.00$ |
| 4 | $[27.75, 32.00]$ | $28, 30, 31, 32$ | $30.25$ |

**Step 6 — Apply Bin-Mean Smoothing**

| Original | Bin | Smoothed |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 15 | 1 | 17.33 |
| 18 | 1 | 17.33 |
| 19 | 1 | 17.33 |
| 21 | 2 | 21.50 |
| 22 | 2 | 21.50 |
| 25 | 3 | 26.00 |
| 27 | 3 | 26.00 |
| 28 | 4 | 30.25 |
| 30 | 4 | 30.25 |
| 31 | 4 | 30.25 |
| 32 | 4 | 30.25 |

---

#### (b) Missing Value Techniques Discussion

**1. Ignore the Tuple:** Drop rows with missing values. Used when missing % < 5% or class label is missing.

**2. Manual Imputation:** Domain expert fills values. Time-consuming; ideal for small datasets.

**3. Global/Constant Imputation:** Replace with a constant (e.g., "Unknown"). Useful for categorical attributes where "missing" is meaningful.

**4. Mean/Median/Mode Imputation:** Use the central tendency.
- *Mean* for symmetric numerical data
- *Median* for skewed numerical data
- *Mode* for categorical data

**5. Hot-Deck Imputation:** Fill missing value using similar records (e.g., matching demographics).

**6. Cold-Deck Imputation:** Use external historical data source.

**7. Regression Imputation:** Predict missing values using a regression model trained on the non-missing features.

**8. K-NN Imputation:** Find the *k* most similar records (using Euclidean or Manhattan distance) and average their values.

**When to prefer Regression over Mean Imputation:**
- When the attribute with missing values is **strongly correlated** with other attributes.
- When the **missingness is not completely at random (MNAR)** — mean imputation will distort the distribution.
- When **preserving variance** is critical (mean imputation reduces variance artificially).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to remove or correct the outlier** before binning — this contaminates the bin means.
> 2. **Confusing IQR calculation** when n is small — use the correct linear interpolation formula.
> 3. In the regression imputation answer, students often **do not name the type of regression** (linear, logistic, multiple) — be specific!
> 4. **Not explaining the difference between MCAR, MAR, and MNAR** missingness — this is a top KTU 2024 marking hotspot.
> 5. Skipping the **disadvantage of mean imputation** (variance reduction / distortion of distribution) loses 1 mark easily.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Data Integration, Reduction & Transformation Focus

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **data integration**. Discuss the major challenges faced during data integration, including **schema integration**, **redundancy detection using correlation**, and **data value conflicts**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the following **data transformation** techniques: **Min-Max Normalization**, **Z-Score Normalization**, and **Decimal Scaling**. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Data Integration

**Definition:**
*Data Integration* is the process of combining data from **multiple heterogeneous sources** (relational databases, flat files, web services, data cubes) into a single, unified, and consistent dataset for analysis.

**Major Challenges:**

**1. Schema Integration:**
- Different sources use different schemas (e.g., one DB calls it `roll_no`, another calls it `student_id`).
- Metadata analysis is required to align attribute meanings.
- Tools: **CORBA**, **Mediator-based architectures**, **Data Warehouse ETL**.

**2. Redundancy:**
- An attribute is redundant if it can be derived from another (e.g., "total_price" is derived from "unit_price" × "quantity").
- Detected via:
  - **Pearson Correlation Coefficient** (for numerical data) — formula:
    $$r_{A,B} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(a_i - \bar{A})(b_i - \bar{B})}{(n-1)\sigma_A \sigma_B}$$
    $\vert r \vert > 0.85$ signals redundancy.
  - **Chi-Square Test** (for categorical data) — high $\chi^2$ indicates dependency.

**3. Data Value Conflicts:**
- Same attribute, different values across sources.
- Common causes:
  - Different units (e.g., metric vs. imperial).
  - Different currencies.
  - Different naming conventions (e.g., "Mr. R. Sharma" vs. "Rahul Sharma").
  - Different scales or codes.
- **Resolution:** Establish a global schema, unit conversion, and conflict-resolution rules.

**Additional Challenges:**
- **Tuple Duplication:** Same record appears in multiple sources — detected via primary keys or similarity matching.
- **Inconsistencies in data updates** — temporal mismatch.

---

#### (b) Data Transformation Techniques

**1. Min-Max Normalization**

Performs a linear transformation on the original data to map values into a specified range, typically $[0, 1]$.

$$v' = \frac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)}$$

> **Example:** $A = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$
> - $\min = 10, \max = 50$, range = $40$
> - $v = 20 \Rightarrow v' = \frac{20 - 10}{40} = 0.25$

**Advantages:**
- Preserves the exact relationships between data points.
- All values fall within a bounded range — useful for neural networks and distance-based algorithms (K-NN, K-Means).

**Disadvantages:**
- Highly sensitive to outliers (they squeeze the rest of the data into a narrow range).

**2. Z-Score Normalization (Standardization)**

$$v' = \frac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$$

> **Example:** $A = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$
> - $\mu = 30, \sigma \approx 14.14$
> - $v = 20 \Rightarrow v' = \frac{20 - 30}{14.14} \approx -0.707$

**Advantages:**
- Less affected by outliers than min-max.
- Produces a distribution with $\mu = 0, \sigma = 1$ — required for **PCA**, **logistic regression**, **SVM**.

**Disadvantages:**
- Does not produce a bounded range — values can lie outside $[0, 1]$.
- Not suitable when the original data is not approximately normal.

**3. Decimal Scaling**

$$v' = \frac{v}{10^j}$$
where $j$ is the smallest integer such that $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$.

> **Example:** $A = \{350, 1234, 87, 9500\}$
> - $\max \vert v \vert = 9500$. $9500 / 10^4 = 0.95 < 1$, so $j = 4$.
> - $v = 1234 \Rightarrow v' = 0.1234$.

**Advantages:**
- Simple; preserves data distribution.
- Useful when the magnitude of values needs to be reduced without changing the shape.

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires knowledge of the maximum absolute value in advance.
- Ineffective if the range of values is not a power of 10.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Technique | Output Range | Outlier Sensitivity | Best Used For |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Min-Max | $[0,1]$ (or any) | **High** | K-NN, Neural Nets, Image Processing |
| Z-Score | Unbounded | **Low** | PCA, SVM, Logistic Regression |
| Decimal Scaling | $\vert v' \vert < 1$ | Medium | Quick magnitude reduction |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Miscounting formula components** — students often miss the denominator $(n-1)$ in correlation. Always use $n-1$ for **sample** standard deviation.
> 2. **Forgetting the threshold value** — the KTU 2024 answer key explicitly asks for the threshold ($\vert r \vert > 0.85$ or similar). Don't just say "high correlation"; state the cut-off.
> 3. **Mixing up Chi-Square and Pearson** — Chi-Square is for **categorical** data; Pearson is for **numerical** data. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for confusing them.
> 4. **Decimal Scaling — students frequently forget to show the "smallest j"** choice rationale. Always justify why you chose that particular $j$.
> 5. **Missing the "data value conflict" example** with proper resolution — for example, currency conversion or unit normalization. A vague answer loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Cheat Sheet — Print This:**

- [x] **Data Preprocessing** = Cleaning + Integration + Reduction + Transformation + Discretization
- [x] **Missing Data Strategies:** Ignore, Manual, Global/Constant, Mean/Median/Mode, Hot-Deck, Cold-Deck, Regression, K-NN
- [x] **Noisy Data Smoothing:** Binning (mean/median/boundary), Regression, Clustering
- [x] **Outlier Detection:** Z-Score ($\vert Z \vert > 3$) and IQR (fences at $Q \pm 1.5 \times IQR$)
- [x] **Data Integration** = combine multiple sources; challenges: schema integration, redundancy, tuple duplication, value conflicts
- [x] **Redundancy Detection:** Pearson correlation (numerical), Chi-Square (categorical); threshold $\vert r \vert > 0.85$
- [x] **Data Reduction Tactic Types:** Dimensionality (PCA), Numerosity (sampling, histograms, regression), Compression (lossless/lossy)
- [x] **Min-Max Normalization:** $v' = \frac{v - \min}{\max - \min}$ → range $[0,1]$; high outlier sensitivity
- [x] **Z-Score Normalization:** $v' = \frac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$ → mean 0, std 1; needed for PCA/SVM
- [x] **Decimal Scaling:** $v' = v / 10^j$; choose smallest $j$ such that $\max \vert v' \vert < 1$
- [x] **Discretization Methods:** Binning, Histogram, Clustering, Decision Tree, ChiMerge (correlation-based)
- [x] **Binning Variants:** Equal-Width (fixed interval) vs Equal-Frequency (fixed count); smoothing by Mean/Median/Boundary
- [x] **Concept Hierarchy:** Multi-level abstraction of attributes (e.g., age → young/middle/old → adult/non-adult)
- [x] **GIGO Principle:** Garbage In → Garbage Out — preprocessing is non-negotiable
- [x] **Time Investment:** Industry reports show 60–80% of project effort goes to preprocessing
- [x] **Always mention MCAR/MAR/MNAR** in missing value theory answers
- [x] **Show the threshold/range** explicitly in normalization answers (don't just describe)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> Every answer must include a **formula**, a **numerical example**, and a **limitation/advantage**. Answer keys are weighted heavily on these three elements. Pure theoretical answers without numbers typically score only 60–70% of the allocated marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mining Frequent Patterns - Associations, Correlations, and Apriori Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mining Frequent Patterns - Associations, Correlations, and Apriori Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Frequent Pattern Mining?

**Frequent Pattern Mining** is a fundamental data mining task that involves discovering patterns (such as itemsets, subsequences, or substructures) that occur frequently in a dataset. A *frequent pattern* is a pattern that appears in a transaction database with a frequency no less than a user-specified threshold called the **minimum support threshold (min_sup)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Frequent pattern mining searches for recurring relationships in a given data set. These patterns can be of several types, including *frequent itemsets*, *frequent subsequences* (considering order), and *frequent substructures* (considering different structural forms such as trees, graphs, or lattices).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework, this topic maps directly to:
- **CO3:** Apply frequent pattern mining and classification algorithms to extract insights from data.
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyze (Level 3 & 4)

---

## 1.2 Market Basket Analysis — The Foundational Use Case

### Intuition: The Supermarket Story

Imagine you are the manager of a large supermarket chain in Kerala. You have a database of **10,000 transactions** (bills) from your Kochi store. You notice something curious:

- Customers who buy **Tea** often also buy **Sugar**.
- Customers who buy **Bread** frequently also buy **Butter**.
- Customers who buy **Diapers** surprisingly often also buy **Beer** (the famous classic example).

You want to ask: **"Which items are frequently bought together so I can place them near each other on the shelf, bundle them in offers, or design cross-selling promotions?"**

This is exactly the problem **Association Rule Mining** solves. It is formally known as **Market Basket Analysis (MBA)**.

### Formal Statement of the Problem

Given a database of transactions, where each transaction is a set of items (a *basket*), find:
1. **All frequent itemsets** (sets of items that appear together often enough).
2. **All strong association rules** of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint itemsets, indicating that the presence of $X$ in a transaction implies the presence of $Y$ with high probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of frequent pattern mining as a *sociologist* studying human purchase behavior. The *sociologist* doesn't ask "what does a single person buy?" but rather "what groups of items are habitually co-purchased?" — and uses that knowledge to influence store layout, marketing, and inventory.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology — The Building Blocks

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Item** | A single product (e.g., *Milk*, *Bread*). |
| **Itemset** | A collection of one or more items. A $k$-itemset contains exactly $k$ items. |
| **Transaction** | A single record (e.g., one customer's bill) containing a set of items. |
| **Database $D$** | The full collection of $N$ transactions. |
| **Support count $\sigma(X)$** | The number of transactions in $D$ that contain the itemset $X$. |
| **Support $s(X)$** | The fraction of transactions in $D$ that contain $X$: $s(X) = \sigma(X) / N$. |
| **Frequent Itemset** | An itemset $X$ is *frequent* if $s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$. |
| **Association Rule** | An implication of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X \cap Y = \emptyset$. |
| **Confidence $c(X \Rightarrow Y)$** | The conditional probability that a transaction containing $X$ also contains $Y$: $c = s(X \cup Y) / s(X)$. |
| **Strong Rule** | A rule that satisfies both **min_sup** and **min_conf** thresholds. |
| **min_sup** | User-defined minimum support (e.g., 0.4 = 40%). |
| **min_conf** | User-defined minimum confidence (e.g., 0.6 = 60%). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Exam Tip:** KTU examiners *love* asking students to distinguish between **support** and **confidence**. Support measures *how often* the pattern appears; confidence measures *how reliable* the implication is.

---

## 1.4 Why Frequent Pattern Mining Matters?

- **Retail & E-commerce:** Cross-selling, recommendation systems (Amazon, Flipkart).
- **Bioinformatics:** Co-occurring gene expressions and protein interactions.
- **Telecommunications:** Identifying call patterns for fraud detection.
- **Banking:** Mining customer transaction patterns for credit risk.
- **Web Mining:** Clickstream analysis, query-log mining.
- **Healthcare:** Co-occurrence of symptoms and diseases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance:** In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, this topic is the gateway to advanced topics like *recommender systems*, *anomaly detection*, and *graph mining* — all of which appear in electives and capstone projects.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Formal Problem Statement

Let $I = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m\}$ be the set of all distinct items in a transaction database $D$, where $D = \{T_1, T_2, \dots, T_N\}$ and each transaction $T_k \subseteq I$.

**Frequent Itemset Mining:** Find all itemsets $X \subseteq I$ such that $s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$.

**Association Rule Mining:** Find all association rules $X \Rightarrow Y$ (with $X, Y \subset I$, $X \cap Y = \emptyset$, $Y \neq \emptyset$) such that:
- $s(X \cup Y) \geq \text{min\_sup}$ (rule holds with sufficient frequency)
- $c(X \Rightarrow Y) \geq \text{min\_conf}$ (rule is sufficiently reliable)

---

## 2.2 The Apriori Algorithm — Foundation of Frequent Pattern Mining

### 2.2.1 The Apriori Property (Anti-Monotonicity / Downward Closure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Apriori Property is the heart of the algorithm and a guaranteed KTU exam question.**

**Statement:** *All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent.*

Equivalently (the contrapositive): *If an itemset is infrequent, then all of its supersets are also infrequent.*

**Proof Intuition:**
- If transaction $T$ contains itemset $\{A, B, C\}$, then $T$ must also contain $\{A, B\}$, $\{A, C\}$, $\{B, C\}$, $\{A\}$, $\{B\}$, $\{C\}$.
- Therefore, the support of any subset cannot exceed the support of the superset.
- Formally: $\forall Y \subseteq X,\ s(Y) \geq s(X)$.

This **monotonicity** property is the key that prunes the exponential search space from $2^m$ possible itemsets to a manageable subset.

### 2.2.2 Two-Step Apriori Algorithm

The Apriori algorithm proceeds in **two major steps**:

**Step 1: Frequent Itemset Generation**
- Find all itemsets whose support $\geq$ min_sup.

**Step 2: Rule Generation**
- From each frequent itemset $L$, generate all strong association rules.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step: Frequent Itemset Generation (Apriori)

The frequent itemset generation uses an **iterative level-wise approach** known as **candidate generation-and-test**:

1. **Initialize $k = 1$.**
2. **Candidate Generation ($C_k$):** Generate candidate itemsets of size $k$.
3. **Support Counting:** Scan the database $D$ to compute $\sigma(X)$ for each $X \in C_k$.
4. **Pruning using Apriori Property:** Remove any candidate whose subset of size $k-1$ is not in $L_{k-1}$.
5. **Frequent Set Selection:** $L_k = \{X \in C_k \mid s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}\}$.
6. **Termination:** If $L_k = \emptyset$, stop; the union $L = L_1 \cup L_2 \cup \dots \cup L_k$ is the set of all frequent itemsets.
7. **Increment $k = k + 1$ and go to Step 2.**

### Candidate Generation: The Join Step (Apriori-Gen)

To generate $C_k$ from $L_{k-1}$:
- **Join step:** $L_{k-1} \bowtie L_{k-1}$ — Join two frequent $(k-1)$-itemsets if their first $k-2$ items are identical.
  - Formally, $l_1$ and $l_2$ are joined if $(l_1[1] = l_2[1]) \land (l_1[2] = l_2[2]) \land \dots \land (l_1[k-2] = l_2[k-2]) \land (l_1[k-1] < l_2[k-1])$.
- **Prune step:** Remove any candidate $c \in C_k$ where some $(k-1)$-subset of $c$ is *not* in $L_{k-1}$.

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step: Association Rule Generation

Given a frequent itemset $L$, generate all **non-empty subsets** $s \subset L$ such that the rule $s \Rightarrow (L - s)$ has confidence $\geq$ min_conf.

**Efficient Rule Generation using Apriori Property for Confidence:**
- Confidence is *anti-monotonic* with respect to the size of the right-hand side.
- If a rule $X \Rightarrow Y - X$ has low confidence, then any rule $X' \Rightarrow Y - X'$ where $X' \subset X$ will have confidence $\leq$ that of the original rule.
- This is exploited by generating rules in a bottom-up fashion: merge two rules with the same consequent to form larger antecedents.

**Algorithm (GenRules):**
- For each frequent itemset $L$, initialize the consequent list to all non-empty subsets of $L$.
- For each consequent $c$:
  - Compute $\text{conf} = s(L) / s(L - c)$.
  - If conf $\geq$ min_conf, output the rule $(L - c) \Rightarrow c$.
  - Else, prune $c$ and all its supersets from consideration.
- Recursively merge the surviving consequents to form longer consequents.

---

## 2.5 Correlation Measures Beyond Support and Confidence

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Special Focus:** The support-confidence framework has a critical weakness — it can be *misleading*. Correlation measures address this.

### 2.5.1 Lift (Interest Factor)

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)} = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{Lift} > 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **positively correlated** (presence of $X$ increases likelihood of $Y$).
- $\text{Lift} = 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **independent** (no correlation).
- $\text{Lift} < 1$: $X$ and $Y$ are **negatively correlated** (presence of $X$ decreases likelihood of $Y$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** A rule with high confidence can still have negative correlation. Example: A rule with 80% confidence that $X$ implies $Y$ when $Y$ occurs in 90% of all transactions is *negatively correlated* because the rule underperforms the base rate of $Y$.

### 2.5.2 $\chi^2$ (Chi-Square) Test

Tests statistical independence between $X$ and $\bar X$ vs. $Y$ and $\bar Y$.

### 2.5.3 All-Confidence

$$\text{all\_conf}(X, Y, Z) = \frac{s(X \cup Y \cup Z)}{\max\{s(X), s(Y), s(Z)\}}$$

Used in mining **hyperclique patterns** (items that are strongly correlated).

### 2.5.4 Conviction

$$\text{Conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{1 - s(Y)}{1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)}$$

- Conviction $= 1$: Independence.
- Conviction $> 1$: Positive dependence.
- Conviction $= \infty$: Perfect implication ($c = 1$).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Meaning** | **Range** |
|----------------------|---------------|-------------|-----------|
| Support of itemset $X$ | $s(X) = \sigma(X) / N$ | Frequency of $X$ in $D$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Support count of $X$ | $\sigma(X)$ | # transactions containing $X$ | $[0, N]$ |
| Confidence of $X \Rightarrow Y$ | $c = s(X \cup Y) / s(X)$ | Reliability of the rule | $[0, 1]$ |
| Lift (Interest) | $\text{Lift} = c(X \Rightarrow Y) / s(Y)$ | Correlation strength | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Conviction | $\text{Conv} = (1 - s(Y)) / (1 - c)$ | Asymmetric correlation | $[0, \infty]$ |
| Cosine | $\text{Cos}(X, Y) = s(X \cup Y) / \sqrt{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$ | Symmetric correlation | $[0, 1]$ |
| All-Confidence | $\text{all\_conf} = s(X \cup Y \cup Z) / \max\{s(X), s(Y), s(Z)\}$ | Hyperclique measure | $[0, 1]$ |
| Apriori Property | $Y \subseteq X \Rightarrow s(Y) \geq s(X)$ | Downward closure | — |
| Number of itemsets in $2^I$ | $2^{\vert I \vert}$ | Search space size | — |
| Apriori Complexity | $O(N \cdot \sum \vert C_k \vert)$ | DB scans | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pitfall — Pipe Symbol:** The expression $\vert I \vert$ is written using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table. Students should always remember $|I|$ denotes the **cardinality** (number of items) of the itemset universe.

---

## 2.7 Closed and Maximal Frequent Itemsets

Two important concepts that compress the output of frequent itemset mining:

- **Closed Frequent Itemset:** A frequent itemset $X$ is *closed* if there is no proper super-itemset $Y \supset X$ with the same support count: $\sigma(X) = \sigma(Y)$. The closure of $X$ is the largest such $Y$.
  - Closed itemsets are a *lossless* compression of all frequent itemsets.
- **Maximal Frequent Itemset:** A frequent itemset $X$ is *maximal* if no proper super-itemset of $X$ is frequent. The set of maximal itemsets is a *lossy* but compact representation.
  - **All maximal itemsets are closed, but not vice versa.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** When the database contains millions of items, returning *all* frequent itemsets is impractical. Closed/maximal itemsets are used in **FP-Growth**, **CHARM**, and other scalable algorithms — frequently appearing in KTU CS electives.

---

## 2.8 Limitations of the Apriori Algorithm

For a complete KTU answer, students should note the four classical weaknesses:

1. **Candidate Generation Bottleneck:** $2^{|I|}$ candidate itemsets are theoretically possible; even after pruning, $C_k$ can be enormous.
2. **Multiple Database Scans:** Apriori requires $k$ full scans of the database (one for each level $k$).
3. **Support-Confidence Paradox:** High confidence does not imply strong correlation (this is why *lift* and *conviction* are needed).
4. **Hard Thresholds:** Choosing min_sup is a trade-off — too low yields too many patterns, too high misses interesting ones.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Apriori Algorithm on a Toy Database

Let us work through a complete, exhaustive example of the Apriori algorithm. This is a **guaranteed KTU question type**.

### 3.1.1 The Input Database

Consider a transaction database $D$ with **$N = 5$ transactions** and **4 distinct items** $I = \{A, B, C, D\}$:

| **TID** | **Items Bought** |
|---------|------------------|
| T1 | $\{A, B, D\}$ |
| T2 | $\{A, C\}$ |
| T3 | $\{A, B, C\}$ |
| T4 | $\{B, C\}$ |
| T5 | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ |

Let **min_sup = 60%** (i.e., 3 out of 5 transactions) and **min_conf = 80%**.

---

### 3.1.2 Step 1: Generate $C_1$ and Compute Support

**Candidate 1-itemsets $C_1$:**

| **Itemset** | **Support Count $\sigma$** | **Support $s$** |
|------------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| $\{A\}$ | T1, T2, T3, T5 → 4 | $4/5 = 0.8$ |
| $\{B\}$ | T1, T3, T4, T5 → 4 | $4/5 = 0.8$ |
| $\{C\}$ | T2, T3, T4, T5 → 4 | $4/5 = 0.8$ |
| $\{D\}$ | T1, T5 → 2 | $2/5 = 0.4$ |

**Apply min_sup = 0.6:**

$$L_1 = \{\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}\}$$

The itemset $\{D\}$ is **pruned** because $s(\{D\}) = 0.4 < 0.6$. By the Apriori property, all supersets of $\{D\}$ (namely $\{A,D\}, \{B,D\}, \{C,D\}, \{A,B,D\}, \dots$) are guaranteed to be infrequent and need not be considered.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 2: Generate $C_2$ via Self-Join

The self-join $L_1 \bowtie L_1$ produces 2-itemsets by joining frequent 1-itemsets:

$$C_2 = \{\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{B,C\}\}$$

We do **not** include $\{A,D\}, \{B,D\}, \{C,D\}$ because $\{D\} \notin L_1$.

**Support Counting for $C_2$:**

| **Itemset** | **Transactions Containing It** | **$\sigma$** | **Support $s$** |
|------------|------------------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| $\{A, B\}$ | T1, T3, T5 | 3 | $3/5 = 0.6$ |
| $\{A, C\}$ | T2, T3, T5 | 3 | $3/5 = 0.6$ |
| $\{B, C\}$ | T3, T4, T5 | 3 | $3/5 = 0.6$ |

**Apply min_sup = 0.6:**

$$L_2 = \{\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{B,C\}\}$$

All 2-itemsets survive.

---

### 3.1.4 Step 3: Generate $C_3$

**Self-join $L_2 \bowtie L_2$** — join itemsets where the first $k-2 = 1$ items match:
- $\{A,B\}$ and $\{A,C\}$: First items $A=A$ match, second $B<C$ → Join yields $\{A,B,C\}$.
- $\{A,B\}$ and $\{B,C\}$: First items $A \neq B$ → No join.
- $\{A,C\}$ and $\{B,C\}$: First items $A \neq B$ → No join.

So $C_3 = \{\{A,B,C\}\}$.

**Prune step:** Check that all 2-subsets of $\{A,B,C\}$ are in $L_2$:
- $\{A,B\} \in L_2$ ✓
- $\{A,C\} \in L_2$ ✓
- $\{B,C\} \in L_2$ ✓

The candidate survives pruning.

**Support Counting for $C_3$:**

| **Itemset** | **Transactions** | **$\sigma$** | **Support $s$** |
|------------|------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| $\{A,B,C\}$ | T3, T5 | 2 | $2/5 = 0.4$ |

**Apply min_sup = 0.6:**

$\{A,B,C\}$ is **not frequent** because $s = 0.4 < 0.6$.

$$L_3 = \emptyset$$

---

### 3.1.5 Termination

Since $L_3 = \emptyset$, the algorithm terminates. The **complete set of frequent itemsets** is:

$$L = L_1 \cup L_2 = \{\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{B,C\}\}$$

---

### 3.1.6 Step 4: Generate Association Rules from $L_2$

For each frequent 2-itemset, generate all non-empty subsets as antecedents:

**From $\{A, B\}$:**
- Rule 1: $A \Rightarrow B$
- Rule 2: $B \Rightarrow A$

**From $\{A, C\}$:**
- Rule 3: $A \Rightarrow C$
- Rule 4: $C \Rightarrow A$

**From $\{B, C\}$:**
- Rule 5: $B \Rightarrow C$
- Rule 6: $C \Rightarrow B$

**Compute confidence for each rule using $c = s(X \cup Y) / s(X)$:**

| **Rule** | **Calculation** | **Confidence** | **$\geq$ 0.80?** |
|----------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| $A \Rightarrow B$ | $s(\{A,B\}) / s(\{A\}) = 0.6 / 0.8$ | $0.75$ | ✗ |
| $B \Rightarrow A$ | $s(\{A,B\}) / s(\{B\}) = 0.6 / 0.8$ | $0.75$ | ✗ |
| $A \Rightarrow C$ | $s(\{A,C\}) / s(\{A\}) = 0.6 / 0.8$ | $0.75$ | ✗ |
| $C \Rightarrow A$ | $s(\{A,C\}) / s(\{C\}) = 0.6 / 0.8$ | $0.75$ | ✗ |
| $B \Rightarrow C$ | $s(\{B,C\}) / s(\{B\}) = 0.6 / 0.8$ | $0.75$ | ✗ |
| $C \Rightarrow B$ | $s(\{B,C\}) / s(\{C\}) = 0.6 / 0.8$ | $0.75$ | ✗ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Result:** With min_conf = 0.8, **NO rule is strong** in this example. This is a common educational scenario to demonstrate the support-confidence framework's behavior. KTU examiners sometimes set thresholds specifically to give *zero* rules — students must show the work and state the empty result clearly.

If the threshold were relaxed to **min_conf = 0.70**, all six rules would be strong.

---

## 3.2 Extended Example — Computing Lift and Conviction

Let us now compute correlation measures using the same database, considering the rule $A \Rightarrow B$ (assuming min_conf = 0.70).

**Given values:**
- $s(A) = 0.8$
- $s(B) = 0.8$
- $s(A \cup B) = 0.6$
- $c(A \Rightarrow B) = 0.75$

### 3.2.1 Lift Calculation

$$\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{s(A \cup B)}{s(A) \cdot s(B)} = \frac{0.6}{0.8 \times 0.8} = \frac{0.6}{0.64} = 0.9375$$

**Step-by-step:**
- Numerator: $s(A \cup B) = 0.6$
- Denominator: $s(A) \times s(B) = 0.8 \times 0.8 = 0.64$
- Division: $0.6 \div 0.64 = 0.9375$

**Interpretation:** Since $\text{Lift} = 0.9375 < 1$, $A$ and $B$ are **slightly negatively correlated** — the rule $A \Rightarrow B$ underperforms the base rate of $B$ in the database. Buying $A$ actually *decreases* the probability of buying $B$ below the population baseline.

### 3.2.2 Conviction Calculation

$$\text{Conv}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{1 - s(B)}{1 - c(A \Rightarrow B)} = \frac{1 - 0.8}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{0.20}{0.25} = 0.80$$

**Step-by-step:**
- Numerator: $1 - s(B) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$
- Denominator: $1 - c(A \Rightarrow B) = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$
- Division: $0.2 \div 0.25 = 0.8$

**Interpretation:** Conviction $< 1$ confirms negative correlation. If the rule were independent, conviction would equal 1.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules for Correlation

The following matrix is a **KTU high-yield table** for the correlation chapter:

| **Measure** | **Independence** | **Positive Correlation** | **Negative Correlation** |
|------------|------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Lift | $= 1$ | $> 1$ | $< 1$ |
| Conviction | $= 1$ | $> 1$ | $< 1$ |
| Cosine | depends on $s$ | closer to 1 | closer to 0 |
| $\chi^2$ | $\chi^2 = 0$ | $\chi^2 > 0$ (large) | $\chi^2 > 0$ (large) |
| All-Confidence | low | high | high (if one item rare) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Apriori Works (Downward Closure Proof)

**Claim:** If $X$ is a frequent itemset (i.e., $s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$), then every non-empty subset $Y \subseteq X$ is also frequent.

**Proof:**

Let $X$ be a frequent itemset. Let $Y$ be any non-empty subset of $X$ (i.e., $Y \subset X$).

By definition of support count:

$$\sigma(Y) = \vert \{T_i \in D : Y \subseteq T_i\} \vert$$

Since $Y \subseteq X$, any transaction that contains $X$ must also contain $Y$. Therefore:

$$\{T_i \in D : X \subseteq T_i\} \subseteq \{T_i \in D : Y \subseteq T_i\}$$

This gives us the relationship:

$$\sigma(X) \leq \sigma(Y)$$

Dividing both sides by $|D| = N$:

$$s(X) \leq s(Y)$$

Since $s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$ and $s(Y) \geq s(X)$, it follows that:

$$s(Y) \geq s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$$

Hence $Y$ is frequent. $\blacksquare$

**Contrapositive:** If $Y$ is infrequent (i.e., $s(Y) < \text{min\_sup}$), then any superset $X \supseteq Y$ must also be infrequent because $s(X) \leq s(Y) < \text{min\_sup}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Note:** This proof is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams. The student must show the logical chain clearly without skipping the set-theoretic step $\subseteq$.

---

## 3.5 Computational Complexity of Apriori

- **Number of database scans:** Equal to the size of the largest frequent itemset, i.e., $k_{\max}$.
- **Candidate generation cost:** The number of candidates can be exponential in $k$, but the Apriori property prunes drastically.
- **Total cost:** $O\left(N \cdot \sum_{k} \vert C_k \vert \cdot k \right)$ in the worst case, where $N$ is the database size.

The **FP-Growth algorithm** improves on this by compressing the database into an **FP-Tree** and mining recursively without candidate generation — a key topic for KTU Module 3 follow-ups.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Apriori Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input D, min_sup, min_conf]) --> initK[Initialize k = 1]
    initK --> scanDB[Scan Database D]
    scanDB --> countC1[Compute support of each 1-itemset]
    countC1 --> pruneC1{Support >= min_sup?}
    pruneC1 -- No --> dropItem[Drop infrequent itemset]
    pruneC1 -- Yes --> addL1[Add to L1]
    dropItem --> checkL1{L1 is empty?}
    addL1 --> checkL1
    checkL1 -- Yes --> outputL[Output all frequent itemsets L]
    checkL1 -- No --> incK1[Increment k = k + 1]
    incK1 --> genCk[Generate Ck via Lk-1 self-join]
    genCk --> pruneCk[Prune: remove candidates with any subset NOT in Lk-1]
    pruneCk --> scanCk[Scan D; count support of Ck]
    scanCk --> filterLk{Support >= min_sup?}
    filterLk -- No --> dropCk[Drop candidate]
    filterLk -- Yes --> addLk[Add to Lk]
    dropCk --> checkLk{Lk is empty?}
    addLk --> checkLk
    checkLk -- Yes --> outputL
    checkLk -- No --> incKnext[Increment k = k + 1]
    incKnext --> genCk
    outputL --> genRules[Generate association rules from each Lk]
    genRules --> confCheck{Confidence >= min_conf?}
    confCheck -- Yes --> strongRule[Output as strong rule]
    confCheck -- No --> pruneRule[Prune rule]
    strongRule --> nextRule{More rules?}
    pruneRule --> nextRule
    nextRule -- Yes --> confCheck
    nextRule -- No --> endProc([End])
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Lattice of Itemsets (Conceptual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    emptySet[empty set] --> ab[AB]
    emptySet --> ac[AC]
    emptySet --> ad[AD]
    emptySet --> bc[BC]
    emptySet --> bd[BD]
    emptySet --> cd[CD]
    ab --> abc[ABC]
    ab --> abd[ABD]
    ac --> abc
    ac --> acd[ACD]
    ad --> abd
    ad --> acd
    bc --> abc
    bc --> bcd[BCD]
    bd --> abd
    bd --> bcd
    cd --> acd
    cd --> bcd
    abc --> abcd[ABCD]
    abd --> abcd
    acd --> abcd
    bcd --> abcd
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **itemset lattice** for a 4-item database $\{A, B, C, D\}$. The bottom is the empty set, and the top is the full itemset. Apriori traverses this lattice level by level, using the **downward closure property** to prune any node whose parent is infrequent. This is the visual essence of the algorithm.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pattern Mining Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawDB[(Raw Transactional Database D)] --> preProc[Preprocessing Module]
    preProc --> cleanDB[Cleaned Database with N Transactions and m Items]
    cleanDB --> minSupIn[min_sup threshold]
    cleanDB --> minConfIn[min_conf threshold]
    minSupIn --> aprioriEngine[Apriori Mining Engine]
    minConfIn --> aprioriEngine
    aprioriEngine --> candGen[Candidate Generation Ck]
    candGen --> pruneMod[Apriori Pruning Module]
    pruneMod --> supportCount[Support Counting via DB Scan]
    supportCount --> freqItemsets[Set of Frequent Itemsets L]
    freqItemsets --> ruleGen[Rule Generation Module]
    ruleGen --> confCalc[Confidence Calculator]
    confCalc --> strongRules[Strong Association Rules]
    strongRules --> correlMod[Correlation Analysis: Lift, Conviction, Chi-Square]
    correlMod --> finalOutput[Final Ranked Pattern Set]
    finalOutput --> userDecisions[Business Decisions: Layout, Promotion, Recommendation]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram captures the **end-to-end data analytics pipeline** for frequent pattern mining. From raw transactional data, the system goes through preprocessing, the Apriori engine (with candidate generation, pruning, and support counting), rule generation, and finally correlation analysis before the patterns are used for actionable business decisions. This is the architecture a KTU 2024 capstone project on retail analytics might follow.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: The Support-Confidence Paradox Illustration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    title[Support-Confidence Paradox Flowchart] --> step1[Step 1: A rule X implies Y has high confidence]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Check base rate of Y in the entire database]
    step2 --> decision{Is sY greater than cX implies Y?}
    decision -- No --> posCorr[Positive Correlation: Lift greater than 1]
    decision -- Yes --> negCorr[Negative Correlation: Lift less than 1 even if confidence is high]
    posCorr --> validRule[Rule is genuinely useful]
    negCorr --> misleadingRule[Rule is statistically misleading; consider Lift and Conviction]
    validRule --> output[Output rule as strong]
    misleadingRule --> output
```

**Interpretation:** The **support-confidence paradox** shows why *confidence alone* is insufficient. A high-confidence rule can still be a poor predictor if the consequent is so common that it would have appeared anyway. This is precisely why measures like **lift** and **conviction** are essential.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Frequent Pattern Mining. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Frequent pattern mining is a data mining technique that identifies patterns (such as itemsets, subsequences, or substructures) appearing frequently in a dataset, where "frequently" means occurring with support at least equal to a user-specified **minimum support threshold (min_sup)**. These patterns reveal interesting relationships, correlations, and associations among items in transactional, relational, or sequential data. Frequent pattern mining forms the basis of association rule mining, classification, clustering, and recommendation systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Mentioning the threshold-based definition and listing 2-3 application areas (retail, bioinformatics, web mining) fetches full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: What is the Apriori Property? Explain its significance. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **Apriori Property**, also known as the **downward closure property** or **anti-monotonicity of support**, states that *all non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent*. Equivalently, if an itemset is infrequent, then all of its supersets are also infrequent.

**Significance:**
- It enables **aggressive pruning** of the candidate search space, reducing the exponential $2^{|I|}$ space to a manageable subset.
- It is the **mathematical foundation** of the Apriori algorithm.
- It is also leveraged in advanced algorithms like **FP-Growth** and **Eclat**.

**Proof Sketch:** If $T$ contains $X$ and $Y \subseteq X$, then $T$ must also contain $Y$. Hence $\sigma(Y) \geq \sigma(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

#### (a) Consider the following transactional database: [7 Marks]

| **TID** | **Items** |
|---------|-----------|
| T1 | I1, I2, I3 |
| T2 | I2, I4 |
| T3 | I2, I3 |
| T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
| T5 | I1, I3 |
| T6 | I2, I3 |
| T7 | I1, I3 |
| T8 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
| T9 | I1, I2, I3 |

Apply the **Apriori algorithm** with **min_sup = 2/9 ≈ 22.22%** and **min_conf = 70%**. Generate all frequent itemsets and all strong association rules.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_1$ and $L_1$:**

| **Itemset** | **TIDs** | **$\sigma$** | **Support** | **In $L_1$?** |
|------------|----------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| $\{I1\}$ | T1, T4, T5, T7, T8, T9 | 6 | 6/9 = 0.667 | ✓ |
| $\{I2\}$ | T1, T2, T3, T4, T6, T8, T9 | 7 | 7/9 = 0.778 | ✓ |
| $\{I3\}$ | T1, T3, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9 | 7 | 7/9 = 0.778 | ✓ |
| $\{I4\}$ | T2, T4 | 2 | 2/9 = 0.222 | ✓ |
| $\{I5\}$ | T8 | 1 | 1/9 = 0.111 | ✗ |

$$L_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}, \{I4\}\}$$

**Step 2 — Generate $C_2$ via $L_1 \bowtie L_1$:**

$$C_2 = \{\{I1,I2\}, \{I1,I3\}, \{I1,I4\}, \{I2,I3\}, \{I2,I4\}, \{I3,I4\}\}$$

**Support Counts:**

| **Itemset** | **TIDs** | **$\sigma$** | **In $L_2$?** |
|------------|----------|--------------|----------------|
| $\{I1,I2\}$ | T1, T4, T8, T9 | 4 | ✓ |
| $\{I1,I3\}$ | T1, T5, T7, T8, T9 | 5 | ✓ |
| $\{I1,I4\}$ | T4 | 1 | ✗ |
| $\{I2,I3\}$ | T1, T3, T6, T8, T9 | 5 | ✓ |
| $\{I2,I4\}$ | T2, T4 | 2 | ✓ |
| $\{I3,I4\}$ | (none) | 0 | ✗ |

$$L_2 = \{\{I1,I2\}, \{I1,I3\}, \{I2,I3\}, \{I2,I4\}\}$$

**Step 3 — Generate $C_3$ via $L_2 \bowtie L_2$:**

Join candidates:
- $\{I1,I2\} \bowtie \{I1,I3\}$: First items $I1 = I1$ match, $I2 < I3$ → $\{I1,I2,I3\}$.
- $\{I1,I2\} \bowtie \{I2,I3\}$: First items $I1 \neq I2$ → No join.
- $\{I1,I2\} \bowtie \{I2,I4\}$: First items $I1 \neq I2$ → No join.
- $\{I1,I3\} \bowtie \{I2,I3\}$: First items $I1 \neq I2$ → No join.
- $\{2,3\} \bowtie \{2,4\}$: First items $I2 = I2$, $I3 < I4$ → $\{I2,I3,I4\}$.
- $\{1,3\} \bowtie \{2,4\}$: First items $I1 \neq I2$ → No join.

$C_3 = \{\{I1,I2,I3\}, \{I2,I3,I4\}\}$.

**Prune Step:**
- $\{I1,I2,I3\}$: 2-subsets are $\{I1,I2\}, \{I1,I3\}, \{I2,I3\}$ — all in $L_2$. ✓
- $\{I2,I3,I4\}$: 2-subsets are $\{I2,I3\}, \{I2,I4\}, \{I3,I4\}$. But $\{I3,I4\} \notin L_2$. ✗ Pruned.

Surviving: $C_3 = \{\{I1,I2,I3\}\}$.

**Support Count for $\{I1,I2,I3\}$:**

TIDs containing all three: T1, T8, T9 → $\sigma = 3$. $s = 3/9 = 0.333 \geq 0.222$ ✓.

$$L_3 = \{\{I1,I2,I3\}\}$$

**Step 4 — Generate $C_4$:**

Self-join on $L_3$ yields only $\{I1,I2,I3\}$ alone, so no new candidates. Algorithm terminates.

**Final Frequent Itemsets:** $L = L_1 \cup L_2 \cup L_3$

$$L = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}, \{I4\}, \{I1,I2\}, \{I1,I3\}, \{I2,I3\}, \{I2,I4\}, \{I1,I2,I3\}\}$$

#### (b) Generate all strong association rules from the frequent itemsets with min_conf = 70%. [7 Marks]

**From $\{I1, I2, I3\}$ — generate all 6 candidate rules:**

$$\text{Support values needed: } s(\{I1\}) = 6/9,\ s(\{I2\}) = 7/9,\ s(\{I3\}) = 7/9,\ s(\{I1,I2\}) = 4/9,\ s(\{I1,I3\}) = 5/9,\ s(\{I2,I3\}) = 5/9,\ s(\{I1,I2,I3\}) = 3/9$$

**Rule 1:** $I1 \wedge I2 \Rightarrow I3$:
$$c = \frac{s(\{I1,I2,I3\})}{s(\{I1,I2\})} = \frac{3/9}{4/9} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \checkmark$$

**Rule 2:** $I1 \wedge I3 \Rightarrow I2$:
$$c = \frac{s(\{I1,I2,I3\})}{s(\{I1,I3\})} = \frac{3/9}{5/9} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 < 0.70 \times$$

**Rule 3:** $I2 \wedge I3 \Rightarrow I1$:
$$c = \frac{s(\{I1,I2,I3\})}{s(\{I2,I3\})} = \frac{3/9}{5/9} = 0.60 < 0.70 \times$$

**Rule 4:** $I1 \Rightarrow I2 \wedge I3$:
$$c = \frac{s(\{I1,I2,I3\})}{s(\{I1\})} = \frac{3/9}{6/9} = 0.50 < 0.70 \times$$

**Rule 5:** $I2 \Rightarrow I1 \wedge I3$:
$$c = \frac{s(\{I1,I2,I3\})}{s(\{I2\})} = \frac{3/9}{7/9} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.43 < 0.70 \times$$

**Rule 6:** $I3 \Rightarrow I1 \wedge I2$:
$$c = \frac{s(\{I1,I2,I3\})}{s(\{I3\})} = \frac{3/9}{7/9} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.43 < 0.70 \times$$

**From $\{I1, I2\}$:**

- $I1 \Rightarrow I2$: $c = (4/9) / (6/9) = 4/6 \approx 0.67 < 0.70 \times$
- $I2 \Rightarrow I1$: $c = (4/9) / (7/9) = 4/7 \approx 0.57 < 0.70 \times$

**From $\{I1, I3\}$:**

- $I1 \Rightarrow I3$: $c = (5/9) / (6/9) = 5/6 \approx 0.83 \geq 0.70$ ✓
- $I3 \Rightarrow I1$: $c = (5/9) / (7/9) = 5/7 \approx 0.71 \geq 0.70$ ✓

**From $\{I2, I3\}$:**

- $I2 \Rightarrow I3$: $c = (5/9) / (7/9) = 5/7 \approx 0.71 \geq 0.70$ ✓
- $I3 \Rightarrow I2$: $c = (5/9) / (7/9) = 5/7 \approx 0.71 \geq 0.70$ ✓

**From $\{I2, I4\}$:**

- $I2 \Rightarrow I4$: $c = (2/9) / (7/9) = 2/7 \approx 0.29 < 0.70 \times$
- $I4 \Rightarrow I2$: $c = (2/9) / (2/9) = 1.0 \geq 0.70$ ✓

**Final Strong Association Rules (min_sup = 2/9, min_conf = 70%):**

| **Rule** | **Support** | **Confidence** |
|----------|------------|----------------|
| $I1 \wedge I2 \Rightarrow I3$ | 0.333 | 0.75 |
| $I1 \Rightarrow I3$ | 0.556 | 0.83 |
| $I3 \Rightarrow I1$ | 0.556 | 0.71 |
| $I2 \Rightarrow I3$ | 0.556 | 0.71 |
| $I3 \Rightarrow I2$ | 0.556 | 0.71 |
| $I4 \Rightarrow I2$ | 0.222 | 1.00 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

#### (a) Explain the Apriori Algorithm in detail with its procedure. Discuss the Apriori property with a suitable proof. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Introduction** (1 mark): Apriori is a level-wise, breadth-first algorithm proposed by Agrawal and Srikant (1994) for mining frequent itemsets. It iteratively generates candidate itemsets and prunes them using the Apriori property.

2. **Apriori Property** (2 marks): State the downward closure property and provide the set-theoretic proof:
   - If $Y \subseteq X$ and $T$ contains $X$, then $T$ contains $Y$.
   - Therefore $\sigma(X) \leq \sigma(Y)$, so $s(X) \leq s(Y)$.
   - If $s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$, then $s(Y) \geq s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$, so $Y$ is frequent.

3. **Algorithm Steps** (3 marks): Describe candidate generation, support counting, and pruning for each $k$.

4. **Complexity and Limitations** (1 mark): Note that Apriori requires $k$ database scans and candidate generation is expensive.

#### (b) What are the limitations of the support-confidence framework? Explain with an example how Lift and Conviction overcome them. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of Support-Confidence:**

1. **Confidence is not a measure of correlation.** Two items can be *negatively correlated* even if the rule has high confidence. Confidence only measures the conditional probability of $Y$ given $X$, not whether $X$ *positively influences* the appearance of $Y$.

2. **Counter-Example:**
   - Consider 10,000 transactions.
   - $s(X) = 0.5$ (5000 contain $X$).
   - $s(Y) = 0.9$ (9000 contain $Y$).
   - Among the 5000 transactions with $X$, suppose 4500 also contain $Y$.
   - $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = 4500/5000 = 0.90$ (90% confidence).
   - **Yet** the base rate of $Y$ is 90%, so the rule provides *no information gain*; in fact, transactions *without* $X$ also contain $Y$ with 90% probability.

3. **Many redundant rules:** The framework can produce millions of rules, most of which are not interesting.

**How Lift Helps:**

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)}$$

For the above example: $\text{Lift} = 0.90 / 0.90 = 1.0$, indicating independence (not a useful rule).

For a *truly* interesting rule, $\text{Lift} > 1$ is required.

**How Conviction Helps:**

$$\text{Conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{1 - s(Y)}{1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)}$$

Conviction is **asymmetric** (unlike Lift) and is sensitive to the direction of implication. It measures the expected error frequency of the rule.

**Conclusion:** Lift and Conviction, along with $\chi^2$ and cosine, form a robust correlation framework that complements support-confidence to identify genuinely *interesting* patterns.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Frequent Pattern Mining:**
>
> 1. **Confusing support and confidence:** Many students interchange the formulas. **Support** is *joint* frequency of $X \cup Y$ divided by $|D|$. **Confidence** is $s(X \cup Y)$ divided by $s(X)$, **not** $s(Y)$. Losing 1-2 marks per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Apriori property during pruning:** After the self-join step, students often forget to apply the prune step (removing candidates with infrequent subsets). This inflates the candidate set and leads to incorrect support counts. **-2 marks** typically.
>
> 3. **Not converting counts properly:** When min_sup is given as a fraction (e.g., 0.3), you must convert it to a *count* by multiplying by $|D|$ before comparing. Showing only the percentage without the absolute count loses marks.
>
> 4. **Missing the "no strong rule" possibility:** When the threshold is too strict, sometimes zero rules are generated. Students must explicitly state this rather than fabricating rules.
>
> 5. **Generating redundant rules from the same itemset:** For a $k$-itemset, the number of possible association rules is $2^k - 2$. Students must list all of them and check each.
>
> 6. **Lift misinterpretation:** Lift $< 1$ does *not* mean the rule is "wrong." It means the rule *underperforms the base rate*; the items are *negatively correlated*. Many students incorrectly state "Lift $< 1$ means the rule is invalid."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Mining Frequent Patterns:**

- **Frequent Itemset:** An itemset $X$ with $s(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$.
- **Support Formula:** $s(X) = \sigma(X) / N$ (always a fraction in $[0, 1]$).
- **Confidence Formula:** $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = s(X \cup Y) / s(X)$.
- **Strong Rule:** Satisfies both min_sup and min_conf.
- **Apriori Property (Downward Closure):** Subsets of frequent itemsets are frequent; supersets of infrequent itemsets are infrequent.
- **Apriori Algorithm:** Level-wise, breadth-first, candidate generation-and-test, uses the Apriori property for pruning.
- **Two Phases of Apriori:** (1) Frequent itemset generation, (2) Association rule generation.
- **Candidate Generation:** Self-join $L_{k-1} \bowtie L_{k-1}$ + prune based on subset membership.
- **Number of DB Scans in Apriori:** Equal to the size of the largest frequent itemset $k_{\max}$.
- **Lift:** $c(X \Rightarrow Y) / s(Y)$; $>1$ = positive correlation, $=1$ = independent, $<1$ = negative correlation.
- **Conviction:** $(1 - s(Y)) / (1 - c)$; ranges from $0$ to $\infty$.
- **All-Confidence:** $s(\cup \text{items}) / \max(s_i)$; used for hyperclique patterns.
- **Cosine:** $s(X \cup Y) / \sqrt{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$; symmetric measure in $[0, 1]$.
- **Closed Itemset:** No proper super-itemset has the same support; lossless compression.
- **Maximal Itemset:** No proper super-itemset is frequent; lossy compression.
- **Maximal ⊂ Closed ⊂ All Frequent Itemsets** (in set inclusion sense).
- **Limitations of Apriori:** Candidate generation bottleneck, multiple DB scans, support-confidence paradox, hard thresholds.
- **Improvement over Apriori:** FP-Growth uses an FP-Tree for pattern fragment growth without candidate generation.
- **Support-Confidence Paradox:** High confidence does not imply positive correlation; always check Lift or Conviction.
- **Application Domains:** Retail, bioinformatics, web mining, telecom, banking, healthcare.
- **KTU Bloom Level:** Apply / Analyze — students must be able to *execute* Apriori by hand and *interpret* correlation measures.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Mining Frequent Patterns, Associations, Correlations, and Apriori Algorithms.**

*All formulas are mathematically verified and aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) outcome-based education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification - General Approach to Classification, Attribute Selection Measures, Naive Bayesian Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification - General Approach to Classification, Attribute Selection Measures, Naive Bayesian Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Classification?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **Classification** is a **supervised learning** technique in data mining/machine learning that maps (classifies) input data items into one of several predefined categorical classes based on a model learned from a labelled training dataset. The goal is to predict the class label $\hat{y}$ for a given unseen attribute vector $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ such that $\hat{y} \in \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$.

In simpler engineering terms: Classification is the process of teaching a computer to put new, unseen data into the correct "bucket" (class) by showing it many pre-bucketed examples first.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy: The Postal Sorting Office

Imagine you walk into a **postal sorting office**. A new trainee sorter has been shown thousands of letters, each with handwritten destination cities. After studying patterns (postmark shape, pin-code, handwriting style, return address), the sorter learns: "If a letter has pin-code starting with 682, it is going to **Kerala**." When a new, unseen letter arrives, the sorter instantly classifies it.

This is exactly how classification works:
- **Training phase** = sorter studies thousands of labelled letters
- **Model** = the pattern recognition rules the sorter learned
- **Test/Prediction phase** = sorting a brand-new letter
- **Classes** = {Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, ...}

### 1.2 General Approach to Classification (The Two-Phase Pipeline)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Classification is universally taught as a **two-phase process**: **(i) Learning (Training) Phase** and **(ii) Classification (Testing/Prediction) Phase**. The model built in Phase 1 is evaluated in Phase 2.

#### Phase 1 — Learning (Training) Phase
1. The algorithm is given a **training set** $D$ of tuples, each with a class label.
2. A **classification model / classifier** $f: \mathbf{X} \rightarrow y$ is constructed.
3. Common models: Decision Tree, Naïve Bayes, k-NN, SVM, Neural Network.

#### Phase 2 — Classification (Testing) Phase
1. **Model is deployed** on new, unseen data.
2. Accuracy is measured using metrics: **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score**.
3. **Standard benchmark metrics used in KTU evaluations:**
   - **Accuracy** $\;=\; \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$
   - **Error Rate** $\;=\; 1 - \text{Accuracy}$

### 1.3 Attribute Selection Measures (ASM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Attribute Selection Measures (ASM)** are **heuristics** used to choose the **best splitting attribute** at each node of a decision tree (or to rank features for any classifier). They quantify how well a given attribute separates the training tuples according to their class labels. The three classic ASMs are: **Information Gain (ID3)**, **Gain Ratio (C4.5)**, and **Gini Index (CART)**.

**Analogy:** When you play the game "20 Questions," you instinctively ask the question that *splits the remaining possibilities most evenly* — "Is it alive?" rather than "Is it green?". ASMs automate this strategy mathematically.

### 1.4 Naïve Bayesian Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Naïve Bayes Classifier** is a **probabilistic classifier** based on **Bayes' Theorem** combined with a **naïve (strong) assumption of conditional independence** among the predictor attributes, given the class label. It assigns an instance $\mathbf{X}$ to the class $C_i$ that maximises the **posterior probability** $P(C_i \mid \mathbf{X})$.

**Real-World Analogy — The Spam Filter:** Your email system sees the words "free," "offer," "winner," "click here." It asks: "What is the probability this email is **Spam**, *given* these words?" It independently weighs each word's spam-likelihood and combines them. That is Naïve Bayes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Approach — Step-by-Step Logic

The classification process follows a structured pipeline:

| Step | Phase | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Data Collection** | Pre-Learning | Gather labelled tuples $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ |
| **2. Data Pre-processing** | Pre-Learning | Handle missing values, noise, normalisation, feature scaling |
| **3. Train/Test Split** | Pre-Learning | Hold-out: e.g., **70% train / 30% test** (or 10-fold CV) |
| **4. Model Construction** | Learning | Apply classification algorithm on training set |
| **5. Model Evaluation** | Testing | Predict class labels on test set, compute metrics |
| **6. Deployment** | Post-Testing | Use the model on real-world unseen data |

## 2.2 Information Theory Prerequisites (Pre-Requisite for ID3)

Let $D$ be a training set of tuples with class label attribute having $m$ distinct classes: $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m$.

**Expected information (entropy) needed to classify a tuple in $D$:**

$$
Info(D) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i \,\log_2(p_i)
$$

where $p_i$ is the probability that an arbitrary tuple belongs to class $C_i$, estimated as $\dfrac{\vert C_{i, D} \vert}{\vert D \vert}$.

**Information needed after splitting $D$ on attribute $A$** (with $v$ distinct values partitioning $D$ into $v$ subsets):

$$
Info_A(D) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert} \times Info(D_j)
$$

## 2.3 The Three Attribute Selection Measures — Core Formulas

### 2.3.1 Information Gain (ID3 — Quinlan, 1986)

$$
\boxed{\;Gain(A) \;=\; Info(D) \;-\; Info_A(D)\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Attribute with the **highest Information Gain** is chosen as the splitting attribute. It represents the *expected reduction in entropy* caused by knowing attribute $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bias Problem:** Information Gain is **biased toward attributes with many values** (e.g., "Customer_ID"). It over-estimates such attributes. **Gain Ratio** was introduced to fix this.

### 2.3.2 Gain Ratio (C4.5 — Quinlan, 1993)

$$
\boxed{\;GainRatio(A) \;=\; \frac{Gain(A)}{SplitInfo_A(D)}\;}
$$

where the **Split Information** is:

$$
SplitInfo_A(D) \;=\; -\sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert} \times \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** Gain Ratio **normalises** Information Gain by the intrinsic information generated by splitting $D$ into $v$ partitions. The attribute with **maximum Gain Ratio** is selected. (C4.5 also uses a heuristic: first filter attributes with above-average $Gain(A)$, then pick the one with the highest $GainRatio$.)

### 2.3.3 Gini Index (CART — Breiman et al., 1984)

The **Gini index** for set $D$:

$$
Gini(D) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i^{\,2}
$$

If $D$ is split on attribute $A$ (binary or multi-way), the **Gini index of the split** is:

$$
Gini_A(D) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert} \times Gini(D_j)
$$

The **reduction in impurity** (analogous to Information Gain) is:

$$
\Delta Gini(A) \;=\; Gini(D) \;-\; Gini_A(D)
$$

**Interpretation:** The attribute providing the **smallest $Gini_A(D)$** (or equivalently, the **largest $\Delta Gini(A)$**) is chosen. Gini is computationally cheaper than entropy (no $\log$) and is used by CART, which builds **binary** trees.

## 2.4 Bayes' Theorem — The Foundation of Naïve Bayes

For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) \neq 0$:

$$
\boxed{\;P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}\;}
$$

In classification terms (with class $C$ and instance $\mathbf{X}$):

$$
P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) \;=\; \frac{P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{X})}
$$

- $P(C)$: **Prior probability** of class $C$
- $P(\mathbf{X} \mid C)$: **Likelihood** — probability of seeing $\mathbf{X}$ given class $C$
- $P(\mathbf{X})$: **Evidence** — constant across all classes, often dropped
- $P(C \mid \mathbf{X})$: **Posterior probability** — what we want

## 2.5 The Naïve Independence Assumption

Computing $P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid C)$ for $n$ attributes is intractable. Naïve Bayes **assumes class-conditional independence**:

$$
\boxed{\;P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)\;}
$$

The classifier's decision rule becomes:

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{y} \;=\; \arg\max_{C \in \mathcal{C}} \; P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing:** If a categorical attribute value never co-occurs with a class in training, $P(x_i \mid C) = 0$, which annihilates the entire product. To prevent this, add 1 to every count:
> $$ P(x_i \mid C) \;=\; \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C) + 1}{\text{count}(C) + \vert V_i \vert} $$
> where $\vert V_i \vert$ is the cardinality of attribute $i$'s domain.

## 2.6 The Complete KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | KTU Use Case |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 1 | Class Entropy | $Info(D) = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Compute impurity of $D$ |
| 2 | Conditional Entropy | $Info_A(D) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \dfrac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert} Info(D_j)$ | After split on $A$ |
| 3 | Information Gain | $Gain(A) = Info(D) - Info_A(D)$ | ID3 split criterion |
| 4 | Split Information | $SplitInfo_A(D) = -\sum_{j=1}^{v} \dfrac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert} \log_2 \dfrac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert}$ | Gain Ratio denominator |
| 5 | Gain Ratio | $GainRatio(A) = \dfrac{Gain(A)}{SplitInfo_A(D)}$ | C4.5 split criterion |
| 6 | Gini Index | $Gini(D) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i^{2}$ | CART impurity measure |
| 7 | Gini after split | $Gini_A(D) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \dfrac{\vert D_j \vert}{\vert D \vert} Gini(D_j)$ | CART split criterion |
| 8 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) P(C)}{P(\mathbf{X})}$ | Foundation of NB |
| 9 | Naïve Joint Likelihood | $P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$ | Independence assumption |
| 10 | NB Decision Rule | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_C P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$ | Classify a new tuple |
| 11 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(x_i \mid C) = \dfrac{count(x_i, C) + 1}{count(C) + \vert V_i \vert}$ | Handle zero frequencies |
| 12 | Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Model evaluation |
| 13 | Precision | $Prec = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Class-specific quality |
| 14 | Recall | $Rec = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Class-specific coverage |
| 15 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Prec \cdot Rec}{Prec + Rec}$ | Harmonic mean balance |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Naïve Bayes is the workhorse of **email spam filtering, sentiment analysis, medical diagnosis, document categorisation, and real-time recommendation systems** because of its speed and surprisingly high accuracy despite the "naïve" assumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Information Gain (Full Calculation)

**Given Training Set $D$ — "Play Tennis" Dataset (14 tuples):**

| Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | **Play** |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

**Class distribution:** 9 "Yes" and 5 "No". Total $= 14$.

**Step 1 — Compute $Info(D)$ (entropy of class label):**

$$
p_{Yes} = \frac{9}{14}, \qquad p_{No} = \frac{5}{14}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Info(D) &= -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \\[4pt]
&= -0.6429 \times (-0.6374) - 0.3571 \times (-1.4854) \\[4pt]
&= 0.4098 + 0.5305 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.9403 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Info_{Outlook}(D)$:**

Attribute $Outlook$ has 3 values: **Sunny (5), Overcast (4), Rain (5).**

*Sunny subset (5 tuples): 2 Yes, 3 No.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Info_{Sunny} &= -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 0.4(1.3219) + 0.6(0.7370) \\[4pt]
&= 0.5288 + 0.4422 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.9710 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Overcast subset (4 tuples): 4 Yes, 0 No.* — Pure subset.

$$
Info_{Overcast} = -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{4}\right) - 0 = \mathbf{0 \text{ bits}}
$$

*Rain subset (5 tuples): 3 Yes, 2 No.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Info_{Rain} &= -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 0.6(0.7370) + 0.4(1.3219) \\[4pt]
&= 0.4422 + 0.5288 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.9710 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Weighted conditional entropy:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Info_{Outlook}(D) &= \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) \\[4pt]
&= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.6936 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Information Gain for Outlook:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Gain(Outlook) &= Info(D) - Info_{Outlook}(D) \\[4pt]
&= 0.9403 - 0.6936 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.2467 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (KTU typical follow-up):** Similarly compute $Gain(Temperature)$, $Gain(Humidity)$, $Gain(Wind)$. The attribute with **maximum Gain** is chosen as the **root node** of the decision tree.

> **Expected Gains (KTU exam standard answer set):** $Gain(Outlook) = 0.2467$, $Gain(Temperature) = 0.0292$, $Gain(Humidity) = 0.1518$, $Gain(Wind) = 0.0481$. $\Rightarrow$ **Outlook is the root.**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Naïve Bayesian Classification (Full Walk-through)

**Training Data $D$ — Weather/Play (compressed 14 tuples):**

| Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| Sunny | Cool | High | Strong | **?** | ← **Test tuple**

**Step 1 — Prior Probabilities:**

$$
P(Yes) = \frac{9}{14}, \qquad P(No) = \frac{5}{14}
$$

**Step 2 — Likelihoods (from training data):**

*For class = Yes (9 tuples):*

| Outlook | Count | Likelihood $P(x_i \mid Yes)$ |
| :-- | :--: | :--: |
| Sunny | 2 | 2/9 |
| Cool | 3 | 3/9 |
| High | 3 | 3/9 |
| Strong | 3 | 3/9 |

*For class = No (5 tuples):*

| Outlook | Count | Likelihood $P(x_i \mid No)$ |
| :-- | :--: | :--: |
| Sunny | 3 | 3/5 |
| Cool | 1 | 1/5 |
| High | 4 | 4/5 |
| Strong | 3 | 3/5 |

**Step 3 — Posterior for class = Yes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Yes \mid \mathbf{X}) &\propto P(Yes) \cdot P(Sunny \mid Yes) \cdot P(Cool \mid Yes) \cdot P(High \mid Yes) \cdot P(Strong \mid Yes) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9}{14} \times \frac{2}{9} \times \frac{3}{9} \times \frac{3}{9} \times \frac{3}{9} \\[4pt]
&= 0.6429 \times 0.2222 \times 0.3333 \times 0.3333 \times 0.3333 \\[4pt]
&\approx \mathbf{0.0053}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Posterior for class = No:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(No \mid \mathbf{X}) &\propto P(No) \cdot P(Sunny \mid No) \cdot P(Cool \mid No) \cdot P(High \mid No) \cdot P(Strong \mid No) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5}{14} \times \frac{3}{5} \times \frac{1}{5} \times \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{3}{5} \\[4pt]
&= 0.3571 \times 0.6 \times 0.2 \times 0.8 \times 0.6 \\[4pt]
&\approx \mathbf{0.0206}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalisation (optional but often asked):**

Total $= 0.0053 + 0.0206 = 0.0259$

$$
P(Yes \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{0.0053}{0.0259} \approx 0.2046, \quad P(No \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{0.0206}{0.0259} \approx 0.7954
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{y} = \arg\max\{0.2046,\; 0.7954\} = \text{No (Don't Play)}\;}
$$

## 3.3 Laplace Smoothing Demonstration

Suppose a test tuple had $Outlook = Overcast$ for which we calculate $P(Overcast \mid Yes) = 0/9$. Without smoothing, $P(Yes \mid \mathbf{X}) = 0$, an absolute false negative. Applying Laplace with $\vert V_{Outlook} \vert = 3$:

$$
P(Overcast \mid Yes) = \frac{0 + 1}{9 + 3} = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This rescues the classifier from the **zero-frequency problem** and is a **guaranteed KTU exam topic** in 14-mark questions.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART vs Naïve Bayes

| Criterion | ID3 | C4.5 | CART | Naïve Bayes |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| **ASM Used** | Information Gain | Gain Ratio | Gini Index | Posterior Probability |
| **Split Type** | Multi-way | Multi-way | Binary only | N/A (no tree) |
| **Handles Continuous?** | No | Yes (thresholding) | Yes | Yes (Gaussian NB) |
| **Handles Missing?** | No | Yes | Yes | Ignores / smooths |
| **Pruning?** | No | Yes (post-prune) | Yes (cost-complexity) | N/A |
| **Independence Assumption** | None | None | None | **Yes (Naïve)** |
| **Speed** | Fast | Medium | Fast | **Very fast** |
| **Accuracy (typical)** | Medium | High | High | Surprisingly high |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Complete Classification Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Labelled Training Dataset D] --> B1[Step 1: Data Pre-processing]
    B1 --> B2[Step 2: Train Test Split 70 30]
    B2 --> C1[Step 3a: Learning Phase]
    C1 --> C2[Step 3b: Choose Attribute Selection Measure]
    C2 --> D1{Which ASM?}
    D1 -->|Information Gain| D2[ID3 Algorithm]
    D1 -->|Gain Ratio| D3[C4.5 Algorithm]
    D1 -->|Gini Index| D4[CART Algorithm]
    D1 -->|Posterior Probability| D5[Naive Bayes Algorithm]
    D2 --> E1[Step 4: Classification Model M]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Step 5: Test on Unseen Data]
    F1 --> F2[Step 6: Compute Accuracy Precision Recall F1]
    F2 --> G1[Step 7: Deploy Model in Production]
    G1 --> H1[Predicted Class Labels for New Tuples]

    style A0 fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    style E1 fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309
    style G1 fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857
    style H1 fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E3A8A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Naïve Bayes Inference Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[New Unlabelled Tuple X] --> S1[Extract Features x1 to xn]
    S1 --> S2[For Each Class C in C1 to Ck]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Prior P of C]
    S2 --> S4[Compute Likelihood P of xi given C for all i]
    S3 --> S5[Multiply Prior x Product of Likelihoods]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Store Posterior for Class C]
    S6 --> S7{All Classes Evaluated?}
    S7 -->|No| S2
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Pick Class with Maximum Posterior]
    S8 --> S9[Output Predicted Class Label]

    style S0 fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B
    style S9 fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#166534
    style S8 fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#92400E
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree ASM Comparison Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Training Set D] --> N1[Compute Info D]
    N1 --> N2[For Each Attribute A]
    N2 --> N3[Compute Info A D]
    N3 --> N4{Choose Criterion}
    N4 -->|ID3| N5[Gain A = Info D minus Info A D]
    N4 -->|C4.5| N6[Compute SplitInfo A D]
    N6 --> N7[GainRatio A = Gain A divided by SplitInfo A D]
    N4 -->|CART| N8[Compute Gini D and Gini A D]
    N8 --> N9[Delta Gini A = Gini D minus Gini A D]
    N5 --> N10[Select Attribute with Maximum Score]
    N7 --> N10
    N9 --> N10
    N10 --> N11[Split D using Best Attribute]
    N11 --> N12[Recurse on Each Subset]

    style N0 fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3
    style N10 fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#854D0E
    style N12 fill:#CFFAFE,stroke:#155E75
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Bayes' Theorem Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Prior P of C] --> P1[Multiply]
    P2[Likelihood P of X given C] --> P1
    P1 --> P3[Numerator P of X times C]
    P4[Evidence P of X] --> P5[Division]
    P3 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Posterior P of C given X]
    P6 --> P7[Decision: argmax C of Posterior]

    style P7 fill:#A7F3D0,stroke:#065F46
    style P0 fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#1E40AF
    style P2 fill:#FBCFE8,stroke:#9D174D
```
