# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define classification in data mining. List and briefly explain the two main phases of the classification process.**

**Model Answer:**
Classification is a data mining technique used to predict the categorical class label of new instances based on a model trained on labelled data. It is a form of **supervised learning**.

**Two main phases:**
1. **Learning (Training) Phase:** A classification model is constructed by analysing a training dataset consisting of tuples with known class labels. The algorithm learns the mapping $f: \mathbf{X} \rightarrow y$.
2. **Classification (Testing) Phase:** The trained model is used to predict the class label of unseen test data, and its performance is evaluated using metrics like **accuracy, precision, recall**, and **F1-score**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the "naïve" assumption in the Naïve Bayesian classifier? Why is it called naïve?**

**Model Answer:**
The **naïve assumption** in the Naïve Bayes classifier is the **class-conditional independence** of attributes — i.e., given the class label $C$, the values of the predictor attributes $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ are assumed to be **mutually independent**.

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)
$$

It is called **naïve** because in real-world data, attributes are almost never truly independent (e.g., in medical diagnosis, "fever" and "cough" are correlated). Despite this unrealistic assumption, the classifier often produces surprisingly accurate results, making it both simple and powerful.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Laplace smoothing** when a zero-frequency value appears — costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up Gini and Information Gain** — they use different formulas; do not interchange.
> 3. **Skipping the "argmax" step** in Naïve Bayes — examiners want the explicit *class assignment*, not just probabilities.
> 4. **Forgetting to normalise** posterior probabilities when the question asks for the "probability" (not just the comparison).
> 5. **Computational errors in $\log_2$** — practice common values: $\log_2(0.5)=-1$, $\log_2(0.25)=-2$, $\log_2(1)=0$.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **General Approach to Classification** with a neat block diagram. Describe in detail the **two phases** of classification, listing the steps involved in each.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Classification is a supervised learning technique that assigns objects to one of several predefined categories based on their attribute values.

**Two Phases:**

**Phase 1 — Learning (Training) Phase:**

1. **Input:** Training dataset $D = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with known class labels.
2. **Algorithm Selection:** Choose classifier (e.g., Decision Tree, Naïve Bayes, k-NN, SVM).
3. **Model Construction:** The learning algorithm analyses the training data and builds the classification model $M$.
4. **Validation:** Apply internal cross-validation to tune hyper-parameters.

**Phase 2 — Classification (Testing/Prediction) Phase:**

1. **Input:** Unlabelled test tuples $\mathbf{X}_{new}$.
2. **Prediction:** Use model $M$ to predict class label $\hat{y} = M(\mathbf{X}_{new})$.
3. **Evaluation Metrics:** Compute **accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score** using confusion matrix elements.
4. **Deployment:** If metrics meet threshold, deploy in production.

*(Students should also include the standard 6-step pipeline block diagram — see SECTION 4.1 for the reference schematic.)*

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Given the following training data, compute the **Information Gain** for the attribute "Income" and identify whether it should be chosen as the root node. **Use ID3.**

| Age | Income | Student | Credit Rating | Buys Computer |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| Youth | High | No | Fair | No |
| Youth | High | No | Excellent | No |
| Middle | High | No | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | No | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Low | Yes | Excellent | No |
| Middle | Low | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| Youth | Medium | No | Fair | No |
| Youth | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Youth | Medium | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| Middle | Medium | No | Excellent | Yes |
| Middle | High | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | No | Excellent | No |

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Class counts:** Yes = 9, No = 5, Total = 14.

**Step 2 — Compute $Info(D)$:**

$$
Info(D) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) = \mathbf{0.9403 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step 3 — Partition by Income:**

*High (4 tuples: 2 Yes, 2 No):*

$$
Info_{High} = -\frac{2}{4}\log_2(0.5) - \frac{2}{4}\log_2(0.5) = 0.5(1) + 0.5(1) = \mathbf{1.0 \text{ bits}}
$$

*Medium (6 tuples: 4 Yes, 2 No):*

$$
Info_{Medium} = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{6}\right) - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{6}\right) = 0.6667(0.585) + 0.3333(1.585) = 0.390 + 0.528 = \mathbf{0.918 \text{ bits}}
$$

*Low (4 tuples: 3 Yes, 1 No):*

$$
Info_{Low} = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = 0.75(0.415) + 0.25(2) = 0.311 + 0.5 = \mathbf{0.811 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step 4 — Weighted conditional entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Info_{Income}(D) &= \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.918) + \frac{4}{14}(0.811) \\[4pt]
&= 0.2857 + 0.3934 + 0.2317 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.9108 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Information Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Gain(Income) &= Info(D) - Info_{Income}(D) \\[4pt]
&= 0.9403 - 0.9108 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.0295 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:** Standard KTU reference gains: $Gain(Age) = 0.2467$, $Gain(Income) = 0.0295$, $Gain(Student) = 0.1518$, $Gain(Credit\,Rating) = 0.0481$. Since $Gain(Income)$ is the **lowest**, it should **not** be chosen as the root. **Age** has the highest gain and is the root.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Naïve Bayesian Classification** with a suitable example. State the **naïve independence assumption** clearly. How is **Laplace (add-1) smoothing** used to handle the zero-frequency problem?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Naïve Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem** with the **naïve assumption of conditional independence** among attributes given the class. It is widely used in **spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and medical diagnosis** because of its simplicity and high speed.

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{X})}
$$

**Naïve Independence Assumption:**

$$
P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)
$$

**Decision Rule:** Predict the class that maximises the posterior.

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C} \; P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)
$$

**Worked Mini-Example (Spam Detection, 4-word dictionary):**

Training: 5 spam, 5 non-spam emails.
- $P(\text{free} \mid \text{Spam}) = 4/5$, $P(\text{free} \mid \text{NotSpam}) = 1/5$
- $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Spam}) = 3/5$, $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{NotSpam}) = 1/5$

New email: "free offer." Then:

$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{free, offer}) \propto \frac{5}{10} \times \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{3}{5} = 0.24
$$

$$
P(\text{NotSpam} \mid \text{free, offer}) \propto \frac{5}{10} \times \frac{1}{5} \times \frac{1}{5} = 0.02
$$

$\therefore$ **Classified as Spam.**

**Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing:** When an attribute value never appears in training for a class, $P(x_i \mid C) = 0$, which makes the entire product zero. To avoid this, add 1 to every numerator and $\vert V_i \vert$ to the denominator:

$$
P(x_i \mid C) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C) + 1}{\text{count}(C) + \vert V_i \vert}
$$

This ensures no probability is ever exactly zero and the classifier remains numerically stable.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** For the following training dataset, classify the new tuple **(Outlook = Rain, Temperature = Mild, Humidity = Normal, Wind = Strong)** using the **Naïve Bayesian classifier**. Show all intermediate computations including prior and likelihood values. Use the "Play Tennis" dataset (9 Yes, 5 No).

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Priors:**

$$
P(Yes) = \frac{9}{14} \approx 0.6429, \qquad P(No) = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.3571
$$

**Step 2 — Likelihoods from training data:**

| Attribute Value | $P(\cdot \mid Yes)$ | $P(\cdot \mid No)$ |
| :-- | :--: | :--: |
| $Outlook = Rain$ | 3/9 = 0.3333 | 2/5 = 0.4000 |
| $Temperature = Mild$ | 4/9 = 0.4444 | 2/5 = 0.4000 |
| $Humidity = Normal$ | 6/9 = 0.6667 | 1/5 = 0.2000 |
| $Wind = Strong$ | 3/9 = 0.3333 | 3/5 = 0.6000 |

**Step 3 — Posterior for Yes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Yes \mid \mathbf{X}) &\propto 0.6429 \times 0.3333 \times 0.4444 \times 0.6667 \times 0.3333 \\[4pt]
&\propto 0.6429 \times 0.03298 \\[4pt]
&\propto \mathbf{0.0212}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Posterior for No:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(No \mid \mathbf{X}) &\propto 0.3571 \times 0.4000 \times 0.4000 \times 0.2000 \times 0.6000 \\[4pt]
&\propto 0.3571 \times 0.01920 \\[4pt]
&\propto \mathbf{0.0069}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalised Posteriors:**

Total $= 0.0212 + 0.0069 = 0.0281$

$$
P(Yes \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{0.0212}{0.0281} \approx 0.7544, \quad P(No \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{0.0069}{0.0281} \approx 0.2456
$$

**Step 6 — Classification:**

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{y} = \arg\max_C P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) = \text{Yes (Play Tennis)}\;}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module-3 Revision Bullets:**

- **Classification** is a **supervised learning** technique predicting categorical labels.
- The **two-phase** process: **Learning Phase** (model building) and **Classification Phase** (prediction/evaluation).
- **Information Gain** is computed as $Gain(A) = Info(D) - Info_A(D)$; **higher is better**.
- **Entropy** $Info(D)$ uses **$\log_2$** and is the **expected information** in bits.
- **Gini Index** is $Gini(D) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$; **lower is better** (CART).
- **Gain Ratio** = $Gain(A) / SplitInfo_A(D)$; fixes the **multi-value bias** of Information Gain.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(C \mid X) = P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C) / P(X)$.
- **Naïve assumption:** $P(x_1, \dots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$ — attributes independent given the class.
- **Decision rule:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_C \; P(C) \prod P(x_i \mid C)$.
- **Laplace (add-1) smoothing** prevents $P(x_i \mid C) = 0$: $P = (count + 1) / (N + \vert V_i \vert)$.
- **Naïve Bayes strengths:** fast, handles high-dimensional data, works with small training sets, robust to noise.
- **Naïve Bayes weaknesses:** poor probability estimates, independence assumption often violated.
- **Confusion matrix** elements: **TP, TN, FP, FN** — basis of all evaluation metrics.
- **Accuracy $\neq$ F1-score** for imbalanced datasets — always specify.
- **Memorise** standard reference Gains: $Outlook = 0.2467$, $Humidity = 0.1518$, $Wind = 0.0481$, $Temperature = 0.0292$ (Play Tennis).
- **Zero-frequency problem** → always apply **Laplace smoothing** in exam numericals.
- **Examiner's red flags:** forgetting $\log$ base 2, swapping numerator/denominator in Bayes, missing the argmax step.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering - K-Means, Agglomerative versus Divisive Hierarchical Clustering, BIRCH, DBSCAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clustering - K-Means, Agglomerative versus Divisive Hierarchical Clustering, BIRCH, DBSCAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Clustering — The Foundational Concept

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Clustering is an **unsupervised learning** technique in data analytics that partitions a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ into $k$ groups (clusters) $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ such that objects within the same cluster exhibit high **intra-cluster similarity**, while objects in different clusters show high **inter-cluster dissimilarity**. No labeled output is provided; the algorithm discovers structure on its own.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** Clustering belongs to the family of **Unsupervised Learning** algorithms. Unlike classification, there is **no ground truth label**. The objective is to discover the inherent grouping structure in data — this is a frequent **2-mark definition question** in KTU exams.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **librarian with 10,000 unlabelled books dumped on the floor**. You are told to organize them — but you don't have a catalog, no categories, no labels. What would you do?

- You would naturally **group books that "look similar"** — same height, same color, same thickness, same topic on the spine.
- You would then place each group on a separate shelf.
- A new book arriving? You find the shelf whose existing books are **closest in features** to this new book and place it there.

That, in essence, is clustering. The computer does the same — it measures "closeness" (using distance metrics like Euclidean, Manhattan, Cosine) and groups data points that are mathematically similar.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Clustering Quality (KTU frequently tests these):**
> 1. **Intra-cluster compactness** — points within a cluster should be tightly packed.
> 2. **Inter-cluster separation** — clusters should be far apart from each other.
> 3. **Robustness to noise and outliers** — especially important for BIRCH and DBSCAN.

---

## 1.2 K-Means Clustering — The Workhorse

**Formal Definition:**
> K-Means is a **centroid-based, partitional clustering** algorithm that aims to partition $n$ observations into exactly $k$ clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also known as the **inertia** or **distortion function**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **school principal assigning students to $k$ classrooms**. The principal:
1. Randomly picks $k$ "lead students" (initial centroids).
2. Every other student joins the lead student they are **most similar to** (closest in academic profile).
3. After everyone is assigned, the **average** of each classroom becomes the new lead.
4. Students may switch classrooms if the new leads are closer.
5. Repeat until **no student switches** — equilibrium reached.

### Mathematical Objective

The K-Means objective function is:

$$J(C, \mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$$

where:
- $k$ = predefined number of clusters
- $C_i$ = the $i$-th cluster
- $\mu_i$ = the centroid (mean) of cluster $C_i$
- $\lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$ = squared Euclidean distance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The K-Means algorithm is **guaranteed to converge**, but it converges to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum. This is why KTU examiners love asking: *"Why does K-Means give different results on different runs?"* — Answer: Because of random initialization of centroids.

---

## 1.3 Hierarchical Clustering — Agglomerative vs. Divisive

**Formal Definition:**
> Hierarchical clustering builds a **tree-like structure (dendrogram)** of clusters. It does not require pre-specifying the number of clusters $k$. There are two strategies: **Agglomerative (bottom-up)** and **Divisive (top-down)**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **corporate org chart**:
- **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up):** You start with every individual employee as their own department. Then you **merge** the two most compatible employees → small team → team → department → division → company.
- **Divisive (Top-Down):** You start with the **entire company as one cluster**. Then you **split** it into two divisions, then departments, then teams, then individual employees.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Comparison Pattern:** Almost every KTU question on hierarchical clustering asks you to **compare** agglomerative and divisive. Use a 4-point comparison table (time complexity, decision point, sensitivity, output).

---

## 1.4 BIRCH — Balanced Iterative Reducing and Clustering using Hierarchies

**Formal Definition:**
> BIRCH is an **incremental, hierarchical clustering algorithm** designed for **large datasets**. It builds a **Clustering Feature (CF) tree** that summarizes data in a compact form, enabling single-pass and multi-pass clustering with **O(n)** time complexity.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **factory assembly line** where a quality inspector must categorize 1 million products. They cannot hold all products in mind. So they:
1. **Group products into small batches** (subclusters) and record only a **summary** (count, sum, sum of squares) → these summaries go into a **CF tree**.
2. They periodically **re-organize** the batches using K-Means.
3. Only the summaries, not the raw products, drive the decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **Clustering Feature (CF) vector** = $(N, LS, SS)$ where $N$ = number of points, $LS$ = linear sum, $SS$ = squared sum. This **3-tuple is the heart of BIRCH** and is heavily tested.

---

## 1.5 DBSCAN — Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise

**Formal Definition:**
> DBSCAN is a **density-based clustering algorithm** that groups together points that are **closely packed** (high density regions) and marks points in low-density regions as **outliers (noise)**. It does not require pre-specifying the number of clusters and can discover clusters of **arbitrary shape**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you are **stargazing in a clear night sky**:
- Stars that are **densely packed** in a region (e.g., the Milky Way band) → form a constellation (cluster).
- A **single bright star** isolated in an empty patch of sky → marked as noise/outlier, not part of any constellation.
- DBSCAN does exactly this: it requires each cluster point to have at least `MinPts` neighbors within a radius `Eps`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Gold:** DBSCAN is the **only algorithm** in this module that **natively detects outliers**. K-Means and Hierarchical clustering force every point into some cluster — DBSCAN does not. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means — Operational Logic in Detail

The K-Means algorithm follows a **two-step iterative procedure** known as **Lloyd's Algorithm (1957)**:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Choose $k$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_k^{(0)}\}$. Common methods:
- **Random selection** from the dataset
- **Forgy method** — randomly choose $k$ data points
- **K-Means++** — probabilistic seeding that spreads initial centroids apart (gives better results)

**Step 2 — Assignment Step (Expectation):**
For each data point $x_j$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest:

$$C_i^{(t)} = \left\{ x_j : \lVert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \rVert^2 \leq \lVert x_j - \mu_l^{(t)} \rVert^2 \; \forall l = 1, 2, \ldots, k \right\}$$

**Step 3 — Update Step (Maximization):**
Recompute each centroid as the **mean** of all points assigned to it:

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Check:**
If $\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \mu_i^{(t)}$ for all $i$, **STOP**. Otherwise, return to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Why is the K-Means objective function non-convex?"* — Because the assignment of each point to a cluster is a **discrete (combinatorial)** decision, even though the centroid update is continuous. This creates a non-convex optimization landscape with many local minima.

### K-Means Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Objective function (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$ | Lower is better |
| Centroid update | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | Vector of feature means |
| Euclidean distance | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_d - y_d)^2}$ | Default K-Means metric |
| Manhattan distance | $d(x, y) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - y_d \vert$ | More robust to outliers |
| Elbow method criterion | Plot $J$ vs $k$; find "elbow" point | Used to choose optimal $k$ |
| Silhouette score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$; higher is better |
| Time complexity | $O(n \cdot k \cdot I \cdot d)$ | $n$ = points, $I$ = iterations, $d$ = dimensions |
| Space complexity | $O((n + k) \cdot d)$ | Stores data + centroids |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** I deliberately wrote $\vert C_i \vert$ instead of $|C_i|$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your exam scripts, use $|C_i|$.

### Engineering Utility of K-Means
- **Image compression (vector quantization)** — reduce 16M colors to 16 representative colors.
- **Customer segmentation** in marketing analytics.
- **Document clustering** and topic discovery.
- **Anomaly detection preprocessing** (points far from any centroid are anomalies).

---

## 2.2 Hierarchical Clustering — Linkage Criteria Deep Dive

The **linkage criterion** determines how inter-cluster distance is computed when deciding which clusters to merge (agglomerative) or split (divisive).

### Agglomerative Algorithm (AGNES)

**Step 1:** Start with $n$ singleton clusters: $C_i^{(0)} = \{x_i\}$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$.

**Step 2:** Compute the $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** $P$ where $P_{ij} = d(x_i, x_j)$.

**Step 3:** Repeat:
- **Find** the two closest clusters $C_a, C_b$ in the current proximity matrix.
- **Merge** them: $C_{\text{new}} = C_a \cup C_b$.
- **Update** the proximity matrix.
- **Record** the merge level (height) in the dendrogram.

**Step 4:** Stop when only **one cluster** remains.

### Linkage Criteria (KTU High-Yield)

| **Linkage** | **Formula** | **Behaviour** | **When to use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Chaining — tends to produce long, elongated clusters | Detecting non-elliptical shapes |
| Complete | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Tends to find compact, equal-sized clusters | Compact spherical clusters |
| Average | $d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Compromise between single and complete | General-purpose |
| Ward's | $\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \lVert \mu_a - \mu_b \rVert^2$ | Minimizes within-cluster variance | Mimics K-Means objective |

### Divisive Algorithm (DIANA — Divisive Analysis)

**Step 1:** Start with one cluster containing all $n$ points.

**Step 2:** At each step, select the cluster with the **largest diameter** (or any suitable splitting criterion).

**Step 3:** Find the point with the **largest average dissimilarity** to other points in the cluster — this becomes the **"splinter" group seed**.

**Step 4:** Iteratively move points to the splinter group if they are **closer to the splinter group** than to the original cluster.

**Step 5:** Repeat until each cluster is a singleton or a stopping criterion is met.

### Hierarchical Clustering Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Value** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Time (Agglomerative) | $O(n^3)$ naively; $O(n^2 \log n)$ with heap | Major bottleneck for large $n$ |
| Time (Divisive) | $O(2^n)$ worst case | Even more expensive |
| Space | $O(n^2)$ for proximity matrix | Memory-heavy |
| Output | Dendrogram | Cut at any height to get $k$ clusters |
| Lance-Williams update | $d(C_a \cup C_b, C_c) = \alpha_a d(C_a, C_c) + \alpha_b d(C_b, C_c) + \beta d(C_a, C_b) + \gamma \vert d(C_a, C_c) - d(C_b, C_c) \vert$ | Unified formula for all linkages |

### Agglomerative vs. Divisive — Side-by-Side Comparison

| **Aspect** | **Agglomerative (AGNES)** | **Divisive (DIANA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| Starting point | $n$ singleton clusters | 1 cluster of all points |
| Decision point | Which 2 clusters to **merge** | Which cluster to **split** |
| Computational cost | $O(n^2 \log n)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(2^n)$ — exponential |
| Memory | $O(n^2)$ proximity matrix | $O(n)$ or $O(n^2)$ |
| Sensitivity to noise | High (irreversible merges) | Can be lower (can re-examine splits) |
| Output | Dendrogram | Dendrogram |
| Reversibility | Cannot undo merges | Cannot undo splits |
| Practical use | More common in industry | Rare in practice (cost) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write that divisive clustering is **"faster"** than agglomerative. The opposite is true — divisive is **exponentially more expensive** because finding the optimal split is NP-hard. The board explicitly tests this misconception.

---

## 2.3 BIRCH — Clustering Feature Tree Deep Dive

### The Clustering Feature (CF) Vector

For a subcluster containing $N$ data points $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ in $d$-dimensional space:

$$\vec{CF} = (N, \vec{LS}, SS)$$

where:
- $N$ = number of points in the subcluster
- $\vec{LS} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ — **linear sum** ($d$-dimensional vector)
- $SS = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i \rVert^2$ — **sum of squared values** (scalar)

### Key Additive Property (Incremental Update)

When two subclusters $CF_1 = (N_1, \vec{LS_1}, SS_1)$ and $CF_2 = (N_2, \vec{LS_2}, SS_2)$ are merged:

$$CF_{\text{merged}} = (N_1 + N_2,\; \vec{LS_1} + \vec{LS_2},\; SS_1 + SS_2)$$

This is what makes BIRCH **single-pass and online** — you never need to revisit raw data.

### Derived Statistics from CF

| **Statistic** | **Formula** |
|---|---|
| Centroid | $\vec{\mu} = \frac{\vec{LS}}{N}$ |
| Radius (avg distance to centroid) | $R = \sqrt{\frac{SS}{N} - \left(\frac{\vec{LS}}{N}\right)^2}$ |
| Diameter (avg pairwise distance) | $D = \sqrt{\frac{2N \cdot SS - 2\lVert \vec{LS} \rVert^2}{N(N-1)}}$ |

### CF Tree Structure

A CF tree is a **height-balanced tree** with three parameters:
- **Branching factor $B$** — max children per non-leaf node
- **Threshold $T$** — max diameter of subclusters in a leaf
- **Maximum leaf size $L$** — max entries per leaf node

```
            Root
           /    \
        CF1     CF2      (non-leaf: CF summary of children)
       /  \     /  \
    CF1a CF1b CF2a CF2b  (leaf nodes: actual subcluster summaries)
```

### The Four Phases of BIRCH

1. **Phase 1 — Build CF Tree:** Scan data once, insert each point into the closest leaf entry. If the entry's diameter exceeds $T$, split the leaf. Produces a compact in-memory summary.
2. **Phase 2 — Condense Tree (Optional):** Rebuild the CF tree with a smaller threshold to remove outliers and compress further.
3. **Phase 3 — Global Clustering:** Apply a global algorithm (e.g., K-Means or Agglomerative) on the **leaf entries** of the CF tree (which are now very few).
4. **Phase 4 — Refine Clusters:** Redistribute all data points to the nearest centroids found in Phase 3 for fine-grained refinement.

### BIRCH Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Value / Formula** |
|---|---|
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ — single pass! |
| Space complexity | $O(B \cdot L)$ — depends on tree size, not on $n$ |
| Additive property | $CF_{1+2} = CF_1 \oplus CF_2$ |
| Handles outliers? | Partially (Phase 2) |
| Assumes cluster shape | Spherical (like K-Means) |
| Sensitive to data order? | Yes — order of insertion matters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"What is the main advantage of BIRCH over K-Means?"* — Answer: **Scalability**. BIRCH scales linearly $O(n)$ with dataset size, while K-Means is $O(nki)$ per iteration. BIRCH is suitable for datasets too large to fit in memory.

---

## 2.4 DBSCAN — Density-Based Clustering Deep Dive

### Two Critical Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Effect of Increasing** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neighborhood radius | $\epsilon$ (Eps) | Maximum radius to search for neighbors | Fewer, larger clusters; fewer noise points |
| Minimum neighbors | **MinPts** | Minimum points required to form a dense region | Stricter density requirement; more noise |

### Three Categories of Points

For any point $p$ in the dataset:

1. **Core Point:** Has at least **MinPts** points (including itself) within $\epsilon$ distance.
$$\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$$
2. **Border Point:** Is within $\epsilon$ of a core point, but has fewer than MinPts neighbors itself.
3. **Noise Point (Outlier):** Is neither a core point nor a border point.

where $N_\epsilon(p) = \{q \in D : d(p, q) \leq \epsilon\}$ is the $\epsilon$-neighborhood of $p$.

### Direct Density-Reachability, Reachability, and Connectivity

- **Directly density-reachable:** $p$ is a core point AND $q \in N_\epsilon(p)$.
- **Density-reachable:** Chain of directly density-reachable points from $p$ to $q$.
- **Density-connected:** There exists $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$.

A **cluster** is defined as a **maximal set of density-connected points**.

### DBSCAN Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

1. **Input:** Dataset $D$, parameters $\epsilon$, MinPts.
2. **Initialize:** Mark all points as unvisited.
3. **For each unvisited point $p$:**
   - Mark $p$ as visited.
   - Compute $N_\epsilon(p)$.
   - **If** $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq$ MinPts: $p$ is a **core point**; create a new cluster; recursively add all density-reachable points to this cluster.
   - **Else:** Mark $p$ as **noise** (temporarily — may be reclassified as border later).
4. **Output:** Set of clusters + noise points.

### DBSCAN Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Value** |
|---|---|
| $\epsilon$-neighborhood | $N_\epsilon(p) = \{q : d(p, q) \leq \epsilon\}$ |
| Core point condition | $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq$ MinPts |
| Time complexity | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial index (e.g., KD-tree); $O(n^2)$ naive |
| Space complexity | $O(n)$ |
| Requires pre-specified $k$? | **No** — discovers $k$ automatically |
| Handles arbitrary shapes? | **Yes** |
| Handles noise? | **Yes** — explicitly labeled as noise |
| Sensitive to parameters? | Highly sensitive to $\epsilon$ and MinPts |
| Curse of dimensionality? | Yes — distance becomes meaningless in high-D |

> [!NOTE]
> **OPTICS Algorithm Note (Frequently Confused):** OPTICS is an extension of DBSCAN that addresses its parameter sensitivity. It produces a **reachability plot** that can be used to extract clusters at multiple density levels. KTU sometimes asks to **differentiate DBSCAN and OPTICS**.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Summary Table

| **Algorithm** | **Best For** | **Worst For** | **Key Strength** |
|---|---|---|---|
| K-Means | Spherical, well-separated, numeric data | Non-convex shapes, outliers, categorical | Speed and simplicity |
| Agglomerative | Small datasets, dendrogram needed | Large datasets | No need to pre-specify $k$ |
| Divisive | Rarely used in practice | Anything (cost-prohibitive) | Theoretical completeness |
| BIRCH | Very large datasets, streaming | Non-spherical clusters | $O(n)$ scalability |
| DBSCAN | Non-convex shapes, noisy data | Varying density clusters, high-D data | Outlier detection, arbitrary shapes |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 K-Means: Derivation of the Centroid Update Rule

**Question:** Why is the centroid of a cluster the **mean** of its points?

### Setup

Given a fixed cluster assignment $C_i$, we want to find the centroid $\mu_i$ that **minimizes the sum of squared distances**:

$$J(\mu_i) = \sum_{x_j \in C_i} \lVert x_j - \mu_i \rVert^2 = \sum_{x_j \in C_i} \sum_{d=1}^{D} (x_{jd} - \mu_{id})^2$$

### Derivation

Take the partial derivative with respect to $\mu_{id}$ (the $d$-th component of $\mu_i$) and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J(\mu_i)}{\partial \mu_{id}} = \sum_{x_j \in C_i} -2(x_{jd} - \mu_{id}) = 0$$

Divide both sides by $-2$:

$$\sum_{x_j \in C_i} (x_{jd} - \mu_{id}) = 0$$

Expand the sum:

$$\sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_{jd} - \sum_{x_j \in C_i} \mu_{id} = 0$$

Since $\mu_{id}$ is constant over the sum:

$$\sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_{jd} = \vert C_i \vert \cdot \mu_{id}$$

Solve for $\mu_{id}$:

$$\mu_{id} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_{jd}$$

Therefore, vectorially:

$$\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$$

The second derivative check (to confirm minimum):

$$\frac{\partial^2 J}{\partial \mu_{id}^2} = 2 \vert C_i \vert > 0$$

Since this is positive for any non-empty cluster, the solution is a **minimum** (not a maximum or saddle point).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expected Answer:** When asked to prove the centroid update formula, you **must** show the differentiation step and the second-order condition. Just stating the formula is worth only 1 mark out of 3–5.

---

## 3.2 DBSCAN: Detailed Walk-Through on a 2D Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** Given the following 2D points, apply DBSCAN with $\epsilon = 1.5$ and MinPts = 3. Identify core, border, and noise points.

$$P_1 = (0, 0), \quad P_2 = (0.5, 0.5), \quad P_3 = (1, 1), \quad P_4 = (5, 5), \quad P_5 = (5.2, 5.1), \quad P_6 = (4.8, 5.3), \quad P_7 = (10, 10)$$

### Step 1: Compute the Euclidean Distance Matrix

Using $d((x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2)) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$:

$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707$$
$$d(P_1, P_3) = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$
$$d(P_1, P_4) = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071$$
$$d(P_2, P_3) = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707$$
$$d(P_2, P_4) = \sqrt{20.25 + 20.25} \approx 6.364$$
$$d(P_3, P_4) = \sqrt{16 + 16} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.657$$
$$d(P_4, P_5) = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.05} \approx 0.224$$
$$d(P_4, P_6) = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.13} \approx 0.361$$
$$d(P_5, P_6) = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.20} \approx 0.447$$
$$d(P_5, P_7) = \sqrt{23.04 + 23.04} \approx 6.787$$
$$d(P_6, P_7) = \sqrt{27.04 + 22.09} \approx 7.024$$

### Step 2: Determine $\epsilon$-Neighborhoods ($\epsilon = 1.5$)

| **Point** | **Neighbors within $\epsilon$** | **Count** |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | $P_2, P_3$ | 2 |
| $P_2$ | $P_1, P_3$ | 2 |
| $P_3$ | $P_1, P_2$ | 2 |
| $P_4$ | $P_5, P_6$ | 2 |
| $P_5$ | $P_4, P_6$ | 2 |
| $P_6$ | $P_4, P_5$ | 2 |
| $P_7$ | (none) | 0 |

### Step 3: Apply the MinPts = 3 Rule

- **Core points** ($\vert N_\epsilon \vert \geq 3$): **None** ❌
- **Border points** (within $\epsilon$ of a core point, but not core themselves): **None** ❌
- **Noise points** (neither core nor border): **All seven points $P_1$ through $P_7$** ❌

### Interpretation

With $\epsilon = 1.5$ and MinPts = 3, **no cluster is formed** because no point has 3 neighbors within radius 1.5. This illustrates DBSCAN's **sensitivity to parameters** — using $\epsilon = 1.0$ wouldn't help, but using $\epsilon = 2.0$ might capture the first cluster.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to **include the point itself** when counting. By convention, $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert$ **includes $p$**. So a core point with MinPts = 3 needs **3 total points** (itself + 2 others), not 3 others.

---

## 3.3 BIRCH: Additive CF Property Derivation

**Question:** Show that the Clustering Feature is additive: $CF_1 \oplus CF_2 = (N_1 + N_2, \vec{LS_1} + \vec{LS_2}, SS_1 + SS_2)$.

### Proof

Let subcluster 1 contain points $\{x_1^{(1)}, x_2^{(1)}, \ldots, x_{N_1}^{(1)}\}$ and subcluster 2 contain $\{x_1^{(2)}, x_2^{(2)}, \ldots, x_{N_2}^{(2)}\}$.

**Component 1 — $N$:**
The merged subcluster contains all points from both:
$$N_{\text{merged}} = N_1 + N_2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Component 2 — $\vec{LS}$:**
The linear sum of the merged subcluster is:
$$\vec{LS}_{\text{merged}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_1} x_i^{(1)} + \sum_{j=1}^{N_2} x_j^{(2)} = \vec{LS_1} + \vec{LS_2} \quad \checkmark$$

**Component 3 — $SS$:**
The sum of squared values:
$$SS_{\text{merged}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_1} \lVert x_i^{(1)} \rVert^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{N_2} \lVert x_j^{(2)} \rVert^2 = SS_1 + SS_2 \quad \checkmark$$

Hence:
$$CF_1 \oplus CF_2 = (N_1 + N_2,\; \vec{LS_1} + \vec{LS_2},\; SS_1 + SS_2) \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The additivity means BIRCH never needs to store raw data. When scanning a new point, you simply update the nearest CF entry's three numbers. This is the mathematical foundation of BIRCH's $O(n)$ time complexity.

---

## 3.4 Complete Numerical K-Means Walk-Through

**Problem (KTU Practice):** Apply K-Means with $k = 2$ on the following 1D points: $\{1, 2, 8, 9, 10\}$. Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = 1, \mu_2 = 2$.

### Iteration 1

**Assignment step:**

| **Point $x$** | $d(x, \mu_1) = \vert x - 1 \vert$ | $d(x, \mu_2) = \vert x - 2 \vert$ | **Assigned to** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | $\mu_1$ |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | $\mu_2$ |
| 8 | 7 | 6 | $\mu_2$ |
| 9 | 8 | 7 | $\mu_2$ |
| 10 | 9 | 8 | $\mu_2$ |

Result: $C_1 = \{1\}$, $C_2 = \{2, 8, 9, 10\}$.

**Update step:**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{1}(1) = 1$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{4}(2 + 8 + 9 + 10) = \frac{29}{4} = 7.25$$

**WCSS computation:**

$$J^{(1)} = (1-1)^2 + (2-7.25)^2 + (8-7.25)^2 + (9-7.25)^2 + (10-7.25)^2$$
$$= 0 + 27.5625 + 0.5625 + 3.0625 + 7.5625 = 38.75$$

### Iteration 2

**Assignment step** (with $\mu_1 = 1, \mu_2 = 7.25$):

| **Point $x$** | $d(x, \mu_1)$ | $d(x, \mu_2)$ | **Assigned to** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 6.25 | $\mu_1$ |
| 2 | 1 | 5.25 | $\mu_1$ |
| 8 | 7 | 0.75 | $\mu_2$ |
| 9 | 8 | 1.75 | $\mu_2$ |
| 10 | 9 | 2.75 | $\mu_2$ |

Result: $C_1 = \{1, 2\}$, $C_2 = \{8, 9, 10\}$.

**Update step:**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1+2}{2} = 1.5$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{8+9+10}{3} = 9.0$$

**WCSS computation:**

$$J^{(2)} = (1-1.5)^2 + (2-1.5)^2 + (8-9)^2 + (9-9)^2 + (10-9)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 2.5$$

### Iteration 3

**Assignment step** (with $\mu_1 = 1.5, \mu_2 = 9.0$): Same as Iteration 2 — no changes. **Convergence reached.**

**Final WCSS:** $J = 2.5$ with clusters $C_1 = \{1, 2\}$ and $C_2 = \{8, 9, 10\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $J$ decreased from 38.75 (Iteration 1) to 2.5 (Iteration 2) — a **93.5% reduction**. K-Means is **monotonically non-increasing** in $J$, which is another way to state its convergence guarantee.

---

## 3.5 Agglomerative Clustering: Worked Example Using Single Linkage

**Problem:** Apply agglomerative clustering with **single linkage** on points $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (1, 0)$, $C = (4, 0)$, $D = (5, 0)$.

### Step 1: Initial Proximity Matrix

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 1 | 4 | 5 |
| B | 1 | 0 | 3 | 4 |
| C | 4 | 3 | 0 | 1 |
| D | 5 | 4 | 1 | 0 |

### Step 2: First Merge

Smallest distance: $d(A, B) = 1$ and $d(C, D) = 1$. Choose $A \cup B$ (arbitrary tie-break).

Update matrix using **single linkage** — minimum distance between any point in each cluster:

$$d(A \cup B, C) = \min(d(A, C), d(B, C)) = \min(4, 3) = 3$$
$$d(A \cup B, D) = \min(d(A, D), d(B, D)) = \min(5, 4) = 4$$

### Step 3: Updated Matrix

| | AB | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| AB | 0 | 3 | 4 |
| C | 3 | 0 | 1 |
| D | 4 | 1 | 0 |

### Step 4: Second Merge

Smallest distance: $d(C, D) = 1$. Merge $C \cup D = CD$.

$$d(AB, CD) = \min(d(A, C), d(A, D), d(B, C), d(B, D)) = \min(4, 5, 3, 4) = 3$$

### Step 5: Final Merge

Only $\{AB\}$ and $\{CD\}$ remain. Merge them at distance 3. **Done.**

### Dendrogram

```
Distance
   3 |-------\
   1 |-\     \
     |A B C D   (cluster AB and CD merge at distance 3)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the dendrogram:** To obtain 2 clusters, "cut" the dendrogram at any height between 1 and 3, giving $\{A, B\}$ and $\{C, D\}$. To obtain 1 cluster, do not cut at all.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input dataset D and k] --> B[Initialize k centroids: mu1, mu2, ..., muK]
    B --> C[Random init / K-Means++ / Forgy]
    C --> D[Assignment Step: Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    D --> E[Compute distance d of xi to each mu_j]
    E --> F[Assign xi to argmin distance]
    F --> G[Update Step: Recompute centroids as mean of assigned points]
    G --> H[Compute new mu_i as mean of C_i]
    H --> I{Convergence Check: Did centroids change?}
    I -->|Yes| D
    I -->|No| J[Output: Final clusters C1, C2, ..., Ck]
    J --> K[End]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style G fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style I fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style J fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00796B
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Clustering — Agglomerative vs. Divisive Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AGNES ["AGGLOMERATIVE (Bottom-Up)"]
        A1[1 Point] --> A2[Merge nearest pair]
        A2 --> A3[Small cluster formed]
        A3 --> A4[Repeat merges]
        A4 --> A5[All points in one cluster]
    end

    subgraph DIANA ["DIVISIVE (Top-Down)"]
        D1[All points in one cluster] --> D2[Find largest diameter cluster]
        D2 --> D3[Split using splinter point]
        D3 --> D4[Smaller clusters formed]
        D4 --> D5[Repeat splits]
        D5 --> D6[Each point is its own cluster]
    end

    style AGNES fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style DIANA fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
```

## 4.3 BIRCH — CF Tree Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Root Node] --> N1[Internal CF Node 1]
    R --> N2[Internal CF Node 2]
    N1 --> L1[Leaf CF Entry 1a]
    N1 --> L2[Leaf CF Entry 1b]
    N2 --> L3[Leaf CF Entry 2a]
    N2 --> L4[Leaf CF Entry 2b]
    L1 --> P1[Points: 1, 2, 3]
    L2 --> P2[Points: 4, 5, 6]
    L3 --> P3[Points: 7, 8, 9]
    L4 --> P4[Points: 10, 11, 12]

    style R fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD
    style N1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style N2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style L1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style L2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style L3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style L4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

## 4.4 DBSCAN — Density-Based Point Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Point p] --> Q{Is N_eps of p greater than or equal to MinPts?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Core Point]
    Q -->|No| S{Is p within eps of any core point?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Border Point]
    S -->|No| U[Noise / Outlier]
    R --> V[Forms new cluster or joins existing]
    T --> W[Joins nearest core point cluster]
    U --> X[Marked as outlier: not in any cluster]

    style P fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style R fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style T fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define clustering. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with respect to clustering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Clustering is an unsupervised machine learning technique that partitions a dataset into groups (clusters) such that data points within the same cluster are more similar to each other than to those in other clusters. It discovers hidden patterns without using pre-labeled output.

**Difference (1.5 marks):**

| **Supervised** | **Unsupervised (Clustering)** |
|---|---|
| Requires labeled training data | No labels required |
| Goal: predict output for new input | Goal: discover inherent group structure |
| Example: Classification, Regression | Example: K-Means, DBSCAN, BIRCH |
| Evaluation: accuracy, precision, recall | Evaluation: WCSS, silhouette score, Davies-Bouldin index |

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a dendrogram? Mention its role in hierarchical clustering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dendrogram** (from Greek *dendron* = tree, *gramma* = drawing) is a **tree-like diagram** that records the sequence of merges (in agglomerative) or splits (in divisive) in hierarchical clustering. (1 mark)

**Role (2 marks):**
1. **Visualization** — provides an intuitive 2D picture of the cluster hierarchy.
2. **Determining $k$** — the number of clusters can be chosen by cutting the dendrogram horizontally at a chosen height.
3. **Revealing cluster structure** — shows which points/clusters merged at what distance, helping interpret the natural grouping.
4. **Cophenetic distance** — the height at which two points are first joined in the dendrogram represents their similarity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 3 Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts. The student must answer one full question. Two alternative choices are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm with its objective function. Discuss its limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):** K-Means clustering partitions $n$ data points into $k$ clusters by minimizing the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS). The algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Initialize** $k$ centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k$ (randomly or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment Step:** Assign each point $x_j$ to the cluster whose centroid is closest:
$$C_i = \{x_j : \lVert x_j - \mu_i \rVert^2 \leq \lVert x_j - \mu_l \rVert^2 \; \forall l\}$$
3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its points:
$$\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$$
4. **Repeat** steps 2–3 until centroids do not change (convergence).

**Objective Function (2 marks):**

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$$

This is the sum of squared Euclidean distances between every point and its assigned centroid. K-Means tries to minimize $J$ by alternating between assignment and centroid update.

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Pre-specification of $k$** — must be set in advance; the wrong $k$ yields poor results.
2. **Sensitivity to initialization** — different initial centroids can lead to different final clusters (local minima problem).
3. **Assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters** — fails for elongated, non-convex, or arbitrarily shaped clusters.
4. **Sensitive to outliers** — outliers pull the centroid towards themselves, distorting clusters.
5. **Curse of dimensionality** — distance metrics become less meaningful in high dimensions.
6. **Linear separability assumption** — implicitly assumes clusters are linearly separable.

---

**(b) Compare Agglomerative and Divisive Hierarchical Clustering algorithms. Explain any three linkage criteria used in agglomerative clustering. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Agglomerative** | **Divisive** |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| Start | $n$ singleton clusters | 1 cluster with all $n$ points |
| Operation | Merge two closest clusters | Split the least cohesive cluster |
| Time complexity | $O(n^2 \log n)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(2^n)$ — exponential |
| Memory | $O(n^2)$ proximity matrix | $O(n)$ |
| Reversibility | Cannot undo merges | Cannot undo splits |
| Practical use | Common (AGNES algorithm) | Rare (computationally expensive) |
| Output | Dendrogram | Dendrogram |

**Linkage Criteria (3.5 marks):**

For two clusters $C_a$ and $C_b$ with means $\mu_a, \mu_b$ and sizes $n_a, n_b$:

**1. Single Linkage (Nearest Neighbor):**
$$d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$
Uses the **shortest** pairwise distance. Tends to produce **chained, elongated** clusters. Sensitive to noise.

**2. Complete Linkage (Farthest Neighbor):**
$$d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$
Uses the **longest** pairwise distance. Produces **compact, equal-sized** clusters. Less sensitive to outliers than single linkage.

**3. Average Linkage:**
$$d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{n_a n_b} \sum_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$
Uses the **average** pairwise distance. A compromise between single and complete linkage. Most commonly used in practice.

**4. (Bonus) Ward's Linkage:**
$$\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \frac{n_a n_b}{n_a + n_b} \lVert \mu_a - \mu_b \rVert^2$$
Minimizes the **increase in within-cluster variance** upon merging. Mimics the K-Means objective. Produces balanced, compact clusters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Explain the BIRCH clustering algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss its key features and advantages over K-Means. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**BIRCH Algorithm (3.5 marks):**

BIRCH (Balanced Iterative Reducing and Clustering using Hierarchies) is a clustering algorithm designed for **very large datasets**. It performs clustering in a **single pass** over the data using a tree structure called the **CF (Clustering Feature) tree**.

A CF vector summarizes a subcluster as a 3-tuple:

$$CF = (N, \vec{LS}, SS)$$

where $N$ = number of points, $\vec{LS}$ = linear sum of points, $SS$ = sum of squared values.

**Block Diagram (in text form, 1 mark):**

```
            ROOT
           /    \
        CF1     CF2
       /  \     /  \
     CF1a CF1b CF2a CF2b   (Leaf entries)
     [N,LS,SS] for each subcluster
```

**Four Phases of BIRCH (1.5 marks):**

1. **Phase 1 — Build CF Tree:** Scan data, insert points into the closest leaf entry. If the leaf's diameter exceeds threshold $T$, split the leaf.
2. **Phase 2 — Condense Tree (Optional):** Rebuild tree with smaller $T$ to remove outliers.
3. **Phase 3 — Global Clustering:** Apply K-Means or Agglomerative clustering on the leaf CF entries.
4. **Phase 4 — Refine:** Reassign original points to nearest centroids for a final polish.

**Key Features & Advantages over K-Means (1 mark):**

| **Aspect** | **BIRCH** | **K-Means** |
|---|---|---|
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ — single pass | $O(nki)$ per iteration |
| Memory | Compact CF tree | Stores all data + centroids |
| Outlier handling | Yes (via CF tree structure) | No |
| Scalability | Excellent for very large data | Limited |
| Incremental | Yes (online) | No (batch) |
| Multi-pass refinement | Yes (Phase 4) | Convergence in single run |

**Additive property (Bonus 0.5 marks):** When merging two subclusters, $CF_1 \oplus CF_2 = (N_1 + N_2, \vec{LS_1} + \vec{LS_2}, SS_1 + SS_2)$, enabling efficient incremental updates.

---

**(b) Explain the DBSCAN algorithm. How does it classify points into core, border, and noise? Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**DBSCAN Algorithm (2.5 marks):**

DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) is a **density-based clustering algorithm** that groups together points packed closely together, marking points in low-density regions as outliers. It uses two parameters:
- **$\epsilon$ (Eps):** The radius of the neighborhood.
- **MinPts:** The minimum number of points required in an $\epsilon$-neighborhood for a point to be considered a core point.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Mark all points as unvisited.
2. For each unvisited point $p$:
   - Mark $p$ as visited.
   - Find its $\epsilon$-neighborhood $N_\epsilon(p) = \{q : d(p, q) \leq \epsilon\}$.
   - **If** $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq$ MinPts: $p$ is a **core point**; create a new cluster; recursively add all density-reachable points.
   - **Else:** Mark $p$ as **noise** (may be reclassified as border later).

**Point Classification (2 marks):**

| **Type** | **Definition** |
|---|---|
| **Core Point** | $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq$ MinPts (the point has enough neighbors to be in a dense region) |
| **Border Point** | $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert <$ MinPts, but $p$ is within $\epsilon$ of some core point |
| **Noise Point** | Neither core nor border; isolated point in low-density region |

**Mathematical condition for core point:**

$$\text{core}(p) \iff \vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$$

**Two points $p$ and $q$ are in the same cluster if they are density-connected**, i.e., there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$ via a chain of core points.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**

1. **No need to pre-specify $k$** — the number of clusters is determined automatically.
2. **Discovers clusters of arbitrary shape** — works for non-spherical, non-convex clusters (unlike K-Means).
3. **Native outlier detection** — noise points are explicitly identified.
4. **Robust to outliers** — outliers do not distort the cluster structure.
5. **Deterministic** — for fixed parameters and distance metric, the result is the same across runs (unlike K-Means).

**Limitations (1 mark):**

1. **Parameter sensitivity** — results are highly sensitive to the choice of $\epsilon$ and MinPts.
2. **Varying density problem** — struggles when clusters have very different densities (one $\epsilon$ cannot fit all).
3. **Curse of dimensionality** — distance measures become less meaningful in high dimensions.
4. **Not entirely deterministic at borders** — border points within $\epsilon$ of multiple clusters may be assigned to either.
5. **Computational cost** — $O(n \log n)$ with spatial index, $O(n^2)$ naively.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Clustering Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing algorithm type vs. strategy:** Many students mix up "agglomerative" with "divisive" when writing. **Always state the strategy (bottom-up vs. top-down) explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "include itself" rule in DBSCAN:** When counting $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert$, **include the point $p$ itself.** A core point with MinPts = 5 needs only 4 other points within $\epsilon$, not 5.
>
> 3. **Confusing WCSS with SS:** WCSS = $\sum \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$ (sum of squared distances to centroid). The **SS** in BIRCH = $\sum \lVert x \rVert^2$ (sum of squared values, not distances). These are different quantities.
>
> 4. **Not stating convergence guarantee:** When writing about K-Means, **always mention**: "K-Means is **guaranteed to converge** in finite steps, but to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum." Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this line.
>
> 5. **Hierarchical clustering complexity:** Writing $O(n^2)$ for agglomerative is **partially correct** — but the **naive** complexity is $O(n^3)$, and the **optimized** is $O(n^2 \log n)$. Specify which.
>
> 6. **BIRCH's CF notation:** Always write CF = $(N, \vec{LS}, SS)$ with the vector arrow over LS to indicate it's a $d$-dimensional vector, not a scalar. $SS$ is a scalar.
>
> 7. **DBSCAN reachability types:** Distinguish **directly density-reachable** (one hop, requires core point) from **density-reachable** (multi-hop chain) from **density-connected** (common ancestor). Examiners love asking this.
>
> 8. **DBSCAN vs. K-Means on outliers:** K-Means **forces** every point into a cluster (no noise concept). DBSCAN has a **native noise** category. This is the single most important practical difference.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 📌 Core Definitions
- [ ] **Clustering:** Unsupervised grouping based on similarity.
- [ ] **K-Means:** Centroid-based, partitional, minimizes WCSS.
- [ ] **Hierarchical:** Tree-based (dendrogram), no pre-specified $k$.
- [ ] **Agglomerative:** Bottom-up merging (AGNES).
- [ ] **Divisive:** Top-down splitting (DIANA).
- [ ] **BIRCH:** Incremental, $O(n)$, CF tree.
- [ ] **DBSCAN:** Density-based, finds noise, arbitrary shapes.

### 📌 Critical Formulas
- [ ] K-Means objective: $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$
- [ ] K-Means centroid: $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$
- [ ] CF vector: $CF = (N, \vec{LS}, SS)$ — additive!
- [ ] DBSCAN core point: $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq$ MinPts
- [ ] Ward's linkage: $\Delta = \frac{n_a n_b}{n_a + n_b} \lVert \mu_a - \mu_b \rVert^2$

### 📌 Complexity Cheat Sheet
- [ ] K-Means: $O(nki)$ per iteration
- [ ] Agglomerative: $O(n^2 \log n)$ to $O(n^3)$
- [ ] Divisive: $O(2^n)$ — exponential
- [ ] BIRCH: $O(n)$ — best scalability
- [ ] DBSCAN: $O(n \log n)$ with index; $O(n^2)$ naive

### 📌 Algorithm Strengths at a Glance
- [ ] K-Means → **fast, simple, scalable**, but assumes spherical clusters
- [ ] Hierarchical → **no need for $k$, interpretable dendrogram**, but expensive
- [ ] BIRCH → **best for huge/streaming data**, $O(n)$ scaling
- [ ] DBSCAN → **finds outliers, arbitrary shapes**, but sensitive to $\epsilon$ + MinPts

### 📌 Decision Guide — "Which Algorithm to Use?"
| **Scenario** | **Recommended Algorithm** |
|---|---|
| Small dataset, want visual hierarchy | Agglomerative (with dendrogram) |
| Large dataset, need speed | BIRCH or K-Means |
| Data has noise/outliers | DBSCAN |
| Clusters are non-spherical | DBSCAN |
| Streaming/online data | BIRCH |
| Need fastest, simplest solution | K-Means |
| Unknown $k$ and no outliers expected | Agglomerative or DBSCAN |

### 📌 KTU 2024 Exam-Day Tips
1. **Always draw a diagram** for hierarchical clustering questions — a dendrogram earns you 1–2 free marks.
2. **State the objective function explicitly** in K-Means answers.
3. **Distinguish "convergence" from "optimality"** — they are NOT the same.
4. **Use proper notation:** $\lVert x - \mu_i \rVert$ for norm, $\vert C_i \vert$ for cardinality.
5. **Mention assumptions** of each algorithm (e.g., K-Means assumes spherical, equal-variance clusters).
6. **For DBSCAN numerical problems,** always show the **distance matrix** first.
7. **For BIRCH,** remember the **four phases**: Build → Condense → Global Cluster → Refine.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Clustering: K-Means, Hierarchical, BIRCH, DBSCAN**
> *Total Marks Coverage in KTU ESE: ~14 marks (1 full Part B question from this module, often appearing as choice A or B).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
