topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Processing - Boolean Retrieval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — TEXT PROCESSING: BOOLEAN RETRIEVAL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Boolean Retrieval** is a classical information retrieval (IR) model in which a query is formulated as a Boolean expression composed of *terms* combined using logical operators — **AND (∧)**, **OR (∨)**, **NOT (¬)** — and the system returns exactly those documents whose binary presence/absence pattern **exactly matches** the truth value of the expression.

It is a **set-theoretic retrieval model** (sometimes called the *exact-match model*) where documents are viewed as *sets of terms* and queries are logical predicates evaluated over those sets.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Analogy

Imagine you walk into a huge library containing **millions of books** (documents). The librarian gives you a **catalog** that lists, for *every single word*, exactly which books contain it.

- You say: *"I want books that contain the word **Data** AND **Analytics**, but NOT **Python**."*
- The librarian doesn't "read" the books. He simply flips through the catalog, intersects the lists of books containing "Data" and "Analytics," and removes any that also contain "Python."
- Whatever remains is handed to you — **exactly**, with no ranking, no scoring, no "best match."

That catalog is the **inverted index**, and the librarian's logic is **Boolean Retrieval**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Boolean retrieval is the foundation of modern search engines (Google, Bing, Elasticsearch). Even ranked systems preprocess documents by building Boolean inverted indexes as their first step.

### 1.2 Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol / Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Corpus** | $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$ | The full collection of $N$ documents |
| **Term / Token** | $t$ | A normalized word after preprocessing |
| **Vocabulary** | $V = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_M\}$ | The set of all unique terms (size $M$) |
| **Postings List** | $\text{posting}(t)$ | Sorted list of doc-IDs containing $t$ |
| **Term-Document Incidence Matrix** | $M \in \{0,1\}^{M \times N}$ | Binary matrix where $M[i,j]=1$ iff $t_i \in d_j$ |
| **Query** | $q$ | Boolean expression over terms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Note:** Boolean retrieval is *exact-match* and *unranked*. There is **no notion of relevance score** — a document is either returned or it is not. This is its biggest limitation and the reason modern systems extend it with **ranked retrieval (TF-IDF, BM25, vector space models).**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Backbone — Three Boolean Operators

A Boolean query is built from three primitive operators. Let $A$ and $B$ be the **sets of documents** that contain terms $t_a$ and $t_b$ respectively.

| Operator | Symbol | Set Operation | Boolean Logic | Example Query |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AND** | $\wedge$ | $A \cap B$ | $A \cdot B$ | `data ∧ analytics` |
| **OR** | $\vee$ | $A \cup B$ | $A + B - A \cdot B$ | `data ∨ analytics` |
| **NOT** | $\neg$ | $A \setminus B$ | $A \cdot \bar{B}$ | `data ∧ ¬python` |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Processing Pipeline (Why & How)

**Step 1 — Document Collection.** Gather all $N$ documents $D = \{d_1, \dots, d_N\}$ that the system will search over.

**Step 2 — Tokenization.** Split each document's text on whitespace and punctuation to obtain raw tokens.

**Step 3 — Normalization (Why?).** Convert tokens to lowercase, remove stopwords (`the`, `is`, `a`), and apply stemming (`running` → `run`). This reduces the vocabulary size $M$ and improves recall.

**Step 4 — Build the Term-Document Incidence Matrix $M$.** This is an $M \times N$ binary matrix:
$$M[i,j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if term } t_i \text{ appears in document } d_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Build the Inverted Index (How?).** For each term $t_i$, store the sorted list of doc-IDs where $M[i,j]=1$. This is the **postings list**.

**Step 6 — Query Parsing.** Parse the Boolean expression into a query tree (operator nodes with term leaves).

**Step 7 — Posting Operations.** Traverse the query tree:
- **AND** → intersect two sorted postings lists (merge-style algorithm).
- **OR** → union two sorted postings lists.
- **NOT** → complement against the universe $D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sorted postings?** Sorted doc-IDs allow $O(x+y)$ time merging using the **two-pointer linear merge** technique — a classic KTU expected derivation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Document Frequency** | $\text{df}(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} M[i,j]$ | Number of docs containing term $t$ |
| **Collection Frequency** | $\text{cf}(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \text{tf}(t, d_j)$ | Total occurrences of $t$ in corpus |
| **Incidence Vector Sum** | $\vec{v}_t \cdot \vec{v}_s = \sum_{j=1}^{N} M[i,j]\,M[k,j]$ | Equals $|D(t) \cap D(s)|$ — used for AND |
| **OR Count (Inclusion-Exclusion)** | $|A \cup B| = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$ | Sizes used in postings union |
| **AND Count** | $|A \cap B| = \vec{v}_a \cdot \vec{v}_b$ | Dot product of incidence vectors |
| **NOT Count** | $|\neg A| = N - \vert A \vert$ | Complement over universe $D$ |
| **Jaccard Similarity (set-based)** | $\text{Jacc}(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Used in extended Boolean models |
| **Query Latency (linear merge)** | $O(\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ | Cost of intersecting two postings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Quantities:** For Boolean retrieval, always express operations in **set notation** with **cardinalities**. The KTU board examiner expects set-theoretic justification, not just algorithmic intuition.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Legal e-Discovery:** Boolean retrieval is the gold standard — lawyers need **deterministic** recall, not probabilistic ranking.
- **Enterprise Search (Elasticsearch `query_string` query):** Industry-standard Boolean syntax (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, parentheses).
- **PubMed / Medical Literature Search:** Boolean queries are the primary interface for biomedical research databases.
- **Cybersecurity:** SIEM tools use Boolean logic over log events.
- **Foundation of Modern Search:** Google's first stage uses a Boolean inverted index; ranking is layered on top.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Building the Term-Document Incidence Matrix

**Setup:** Consider a toy corpus of **4 documents** after tokenization and stopword removal:

- $d_1$: *"data analytics python"*
- $d_2$: *"machine learning data"*
- $d_3$: *"analytics machine learning"*
- $d_4$: *"python data analytics"*

**Step 1 — Identify Vocabulary.**
$$V = \{\text{data}, \text{analytics}, \text{python}, \text{machine}, \text{learning}\}$$
So $M = 5$ terms and $N = 4$ documents.

**Step 2 — Compute Binary Matrix Entries.**
For each $M[i,j]$, we check presence:

- `data`: present in $d_1, d_2, d_4$ → not in $d_3$
- `analytics`: present in $d_1, d_3, d_4$ → not in $d_2$
- `python`: present in $d_1, d_4$ → not in $d_2, d_3$
- `machine`: present in $d_2, d_3$ → not in $d_1, d_4$
- `learning`: present in $d_2, d_3$ → not in $d_1, d_4$

**Step 3 — Construct $M$.**

| Term \ Doc | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **data** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **analytics** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **python** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **machine** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| **learning** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

### 3.2 Worked Example — Evaluating a Boolean Query Using Bitwise Logic

**Query:** $q = \text{analytics} \wedge \neg\text{python}$

**Step 1 — Extract incidence vectors.**
$$\vec{v}_{\text{analytics}} = (1, 0, 1, 1)$$
$$\vec{v}_{\text{python}} = (1, 0, 0, 1)$$

**Step 2 — Apply NOT to python vector (bitwise complement).**
$$\neg\vec{v}_{\text{python}} = (0, 1, 1, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Apply AND (bitwise product).**
$$\vec{v}_{\text{analytics}} \wedge \neg\vec{v}_{\text{python}} = (1 \cdot 0,\ 0 \cdot 1,\ 1 \cdot 1,\ 1 \cdot 0) = (0, 0, 1, 0)$$

**Step 4 — Interpret result.**
The vector $(0, 0, 1, 0)$ means only $d_3$ satisfies the query.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The examiner expects the computation to be shown in **vectorized bitwise form**, not via set intersection prose. Always show the bitwise operation as the *primary* derivation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Posting List Operations (Linear Merge)

**Query:** $q = \text{data} \wedge \text{analytics}$

**Postings (sorted):**
- $\text{posting}(\text{data}) = \{1, 2, 4\}$
- $\text{posting}(\text{analytics}) = \{1, 3, 4\}$

**Two-pointer merge:**

```
ptr1 → d1 = 1     ptr2 → d2 = 1    → MATCH → output {1}, advance both
ptr1 → d1 = 2     ptr2 → d2 = 3    → 2 < 3   → advance ptr1
ptr1 → d1 = 4     ptr2 → d2 = 3    → 4 > 3   → advance ptr2
ptr1 → d1 = 4     ptr2 → d2 = 4    → MATCH → output {4}, advance both
ptr1 exhausted → STOP
```

**Final answer:** $\{d_1, d_4\}$.

### 3.4 Set-Theoretic Cardinality Derivations

Suppose $|A| = 1500$, $|B| = 800$, and $|A \cap B| = 200$ in a corpus of $N = 10000$.

**Derivation of OR count (Inclusion-Exclusion):**
$$\begin{aligned}
|A \cup B| &= |A| + |B| - |A \cap B| \\
&= 1500 + 800 - 200 \\
&= 2100
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation of AND count (already given):**
$$|A \cap B| = 200$$

**Derivation of NOT count:**
$$|\neg A| = N - |A| = 10000 - 1500 = 8500$$

**Derivation of NOT-AND count:**
$$\begin{aligned}
|A \cap \neg B| &= |A| - |A \cap B| \\
&= 1500 - 200 \\
&= 1300
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This cardinality arithmetic is what an IR engine uses internally to estimate **query selectivity** and choose the optimal **query plan** (which subtree to evaluate first to minimize intermediate result size).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boolean Retrieval System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Document Corpus D"] --> B["Tokenization Module"]
    B --> C["Stopword Removal"]
    C --> D["Stemming and Normalization"]
    D --> E["Vocabulary Builder V"]
    D --> F["Inverted Index Builder"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Postings Lists on Disk"]
    H["User Boolean Query q"] --> I["Query Parser"]
    I --> J["Query Tree Builder"]
    J --> K["Postings Merge Engine"]
    G --> K
    K --> L["AND OR NOT Operators"]
    L --> M["Result Set of Doc IDs"]
    M --> N["Document Ranker Optional TF-IDF"]
    N --> O["Final Ranked Results to User"]
```

### 4.2 Query Tree Representation for a Compound Boolean Query

**Query:** $q = (data \wedge analytics) \vee \neg python$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OR root"] --> B["AND subtree"]
    A --> C["NOT subtree"]
    B --> D["term data"]
    B --> E["term analytics"]
    C --> F["term python"]
```

### 4.3 Inverted Index Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Document 1"] --> B["Tokenizer"]
    A2["Document 2"] --> B
    A3["Document N"] --> B
    B --> C["Sorted Term Pairs"]
    C --> D["Group by Term"]
    D --> E["Postings List 1"]
    D --> F["Postings List 2"]
    D --> G["Postings List M"]
    E --> H["Inverted Index on Disk"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

### 4.4 Bitwise Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Load postings for t1"] --> B["Load postings for t2"]
    B --> C{"Operator?"}
    C --> D["AND bitwise product"]
    C --> E["OR bitwise sum minus AND"]
    C --> F["NOT complement"]
    D --> G["Result vector"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Decode nonzero positions to doc IDs"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a block diagram instead of a physical sketch?** Boolean retrieval is a *logical/data-structure* concept, not a physical system. A functional block diagram conveys data flow and operator semantics more accurately than any mechanical drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define Boolean Retrieval. List its three primitive operators with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Boolean Retrieval is an information retrieval model in which documents are retrieved by exactly matching a Boolean expression built from terms and logical operators.
- **AND (∧):** `data ∧ analytics` — returns docs containing both.
- **OR (∨):** `data ∨ analytics` — returns docs containing either.
- **NOT (¬):** `data ∧ ¬python` — returns docs with `data` but without `python`.
*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for operators with examples.)*

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** What is an *inverted index*? Why is it preferred over a forward index for Boolean retrieval?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An inverted index is a data structure that maps each vocabulary term to the sorted list (postings list) of document IDs containing it. It is preferred over a forward index because:
1. Queries ask *"which docs contain term $t$?"* — the inverted index answers this in **$O(1)$ lookup + linear postings scan**.
2. A forward index would require scanning **every document** for every query term, costing $O(N)$.
*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the comparative advantage.)*

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Construct the **Term-Document Incidence Matrix** for the following corpus of 5 documents. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

- $d_1$: *"information retrieval system"*
- $d_2$: *"database and information"*
- $d_3$: *"retrieval system design"*
- $d_4$: *"information system database"*
- $d_5$: *"design and retrieval"*

**(b)** Using the matrix from part (a), evaluate the Boolean query:
$$q = \text{information} \wedge \neg\text{design}$$
Show your work using **bitwise vector operations**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step 1 — Vocabulary construction:**
$$V = \{\text{information}, \text{retrieval}, \text{system}, \text{database}, \text{design}, \text{and}\}$$
Six terms → $M = 6$ rows; $N = 5$ docs.

**Step 2 — Presence check per document:**
- `information`: $d_1, d_2, d_4$
- `retrieval`: $d_1, d_3, d_5$
- `system`: $d_1, d_3, d_4$
- `database`: $d_2, d_4$
- `design`: $d_3, d_5$
- `and`: $d_2, d_5$

**Step 3 — Construct matrix:**

| Term | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ | $d_5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| information | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| retrieval | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| system | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| database | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| design | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| and | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

*(7 marks: 2 for vocabulary, 3 for matrix entries, 2 for clean presentation.)*

**(b) Step 1 — Extract incidence vectors:**
$$\vec{v}_{\text{information}} = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$$
$$\vec{v}_{\text{design}} = (0, 0, 1, 0, 1)$$

**Step 2 — Apply NOT (bitwise complement):**
$$\neg\vec{v}_{\text{design}} = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Apply AND (bitwise product):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{v}_{\text{information}} \wedge \neg\vec{v}_{\text{design}} &= (1\cdot 1,\ 1\cdot 1,\ 0\cdot 0,\ 1\cdot 1,\ 0\cdot 0) \\
&= (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret result:**
Positions with 1 are $d_1, d_2, d_4$.

**Answer:** $\boxed{\{d_1, d_2, d_4\}}$

*(7 marks: 2 for vector extraction, 2 for NOT, 2 for AND, 1 for final answer.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Given a corpus of $N = 10000$ documents with the following statistics for terms $A$ and $B$:
- $|A| = 2000$ (docs containing $A$)
- $|B| = 1500$ (docs containing $B$)
- $|A \cap B| = 600$

Compute:
1. $|A \cup B|$ using Inclusion-Exclusion.
2. $|\neg A|$ and $|\neg B|$.
3. $|A \cap \neg B|$.
4. The **Jaccard similarity** $\text{Jacc}(A, B)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **two-pointer linear merge algorithm** for computing the AND of two postings lists. Trace it on:
- $\text{posting}(A) = \{2, 5, 8, 12, 17\}$
- $\text{posting}(B) = \{1, 5, 8, 14, 17, 20\}$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**
**1. Union via Inclusion-Exclusion:**
$$\begin{aligned}
|A \cup B| &= |A| + |B| - |A \cap B| \\
&= 2000 + 1500 - 600 \\
&= 2900
\end{aligned}$$

**2. Negations:**
$$|\neg A| = N - |A| = 10000 - 2000 = 8000$$
$$|\neg B| = N - |B| = 10000 - 1500 = 8500$$

**3. Difference:**
$$|A \cap \neg B| = |A| - |A \cap B| = 2000 - 600 = 1400$$

**4. Jaccard:**
$$\text{Jacc}(A,B) = \dfrac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|} = \dfrac{600}{2900} \approx 0.2069$$

*(7 marks: 1 each for sub-parts 1, 2, 3, 4 with proper formulas.)*

**(b)** **Algorithm:**

Maintain two pointers, $p$ and $q$, at the front of sorted postings $A$ and $B$. Repeatedly compare doc-IDs:
- If $A[p] = B[q]$: emit the ID and advance both pointers.
- If $A[p] < B[q]$: advance $p$.
- If $A[p] > B[q]$: advance $q$.
- Stop when either list is exhausted.

**Trace:**

| Step | $A[p]$ | $B[q]$ | Comparison | Action | Result So Far |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 1 | $2 > 1$ | advance $q$ | — |
| 2 | 2 | 5 | $2 < 5$ | advance $p$ | — |
| 3 | 5 | 5 | equal | emit 5, advance both | $\{5\}$ |
| 4 | 8 | 8 | equal | emit 8, advance both | $\{5, 8\}$ |
| 5 | 12 | 14 | $12 < 14$ | advance $p$ | $\{5, 8\}$ |
| 6 | 17 | 14 | $17 > 14$ | advance $q$ | $\{5, 8\}$ |
| 7 | 17 | 17 | equal | emit 17, advance both | $\{5, 8, 17\}$ |
| 8 | — | 20 | $A$ exhausted | STOP | $\{5, 8, 17\}$ |

**Final result:** $\text{posting}(A) \wedge \text{posting}(B) = \{5, 8, 17\}$

**Time complexity:** $O(|A| + |B|)$.

*(7 marks: 3 for algorithm explanation, 4 for the trace table and final answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply stopword removal before constructing the incidence matrix** → vocabulary mismatch → wrong answer.
> 2. **Confusing $\cap$ (AND) with $\cup$ (OR) during bitwise evaluation** → entire 7-mark sub-question can be marked zero.
> 3. **Not showing the vectorization step explicitly** → examiner deducts 1–2 marks for "skipping intermediate work."
> 4. **Writing AND as `+` instead of `·` in Boolean algebra** → convention error.
> 5. **Failing to sort postings before merging** → invalidates the two-pointer trace.
> 6. **Computing $|A \cap \neg B|$ as $|A| + |\neg B|$ instead of $|A| - |A \cap B|$** → set-theory error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Boolean Retrieval** = *exact-match, unranked* IR model based on set operations.
- Three operators: **AND ($\wedge$)**, **OR ($\vee$)**, **NOT ($\neg$)** — implementable via set intersection, union, complement.
- **Inverted Index** maps each term to a sorted postings list — the workhorse data structure.
- **Term-Document Incidence Matrix** $M$ is binary, size $M \times N$ — large but conceptually clear.
- **Bitwise vector operations** are the standard KTU evaluation method for queries.
- **Two-pointer linear merge** runs in $O(\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ time — must be shown via trace table.
- **Inclusion-Exclusion:** $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$ is essential.
- **Jaccard Similarity** $\dfrac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}$ measures overlap, range $[0,1]$.
- Limitations: no ranking, no partial match — motivates **TF-IDF / BM25 / Vector Space Models**.
- Real-world: PubMed, Elasticsearch `query_string`, legal e-discovery, SIEM tools.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example IR Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Example IR Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — The "IR Problem" in KTU Context

An **Information Retrieval (IR) Problem** is a computational task that aims to *find relevant documents* from a large, unstructured (or semi-structured) collection in response to a user's information need, expressed as a **query**. In the KTU 2024 Data Analytics syllabus (Module 4), the canonical "Example IR Problem" tests a student's ability to *hand-compute* the full retrieval pipeline: **Inverted Index → Term Frequency (tf) → Inverse Document Frequency (idf) → tf–idf Weighting → Vector-Space Similarity → Ranking & Evaluation (Precision/Recall).**

Mathematically, the retrieval problem is formulated as:

$$ \text{Rank}: \text{sim}(q, d_i) \;\; \text{for} \;\; i = 1, 2, \dots, N $$

where $q$ is the query, $d_i$ is the $i$-th document, $N$ is the total number of documents, and $\text{sim}$ is a similarity function (commonly **Cosine Similarity**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The module explicitly demands hands-on numerical evaluation of Boolean retrieval, tf–idf vectors, and cosine similarity. Memorising the *formulas* alone is insufficient — you must demonstrate **step-by-step numerical substitution** to score full marks.

## 1.2 The Working Corpus (Reference Example)

We will use this fixed corpus throughout the notes — a classic pedagogical example used in IR textbooks.

| Doc ID | Document Text |
|:------:|:--------------|
| $D_1$ | "shipment of gold damaged in a fire" |
| $D_2$ | "delivery of silver arrived in a silver truck" |
| $D_3$ | "shipment of gold arrived in a truck" |
| $D_4$ | "purchase of silver and gold arrived in a truck" |

**Query ($q$):** `"gold silver truck"`

Total documents: $N = 4$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Card-Catalogue"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a massive library with **10 lakh books** but **no catalogue**. A student walks in and asks, *"Do you have books on gold and silver shipments?"* The librarian, instead of scanning every book, checks a **single index card** labelled "gold" — which points to the shelf numbers: D1, D3, D4. This index is the **Inverted Index**. The librarian then *ranks* those books: "How strongly does each book talk about gold *and* silver *and* trucks?" — that ranking score is the **Cosine Similarity** between the book and the question.

In data-analytics terms:

- The **Inverted Index** is the data structure that makes retrieval $O(\text{posting list length})$ instead of $O(N)$.
- **tf–idf** decides *which words matter*: a word in *every* document is useless; a word in *one* document is highly discriminative.
- **Cosine Similarity** measures the *angle* between the query and document as vectors — the smaller the angle, the more relevant the document.

## 1.4 What "Relevance" Means Here

For grading purposes, a document is **relevant** to a query if a human annotator judges its content as addressing the user's information need. The system retrieves a *top-k* set; the **Precision** is the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant, and **Recall** is the fraction of *all* relevant documents that were retrieved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6-Stage IR Pipeline (Operational Logic)

1. **Tokenisation & Stop-word Removal** → split text into terms, drop common words (`of`, `in`, `a`, `the`).
2. **Inverted Index Construction** → for every unique term, store the list of documents it appears in.
3. **Term Frequency (tf) Computation** → count how many times a term appears in a document.
4. **Inverse Document Frequency (idf) Computation** → measure how *rare* (hence, discriminative) a term is across the corpus.
5. **tf–idf Weighting** → multiply tf × idf to obtain a per-(term, document) importance weight.
6. **Vector Construction & Cosine Similarity** → represent query and documents as tf–idf vectors; rank by cosine.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why an Inverted Index?** A linear scan of all documents is $O(N)$ per query. An inverted index maps a term directly to its postings, reducing the search to the size of the relevant posting lists.
- **Why tf alone is not enough?** The term *"the"* may appear 100 times in one document but is uninformative. We need **idf** to *down-weight* globally common terms.
- **Why Cosine and not Euclidean?** Document lengths vary. Cosine normalises for length: it measures the **directional alignment** of two vectors, not their magnitude.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | Term Frequency | $\text{tf}(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | Raw count of term $t$ in document $d$ |
| 2 | Document Frequency | $\text{df}(t) = \vert \{d : t \in d\} \vert$ | Number of documents containing $t$ |
| 3 | Inverse Document Frequency | $\text{idf}(t) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ | $N$ = total documents; uses $\log_{10}$ |
| 4 | tf–idf Weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$ | Per-term per-document importance |
| 5 | Vector Norm | $\Vert v \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i} v_i^2}$ | Euclidean (L2) norm |
| 6 | Dot Product | $q \cdot d = \sum_{i} q_i \, d_i$ | Sum of element-wise products |
| 7 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(q,d) = \dfrac{q \cdot d}{\Vert q \Vert \cdot \Vert d \Vert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$, typically $[0,1]$ for tf–idf |
| 8 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{\vert R_r \vert}{\vert R \vert}$ | $R_r$ = relevant retrieved, $R$ = retrieved |
| 9 | Recall | $R_c = \dfrac{\vert R_r \vert}{\vert \text{REL} \vert}$ | $\text{REL}$ = total relevant in corpus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power **search engines (Google, Bing)**, **legal e-discovery**, **e-commerce search (Amazon, Flipkart)**, **biomedical literature search (PubMed)**, and **enterprise knowledge management systems (SharePoint, Confluence)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Example

This section solves the **complete IR problem** for the corpus defined in §1.2.

## 3.1 Stage 1 — Tokenisation & Stop-Word Removal

We remove stop-words (`of`, `in`, `a`, `and`, `the`) and obtain the term sets per document:

| Document | Filtered Terms |
|:--------:|:--------------|
| $D_1$ | shipment, gold, damaged, fire |
| $D_2$ | delivery, silver, arrived, silver, truck |
| $D_3$ | shipment, gold, arrived, truck |
| $D_4$ | purchase, silver, gold, arrived, truck |

The unique vocabulary is:

$$ V = \{\text{arrived, damaged, delivery, fire, gold, purchase, shipment, silver, truck}\} $$

So $\vert V \vert = 9$ terms.

## 3.2 Stage 2 — Inverted Index Construction

For each term, we record the documents in which it appears:

| Term | Postings List (Document IDs) |
|:----:|:------------------------------|
| arrived | $\{D_2, D_3, D_4\}$ |
| damaged | $\{D_1\}$ |
| delivery | $\{D_2\}$ |
| fire | $\{D_1\}$ |
| gold | $\{D_1, D_3, D_4\}$ |
| purchase | $\{D_4\}$ |
| shipment | $\{D_1, D_3\}$ |
| silver | $\{D_2, D_4\}$ |
| truck | $\{D_2, D_3, D_4\}$ |

### 3.2.1 Boolean Query Processing

Query: `gold AND silver AND truck`

Using the postings lists, we compute the intersection:

$$ \text{gold} \cap \text{silver} = \{D_1, D_3, D_4\} \cap \{D_2, D_4\} = \{D_4\} $$

$$ \{D_4\} \cap \text{truck} = \{D_4\} \cap \{D_2, D_3, D_4\} = \{D_4\} $$

**Boolean result:** Only $D_4$ matches.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pro Tip:** When asked "process the Boolean query", you must show the *intermediate intersection* at every step. Skipping directly to the final answer costs marks.

## 3.3 Stage 3 — Term Frequency Matrix (tf)

We count occurrences of each term per document:

| Term \ Doc | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
|:----------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| arrived   | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| damaged   | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| delivery  | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| fire      | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| gold      | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| purchase  | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| shipment  | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| silver    | 0 | 2 | 0 | 1 |
| truck     | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

## 3.4 Stage 4 — Inverse Document Frequency (idf)

We compute $\text{idf}(t) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ for each term, with $N = 4$:

| Term | $\text{df}(t)$ | $\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | $\text{idf}(t)$ |
|:----:|:--------------:|:------------------------:|:---------------:|
| arrived | 3 | $4/3 = 1.333$ | $\log_{10}(1.333) = 0.1249$ |
| damaged | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $\log_{10}(4.000) = 0.6021$ |
| delivery | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $\log_{10}(4.000) = 0.6021$ |
| fire | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $\log_{10}(4.000) = 0.6021$ |
| gold | 3 | $4/3 = 1.333$ | $\log_{10}(1.333) = 0.1249$ |
| purchase | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $\log_{10}(4.000) = 0.6021$ |
| shipment | 2 | $4/2 = 2.000$ | $\log_{10}(2.000) = 0.3010$ |
| silver | 2 | $4/2 = 2.000$ | $\log_{10}(2.000) = 0.3010$ |
| truck | 3 | $4/3 = 1.333$ | $\log_{10}(1.333) = 0.1249$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Terms appearing in *all* documents (or many) get a *low* idf; terms in *one* document get a *high* idf of $0.6021$.

## 3.5 Stage 5 — tf–idf Weight Matrix

Now compute $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \times \text{idf}(t)$:

| Term \ Doc | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
|:----------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| arrived   | 0       | $1 \times 0.1249 = 0.1249$ | 0.1249 | 0.1249 |
| damaged   | 0.6021  | 0       | 0       | 0       |
| delivery  | 0       | 0.6021  | 0       | 0       |
| fire      | 0.6021  | 0       | 0       | 0       |
| gold      | 0.1249  | 0       | 0.1249  | 0.1249  |
| purchase  | 0       | 0       | 0       | 0.6021  |
| shipment  | 0.3010  | 0       | 0.3010  | 0       |
| silver    | 0       | $2 \times 0.3010 = 0.6020$ | 0       | 0.3010  |
| truck     | 0       | 0.1249  | 0.1249  | 0.1249  |

## 3.6 Stage 6 — Query Vector

The query is `gold silver truck`. Term frequencies in $q$ are all 1, so the query tf–idf vector (across the 9-term vocabulary) is:

$$ \vec{q} = (0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.1249,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.3010,\ 0.1249) $$

(in the order: arrived, damaged, delivery, fire, gold, purchase, shipment, silver, truck).

## 3.7 Stage 7 — Document Norms $\Vert d_i \Vert$

Each document's norm is computed as the square root of the sum of squared tf–idf weights.

**For $D_1$:**

$$ \Vert D_1 \Vert = \sqrt{0.6021^2 + 0.6021^2 + 0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.3625 + 0.3625 + 0.0156 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.8312} = 0.9117 $$

**For $D_2$:**

$$ \Vert D_2 \Vert = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.6021^2 + 0.6020^2 + 0.1249^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.3625 + 0.3624 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.7561} = 0.8695 $$

**For $D_3$:**

$$ \Vert D_3 \Vert = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.1249^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.1374} = 0.3707 $$

**For $D_4$:**

$$ \Vert D_4 \Vert = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.1249^2 + 0.6021^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.1249^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0.3625 + 0.0906 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.4999} = 0.7070 $$

## 3.8 Stage 8 — Query Norm $\Vert q \Vert$

Only the query terms `gold`, `silver`, `truck` have non-zero components:

$$ \Vert q \Vert = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.1249^2} $$

$$ = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.1218} = 0.3490 $$

## 3.9 Stage 9 — Dot Products $q \cdot d_i$

**$q \cdot D_1$:** Only `gold` overlaps.

$$ q \cdot D_1 = 0.1249 \times 0.1249 = 0.0156 $$

**$q \cdot D_2$:** `silver` and `truck` overlap.

$$ q \cdot D_2 = (0.3010 \times 0.6020) + (0.1249 \times 0.1249) = 0.1812 + 0.0156 = 0.1968 $$

**$q \cdot D_3$:** `gold` and `truck` overlap.

$$ q \cdot D_3 = (0.1249 \times 0.1249) + (0.1249 \times 0.1249) = 0.0156 + 0.0156 = 0.0312 $$

**$q \cdot D_4$:** `gold`, `silver`, `truck` all overlap.

$$ q \cdot D_4 = (0.1249 \times 0.1249) + (0.3010 \times 0.3010) + (0.1249 \times 0.1249) = 0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.0156 = 0.1218 $$

## 3.10 Stage 10 — Cosine Similarity & Final Ranking

$$ \cos(q, D_1) = \frac{0.0156}{0.3490 \times 0.9117} = \frac{0.0156}{0.3182} = 0.0490 $$

$$ \cos(q, D_2) = \frac{0.1968}{0.3490 \times 0.8695} = \frac{0.1968}{0.3035} = 0.6484 $$

$$ \cos(q, D_3) = \frac{0.0312}{0.3490 \times 0.3707} = \frac{0.0312}{0.1294} = 0.2411 $$

$$ \cos(q, D_4) = \frac{0.1218}{0.3490 \times 0.7070} = \frac{0.1218}{0.2467} = 0.4937 $$

### 3.10.1 Final Ranked List

| Rank | Document | Cosine Score |
|:----:|:--------:|:------------:|
| 1 | $D_2$ | **0.6484** |
| 2 | $D_4$ | **0.4937** |
| 3 | $D_3$ | **0.2411** |
| 4 | $D_1$ | **0.0490** |

## 3.11 Stage 11 — Precision & Recall

Suppose the **ground-truth relevant set** (human-annotated) is $\text{REL} = \{D_2, D_3, D_4\}$.

If the system retrieves the **top-2** documents $\{D_2, D_4\}$:

- **Retrieved (R):** $\{D_2, D_4\}$ → $\vert R \vert = 2$
- **Relevant Retrieved ($R_r$):** $\{D_2, D_4\}$ (both are in REL) → $\vert R_r \vert = 2$

$$ P = \frac{\vert R_r \vert}{\vert R \vert} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.000 $$

$$ R_c = \frac{\vert R_r \vert}{\vert \text{REL} \vert} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.667 $$

If the system retrieves the **top-3** documents $\{D_2, D_4, D_3\}$:

- $\vert R \vert = 3$, $\vert R_r \vert = 3$

$$ P = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000, \quad R_c = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000 $$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The IR Retrieval Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    corpusA[Corpus D1 D2 D3 D4] --> tokA[Tokeniser and Stopword Removal]
    tokA --> invA[Inverted Index Builder]
    invA --> tfA[Term Frequency Counter]
    tfA --> idfA[IDF Calculator log10 N by df]
    idfA --> wtA[tf-idf Weight Matrix]
    
    queryA[User Query gold silver truck] --> qtfA[Query TF Vector]
    qtfA --> qwtA[Query tf-idf Vector]
    
    wtA --> normA[Document Norms L2]
    qwtA --> qnormA[Query Norm L2]
    
    normA --> cosA[Cosine Similarity Engine]
    qnormA --> cosA
    wtA --> cosA
    
    cosA --> rankA[Ranking Module]
    rankA --> outA[Top-k Ranked Documents]
    outA --> evalA[Precision and Recall Evaluator]
    relA[Ground Truth Relevant Set] --> evalA
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Inverted Index as an Adjacency Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    termG[Term: gold] --> postG[Postings: D1, D3, D4]
    termS[Term: silver] --> postS[Postings: D2, D4]
    termT[Term: truck] --> postT[Postings: D2, D3, D4]
    termA[Term: arrived] --> postA[Postings: D2, D3, D4]
    termSH[Term: shipment] --> postSH[Postings: D1, D3]
    termD[Term: damaged] --> postD[Postings: D1]
    termF[Term: fire] --> postF[Postings: D1]
    termDE[Term: delivery] --> postDE[Postings: D2]
    termP[Term: purchase] --> postP[Postings: D4]
```

## 4.3 Flow Diagram — Cosine Similarity Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    qVec[Query Vector q] --> dotA[Compute dot product q times d]
    dVec[Document Vector d] --> dotA
    qVec --> qNormS[Compute query norm]
    dVec --> dNormS[Compute document norm]
    qNormS --> divA[Compute cosine = dot divided by qnorm times dnorm]
    dNormS --> divA
    dotA --> divA
    divA --> scoreA[Output similarity score]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Inverted Index. Why is it preferred over a forward index in large-scale IR systems?**

**Model Answer:**

> An **Inverted Index** is a data structure that maps each unique term in the vocabulary to the list (posting list) of documents in which it appears. Formally, for vocabulary $V$:
>
> $$\text{Index} : V \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(\{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_N\})$$
>
> where $\mathcal{P}$ is the power set (i.e., list of document IDs).
>
> A **Forward Index**, in contrast, maps *each document* to the list of terms it contains: $D_i \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(V)$.
>
> In a large-scale IR system, a query typically contains only a *few* terms. With a forward index, the system would have to scan *all* $N$ documents. With an inverted index, the system accesses only the posting lists of the *query terms*, giving complexity $O(\sum_{t \in q} \vert \text{Postings}(t) \vert)$ instead of $O(N)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the tf–idf weighting scheme. Why is the logarithm used in the idf formula?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **tf–idf weight** of a term $t$ in document $d$ is the product of its term frequency and its inverse document frequency:
>
> $$w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$$
>
> - **tf** captures the *local* importance of $t$ within $d$ (more occurrences → more relevant).
> - **idf** captures the *global* rarity of $t$ across the corpus (rarer terms → more discriminative).
>
> The **logarithm** is used to **dampen** the effect of idf. Without the log, a term appearing in 1 out of $10^6$ documents would have idf $= 10^6$, dwarfing the tf component. The log compresses this range, ensuring that both tf and idf contribute on comparable scales. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Loss #1:** Forgetting to **drop stop-words** before computing tf–idf (lose up to 2 marks).
> - **Common Loss #2:** Using **natural log ($\ln$)** instead of $\log_{10}$ (or vice versa) without stating your choice — KTU expects you to declare the base, and *most* model answers use $\log_{10}$. Pick one and **state it explicitly**.
> - **Common Loss #3:** Skipping the **query vector construction** and jumping straight to cosine — examiners expect a full 6-stage pipeline.
> - **Common Loss #4:** Forgetting to **square the components** inside the norm — i.e., writing $\Vert d \Vert = \sum w_i$ instead of $\sqrt{\sum w_i^2}$.
> - **Common Loss #5:** Rounding cosine to *more than 4 decimal places* — a common source of arithmetic errors. Use 4-decimal precision.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[Full IR Pipeline]*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Consider the following document collection:

- **$D_1$:** *"data mining extracts patterns from data"*
- **$D_2$:** *"machine learning enables predictive analytics"*
- **$D_3$:** *"data analytics uses statistics and machine learning"*
- **$D_4$:** *"pattern mining is a part of data mining"*

**Query ($q$):** `"data mining machine learning"`

**(a)** Construct the **Inverted Index** for the corpus (after stop-word removal). Process the Boolean query `data AND mining AND machine` using the inverted index. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **tf–idf weight matrix** (using $\log_{10}$). Represent the query as a tf–idf vector. Compute the **cosine similarity** of the query with each document and rank them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Inverted Index & Boolean Query Processing**

**Step 1: Stop-word removal.** Removing `from`, `is`, `a`, `of`, `the`, `and` — but note `and` appears in $D_3$ ("statistics and machine learning"). Removing it:

| Document | Filtered Terms |
|:--------:|:---------------|
| $D_1$ | data, mining, extracts, patterns, data |
| $D_2$ | machine, learning, enables, predictive, analytics |
| $D_3$ | data, analytics, statistics, machine, learning |
| $D_4$ | pattern, mining, data, mining |

**Step 2: Inverted Index construction.**

| Term | Postings |
|:----:|:---------|
| analytics | $\{D_2, D_3\}$ |
| data | $\{D_1, D_3, D_4\}$ |
| enables | $\{D_2\}$ |
| extracts | $\{D_1\}$ |
| learning | $\{D_2, D_3\}$ |
| machine | $\{D_2, D_3\}$ |
| mining | $\{D_1, D_4\}$ |
| patterns | $\{D_1\}$ |
| pattern | $\{D_4\}$ |
| predictive | $\{D_2\}$ |
| statistics | $\{D_3\}$ |

**Step 3: Boolean query `data AND mining AND machine`.**

$$ \text{data} \cap \text{mining} = \{D_1, D_3, D_4\} \cap \{D_1, D_4\} = \{D_1, D_4\} $$

$$ \{D_1, D_4\} \cap \text{machine} = \{D_1, D_4\} \cap \{D_2, D_3\} = \varnothing $$

**Boolean result:** Empty set — *no document* contains all three terms. **(7 marks: 2 for index, 3 for intersection steps, 2 for conclusion)**

---

**Part (b) — tf–idf, Query Vector, Cosine Similarity**

**Step 1: Term frequencies** (treating `pattern` and `patterns` as the same stem — `pattern`):

| Term \ Doc | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
|:----------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| analytics  | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| data       | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| enables    | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| extracts   | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| learning   | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| machine    | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| mining     | 1 | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| pattern    | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| predictive | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| statistics | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 2: idf** ($N=4$):

| Term | df | idf = $\log_{10}(4/\text{df})$ |
|:----:|:--:|:------------------------------:|
| analytics  | 2 | 0.3010 |
| data       | 3 | 0.1249 |
| enables    | 1 | 0.6021 |
| extracts   | 1 | 0.6021 |
| learning   | 2 | 0.3010 |
| machine    | 2 | 0.3010 |
| mining     | 2 | 0.3010 |
| pattern    | 2 | 0.3010 |
| predictive | 1 | 0.6021 |
| statistics | 1 | 0.6021 |

**Step 3: tf–idf weights** (only non-zero):

| Term \ Doc | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
|:----------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| analytics  | 0       | 0.3010  | 0.3010  | 0       |
| data       | 0.2498  | 0       | 0.1249  | 0.1249  |
| learning   | 0       | 0.3010  | 0.3010  | 0       |
| machine    | 0       | 0.3010  | 0.3010  | 0       |
| mining     | 0.3010  | 0       | 0       | 0.6020  |

**Step 4: Query vector** (`data`, `mining`, `machine` — all tf=1):

$$ \vec{q} = (0,\ 0.1249,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.3010,\ 0.3010,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0) $$

$$ \Vert q \Vert = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2} = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.1968} = 0.4436 $$

**Step 5: Cosine scores:**

$$ \cos(q, D_1) = \frac{0.0156 + 0.0906}{0.4436 \cdot \sqrt{0.0624 + 0.0906 + 0.0156 + 0.0906}} = \frac{0.1062}{0.4436 \times 0.5115} = \frac{0.1062}{0.2269} = 0.4681 $$

(Detail: $\Vert D_1 \Vert = \sqrt{0.2498^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.6021^2 + 0.3010^2} = \sqrt{0.0624 + 0.0906 + 0.3625 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.6061} = 0.7785$)

$$ \cos(q, D_2) = \frac{0.3010 \times 0.3010}{0.4436 \times \sqrt{0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2}} = \frac{0.0906}{0.4436 \times 0.6020} = \frac{0.0906}{0.2670} = 0.3393 $$

$$ \cos(q, D_3) = \frac{0.1249 \times 0.1249 + 0.3010 \times 0.3010 + 0.3010 \times 0.3010}{0.4436 \times \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2}} $$

$$ = \frac{0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.0906}{0.4436 \times \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.0906 + 0.0906 + 0.0906}} = \frac{0.1968}{0.4436 \times \sqrt{0.3780}} = \frac{0.1968}{0.4436 \times 0.6148} = \frac{0.1968}{0.2727} = 0.7217 $$

$$ \cos(q, D_4) = \frac{0.1249 \times 0.1249 + 0.3010 \times 0.6020}{0.4436 \times \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.6020^2 + 0.3010^2}} $$

$$ = \frac{0.0156 + 0.1812}{0.4436 \times \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.3624 + 0.0906}} = \frac{0.1968}{0.4436 \times 0.6847} = \frac{0.1968}{0.3037} = 0.6480 $$

**Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Doc | Cosine |
|:----:|:---:|:------:|
| 1 | $D_3$ | **0.7217** |
| 2 | $D_4$ | **0.6480** |
| 3 | $D_1$ | **0.4681** |
| 4 | $D_2$ | **0.3393** |

**(7 marks: 2 for tf–idf, 2 for query vector, 2 for cosine, 1 for ranking)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[Alternative — Evaluation Focus]*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

For the same corpus as Question A, suppose the **ground-truth relevant set** is $\text{REL} = \{D_3, D_4\}$ (i.e., the user considers only $D_3$ and $D_4$ to be truly relevant to the query `data mining machine learning`).

**(a)** Compute **Precision** and **Recall** if the system retrieves the **top-1** document based on cosine ranking. Then, redo the calculation for the **top-2** and **top-3** retrieved documents. Comment on the **Precision–Recall trade-off**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why **Cosine Similarity** is preferred over **Euclidean Distance** in the Vector Space Model. Derive the formula relating the two. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Precision, Recall & Trade-off**

Using the cosine ranking from Question A: $D_3 > D_4 > D_1 > D_2$.

**Top-1 retrieval: $\{D_3\}$**

- $\vert R \vert = 1$, $\vert R_r \vert = 1$ (since $D_3 \in \text{REL}$)

$$ P_1 = \frac{1}{1} = 1.000, \quad R_1 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500 $$

**Top-2 retrieval: $\{D_3, D_4\}$**

- $\vert R \vert = 2$, $\vert R_r \vert = 2$

$$ P_2 = \frac{2}{2} = 1.000, \quad R_2 = \frac{2}{2} = 1.000 $$

**Top-3 retrieval: $\{D_3, D_4, D_1\}$**

- $\vert R \vert = 3$, $\vert R_r \vert = 2$ (since $D_1 \notin \text{REL}$)

$$ P_3 = \frac{2}{3} = 0.667, \quad R_3 = \frac{2}{2} = 1.000 $$

| Top-k | Precision | Recall |
|:-----:|:---------:|:------:|
| 1 | 1.000 | 0.500 |
| 2 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| 3 | 0.667 | 1.000 |

**Comment on Trade-off:** As $k$ increases, recall monotonically *increases* (or stays the same) because we retrieve more documents from the relevant set. Precision, however, may *decrease* when additional retrieved documents are *irrelevant*. The **Precision–Recall curve** captures this trade-off, and the **F1-score** provides a single summary metric. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Cosine vs Euclidean**

For two vectors $q$ and $d$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$:

$$ \text{Euclidean distance: } \; \delta_E(q, d) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (q_i - d_i)^2} = \Vert q - d \Vert $$

$$ \text{Cosine similarity: } \; \cos(q, d) = \frac{q \cdot d}{\Vert q \Vert \cdot \Vert d \Vert} $$

**Derivation of the relationship.** The squared Euclidean distance expands as:

$$ \delta_E^2(q, d) = \Vert q \Vert^2 + \Vert d \Vert^2 - 2 \, q \cdot d $$

Rearranging the cosine formula:

$$ q \cdot d = \cos(q, d) \cdot \Vert q \Vert \cdot \Vert d \Vert $$

Substituting:

$$ \delta_E^2(q, d) = \Vert q \Vert^2 + \Vert d \Vert^2 - 2 \cos(q, d) \cdot \Vert q \Vert \cdot \Vert d \Vert $$

**Why cosine is preferred:** Document vectors in IR often have *different magnitudes* because documents have different lengths (more words → larger norm). Euclidean distance is biased toward *long* documents because the term-by-term differences add up. Cosine normalises by the magnitudes, so it measures *directional alignment* — i.e., *how similarly the documents distribute their topics* — independent of length. **(7 marks: 2 for formulas, 3 for derivation, 2 for justification)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Example IR Problem**

- **Inverted Index** maps *term → list of documents*; used to make Boolean queries fast.
- **Boolean AND query** = *intersection* of postings lists; **OR query** = *union*; **NOT** = *set difference*.
- **tf (term frequency)** is the *raw count* of a term in a document; gives local importance.
- **idf (inverse document frequency)** is $\log_{10}(N / \text{df}(t))$; gives *global rarity* — rare terms score high.
- **tf–idf weight** $= \text{tf} \times \text{idf}$; combines local and global importance.
- **Stop-words** (of, in, a, the, and) are *removed* before computing tf–idf — always.
- **Vector Space Model (VSM)** represents documents and queries as tf–idf vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$.
- **Cosine Similarity** $\cos(q,d) = \dfrac{q \cdot d}{\Vert q \Vert \cdot \Vert d \Vert}$ — measures angle, not magnitude.
- **Cosine range** is $[0, 1]$ for non-negative tf–idf; closer to 1 = more similar.
- **Precision** $= \vert R_r \vert / \vert R \vert$; **Recall** $= \vert R_r \vert / \vert \text{REL} \vert$.
- **F1-score** $= 2 P R / (P + R)$ — harmonic mean of precision and recall.
- **Log base**: KTU expects $\log_{10}$ unless explicitly stated; always declare your base.
- **Stemming** (pattern ≈ patterns, mining ≈ mined) is often applied — confirm whether your exam corpus requires it.
- **Arithmetic precision**: Carry at least **4 decimal places** to avoid cumulative rounding errors.
- **Always** show the full pipeline: tokenise → index → tf → idf → tf–idf → vector → cosine → rank.
- **Common ranking tie-breaker**: If two documents have the *same* cosine score, break ties by document ID (lower ID first, by convention).
