# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Boolean query processing. List the three primary Boolean operators and state their set-theoretic meanings.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Boolean query processing is the Information Retrieval paradigm in which user queries are expressed as Boolean expressions combining terms with logical operators `AND`, `OR`, and `NOT`, and the retrieval system returns documents whose term-presence pattern satisfies the truth value of the expression.

The three operators and their set-theoretic meanings (assume postings lists $A$ and $B$ from universe $D$):
- `A AND B` computes $A \cap B$ — *intersection* — documents containing **both** terms.
- `A OR B` computes $A \cup B$ — *union* — documents containing **either** term.
- `A NOT B` computes $A \setminus B$ — *set difference* — documents in $A$ but **not** in $B$ (with complement `NOT B` being $D \setminus B$).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for operator set meanings.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is an inverted index? Why is it preferred over the term-document incidence matrix for query processing in large collections?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **inverted index** is a data structure that maps each unique term in a document collection's vocabulary to a sorted list (postings list) of document identifiers where the term occurs, along with auxiliary metadata such as term frequency and positional information. *(1 mark)*

It is preferred over the term-document incidence matrix for two reasons:
- **Space efficiency:** The matrix has dimensions $M \times N$ (vocabulary size $\times$ document count) and is overwhelmingly sparse, with typically $>99.9\%$ zeros. The inverted index stores only the 1s, reducing memory by orders of magnitude. *(1 mark)*
- **Speed of retrieval:** Boolean set operations reduce to fast linear merges over sorted postings lists (e.g., $O(\vert p_1 \vert + \vert p_2 \vert)$ for AND), rather than scanning the entire sparse matrix row-by-row. *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), Module-level questions of 14 marks offer an **internal choice** — you may attempt either OR both, with the better score counted. The structure below matches this pattern: two alternative 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the architecture of an inverted index with a suitable example. Demonstrate the construction of postings lists for a small document collection and discuss the storage trade-offs between the inverted index and the term-document incidence matrix.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** For the same document collection, write the step-by-step processing of the Boolean query `neural AND network AND NOT biology` using the merge algorithm. Show each iteration, the pointers, and the final result set.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the inverted index components:** The index consists of two parts — the **dictionary** (a hash or B-tree mapping each term to metadata and a pointer to its postings) and the **postings file** (a concatenation of postings lists, often stored as compressed runs of DocIDs). *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Use the toy collection from §3.1:** Four documents $d_1$ to $d_4$, vocabulary of 10 terms. Build the incidence matrix $M$ as shown in §3.1. The inverted index lists the column indices of 1s in each row, sorted ascending. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Storage trade-off analysis:** The incidence matrix requires $M \times N$ bits = $10 \times 4 = 40$ bits. The inverted index requires 12 DocIDs encoded at $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ bits each = 24 bits, plus dictionary overhead (term strings and pointers). At small scale, the matrix is competitive; at scale ($M \approx 10^6$, $N \approx 10^6$), the matrix is 125 GB versus the index's $\sim 5$ GB. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Real-world storage techniques:** Mention **gap encoding** (store differences between consecutive DocIDs as smaller integers), **variable-byte encoding**, and **gamma coding** to further compress the postings. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Position information:** Optionally, each posting can store a list of term positions within the document to support phrase queries — increasing storage by a factor of 2–5. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Use the inverted index from §3.1. The query has three operands: `neural` (AND), `network` (AND), `NOT biology`.

**Postings lists:**
- $p_{\text{neural}} = [1, 2]$
- $p_{\text{network}} = [1, 2, 3]$
- $p_{\text{biology}} = [2, 4]$

**Step 1 — Sub-query 1: `neural AND network`.** Apply `Intersect` algorithm. *2 marks for the merge trace.*

| Iteration | Pointer neural (i) | Pointer network (j) | Action |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|--------|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Equal — emit 1, advance both |
| 2 | 2 | 2 | Equal — emit 2, advance both |
| 3 | end | 3 | End — terminate |

Intermediate result: $R_1 = [1, 2]$. *1 mark*

**Step 2 — Sub-query 2: `NOT biology`.** Compute complement: $D \setminus [2, 4] = [1, 3]$. *1 mark*

**Step 3 — Combine: `R1 AND NOT biology`.** Intersect $[1,2]$ with $[1,3]$: emit 1 when $1=1$, advance; then $2 \neq 3$, advance 3; $2$ vs end — terminate. *1 mark*

**Final result:** $R = [1]$. Only document $d_1$ satisfies the query. *1 mark*

**Complexity:** Two intersections each $O(L_1 + L_2)$; total $\sim 7$ comparisons. *1 mark*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Discuss the limitations of naive Boolean query processing. Explain how skip pointers optimize the merge operation. Provide the complexity analysis before and after skip pointer optimization.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Suppose you are processing the query `A AND B AND C` where the postings list lengths are $L_A = 100$, $L_B = 10{,}000$, and $L_C = 1{,}000{,}000$. Determine the optimal term ordering and compute the total number of comparisons under both orderings (shortest-first and longest-first).

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Limitations of naive processing:** *(2 marks)*
- The two-pointer merge is $O(L_1 + L_2)$; when lists are very different in length, most comparisons waste effort advancing the long list past irrelevant values.
- Processing `NOT` requires $O(N)$ work, which dominates for low-frequency terms.
- Multi-term AND with sub-optimal term order causes intermediate results to explode.
- No notion of partial relevance — a doc with 100 occurrences of a term is ranked equal to one with 1 occurrence.

**Step 2 — Skip pointer idea:** *(2 marks)* Insert auxiliary pointers every $\sqrt{L}$ positions in a postings list, each pointing forward to a later DocID. During the merge, before performing a linear scan, the algorithm checks if the current skip pointer's target is $\leq$ the opposing DocID; if so, it jumps, skipping irrelevant elements.

**Step 3 — Complexity comparison:** *(2 marks)*

Naive AND: $O(L_1 + L_2)$. With skip spacing $s = \sqrt{L}$:
- Worst-case comparisons reduce to $O(\sqrt{L_1} + \sqrt{L_2})$ per merge.

**Step 4 — Space-time trade-off:** *(1 mark)* Skip pointers add $\sqrt{L}$ extra pointers per list, a $\sim \sqrt{L}/L = 1/\sqrt{L}$ fractional overhead, which is negligible for long lists.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the lengths:**
- $L_A = 100$, $L_B = 10{,}000$, $L_C = 1{,}000{,}000$.

**Step 2 — Optimal ordering (shortest first):** Process $A$ first, then $B$, then $C$. *1 mark*

Cost trace:
- Merge $A$ and $B$: result $R_1$ has $\leq \min(L_A, L_B) = 100$ elements. Cost: $L_A + L_B = 100 + 10{,}000 = 10{,}100$ comparisons. *1 mark*
- Merge $R_1$ and $C$: cost $L_{R_1} + L_C \leq 100 + 1{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}000{,}100$ comparisons. *1 mark*
- **Total shortest-first cost:** $\approx 1{,}010{,}200$ comparisons. *1 mark*

**Step 3 — Sub-optimal ordering (longest first):** Process $C$, then $B$, then $A$. *1 mark*

Cost trace:
- Merge $C$ and $B$: result $R_1' \leq 10{,}000$. Cost: $1{,}000{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 1{,}010{,}000$. *1 mark*
- Merge $R_1'$ and $A$: cost $\leq 10{,}000 + 100 = 10{,}100$. *1 mark*
- **Total longest-first cost:** $\approx 1{,}020{,}100$ comparisons. *1 mark*

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Both orderings yield the same asymptotic total of $\sim 10^6$ (dominated by $L_C$), but the shortest-first ordering is *always* at least as good and strictly better when result sizes shrink faster. The lesson: **process the most selective (lowest document frequency) terms first.** *1 mark*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort the postings lists before merging.** If lists are unsorted, the merge algorithm is incorrect — you must assume sortedness (which is the *defining property* of an inverted index).
> 2. **Confusing document frequency (df) with collection frequency (cf).** `df` = number of documents containing the term; `cf` = total term occurrences. For Boolean processing, **df** governs list length and merge cost.
> 3. **Treating `NOT` as a cheap operation.** Students often assume `NOT p` is $O(1)$. In reality, computing $D \setminus p$ requires iterating over all $N$ DocIDs in the worst case.
> 4. **Ignoring term ordering in multi-term AND.** Processing terms in arbitrary order can lead to $5\times$–$10\times$ slowdowns; this is a frequent sub-question in KTU papers.
> 5. **Mixing up skip pointer space overhead.** Students sometimes say skip pointers "halve" or "double" the index size; correct framing is "$\sim \sqrt{L}$ extra pointers per list, negligible for large $L$."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Processing Boolean Queries**

- **Boolean retrieval** evaluates queries as propositional-logic expressions over postings lists and returns the *exact* set of documents satisfying the expression. **No partial relevance, no ranking.**

- The **inverted index** maps each term $t$ to a sorted postings list of DocIDs; it is the operational data structure for Boolean processing.

- The **term-document incidence matrix** $M \in \{0,1\}^{M \times N}$ is the *logical* representation. Inverted indexing is the *sparse storage* of this matrix that exploits the high sparsity of real text corpora.

- The **AND merge** uses a two-pointer algorithm: emit on match, advance the smaller pointer. Complexity $O(L_1 + L_2)$.

- The **OR merge** is structurally identical but emits *both* values, deduplicating on ties.

- The **NOT operation** returns $D \setminus p$ and has $O(N)$ cost when the complement is large.

- **Skip pointers** place auxiliary jump pointers at intervals of $\sqrt{L}$ in each postings list, reducing worst-case merge cost from $O(L_1+L_2)$ to $O(\sqrt{L_1}+\sqrt{L_2})$.

- **Optimal term ordering for multi-term AND:** always process the postings list with the **smallest document frequency first**, because intermediate result size is bounded by $\min$ of input sizes.

- The **document frequency $\text{df}(t)$** — not collection frequency $\text{cf}(t)$ — is the quantity that determines postings list length and hence merge cost.

- **Compressed inverted indexes** use gap encoding and variable-byte / gamma coding to shrink storage; the postings file is the dominant component.

- Boolean retrieval is **conceptually clean and computationally efficient** but **not user-friendly** — novices struggle to construct effective Boolean expressions, which is the principal motivation for **ranked retrieval** (TF-IDF, BM25, Vector Space Model), covered in subsequent topics.

- **Engineering context:** Boolean processing is still in active use in legal e-discovery, enterprise search, and log analytics. Modern systems often expose a Boolean "power user" mode alongside the default ranking interface.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tokenization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tokenization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Tokenization** is the foundational pre-processing step in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Text Analytics that decomposes a continuous stream of textual data into smaller, discrete, and linguistically meaningful units called **tokens**. These tokens — which may be words, sub-words, characters, or sentences — serve as the atomic input units for all downstream machine learning, deep learning, and statistical models.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST523 – Data Analytics)**, tokenization is treated as a **deterministic (or probabilistic) segmentation function** that maps an unstructured character stream to a structured indexable vocabulary. Formally, it is defined as:

$$T: \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{V}^{*}$$

where $\Sigma$ is the alphabet (characters) and $\mathcal{V}$ is the token vocabulary.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Postal Sorting System**
> Imagine the **India Post sorting facility in Kochi or Bengaluru**. Millions of unsorted letters arrive as a chaotic pile. Before any delivery route can be planned, each letter must be **separated, stamped with a barcode, and categorized** (by PIN code, then by post office, then by street). Tokenization does the same for text: the raw sentence is the "pile of letters," and each token is a "barcoded parcel" that downstream algorithms (classifiers, transformers, LLMs) can actually process.

Without tokenization, a model sees only an opaque stream of Unicode characters — it cannot count words, measure sentiment, or learn embeddings. Tokenization is therefore the **linguistic equivalent of data ingestion in ETL pipelines**.

### 1.3 Why Tokenization is Mandatory in Data Analytics

| Raw Text Problem | Tokenization Solution |
|---|---|
| Cannot compute TF-IDF or Bag-of-Words | Produces discrete term frequency counts |
| Neural networks require fixed numerical inputs | Tokens map to integer IDs via a vocabulary |
| Cannot handle Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) words | Sub-word tokenizers (BPE, WordPiece) decompose unknowns |
| Multilingual text is incompatible | Character & SentencePiece tokenizers handle Unicode uniformly |

### 1.4 Standard Tokenization Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

- **Vocabulary Size ($\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$):** Total number of unique tokens. Typically **30,000 – 100,000** for modern LLM tokenizers.
- **Token-to-Word Ratio (TWR):** Average number of tokens per English word. Standard value ≈ **1.3 – 1.5** for BPE-based tokenizers like GPT.
- **OOV Rate:** Percentage of input tokens absent from vocabulary. Goal: **< 1%** in modern systems.
- **Tokens per Second (TPS):** Throughput of the tokenizer pipeline. Standard: **> 50,000 TPS** on CPU.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Tokenization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **four canonical levels**. Memorize the granularity trade-off.

| Level | Unit | Example ("NLP is powerful!") | $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ | Sequence Length |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paragraph / Document** | Block | Whole corpus | Small | 1 |
| **Sentence** | Sentence | `["NLP is powerful!"]` | Small | 1 |
| **Word** | Word | `["NLP", "is", "powerful", "!"]` | Large (500K+) | 4 |
| **Sub-word** | Morpheme | `["NLP", "is", "power", "ful", "!"]` | Medium (32K) | 5 |
| **Character** | Character | `["N","L","P"," ","i","s",...]` | Tiny (~256) | 16 |

### 2.2 The Taxonomy of Tokenization Algorithms

#### 2.2.1 Rule-Based (Deterministic) Tokenizers
1. **Whitespace Tokenizer** — splits on ` `, `\t`, `\n`. Fastest, but treats `"powerful!"` as one token.
2. **Punctuation Tokenizer** — splits on regex `[^\w\s]`. Separates punctuation but merges contractions incorrectly.
3. **Whitespace + Punctuation** — combines both; the de-facto baseline in `nltk.word_tokenize`.
4. **Regex Tokenizer** — fully customizable, e.g., `r'\b\w+\b'` for alphanumeric boundaries.

#### 2.2.2 Statistical / Probabilistic Tokenizers
1. **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** — iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent byte pair. Used in **GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, RoBERTa**.
2. **WordPiece** — similar to BPE but selects merges by **likelihood maximization** (not raw frequency). Used in **BERT, DistilBERT**.
3. **Unigram Language Model** — starts with a large vocabulary and **prunes** tokens that minimally decrease the loss. Used in **XLNet, ALBERT**.
4. **SentencePiece** — language-agnostic (treats text as a raw byte stream, no pre-tokenization). Used in **T5, LLaMA, mBERT**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tokenization Function | $T: \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{V}^{*}$ | Formal definition |
| 2 | BPE Merge Score | $\text{score}(x,y) = \frac{\text{count}(xy)}{\text{count}(x) \cdot \text{count}(y)}$ | Choosing best merge |
| 3 | WordPiece Likelihood | $P(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i)$ | Selecting merges |
| 4 | Unigram Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}} \log P(s)$ | Vocabulary pruning |
| 5 | OOV Rate | $\text{OOV} = \frac{\vert \{t : t \notin \mathcal{V}\} \vert}{\vert T \vert} \times 100\%$ | Evaluating coverage |
| 6 | Token-to-Word Ratio | $\text{TWR} = \frac{\vert T \vert}{\vert W \vert}$ | Tokenizer efficiency |
| 7 | Vocabulary Coverage | $\text{Cov} = 1 - \text{OOV}$ | Quality benchmark |
| 8 | Tokens/sec Throughput | $\text{TPS} = \frac{N_{\text{tokens}}}{t_{\text{elapsed}}}$ | Pipeline benchmarking |
| 9 | Cross-Entropy per Token | $H = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 q_i$ (bits/token) | Compression quality |
| 10 | Zipfian Vocabulary Law | $\text{rank} \times \text{freq} \approx \text{const}$ | Predicting long-tail |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Equation (2) — the BPE merge score — is the most frequently asked formula. Notice the **division by the product of individual counts**, not the sum. This normalizes against frequent characters that always co-occur.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Tokenization

- **Search Engines (Elasticsearch, Solr):** Word tokenization feeds inverted indices.
- **Machine Translation (Google Translate):** SentencePiece tokenization handles 100+ languages uniformly.
- **Sentiment Analytics:** Word tokens enable lexicon lookup (e.g., VADER, AFINN).
- **LLMs (GPT, BERT, LLaMA):** Sub-word tokenization resolves the OOV problem and reduces sequence length.
- **Bioinformatics:** Character-level tokenization of DNA/Protein sequences (A, T, G, C).
- **Cybersecurity:** Tokenization of URLs and code snippets for phishing/malware detection.

### 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why pre-tokenize?** Modern sub-word algorithms need word boundaries to compute initial pair statistics.
- **Why normalize first?** Lowercasing, Unicode normalization (NFC/NFD), and accent stripping reduce vocabulary explosion.
- **Why byte-level fallback?** Guarantees **zero OOV** — any character in Unicode (or 256-byte space) is always representable.
- **Why add special tokens?** `[CLS]`, `[SEP]`, `[PAD]`, `[UNK]`, `[BOS]`, `[EOS]` signal model structure (BERT, GPT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — BPE Algorithm (Sennrich et al., 2016)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **highest-yield derivation** in Module 4. KTU examiners expect step-by-step numerical demonstration.

**Input Corpus (4 sentences, tokenized by whitespace + end-of-word marker `</w>`):**
```
"low" ×5,  "lower" ×2,  "newest" ×6,  "widest" ×3
```

**Step 1 — Initialize vocabulary as character set.**

$$\mathcal{V}_0 = \{ l, o, w, e, r, n, s, t, i, d, </w> \}$$

**Step 2 — Represent each word as a sequence of characters + end marker.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{low}     &\rightarrow (l, o, w, </w>) \\
\text{lower}   &\rightarrow (l, o, w, e, r, </w>) \\
\text{newest}  &\rightarrow (n, e, w, e, s, t, </w>) \\
\text{widest}  &\rightarrow (w, i, d, e, s, t, </w>)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Count all adjacent symbol pairs in the corpus.**

| Pair | Count |
|---|---|
| (l, o) | 7 |
| (o, w) | 7 |
| (w, </w>) | 5 |
| (w, e) | 8 |
| (e, r) | 2 |
| (r, </w>) | 2 |
| (n, e) | 6 |
| (e, w) | 6 |
| (e, s) | 9 |
| (s, t) | 9 |
| (t, </w>) | 9 |
| (w, i) | 3 |
| (i, d) | 3 |
| (d, e) | 3 |

**Step 4 — Select the most frequent pair. Tie-break: lexicographic order.**

The most frequent pair is **(e, s)** with count **9** (tied with `(s,t)` and `(t,</w>)`, but `(e,s)` wins lexicographically).

**Step 5 — Merge (e, s) → es. Update vocabulary and corpus.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{V}_1 &= \mathcal{V}_0 \cup \{es\} \\
\text{newest}  &\rightarrow (n, e, w, es, t, </w>) \\
\text{widest}  &\rightarrow (w, i, d, es, t, </w>)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Recompute pair counts. Now `(s, t)` and `(t, </w>)` remain tied at 9.**

Merge `(s, t) → st`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{V}_2 &= \mathcal{V}_1 \cup \{st\} \\
\text{newest}  &\rightarrow (n, e, w, es, st, </w>) \\
\text{widest}  &\rightarrow (w, i, d, es, st, </w>)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Merge `(t, </w>) → t</w>`.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{V}_3 &= \mathcal{V}_2 \cup \{t</w>\} \\
\text{newest}  &\rightarrow (n, e, w, es, st</w>) \\
\text{widest}  &\rightarrow (w, i, d, es, st</w>)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Continue until target vocabulary size is reached (e.g., $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 20$).**

This iterative procedure is the **byte-pair encoding algorithm** in its entirety.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — OOV Rate Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Given vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ and a test sentence, compute the OOV rate."*

**Given:**
- $\mathcal{V} = \{ \text{the}, \text{cat}, \text{sat}, \text{on}, \text{mat} \}$, $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 5$
- Test sentence: `"the dog sat on the rug"`
- Tokenized (whitespace): $T = [\text{the}, \text{dog}, \text{sat}, \text{on}, \text{the}, \text{rug}]$
- Total tokens: $\vert T \vert = 6$

**Step 1 — Identify tokens not in $\mathcal{V}$.**

$$
T_{\text{OOV}} = \{ \text{dog}, \text{rug} \}, \quad \vert T_{\text{OOV}} \vert = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the OOV formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OOV Rate} &= \frac{\vert T_{\text{OOV}} \vert}{\vert T \vert} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2}{6} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute vocabulary coverage.**

$$
\text{Cov} = 1 - \text{OOV Rate} = 1 - 0.3333 = 0.6667 \;\; (66.67\%)
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Tokens-Per-Second Benchmark

**Given:** A tokenizer processes a corpus of **1,200,000 tokens** in **24 seconds**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TPS} &= \frac{N_{\text{tokens}}}{t_{\text{elapsed}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{24} \\[4pt]
&= 50{,}000 \;\; \text{tokens/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Regex Tokenizer

**Regex Pattern:** `r'[A-Za-z]+|[0-9]+|[^\s\w]'`

**Input:** `"DataAnalytics2024 costs ₹50,000!"`

**Token-by-token match:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Match 1: } & \texttt{"DataAnalytics"} \rightarrow \text{[A-Za-z]+} \\
\text{Match 2: } & \texttt{"2024"} \rightarrow \text{[0-9]+} \\
\text{Match 3: } & \texttt{" "} \rightarrow \text{skipped (\textbackslash s)} \\
\text{Match 4: } & \texttt{"costs"} \rightarrow \text{[A-Za-z]+} \\
\text{Match 5: } & \texttt{" "} \rightarrow \text{skipped} \\
\text{Match 6: } & \texttt{"₹"} \rightarrow \text{[^\textbackslash s\textbackslash w]} \\
\text{Match 7: } & \texttt{"50"} \rightarrow \text{[0-9]+} \\
\text{Match 8: } & \texttt{","} \rightarrow \text{[^\textbackslash s\textbackslash w]} \\
\text{Match 9: } & \texttt{"000"} \rightarrow \text{[0-9]+} \\
\text{Match 10: } & \texttt{"!"} \rightarrow \text{[^\textbackslash s\textbackslash w]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output token list:**
$$T = [\text{DataAnalytics}, \text{2024}, \text{costs}, ₹, 50, \text{,}, 000, \text{!}]$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Whitespace | Word | BPE | WordPiece | SentencePiece | Unigram |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Coarse | Word | Sub-word | Sub-word | Sub-word | Sub-word |
| **OOV Handling** | None | None | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Language Agnostic** | No | No | Partial | Partial | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Pre-tokenization** | Required | Required | Required | Required | **Not required** | Required |
| **Used By** | Search engines | BoW, TF-IDF | GPT-2/3/4 | BERT | T5, LLaMA | XLNet |
| **Vocabulary Size** | 10K–50K | 100K–500K | 32K–100K | 30K | 32K | 32K |
| **Deterministic?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No (sampling)** |
| **Speed (TPS)** | > 1M | > 500K | ~ 80K | ~ 60K | ~ 50K | ~ 40K |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Tokenization Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Unicode Normalization NFC/NFD]
    B --> C[Lowercasing and Accent Stripping]
    C --> D[Language Detection Module]
    D --> E[Pre-tokenizer Whitespace Split]
    E --> F{Sub-word Algorithm}
    F -->|Frequency Based| G[BPE Byte Pair Encoding]
    F -->|Likelihood Based| H[WordPiece]
    F -->|Probabilistic Pruning| I[Unigram LM]
    F -->|Byte Level No PreToken| J[SentencePiece]
    G --> K[Special Token Injection CLS SEP PAD BOS EOS]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Integer ID Mapping via Vocabulary Lookup]
    L --> M[Attention Mask and Token Type IDs]
    M --> N[Final Tensor for Neural Model Input]
```

### 4.2 BPE Iterative Merge State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Step 0: Char Vocab 11 symbols] --> S1[Step 1: Merge e,s to es]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Merge s,t to st]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Merge t, endW to t endW]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Merge es,t to est]
    S4 --> S5[Step k: Final Vocab 20 symbols]
    S5 --> S6[Encode New Words via Greedy Longest Match]
```

### 4.3 Tokenization Granularity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Paragraph] --> S1[Sentence 1]
    P --> S2[Sentence 2]
    P --> S3[Sentence N]
    S1 --> W1[Word 1]
    S1 --> W2[Word 2]
    W1 --> SW1[Sub-word unit 1]
    W1 --> SW2[Sub-word unit 2]
    SW1 --> C1[Character a]
    SW1 --> C2[Character b]
    SW1 --> C3[Character c]
```

### 4.4 Tokenizer Decision Tree (Algorithm Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need OOV Robustness?} -->|Yes| Q2{Pre-tokenization Available?}
    Q1 -->|No| R1[Use Whitespace or Word Tokenizer]
    Q2 -->|Yes, clean scripts| R2[Use BPE or WordPiece]
    Q2 -->|No, multilingual or CJK| R3[Use SentencePiece]
    Q2 -->|Yes, want probabilistic| R4[Use Unigram LM]
    R2 --> R5[Best for English LLMs and BERT family]
    R3 --> R6[Best for T5 LLaMA mBART]
    R4 --> R7[Best for XLNet and ALBERT]
```

### 4.5 Token-to-Embedding Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Token ID 1045] --> E1[Embedding Lookup Table]
    T2[Token ID 2214] --> E1
    T3[Token ID 1996] --> E1
    E1 --> V1[Dense Vector dim 768]
    E1 --> V2[Dense Vector dim 768]
    E1 --> V3[Dense Vector dim 768]
    V1 --> POS[Positional Encoding Added]
    V2 --> POS
    V3 --> POS
    POS --> TRANS[Transformer Encoder Block]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define tokenization. List and briefly explain the four levels of tokenization granularity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tokenization is the process of splitting a continuous text stream into smaller linguistic units called tokens, which serve as the atomic inputs for NLP and data analytics pipelines.

The four levels of granularity are:
1. **Sentence tokenization** — splits text into sentences using punctuation heuristics (e.g., `.`, `?`, `!`).
2. **Word tokenization** — splits sentences into words using whitespace and punctuation rules.
3. **Sub-word tokenization** — decomposes rare or unknown words into frequent morphemes (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram).
4. **Character tokenization** — splits text into individual Unicode characters; guarantees zero OOV.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) and WordPiece tokenization. State one model that uses each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BPE | WordPiece |
|---|---|---|
| Merge Criterion | Highest **raw frequency** of adjacent pair | Highest **likelihood increase** after merge |
| Selection Rule | $\arg\max \text{count}(x,y)$ | $\arg\max \frac{P(xy)}{P(x)P(y)}$ |
| Probabilistic? | No (deterministic) | Partially (likelihood-based) |
| Used By | **GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, RoBERTa** | **BERT, DistilBERT, Electra** |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse OOV Rate with Vocabulary Coverage.** Coverage = $1 - \text{OOV}$. Many students swap these formulas.
> - **BPE merge score** uses the **product** $P(x) \cdot P(y)$ in the denominator, not the sum.
> - Always state **assumptions** (case sensitivity, language, normalization) before computing token counts.
> - For Regex questions, **draw the pattern** and trace character-by-character — partial matching is the #1 cause of mark loss.
> - In comparison tables, every row MUST have an entry; empty cells invite zero marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **end-to-end pipeline of sub-word tokenization** using Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE). Start from raw text ingestion and end with integer ID mapping. State one advantage and one disadvantage of BPE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following mini-corpus:
```
"deep" ×4,   "deeper" ×3,   "deepest" ×2,   "deeply" ×1
```
Apply the **BPE algorithm step-by-step** until you obtain a vocabulary of **15 symbols**. Show all pair frequency tables and merge operations explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Pipeline Diagram (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Raw Text} \rightarrow \text{Normalization} \rightarrow \text{Pre-tokenization} \rightarrow \text{BPE Merges} \rightarrow \text{Vocabulary} \rightarrow \text{ID Mapping}
$$

**Stages explained:**

1. **Normalization (1 mark):** Apply Unicode NFC, lowercase, strip accents to reduce vocabulary size.
2. **Pre-tokenization (1 mark):** Whitespace split to obtain initial word boundaries.
3. **BPE Merges (1 mark):** Iteratively merge the most frequent adjacent symbol pair; add new symbol to vocabulary; repeat.
4. **ID Mapping (1 mark):** Each symbol in the final vocabulary is assigned a unique integer ID via a hash map; tokens of new words are produced by greedy longest-match against the vocabulary.

**Advantage:** Robust OOV handling — any unseen word can be decomposed into known sub-units.

**Disadvantage:** Greedy and deterministic; cannot capture multiple valid segmentations probabilistically.

---

**(b) [7 marks] — BPE Step-by-Step**

**Step 0 — Initialize character vocabulary from corpus:**

$$\mathcal{V}_0 = \{ d, e, p, r, s, t, l, y, </w> \}, \quad \vert \mathcal{V}_0 \vert = 9$$

**Step 1 — Represent words with end-of-word marker:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{deep}     &\rightarrow (d, e, e, p, </w>) \times 4 \\
\text{deeper}   &\rightarrow (d, e, e, p, e, r, </w>) \times 3 \\
\text{deepest}  &\rightarrow (d, e, e, p, e, s, t, </w>) \times 2 \\
\text{deeply}   &\rightarrow (d, e, e, p, l, y, </w>) \times 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial pair frequencies:**

| Pair | Count |
|---|---|
| (d, e) | 10 |
| (e, e) | 10 |
| (e, p) | 10 |
| (p, </w>) | 4 |
| (p, e) | 6 |
| (e, r) | 3 |
| (r, </w>) | 3 |
| (e, s) | 2 |
| (s, t) | 2 |
| (t, </w>) | 2 |
| (p, l) | 1 |
| (l, y) | 1 |
| (y, </w>) | 1 |

**Step 3 — Most frequent pair:** All of `(d,e)`, `(e,e)`, `(e,p)` are tied at count 10. Lexicographic tie-break: `(d, e)` wins.

**Merge (d, e) → de:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{V}_1 &= \mathcal{V}_0 \cup \{de\} = 10 \text{ symbols} \\
\text{deep}     &\rightarrow (de, e, p, </w>) \times 4 \\
\text{deeper}   &\rightarrow (de, e, p, e, r, </w>) \times 3 \\
\text{deepest}  &\rightarrow (de, e, p, e, s, t, </w>) \times 2 \\
\text{deeply}   &\rightarrow (de, e, p, l, y, </w>) \times 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recompute pair frequencies and continue merging** until $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 15$.

Through subsequent merges of `(e,e)`, `(e,p)`, `(p,</w>)`, `(p,e)`, `(e,r)`, `(r,</w>)` (6 more merges), we obtain $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 9 + 6 = 15$ symbols. ✓

**Final vocabulary (illustrative):**
$$\mathcal{V} = \{ d, e, p, r, s, t, l, y, </w>, de, ee, ep, p</w>, pe, er, r</w> \}, \quad \vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 15 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) rate** in tokenization. A tokenizer has a vocabulary $\mathcal{V} = \{ \text{the}, \text{quick}, \text{brown}, \text{fox}, \text{jumps} \}$ and processes the sentence `"the lazy dog jumps over the fox"`. **(7 marks)**
   1. Compute the OOV rate.
   2. Compute the vocabulary coverage.
   3. Suggest a tokenization strategy to **reduce** the OOV rate to below 5%, with justification.

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Word tokenization, BPE, and SentencePiece** across the following dimensions: granularity, OOV handling, language support, pre-tokenization requirement, and a real-world model example. Present your answer in a comparison table followed by a 100-word summary. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Concept (2 marks):** OOV rate measures the fraction of input tokens absent from the tokenizer's vocabulary. A high OOV rate degrades model performance because unknown tokens are either dropped or mapped to a generic `[UNK]` placeholder, losing semantic information.

**Step 1 — Tokenize the sentence by whitespace:**

$$T = [\text{the}, \text{lazy}, \text{dog}, \text{jumps}, \text{over}, \text{the}, \text{fox}]$$
$$\vert T \vert = 7$$

**Step 2 — Identify OOV tokens:**

$$
T_{\text{OOV}} = \{ \text{lazy}, \text{dog}, \text{over} \}, \quad \vert T_{\text{OOV}} \vert = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute OOV rate (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OOV Rate} &= \frac{\vert T_{\text{OOV}} \vert}{\vert T \vert} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3}{7} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 42.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute coverage (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Cov} = 1 - 0.4286 = 0.5714 \;\; (57.14\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Strategy to reduce OOV below 5% (2 marks):**

Adopt a **sub-word tokenizer such as BPE or WordPiece** with a vocabulary size of ~30,000. The unseen words decompose as:
- `lazy` → `["la", "zy"]`
- `dog` → `["dog"]` (single token, present in sub-word vocab)
- `over` → `["over"]`

This reduces OOV to **0%** because all characters are covered by sub-word units, well below the 5% threshold. Justification: sub-word tokenization guarantees **zero OOV** via byte-level fallback in modern implementations.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Dimension | Word Tokenization | BPE | SentencePiece |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Word | Sub-word | Sub-word (byte-level) |
| **OOV Handling** | None — unknown words lost | Excellent — sub-units cover all | Excellent — byte fallback |
| **Language Support** | English-centric | Mostly English | **100+ languages, CJK, code** |
| **Pre-tokenization** | Required | Required | **Not required** |
| **Example Model** | TF-IDF, Naive Bayes | GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4 | T5, LLaMA, mBART |

**100-Word Summary (2 marks):**

> Word tokenization is the simplest and fastest method but suffers from high OOV rates and language limitations. BPE addresses the OOV problem by decomposing rare words into frequent sub-units using iterative pair merging, making it ideal for English-dominant LLMs like GPT-3. However, BPE still relies on language-specific pre-tokenization. SentencePiece overcomes this by operating directly on raw byte streams, eliminating the need for pre-tokenization and providing true multilingual support. It is therefore preferred for diverse, code-mixed, and non-Latin script corpora. All three methods trade off vocabulary size against sequence length, with sub-word methods offering the best balance for modern neural architectures.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tokenization definition:** $T: \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{V}^{*}$ — maps characters to vocabulary tokens.
- **Four granularity levels:** Sentence, Word, Sub-word, Character — trade-off between $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ and sequence length.
- **BPE merge rule:** Pick the **most frequent adjacent pair**; break ties lexicographically. Repeat until target vocab size.
- **WordPiece merge rule:** Maximize **likelihood ratio** $\frac{P(xy)}{P(x)P(y)}$, not raw frequency.
- **Unigram:** Starts large, prunes to minimize **negative log-likelihood** loss.
- **SentencePiece:** Language-agnostic byte-level tokenizer; **no pre-tokenization** required.
- **OOV Rate formula:** $\text{OOV} = \frac{\vert T_{\text{OOV}} \vert}{\vert T \vert} \times 100\%$.
- **Coverage formula:** $\text{Cov} = 1 - \text{OOV Rate}$.
- **TPS formula:** $\text{TPS} = \frac{N_{\text{tokens}}}{t_{\text{elapsed}}}$.
- **Special tokens:** `[CLS]`, `[SEP]`, `[PAD]`, `[UNK]`, `[BOS]`, `[EOS]` — mandatory in BERT/GPT pipelines.
- **Unicode normalization:** Apply **NFC** (canonical composition) before tokenization to merge equivalent glyphs.
- **Models using BPE:** GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, RoBERTa.
- **Models using WordPiece:** BERT, DistilBERT, Electra.
- **Models using SentencePiece:** T5, LLaMA, mBART, XLM-RoBERTa.
- **Models using Unigram:** XLNet, ALBERT.
- **Zero-OOV guarantee:** Only achievable with **byte-level or character-level** tokenization.
- **Zipf's Law applies:** The 1,000 most frequent words cover ~80% of any English corpus — sub-word vocabularies exploit this.
- **Throughput benchmark:** > 50,000 tokens/sec is production-grade on CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For any 14-mark BPE question, draw a **frequency table**, perform **at least 3 explicit merge steps**, and conclude with the **final vocabulary list and size**. Examiners allocate marks for *each visible transition*, not just the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stemming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stemming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stemming** is a rudimentary heuristic process in **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Text Analytics** that chops off the end of words in order to obtain a common base form (the *stem*), often by removing derivational affixes. The resulting stem need not be a valid word in the language, but it serves as a canonical index term for **Information Retrieval (IR)** and document clustering tasks.

Mathematically, stemming can be represented as a morphological reduction function:

$$S(w) \rightarrow w_{stem}$$

where $S$ is the stemming operator, $w$ is the input word token, and $w_{stem}$ is the reduced root form. The mapping is **many-to-one**, meaning multiple surface forms collapse onto a single stem.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Token** | A single word occurrence in a corpus |
| **Stem** | The truncated root form after suffix removal |
| **Corpus** | A large, structured collection of text documents |
| **Affix** | A morpheme attached before (prefix) or after (suffix) a root |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a library's "Topic Finder" index.** Instead of writing separate index cards for `running`, `runs`, `ran`, and `runner`, the librarian snips the ends off the words so all variations fall under a single heading: **`run`**. This is exactly what a stemmer does — it trims words so search engines and text analyzers can group related concepts together, even when writers use different word forms.

In other words, **stemming is the "search-and-grep" approach to word reduction** — fast, rule-based, and forgiving of non-dictionary outputs. For example:

- `connection` ➡️ `connect`
- `connected` ➡️ `connect`
- `connecting` ➡️ `connect`
- `studies` ➡️ `studi` (notice: not a real word, but a valid stem)
- `studying` ➡️ `studi`

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Check Mnemonic:** Think **STEM = "Strip The End Mechanically"** — stemming is mechanical suffix-stripping without consulting a dictionary.

## 1.3 Syllabus Relevance & KTU Context

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PECST523 – Data Analytics**, Module 4), Stemming is a **core text-preprocessing primitive** that precedes tokenization-level analysis, TF-IDF computation, and document classification. It is heavily examined under:

- **CO4: Apply text preprocessing techniques on unstructured data** (highest weightage)
- **CO3: Implement feature engineering for text corpora**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Stemming? — The Information Retrieval Problem

In an unstemmed corpus, the words `compute`, `computer`, `computing`, `computed`, and `computation` are treated as **5 independent features**. This leads to:

1. **Sparsity explosion** in the term-document matrix.
2. **Poor recall** in search engines (a query for *"computing"* misses documents containing only *"compute"*).
3. **Inflated vocabulary size**, increasing memory and compute cost.

Stemming consolidates these into a single dimension $\rightarrow$ **denser, more semantically coherent feature space**.

## 2.2 Categories of Stemming Algorithms

Stemmers are broadly classified into three families:

| Category | Mechanism | Example Algorithm | Output Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-Based (Truncation)** | Applies iterative suffix-stripping rules in phases | **Porter Stemmer (1980)** | Moderate — fast, widely used |
| **Rule-Based (Aggressive)** | Iterates over 100+ rules; very aggressive | **Lancaster Stemmer (Paice-Husk)** | High over-stemming risk |
| **Lookup / Dictionary-Based** | Replaces inflected forms using a morphological lexicon | **Krovetz Stemmer** | Linguistically accurate but slow |
| **Statistical / ML-Based** | Learns optimal rules from corpus statistics | **Snowball**, **HMM-based stemmers** | High accuracy, language-specific |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Porter Stemmer is the de-facto KTU exam algorithm.** It is mandatory to know its 5 phases and the **measure function (m)**.

## 2.3 The Porter Measure Function $m(w)$

The Porter algorithm uses a *vowel-consonant sequence count* on the word stem (after removing plurals). This is called the **measure**, denoted $m$.

**Definition of measure $m$:**

$$m(w) = \left[ \text{Number of } (VC) \text{ sequences in } w \right]$$

Where:
- $V$ = a single vowel $a, e, i, o, u$ (or $y$ preceded by a consonant)
- $C$ = a single consonant (anything not in $V$)

**Examples:**

$$m(\text{``tree''}) = 0 \quad \text{(no VC sequence — C C V V)}$$

$$m(\text{``trouble''}) = 1 \quad \text{(t r o u b l e → VC once)}$$

$$m(\text{``private''}) = 2 \quad \text{(pr iv at e → VC, VC)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** A stem is "substantial" if $m \geq 1$. Rules in Porter Phases 2–5 typically require a minimum measure to prevent over-truncation of short words.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stemming Operator** | $S(w) \rightarrow w_{stem}$ | Defines the reduction mapping |
| 2 | **Porter Measure** | $m(w) = \text{count}(VC\text{-sequences})$ | Decides if a stem is "heavy" enough for further stripping |
| 3 | **Vowel Condition** | $*v*$ — stem contains a vowel | Common condition for rule application |
| 4 | **Double Consonant** | $*d$ — stem ends with double consonant (e.g., "tt", "ss") | Gates certain phase-5 rules |
| 5 | **CVC Pattern** | $*o$ — stem ends in CVC where final C is not $w, x, y$ | Used in the "-E" removal rule |
| 6 | **Porter Phase 1a** | SSES→SS, IES→I, SS→SS, S→ | Removes English plurals |
| 7 | **Porter Phase 1b** | (ED\|ING)→ when vowel in stem | Strips past tense / gerund |
| 8 | **Porter Phase 1c** | Y→I when preceding vowel in stem | Normalises "y" to "i" |
| 9 | **Lancaster Iterations** | Up to 120+ rules; recursive | Aggressive suffix removal |
| 10 | **Stemming vs Lemmatization** | Stem = heuristic chop; Lemma = dictionary lookup | Critical exam distinction |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Stemming |
|---|---|
| **Search Engines (Google, Bing)** | Query-document matching for higher recall |
| **Document Classification (Spam filtering)** | Reduces feature dimensionality for Naive Bayes / SVM |
| **Sentiment Analysis (NLP pipelines)** | Groups "loved", "loves", "loving" → "love" for uniform sentiment scoring |
| **Topic Modeling (LDA, NMF)** | Improves coherence by collapsing inflectional variants |
| **Chatbots & Virtual Assistants** | Better intent recognition across word variations |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Porter Stemmer on a Sample Word

Let us trace the Porter algorithm on the word **"CONNECTIONS"** and derive the resulting stem step-by-step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every transition below is a direct application of the official Porter (1980) ruleset. No step is skipped.

### Step 1 — Initial Word

$$w_0 = \text{``connections''}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Phase 1a (Plural Removal)

Phase 1a rules (in order of priority):

- SSES $\rightarrow$ SS
- IES $\rightarrow$ I
- SS $\rightarrow$ SS
- S $\rightarrow$ (empty)

The word "connections" ends in **"ions"** — none of the Phase 1a suffixes match (the "s" is preceded by "ion", not a simple plural morpheme). So:

$$w_1 = \text{``connections''} \quad (\text{no change})$$

### Step 3 — Apply Phase 1b (Past Tense & Gerunds)

Phase 1b rules trigger only if the stem contains a vowel $(*v*)$. Since "connections" contains vowels $o, e, i$, the condition $*v*$ is satisfied. However, "connections" does not end in `eed`, `ed`, or `ing`, so:

$$w_2 = \text{``connections''} \quad (\text{no change})$$

### Step 4 — Apply Phase 1c (Y → I)

Rule: $(*v*)Y \rightarrow I$ — Replace $y$ with $i$ if there is a vowel in the stem.

"connections" does not end in $y$, so:

$$w_3 = \text{``connections''} \quad (\text{no change})$$

### Step 5 — Apply Phase 2 (Derivational Suffix Removal)

Phase 2 rules are conditional on $m > 0$. The Porter measure of "connections":

$$\text{connections} = \underbrace{C}_{c} \underbrace{V}_{o} \underbrace{C}_{nn} \underbrace{V}_{e} \underbrace{C}_{c} \underbrace{V}_{i} \underbrace{C}_{t} \underbrace{V}_{i} \underbrace{C}_{ons}$$

Counting VC sequences: `(co)(nn)(ec)(ti)(on)(s)` → after careful parsing, $m(\text{connections}) = 3$.

The suffix **"TIONS"** matches the rule `TIONAL → TION`. Applying this rule:

$$w_4 = \text{``connection''} \quad (\text{from TIONS} \rightarrow \text{TION then drop 'a'})$$

> **Correction via direct rule application:**
> Porter's exact Phase 2 table includes: `ational → ate`, `tional → tion`, `enci → ence`, `anci → ance`, `izer → ize`, `ization → ize`, etc.
> "connections" ends in **"TIONS"** — no exact Phase 2 rule matches. Moving to Phase 3.

$$w_4 = \text{``connections''} \quad (\text{Phase 2 no match})$$

### Step 6 — Apply Phase 3 (Derivational Suffix Removal)

Phase 3 includes: `icate → ic`, `ative → `, `alize → al`, `iciti → ic`, `ical → ic`, `ful → `, `ness → `.

"connections" does not end in any of these. So:

$$w_5 = \text{``connections''} \quad (\text{no change})$$

### Step 7 — Apply Phase 4 (Long-Suffix Removal)

Phase 4 removes: `al`, `ance`, `ence`, `er`, `ic`, `able`, `ible`, `ant`, `ement`, `ment`, `ent`, `ion`, `ou`, `ism`, `ate`, `iti`, `ous`, `ive`, `ize`.

The word ends in **"IONS"** — matching the rule `ION → ` (empty), provided the stem ends in $s$ or $t$ (i.e., $*S$ or $*T$).

Stem of "connections" (after dropping "ions") = "connect" → ends in $t$. Condition satisfied.

$$w_6 = \text{``connect''} \quad \text{(from ``connections'' minus ``ions'')}$$

### Step 8 — Apply Phase 5a (Final 'E' Removal)

Rule: $(m>1) E \rightarrow $ (empty) OR $(m=1 \text{ and not } *o) E \rightarrow $ (empty).

Measure of "connect" = `con nect` → VC, VC → $m = 2 > 1$.

"connect" does not end in `e`, so no action:

$$w_7 = \text{``connect''} \quad (\text{no change})$$

### Step 9 — Final Result

$$\boxed{S(\text{``connections''}) = \text{``connect''}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Three Stemming Algorithms Compared

Let us apply three stemmers to the same word list and tabulate the outputs. This matrix is **exam-favorite** for KTU Part B questions.

| Original Word | **Porter (1980)** | **Lancaster (Paice)** | **Snowball (Porter2)** | **Lemmatization (WordNet)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **connection** | connect | connect | connect | connection |
| **connected** | connect | connect | connect | connect |
| **connecting** | connect | connect | connect | connecting |
| **studies** | studi | study | studi | study |
| **studying** | studi | study | studi | studying |
| **university** | univers | univers | univers | university |
| **universe** | univers | univers | univers | universe |
| **running** | run | run | run | running |
| **runs** | run | run | run | run |
| **happiness** | happi | happy | happi | happiness |
| **happily** | happili | happy | happili | happily |
| **troubles** | troubl | trubl | troubl | trouble |
| **troubling** | troubl | trubl | troubl | troubling |

### Key Observations from the Matrix

> [!TIP]
> 1. **Lancaster is the most aggressive** — it produces shorter stems at the risk of **over-stemming** (merging unrelated words).
> 2. **Porter is moderate** — a balance of speed and accuracy.
> 3. **Lemmatization is linguistically pure** — but slower because it consults a dictionary (WordNet).
> 4. **Snowball = Porter improved** — multilingual support, cleaner code.

## 3.3 Error Categories in Stemming

| Error Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Over-stemming** | Two unrelated words collapse to the same stem | `university` and `universe` → both `univers` |
| **Under-stemming** | Two related words remain distinct | `connection` and `connected` should both map to `connect` — handled correctly here, but `alumnus` vs `alumni` fails in naïve stemmers |
| **False-positive stemming** | Stemmer truncates a valid word incorrectly | `data` → `dat` (loss of semantic content) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Text Preprocessing Pipeline (with Stemming Position)

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **full KTU Module 4 text analytics pipeline**, highlighting where stemming operates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawDoc[Raw Text Document] --> tokenize[Tokenization]
    tokenize --> normalize[Normalization]
    normalize --> stopword[Stopword Removal]
    stopword --> stemChoice{Stemming or Lemmatization}
    stemChoice --> porterStem[Porter Stemmer]
    stemChoice --> lemma[WordNet Lemmatizer]
    porterStem --> corpus[Cleaned Corpus]
    lemma --> corpus
    corpus --> tdidf[TF-IDF / BoW Vectorization]
    tdidf --> model[ML Model / IR Index]
    model --> output[Document Classification / Search Results]
```

## 4.2 Porter Stemmer Internal Phase Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startIn[Input Word] --> p1a[Phase 1a: Plurals]
    p1a --> p1b[Phase 1b: Past Tense and Gerund]
    p1b --> p1c[Phase 1c: Y to I]
    p1c --> p2[Phase 2: Derivational Suffixes]
    p2 --> p3[Phase 3: Derivational Suffixes]
    p3 --> p4[Phase 4: Long Suffix Removal]
    p4 --> p5a[Phase 5a: Final E Removal]
    p5a --> p5b[Phase 5b: Double Letter Reduction]
    p5b --> finalOut[Final Stem Output]
```

## 4.3 Stemmer Family Classification (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stemFamily[Stemming Algorithms]
    stemFamily --> ruleBased[Rule Based]
    stemFamily --> lookup[Lookup / Dictionary]
    stemFamily --> ml[Statistical / ML Based]
    ruleBased --> porter[Porter 1980]
    ruleBased --> lancaster[Lancaster Paice Husk]
    ruleBased --> snowball[Snowball Porter2]
    lookup --> krovetz[Krovetz Stemmer]
    ml --> hmm[HMM Based Stemmer]
    ml --> neural[Neural Morphological Models]
```

## 4.4 Porter Measure Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputW[Input Word] --> seqParse[Parse as V and C Sequence]
    seqParse --> countVC[Count VC Pairs]
    countVC --> mVal[Measure m Equals Count]
    mVal --> checkM{m greater than 0}
    checkM --> yesRule[Apply Suffix Removal Rule]
    checkM --> noSkip[Skip Rule Preserve Word]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define stemming. How does it differ from lemmatization?**

**Model Answer:**

Stemming is a text preprocessing technique that reduces inflected or derived words to their root form (the *stem*) by applying heuristic rules to chop off word endings. It is primarily used in Information Retrieval to improve recall by treating morphological variants as equivalent. For example, "running", "runs", and "ran" all map to the stem `run`.

**Lemmatization**, in contrast, uses a vocabulary and morphological analysis to return the **lemma** — the dictionary base form of a word. It considers the part-of-speech and context, producing linguistically valid words. For example, the lemma of "better" is `good` (not `bet`), and the lemma of "running" is `run` only when used as a verb.

| Aspect | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Output Validity | May be non-word | Always valid word |
| Dictionary Use | No | Yes (WordNet) |
| POS Awareness | No | Yes |

**(3 marks — full credit if both definitions and a 1-line difference are given.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the Porter measure $m$ with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The Porter measure $m(w)$ of a word $w$ is the number of alternating **Vowel–Consonant (VC) sequences** found in the word after stripping plurals. A vowel is one of $a, e, i, o, u$ (or $y$ when preceded by a consonant), and a consonant is any other letter.

**Example:** For the word `trouble`:
- Sequence: $C(C) V(o) C(b) V(u) C(l) C(e)$ — note the rule that $y$ is treated as vowel only if preceded by a consonant.
- Stripping the trailing `e` (plurals-like), stem = `troubl`.
- VC sequences in `troubl`: `(tr)(ou)(bl)` → $m = 1$.

The measure is used as a gating condition in Porter Phases 2–5 to prevent over-stemming of short words.

**(3 marks — include the formal definition and a worked example.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by **(a) confusing stemming with lemmatization**, **(b) skipping the formal $m(w)$ definition**, or **(c) failing to state the condition $*v*$ explicitly when quoting Porter rules. Always write the rule + the condition together.

---

### 📝 **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Stemming Algorithms*

**[a] (7 marks)** Explain the **Porter Stemmer algorithm** in detail. List all five phases and describe the rules applied in **Phase 1a** and **Phase 1b** with examples.

**[b] (7 marks)** Compare the **Porter**, **Lancaster**, and **Snowball** stemmers on the words: *connection, studies, happiness, university, running*. Discuss the trade-off between **over-stemming** and **under-stemming**.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Porter Stemmer Algorithm (7 marks)

The Porter Stemmer (1980) is a rule-based, 5-phase algorithm that iteratively applies suffix-stripping rules. Each phase transforms the word's surface form, and rules within a phase are checked in a specific order.

**The five phases:**

1. **Phase 1a** — Plural and simple suffix removal.
2. **Phase 1b** — Past tense and gerund suffix removal.
3. **Phase 1c** — Y → I normalization.
4. **Phase 2** — Derivational suffix removal (measure-gated).
5. **Phase 3** — Further derivational suffix removal (measure-gated).
6. **Phase 4** — Long-suffix removal (measure-gated, with $*S$ or $*T$ condition).
7. **Phase 5a & 5b** — Final 'e' removal and double-letter reduction.

**Phase 1a rules (with examples):**

| Rule | Example | Output |
|---|---|---|
| `SSES → SS` | `caresses` | `caress` |
| `IES → I` | `ponies` | `poni` |
| `SS → SS` | `caress` | `caress` |
| `S → ∅` | `cats` | `cat` |

**Phase 1b rules (with examples):**

| Rule | Condition | Example | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| `(m>0) EED → EE` | stem has $m>0$ | `agreed` | `agree` |
| `(*v*) ED → ∅` | stem has vowel | `plastered` | `plaster` |
| `(*v*) ING → ∅` | stem has vowel | `motoring` | `motor` |

If a word is altered in Phase 1b, additional cleanup rules apply (e.g., `AT → ATE`, `BL → BLE`, `IZ → IZE`, `BB → B`, `(*d and not (*L or *S or *Z)) → single letter`).

**(7 marks — full credit for all 5 phases, both Phase 1a/1b rules, and 2 worked examples.)**

---

#### (b) Comparative Stemmer Analysis (7 marks)

| Original | Porter | Lancaster | Snowball |
|---|---|---|---|
| connection | connect | connect | connect |
| studies | studi | study | studi |
| happiness | happi | happy | happi |
| university | univers | univers | univers |
| running | run | run | run |

**Trade-off Discussion (KTU high-yield):**

- **Lancaster** strips more aggressively (e.g., `happiness → happy`, `studies → study`) — **higher risk of over-stemming**, which can merge semantically distinct words (e.g., `university` and `universe` both → `univers`).
- **Porter** offers a balanced reduction (e.g., `happiness → happi`, `studies → studi`) — stems are shorter than the lemma but longer than Lancaster's.
- **Snowball (Porter2)** refines Porter for multi-language support and better Unicode handling.

**Over-stemming vs. Under-stemming:**

- **Over-stemming** = different words mapped to the same stem (loss of discriminative power). Example: `universe` and `university` → `univers`.
- **Under-stemming** = similar words remain distinct. Example: A naïve stemmer might keep `fishing` and `fished` separate.

**Conclusion:** Choose **Porter** for general IR; **Lancaster** for aggressive dimension reduction; **Lemmatization** when accuracy outweighs speed.

**(7 marks — full tabular output + clear over/under-stemming examples.)**

---

### 📝 **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Stemming: Theory & Applications*

**[a] (7 marks)** Define the **Porter measure $m(w)$** formally. Compute $m$ for the words: *trouble, private, university, cat, dog*. Explain why $m$ is critical to the algorithm.

**[b] (7 marks)** Discuss the role of stemming in **Information Retrieval (IR)** with a diagram. Explain how stemming affects **precision** and **recall** in a search engine.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Porter Measure Computation (7 marks)

**Formal definition:**

$$m(w) = \text{Number of } (VC) \text{ sequences in the word stem}$$

where $V$ denotes a sequence of one or more vowels and $C$ denotes a sequence of one or more consonants, applied after Phase 1a and 1b cleanup. Each transition from a vowel-group to a consonant-group increments the count by 1.

**Worked computations:**

| Word | Letter Classification | VC Pairs | Measure $m$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **trouble** | C(tr) V(ou) C(b) C(l) e-drop → `troubl` | (tr), (ou), (bl) | $m = 1$ |
| **private** | C(pr) V(i) C(v) V(a) C(t) e-drop → `privat` | (pr), (iv), (at) | $m = 2$ |
| **university** | C → V → C → V → C → V → C → V → C → V → C → drop | many | $m = 4$ |
| **cat** | C(c) V(a) C(t) | (ca), (at) | $m = 1$ |
| **dog** | C(d) V(o) C(g) | (do), (og) | $m = 1$ |

**Why $m$ is critical:**

The measure acts as a **safety threshold** — Porter rules in Phases 2–5 require $m \geq 1$ (or $m > 1$) so that short, irregular words are not over-truncated. Without this gate, words like `is` or `we` would lose all their letters.

**(7 marks — formal definition + table of 5 values + 2-sentence justification.)**

---

#### (b) Stemming in Information Retrieval (7 marks)

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userQuery[User Query: running shoes] --> stemQ[Apply Stemmer]
    stemQ --> qStem[Stemmed Query: run shoe]
    qStem --> idx[Search Inverted Index]
    idx --> results[Matched Documents]
    results --> ranked[Ranked Results Page]
    idx -. uses .-> corpusStem[Pre-stemmed Corpus Index]
```

**Effect on Precision and Recall:**

- **Recall ↑ (improves):** Stemming matches more documents because morphological variants are treated as equivalent. Query *"running"* now also matches documents containing *"run"*, *"runs"*, *"ran"*.
- **Precision ↓ (may degrade):** Stemming can over-stem, returning irrelevant documents. Query *"university"* might match documents about *"universe"* because both stem to `univers`.

**Engineering trade-off:** The F1-score determines the optimal stemming aggressiveness. Modern search engines (Google, Elasticsearch) use **light stemming** (Porter or Snowball) to maximize F1.

**(7 marks — diagram + precision/recall explanation + 1 real-engine example.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Stemming**

- ✅ **Stemming** = heuristic suffix-stripping to a common base form; output may not be a real word.
- ✅ **Lemmatization** = dictionary + morphology-aware reduction; output is always a valid lemma.
- ✅ **Porter Stemmer (1980)** is the KTU-favoured algorithm; **5 phases** plus a 5b double-letter step.
- ✅ **Measure $m(w)$** = count of (VC) sequences; gates Phases 2–5.
- ✅ **Conditions:** $*v*$ (vowel in stem), $*d$ (ends in double consonant), $*o$ (CVC with non-$w,x,y$ final C).
- ✅ **Phase 1a** = SSES→SS, IES→I, SS→SS, S→∅.
- ✅ **Phase 1b** = (m>0) EED→EE, (*v*) ED→∅, (*v*) ING→∅.
- ✅ **Phase 1c** = (*v*) Y → I.
- ✅ **Lancaster** = aggressive, 100+ rules, higher over-stemming risk.
- ✅ **Snowball** = Porter's improved multilingual successor.
- ✅ **Over-stemming** = unrelated words collapse; **Under-stemming** = related words remain distinct.
- ✅ **Recall ↑, Precision ↓** when stemming is applied to IR.
- ✅ **Applications:** search engines, sentiment analysis, topic modeling, spam filtering.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always state the rule **and** its gating condition (e.g., "`TIONAL → TION` when $m > 0$").

---

> **End of Module 4 — Stemming Notes | PECST523 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Phrase Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phrase Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Phrase Query (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *phrase query* is an Information Retrieval (IR) query in which the user submits a **contiguous sequence of terms** (e.g., "machine learning algorithms") and expects the retrieval system to return only those documents in which these terms appear **in that exact order, adjacent to one another**, with no intervening words.

Unlike a standard Boolean or ranked-retrieval query — which treats the query as an unordered bag of words — a phrase query preserves **word order and proximity**, making it a fundamental tool for **proximity-aware retrieval** in modern search engines.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme PECST523 (Data Analytics) syllabus, phrase queries fall under **Module 4: Indexing and Retrieval**, and they are typically taught immediately after the discussion of inverted indexes and before advanced topics such as tolerant retrieval and ranking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Bookmark Hunt"**

Imagine you are searching for a specific sentence in a 1000-page novel — say, *"the brown fox jumps"*.

- A **standard inverted index** (bag-of-words) would tell you: *"Page 12 has 'the', 'brown', 'fox', and 'jumps'… Page 47 also has them."* — but it does NOT tell you whether they appear as a phrase.
- A **phrase query engine** behaves like a librarian who remembers not just *which* pages mention each word, but also the **exact positions** of those words on every page. So the librarian can confidently say: *"On Page 12, the words appear in positions 4, 5, 6, 7 in that order — yes, that's the phrase!"*

This is the essence of phrase queries: **knowing *where* the words are, not just *whether* they are.**

## 1.3 Why Phrase Queries Matter in Data Analytics

| Use Case | Why a Phrase Query is Needed |
|---|---|
| **Web Search Engines** | A user typing *"President of the United States"* expects a document containing that exact phrase, not a random mix. |
| **Legal/Patent Analytics** | Lawyers search for *exact terminology* — a single word mismatch can change meaning. |
| **Biomedical Text Mining** | Phrases like *"breast cancer"* must not be confused with documents containing "breast" and "cancer" 30 pages apart. |
| **News/Social Media Analytics** | Tracking brand mentions like *"iPhone 15 Pro"* requires exact-phrase detection. |

## 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Term:** A normalized word (after stemming, case-folding).
- **Posting:** An entry in the inverted index listing a document containing a term.
- **Positional Posting:** A posting that *also* records the term's exact position(s) in the document.
- **Biword:** A pair of consecutive terms treated as a single vocabulary unit.
- **Proximity Operator:** A query operator (e.g., `/k`) that demands terms be within *k* positions of each other.
- **k-gram:** A sequence of *k* characters used for partial / wildcard matching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Within Module 4 of PECST523, students must be able to (i) **define a phrase query**, (ii) **distinguish biword indexes from positional indexes**, and (iii) **describe the phrase query processing algorithm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Principal Approaches to Phrase Queries

There are two canonical data structures for answering phrase queries in IR systems:

### 2.1.1 Biword Indexes

A **biword index** treats every pair of consecutive terms in the document collection as a single vocabulary entry.

- **Construction:** For text `t1 t2 t3 t4 ...`, generate biwords `(t1,t2)`, `(t2,t3)`, `(t3,t4)`, ...
- **Index Entry:** `<(t_i, t_{i+1}) : postings list of documents containing the pair>`
- **Query Processing:** A query of *n* words is decomposed into *(n − 1)* biwords, and a Boolean **AND** is run across their postings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strengths:** Simple; biword indexes answer any 2-word phrase query in a single intersection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Weakness:** They cannot answer longer phrase queries (3+ words) without a *positional* layer. Also, biword vocabularies grow quadratically with the size of the term vocabulary.

### 2.1.2 Positional Indexes (The Standard Approach)

A **positional index** is an inverted index in which every posting stores **all positions** at which the term occurs in the document.

- **Index Entry:** `<term : (docID, [pos1, pos2, pos3, ...]), (docID, [...]), ...>`

This is the de-facto industry standard (e.g., Lucene/Elasticsearch uses positional indexes).

## 2.2 Phrase Query Processing Algorithm (Positional Intersection)

Given a phrase of *n* terms `t1 t2 ... tn`, the algorithm performs *n − 1* positional intersections:

1. Fetch postings of `t1` and `t2`.
2. Walk both postings *simultaneously* (merge-style).
3. For each common document, scan the two position lists to find at least one pair of positions `(p, q)` such that `q = p + 1`.
4. Pass the *residual position list* (positions of `t2` that have a match) to the next stage.
5. Intersect this with the postings of `t3` looking for `r = q + 1`, and so on.

The total time complexity for a phrase of length *n* is:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} O(\text{matching at stage } i)$$

In the worst case, the complexity per stage is bounded by the *positional posting length*, but in practice it is highly efficient because residual lists shrink rapidly.

## 2.3 Mixed Strategy (Biword + Positional)

To balance index size and query power, modern systems (e.g., the textbook by Manning, Raghavan, and Schütze) recommend a **hybrid**:

- **Index every biword** that is *frequent* (above a threshold) as a normal term.
- For *infrequent* biwords, fall back on the positional index.

This keeps the index compact while still answering long-phrase queries.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes every formula, definition, and boundary condition a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the ESE on this topic.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Standard posting (no positions) | $\langle \text{term} : d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n \rangle$ | $d_i$ = docID |
| **Positional posting** | $\langle \text{term} : (d_i, [p_{i,1}, p_{i,2}, \ldots])\rangle$ | $p_{i,j}$ = token position in $d_i$ |
| Biword construction | $\text{biword}_k = (t_k, t_{k+1})$ for all $k$ | Cardinality $\leq \vert V \vert^2$ |
| Number of biwords in a doc of length $L$ | $L - 1$ | Per document |
| Positional index size overhead | $\approx 2 \times$ standard inverted index | Empirical; depends on $avg\_positions\_per\_term$ |
| Phrase match condition (stage $i$) | $p_{i+1} = p_i + 1$ | Adjacent positions |
| Proximity match (within $k$) | $\vert p_{i+1} - p_i \vert \leq k$ | Order-preserving |
| k-gram example ($k=3$) | "phrase" → \$, "ph", "phr", "hra", "ras", "ase", "se\$" | 1-grams and biwords are not used alone |
| Wildcard `*` translation | `*` → `\$$$`; use k-gram index | $k$ typically 3 |
| Vocabulary size scaling | Biwords: $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ ; Positional postings: $O(T)$ | $T$ = total tokens |

> **CRITICAL LATEX NOTE:** In the table above, vertical bars for absolute value are written as `\vert V \vert`, `\vert p_{i+1} - p_i \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** All major engines use positional indexes augmented with biword shortcuts for high-frequency collocations.
- **E-commerce Search (Amazon, Flipkart):** A search for *"noise cancelling headphones"* must return products whose title contains the *exact* phrase, not random "noise" and "headphones".
- **Plagiarism Detection (Turnitin):** Detecting copied *phrases* is the heart of academic-integrity software.
- **Voice Assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant):** "Play **Hotel California**" requires exact-phrase matching on song titles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Phrase Query Processing on a Positional Index

### 3.1.1 Sample Document Collection

Consider the following three documents (toy corpus):

> **Doc 1:** *Data science is fun .*
> **Doc 2:** *Big data analytics is powerful .*
> **Doc 3:** *Data analytics drives modern data science .*

> [!NOTE]
> Periods, commas, etc., are typically treated as separate tokens by IR systems. We will number all tokens positionally for clarity.

### 3.1.2 Tokenized Representation (with positions)

| **Doc** | **Token Stream (position : token)** |
|---|---|
| Doc 1 | 1:Data  2:science  3:is  4:fun |
| Doc 2 | 1:Big  2:data  3:analytics  4:is  5:powerful |
| Doc 3 | 1:Data  2:analytics  3:drives  4:modern  5:data  6:science |

### 3.1.3 Building the Positional Inverted Index

For brevity, we show only the postings for the three query-relevant terms: *Data*, *analytics*, *science*.

$$
\text{data} \;\to\; \big[(1, [1]),\; (2, [2]),\; (3, [1, 5])\big]
$$

$$
\text{analytics} \;\to\; \big[(2, [3]),\; (3, [2])\big]
$$

$$
\text{science} \;\to\; \big[(1, [2]),\; (3, [6])\big]
$$

The notation $(d, [p_1, p_2, \ldots])$ means: *term appears in document d at positions $p_1, p_2, \ldots$*

### 3.1.4 Query: `"data analytics science"` (a 3-word phrase)

We need to find documents where *data*, *analytics*, *science* appear in adjacent positions `p, p+1, p+2`.

**Step 1 — Intersect postings of "data" and "analytics"**

- "data" postings: (1, [1]), (2, [2]), (3, [1, 5])
- "analytics" postings: (2, [3]), (3, [2])
- Common doc: 2 and 3.

**Step 2 — Check positional adjacency in common docs**

For **Doc 2**:
- data positions in Doc 2: $[2]$
- analytics positions in Doc 2: $[3]$
- Is $3 = 2 + 1$? **Yes ✓** → Match at position 2.
- Residual list for "analytics" going forward: $[3]$

For **Doc 3**:
- data positions in Doc 3: $[1, 5]$
- analytics positions in Doc 3: $[2]$
- For data@1, is analytics@2 equal to 1+1=2? **Yes ✓** → Match.
- For data@5, is analytics@2 equal to 5+1=6? **No ✗** → No match.
- Residual list: $[2]$

**Step 3 — Intersect residual with postings of "science"**

- "science" postings: (1, [2]), (3, [6])
- For Doc 2 → no "science" appears in Doc 2's postings → **Doc 2 eliminated** ✗
- For Doc 3 → science positions = $[6]$
- Check: analytics residual position is 2; require science at $2+1 = 3$? No, science is at 6, not 3. **No match** ✗

**Step 4 — Final Result**

$$
\text{Result set for "data analytics science"} = \emptyset
$$

This is correct! No document contains the *exact* three-word phrase in order, even though the words individually appear in Docs 1, 2, and 3.

### 3.1.5 Query: `"data analytics"` (2-word phrase)

**Step 1 — Intersect postings**
- Common docs: 2 and 3.

**Step 2 — Positional adjacency**

Doc 2: data@2, analytics@3 → $3 = 2+1$ ✓ → **MATCH**

Doc 3: data@1, analytics@2 → $2 = 1+1$ ✓ → **MATCH**
Doc 3: data@5, analytics@2 → $2 = 5+1$? **No** ✗

**Step 3 — Final Result**

$$
\text{Result set for "data analytics"} = \{2,\; 3\}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Indexing Schemes

| **Criterion** | **Standard Inverted Index** | **Biword Index** | **Positional Index** | **Mixed (Biword + Positional)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Answers single-word query? | Yes ✓ | No ✗ (vocab has pairs only) | Yes ✓ | Yes ✓ |
| Answers 2-word phrase? | No ✗ | Yes ✓ (one AND) | Yes ✓ (intersect + adjacency) | Yes ✓ |
| Answers 3+-word phrase? | No ✗ | Needs positional layer | Yes ✓ | Yes ✓ |
| Answers proximity `/k`? | No ✗ | No ✗ | Yes ✓ | Yes ✓ |
| Index size | Smallest ($O(T)$) | Largest ($O(\vert V \vert^2)$) | ~2× standard | Moderate |
| Query speed (phrase) | N/A | Fastest for 2-grams | Fast | Fastest overall |
| Used in production? | Rarely alone | Rarely alone | Yes (Lucene, ES) | Yes (Google-scale systems) |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Index-Size Overhead for Positional Indexes

Let:
- $T$ = total number of tokens in the collection
- $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size (number of unique terms)
- Standard postings count: approximately $\sum_{t \in V} df_t = T$, since each token contributes one posting entry.

For a **positional index**, each posting is augmented with a position integer.

Assuming 4 bytes for docID and 4 bytes for a position (typical IR textbook assumption):

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{positional}} \approx 2 \times \text{Size}_{\text{standard}}
$$

In detail:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{positional}} = 4 \cdot T_{\text{docIDs}} + 4 \cdot T_{\text{positions}} = 4T + 4T = 8T \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{standard}} = 4T \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \text{Overhead factor} = \frac{8T}{4T} = 2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** Storing positions roughly *doubles* the index size — an acceptable trade-off given the dramatic gain in retrieval power (phrase + proximity queries).

## 3.4 Formal Definition — Phrase Match Predicate

For a phrase $P = (t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ and a document $d$, we say $d$ *matches* $P$ if and only if:

$$
\exists \, p \in \mathbb{N} \;\; \text{such that} \;\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \big( t_i \text{ occurs in } d \text{ at position } p + i - 1 \big)
$$

Equivalently, using positional postings:

$$
\text{match}(P, d) \;=\; \bigvee_{p} \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n-1} \big( (p+i) \in \text{posList}(d, t_{i+1}) \;\big\vert\; p \in \text{posList}(d, t_i) \big)
$$

This predicate is the **logical foundation** of every phrase-query algorithm, and a KTU 2024 examiner expects students to recognize it (even if not in full symbolic form).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Phrase Query Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input: Phrase Query] --> B[Tokenizer and Normalizer]
    B --> C[Query Term List: t1, t2, ..., tn]
    C --> D{Index Type Available?}
    D -- Biword Index --> E[Decompose into Biwords]
    E --> F[AND of Biword Postings]
    D -- Positional Index --> G[Fetch Postings of t1 and t2]
    G --> H[Merge Walk: Find Common Docs]
    H --> I[Check Positional Adjacency p2 = p1 + 1]
    I --> J{Adjacency Found?}
    J -- No --> K[Discard Document]
    J -- Yes --> L[Build Residual Position List]
    L --> M{More Terms in Phrase?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Intersect Residual with t i+1]
    N --> I
    M -- No --> O[Add Document to Result Set]
    O --> P[Final Ranked Result List]
    F --> P
    K --> P
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Positional Inverted Index Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VOCAB[Vocabulary Layer]
        T1[Term: data]
        T2[Term: analytics]
        T3[Term: science]
    end

    subgraph POST1[Postings: data]
        P1A[Doc 1, Pos 1]
        P1B[Doc 2, Pos 2]
        P1C[Doc 3, Pos 1 and 5]
    end

    subgraph POST2[Postings: analytics]
        P2A[Doc 2, Pos 3]
        P2B[Doc 3, Pos 2]
    end

    subgraph POST3[Postings: science]
        P3A[Doc 1, Pos 2]
        P3B[Doc 3, Pos 6]
    end

    T1 --> P1A
    T1 --> P1B
    T1 --> P1C
    T2 --> P2A
    T2 --> P2B
    T3 --> P3A
    T3 --> P3B
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of Indexing Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Phrase Query: n terms] --> S{Strategy?}
    S -- Biword --> SW1[Split into n-1 biwords]
    SW1 --> SW2[AND all biword postings]
    SW2 --> SW3[Return matching docs]
    S -- Positional --> SP1[Fetch postings of t1 and t2]
    SP1 --> SP2[Merge walk for common docs]
    SP2 --> SP3[Check adjacency p2 = p1 + 1]
    SP3 --> SP4{All n terms matched?}
    SP4 -- No --> SP5[Discard doc]
    SP4 -- Yes --> SP6[Add doc to result]
    SP6 --> SP7{More docs?}
    SP7 -- Yes --> SP2
    SP7 -- No --> SP8[Return result set]
    S -- Mixed --> SM1[Use biword for frequent 2-grams]
    SM1 --> SM2[Fall back on positional for rare pairs]
    SM2 --> SP1
    SM2 --> SW1
```

## 4.4 Concept Map — Phrase Queries in the IR Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IR[Information Retrieval System] --> IDX[Inverted Index]
    IDX --> S1[Standard Index: term -> docs]
    IDX --> S2[Biword Index: pair -> docs]
    IDX --> S3[Positional Index: term -> doc, positions]
    S1 --> R1[Boolean and Ranked Retrieval]
    S2 --> PQ[Phrase Query Engine]
    S3 --> PQ
    PQ --> APPS[Applications: Web Search, E-commerce, Legal, Bio]
    S1 --> TR[Tokenization and Normalization]
    S2 --> TR
    S3 --> TR
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)
