### Q1. Define a phrase query. How does it differ from a Boolean query?  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *phrase query* is a query in which the user specifies a sequence of terms in a specific order (e.g., `"machine learning"`) and expects the system to return only those documents in which these terms appear contiguously and in the given order.
> In contrast, a **Boolean query** treats the query terms as an *unordered set* and uses operators (AND, OR, NOT) to combine them.
> The key difference is **word order and adjacency**: phrase queries preserve both, Boolean queries ignore them.

---

### Q2. What is a positional index? Why is it preferred over a biword index for long phrase queries?  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *positional index* is an inverted index in which each posting records, in addition to the document ID, the **positions** at which the term occurs in the document.
> It is preferred over a biword index for long phrase queries because biword indexes can only directly answer 2-word phrases (a *3-word* phrase would still need a positional layer or a triword index, both of which are infeasible at scale). A positional index, on the other hand, naturally generalizes to phrases of *any* length via sequential positional intersections.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure of a positional inverted index**. How does it support phrase queries? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step algorithm** for processing a phrase query `"t1 t2 t3 t4"` using a positional index. Illustrate with a small example. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A positional inverted index extends the standard inverted index by appending, to each posting, a list of term positions within the document.

**Structure (3 marks):**
For each term $t$ in the vocabulary, the index stores:
$$
\langle t : [(d_1, [p_{1,1}, p_{1,2}, \ldots]), (d_2, [\ldots]), \ldots] \rangle
$$
A textual/diagrammatic representation is given below (also shown in Section 4.2 above):

| Term | Posting |
|---|---|
| data | (1, [1]); (2, [2]); (3, [1, 5]) |
| analytics | (2, [3]); (3, [2]) |
| science | (1, [2]); (3, [6]) |

**How it supports phrase queries (2 marks):**
- It preserves **word order** because each position is relative to the token stream.
- It allows the algorithm to verify **adjacency** ($p_{i+1} = p_i + 1$) by walking position lists in lockstep.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Algorithm (4 marks):**

```
Input:  Phrase P = t1 t2 ... tn; positional index I
Output: Set of documents R containing P as a phrase

1.  R ← ∅
2.  Fetch postings Post(t1) and Post(t2)
3.  for each common document d in Post(t1) and Post(t2):
4.      for each position p1 in posList(d, t1):
5.          if (p1 + 1) ∈ posList(d, t2):
6.              residual ← {p1 + 1}
7.              match ← TRUE
8.              for i = 3 to n:
9.                  newRes ← ∅
10.                 for each p in residual:
11.                     if (p + 1) ∈ posList(d, ti): newRes ← newRes ∪ {p + 1}
12.                 if newRes = ∅: match ← FALSE; break
13.                 residual ← newRes
14.             if match: R ← R ∪ {d}
15. return R
```

**Worked example (3 marks):** The same toy corpus from Section 3.1 above. For query `"data analytics"` the algorithm returns docs {2, 3}, and for `"data analytics science"` it returns ∅ — exactly as derived step-by-step in Section 3.1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **biword indexes** and **positional indexes** for phrase queries. Discuss the size–performance trade-offs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **mixed indexing strategy** with an example. Why do modern search engines prefer it? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):** (Reproduced in condensed form; the full version is in Section 3.2 above.)

| Aspect | Biword Index | Positional Index |
|---|---|---|
| Vocabulary | Pairs → $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Singletons → $O(\vert V \vert)$ |
| Phrase length handled | 2 words directly | Any length |
| Index size | Very large | ~2× standard |
| Query algorithm | Simple AND | Positional intersection |
| Proximity queries | Not supported | Supported |

**Trade-off discussion (3 marks):**
- Biword indexes are blazingly fast for 2-word phrases but explode in size and cannot handle longer phrases.
- Positional indexes scale gracefully to phrases of arbitrary length and support proximity operators, at the cost of roughly doubling the index size — a price worth paying in any serious IR system.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The *mixed strategy* indexes **frequent biwords** (above a pre-set threshold) directly as vocabulary terms, while **retaining the full positional index** to answer queries involving rarer pairs or longer phrases.

**Example (3 marks):**
Suppose the biword `"United States"` appears 100,000 times in the corpus, while `"United Kingdom"` appears only 12 times.
- We *index* `("United","States")` as a single vocabulary term.
- We *do not* index `("United","Kingdom")` as a biword; instead, the query is answered by intersecting positional postings of "United" and "Kingdom".

**Why modern engines prefer it (2 marks):**
- Frequent phrases (named entities, idioms) are answered with the speed of a Boolean AND.
- Rare phrases and long queries fall back to the positional index, avoiding the $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ blow-up of full biword indexing.
- This hybrid yields the best balance of **index size, query latency, and recall**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing biword and positional indexes:** Many students write "biword indexes store positions" — they do not. State clearly that *biword indexes store pairs* and *positional indexes store positions*.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention word order:** A phrase query requires both adjacency *and* order. Saying only "the words must be next to each other" is incomplete.
> 3. **Ignoring residual position lists:** When describing the phrase algorithm, students often forget to pass the *residual* list forward — this is the crux of the algorithm and is heavily marked.
> 4. **Confusing `k`-gram with `k`-word proximity:** A *k-gram* is a character sequence (for wildcards); a *k-proximity* is a word-window operator. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Index-size overestimation:** Writing that positional indexes are "10× larger" is wrong — empirically it is ~2×. Citing the correct factor earns full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Phrase Queries (Module 4, PECST523)**

- [x] A **phrase query** demands *ordered, contiguous* occurrence of terms in a document.
- [x] **Biword indexes**: index every *pair* of consecutive terms; fast for 2-word phrases; vocabulary grows as $\vert V \vert^2$.
- [x] **Positional indexes**: store, for each term posting, the list of *positions* in the document; answer phrases of *any* length.
- [x] The phrase query algorithm performs **sequential positional intersections**, propagating a *residual position list* from one stage to the next.
- [x] Adjacency check: $p_{i+1} = p_i + 1$; proximity check: $\vert p_{i+1} - p_i \vert \leq k$.
- [x] The **mixed strategy** indexes frequent biwords directly + retains positional index for the rest — the production standard.
- [x] Positional indexes cost roughly **2×** the storage of standard inverted indexes.
- [x] **`k`-grams** are character sequences of length $k$, used for wildcard/tolerant retrieval — *not* the same as word-proximity operators.
- [x] Real-world systems: Lucene, Elasticsearch, Google, Bing all use positional indexes (often with biword shortcuts).
- [x] **KTU 2024 must-know:** definition, the two index types, the positional-intersection algorithm, and a working example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Space Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vector Space Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Vector Space Model (VSM) — Core Definition & Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Space Model (VSM)** is an algebraic model widely used in **Information Retrieval (IR)** and **Text Mining** that represents both **documents** and **queries** as vectors in a high-dimensional Euclidean space, where each dimension corresponds to a unique **index term** (word/token) drawn from the document collection's vocabulary. The relevance of a document to a query is computed as a function of the **similarity** (e.g., cosine similarity) between their corresponding vectors.

Formally, given a vocabulary $V = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$ of $n$ unique terms, every document $D_j$ in the collection is represented as:

$$\vec{D_j} = (w_{1j}, w_{2j}, \dots, w_{nj})$$

and a query $Q$ is represented as:

$$\vec{Q} = (w_{1q}, w_{2q}, \dots, w_{nq})$$

where $w_{ij}$ is the **weight** assigned to term $t_i$ in document $D_j$, typically computed using the **TF-IDF** (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency) scheme.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Library Analogy):** Imagine a library where every book is described using exactly the same set of 10,000 attributes (size, color, year, topic-tags, etc.). You place each book at a point in a 10,000-dimensional room based on its attribute values. When a user walks in asking for a "red book about space from 2020", they too are placed at a point in that room. The closer any book is to the user, the more relevant it is. **VSM does exactly this for text** — every document and every query is converted into a coordinate in a giant mathematical "room" (vector space), and the system ranks documents by **distance/angle** to the query.

### 1.3 Key Properties of VSM

- **Geometric Representation:** Documents and queries exist as points/directions in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Term Independence Assumption:** VSM assumes terms are *independent* (the "orthogonality assumption"), which is a simplification since terms in real text are correlated.
- **Partial Matching:** Unlike Boolean IR, VSM supports *graded* (ranked) matching using continuous similarity scores.
- **Ranking Output:** Results are sorted in descending order of similarity — the top-$k$ documents are returned.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** VSM is the *de facto* standard for ranking in search engines like Google (in its foundational form) and remains a **mandatory topic** in Module 4 of PECST523 — expect it in 14-mark derivations and 3-mark short questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of VSM

The Vector Space Model operates in the following sequential stages:

1. **Text Pre-processing**
   - Tokenization → split text into individual terms
   - Stop-word removal → discard common words (*the, is, a, an*)
   - Stemming/Lemmatization → reduce words to root form (*running → run*)
   - Case-folding → convert all to lowercase

2. **Vocabulary Construction**
   - Build the global dictionary $V = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$ from all unique terms in the corpus.

3. **Term Weight Computation**
   - For each document $D_j$ and term $t_i$, compute the weight $w_{ij}$ using **TF-IDF**.

4. **Vector Construction**
   - Build $\vec{D_j} = (w_{1j}, w_{2j}, \dots, w_{nj})$ for all documents and the query.

5. **Similarity Scoring**
   - Compute **Cosine Similarity** between $\vec{Q}$ and each $\vec{D_j}$.

6. **Ranking**
   - Sort documents in descending order of similarity score; return top-$k$.

## 2.2 Why VSM Works — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Tokenization / Stemming:** Reduces dimensionality and groups semantically related terms.
- **TF-IDF Weighting:** Reweights terms so that *rare-but-discriminative* words (e.g., "quantum") dominate over *frequent-but-uninformative* words (e.g., "the").
- **Cosine Similarity:** Normalizes for document length, focusing on **directional alignment** rather than raw magnitude — a 5-line document and a 500-line document about the same topic can have the *same* similarity to a query.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Term Frequency (TF)** | $\text{tf}(t, d) = f_{t,d}$ | Raw count of term $t$ in document $d$ (integer, dimensionless) |
| 2 | **Log-Normalized TF** | $\text{tf}_{\text{norm}}(t, d) = 1 + \log_{10}(f_{t,d})$ | Dampens impact of very high counts |
| 3 | **Document Frequency (DF)** | $\text{df}(t) = \vert\{d \in C : t \in d\}\vert$ | Number of docs containing term $t$ |
| 4 | **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** | $\text{idf}(t) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ | Inverse measure of term commonality (dimensionless) |
| 5 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$ | Combined discriminative weight (dimensionless) |
| 6 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(Q, D_j) = \dfrac{\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_j}}{\vert\vec{Q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{D_j}\vert}$ | Angle between vectors, range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 7 | **Dot Product** | $\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq} \, w_{ij}$ | Sum of element-wise products |
| 8 | **Euclidean Norm** | $\vert\vec{X}\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | Magnitude/length of vector |
| 9 | **Number of Docs in Collection** | $N$ | Total documents (integer) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Logarithm Base:** The KTU board accepts both **base-10** and **natural log (base $e$)** for IDF. However, when using $1 + \log(f)$ in TF, students **must** add the 1 to avoid $\log(0)$ errors. Always state the base explicitly in your exam answer to earn full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** The original scoring backbone of modern search.
- **Document Classification & Clustering:** K-means, KNN operate natively in vector space.
- **Recommender Systems:** User–item interactions represented as TF-IDF-style vectors.
- **Plagiarism Detection & Duplicate Detection:** Cosine similarity detects near-duplicate documents.
- **Legal E-Discovery & Biomedical IR:** Ranking clinical notes and case files.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Complete TF-IDF + Cosine Similarity

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Corpus of 3 Documents)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \text{``data science analytics''} \\
D_2 &= \text{``machine learning data''} \\
D_3 &= \text{``deep learning analytics data''} \\
Q  &= \text{``data analytics''}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Vocabulary $V$** (after tokenization & lowercasing): $\{ \text{data}, \text{science}, \text{analytics}, \text{machine}, \text{learning}, \text{deep} \}$

So $n = 6$ dimensions.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Raw Term Frequencies

| Term $t_i$ | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $Q$ |
|------------|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| data | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| science | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| analytics | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| machine | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| learning | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| deep | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Document Frequency $\text{df}(t)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{df}(\text{data}) &= 3 \quad (\text{appears in all 3 docs}) \\
\text{df}(\text{science}) &= 1 \\
\text{df}(\text{analytics}) &= 2 \\
\text{df}(\text{machine}) &= 1 \\
\text{df}(\text{learning}) &= 2 \\
\text{df}(\text{deep}) &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Total documents $N = 3$.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute IDF using $\text{idf}(t) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{idf}(\text{data})      &= \log_{10}(3/3) = \log_{10}(1) = 0 \\
\text{idf}(\text{science})   &= \log_{10}(3/1) = \log_{10}(3) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{idf}(\text{analytics}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{idf}(\text{machine})   &= \log_{10}(3/1) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{idf}(\text{learning})  &= \log_{10}(3/2) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{idf}(\text{deep})      &= \log_{10}(3/1) \approx 0.4771
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute TF-IDF Weights $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \text{idf}(t)$

Since $\text{tf} = 1$ for every present term, weights reduce to IDF values:

| Term | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $Q$ |
|------|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| data | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| science | 0.4771 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| analytics | 0.1761 | 0 | 0.1761 | 0.1761 |
| machine | 0 | 0.4771 | 0 | 0 |
| learning | 0 | 0.1761 | 0.1761 | 0 |
| deep | 0 | 0 | 0.4771 | 0 |

Resulting vectors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{D_1} &= (0, \, 0.4771, \, 0.1761, \, 0, \, 0, \, 0) \\
\vec{D_2} &= (0, \, 0, \, 0, \, 0.4771, \, 0.1761, \, 0) \\
\vec{D_3} &= (0, \, 0, \, 0.1761, \, 0, \, 0.1761, \, 0.4771) \\
\vec{Q}   &= (0, \, 0, \, 0.1761, \, 0, \, 0, \, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute Cosine Similarity $\text{sim}(Q, D_j) = \dfrac{\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_j}}{\vert\vec{Q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{D_j}\vert}$

**For $D_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_1} &= (0)(0) + (0)(0.4771) + (0.1761)(0.1761) + (0)(0) + (0)(0) + (0)(0) \\
&= 0.0310 \\
\vert\vec{Q}\vert &= \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = 0.1761 \\
\vert\vec{D_1}\vert &= \sqrt{0^2 + 0.4771^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{0.2276 + 0.0310} = \sqrt{0.2586} = 0.5085 \\
\text{sim}(Q, D_1) &= \dfrac{0.0310}{0.1761 \times 0.5085} = \dfrac{0.0310}{0.0895} \approx 0.3464
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $D_2$:** No shared non-zero dimensions, so $\text{sim}(Q, D_2) = 0$.

**For $D_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_3} &= (0)(0) + (0)(0) + (0.1761)(0.1761) + (0)(0) + (0)(0.1761) + (0)(0.4771) \\
&= 0.0310 \\
\vert\vec{D_3}\vert &= \sqrt{0 + 0 + 0.0310 + 0 + 0.0310 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.2896} = 0.5382 \\
\text{sim}(Q, D_3) &= \dfrac{0.0310}{0.1761 \times 0.5382} = \dfrac{0.0310}{0.0948} \approx 0.3271
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.7 Final Ranking

$$
\boxed{\text{sim}(Q, D_1) = 0.3464 \;\; > \;\; \text{sim}(Q, D_3) = 0.3271 \;\; > \;\; \text{sim}(Q, D_2) = 0.0000}
$$

**Retrieved order:** $D_1 \rightarrow D_3 \rightarrow D_2$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Cosine Similarity (Why we divide by norms)

The cosine of the angle $\theta$ between two non-zero vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is given by the geometric identity:

$$
\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vec{A}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{B}\vert}
$$

This is **scale-invariant**: if $\vec{A}$ is multiplied by any scalar $k$, $\cos\theta$ remains unchanged. Hence, two documents of vastly different lengths but identical term proportions will receive the *same* similarity score — exactly the property required for text IR.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VSM End-to-End Pipeline (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Document Corpus D1 D2 ... Dn] --> B[Text Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B1 --> B2[Stopword Removal]
    B2 --> B3[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    B3 --> C[Vocabulary Construction V = t1 t2 ... tn]
    C --> D[TF Computation]
    C --> E[DF and IDF Computation]
    D --> F[TF IDF Weighting Module]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Document Vector Matrix n x N]
    Q[User Query Q] --> Q1[Query Preprocessing]
    Q1 --> F1[Query TF IDF Vector]
    F --> H[Cosine Similarity Engine]
    F1 --> H
    H --> I[Ranking Module]
    I --> J[Top k Relevant Documents Returned to User]
    G --> H
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Vector Space Diagram (3 Terms Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin] -->|t1 axis data| B[Point D1 data science analytics]
    A -->|t2 axis science| C[Point D2 machine learning data]
    A -->|t3 axis analytics| D[Point D3 deep learning analytics data]
    A -->|query direction| E[Query Vector Q data analytics]
    E -.similarity theta1.-> B
    E -.similarity theta3.-> D
    C -.theta2 angle 90 deg.-> E
```

## 4.3 Cosine Similarity Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Ranking Documents] --> B[Compute TF for all terms in doc and query]
    B --> C[Compute IDF using log N by df]
    C --> D[Form TF IDF vectors for each doc and query]
    D --> E[Compute dot product Q dot Dj]
    E --> F[Compute Euclidean norms for Q and Dj]
    F --> G[Divide dot product by product of norms]
    G --> H[Obtain similarity score in 0 to 1]
    H --> I[Sort all docs in descending order of score]
    I --> J[Return Top k to user]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Block 1 shows the complete offline + online pipeline. Block 2 conceptualizes the *angle* between query and document vectors — the smaller the angle, the higher the relevance. Block 3 zooms into the cosine-similarity computation sub-routine.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Vector Space Model. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** is an algebraic model of Information Retrieval that represents each document $D_j$ and a user query $Q$ as vectors in an $n$-dimensional Euclidean space, where $n$ is the size of the vocabulary. Each dimension corresponds to a unique index term, and the coordinate value represents the term's weight (e.g., TF-IDF). The relevance of a document to the query is computed using a **similarity measure**, most commonly **cosine similarity**, defined as:

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_j) = \dfrac{\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_j}}{\vert\vec{Q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{D_j}\vert}
$$

VSM supports *partial matching* and produces a *ranked* output of documents, unlike the Boolean model which only returns unranked sets.

### Question 2: What is the significance of IDF in the Vector Space Model? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**IDF (Inverse Document Frequency)** assigns a higher weight to terms that appear in fewer documents, and a lower weight to terms appearing in many. It is defined as:

$$
\text{idf}(t) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)
$$

The significance is: (i) it **suppresses common terms** like "the", "is" that carry little discriminative power, (ii) it **boosts rare terms** like "quantum" or "blockchain" that are highly informative for retrieval, and (iii) it **improves ranking accuracy** by giving IDF-weighted vectors a more discriminative geometry in the vector space.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the logarithm base** — always state `log base 10` or `ln` explicitly.
> 2. **Dividing by zero** when a term appears in *all* documents (df = N) — IDF becomes 0; do NOT panic, just write 0.
> 3. **Not normalizing** with norms in cosine similarity — students often compute only the dot product and lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up TF and DF** — TF is *within a document*; DF is *across the collection*. Confusing them is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Skipping units/dimensions** in the formula sheet recall — KTU awards partial marks for showing awareness that the score is dimensionless in $[0, 1]$.

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

#### (a) Explain the Vector Space Model with a neat diagram. Discuss the term-weighting scheme TF-IDF in detail. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Vector Space Model Overview:**
In VSM, both documents and queries are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional space. Each dimension corresponds to a term in the vocabulary. The geometric distance (or angle) between vectors determines their relevance.

**Diagram (refer Section 4.2 above).**

**TF-IDF Term Weighting:**

The combined weight is:

$$
w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \text{idf}(t) = f_{t,d} \times \log\!\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)
$$

- **TF Component ($f_{t,d}$):** captures the *local* importance of a term in a single document. A term appearing 10 times in a doc is more central to that doc than a term appearing once.
- **IDF Component ($\log(N/\text{df}(t))$):** captures the *global* rarity of a term across the corpus. A term in only 1 of 1000 documents is highly discriminative.
- **Product:** combines local + global importance, producing a *discriminative weight* used as the vector coordinate.

**Properties:**
- TF-IDF is **non-negative** and **dimensionless**.
- The common term "data" with $\text{df} = N$ gets weight 0 (no contribution).
- A unique term like "blockchain" with $\text{df} = 1$ gets the maximum log-weight.

**(b) Given the following three documents and a query, compute the TF-IDF vectors and rank the documents using cosine similarity. (7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \text{``AI is the future''} \\
D_2 &= \text{``AI powers the future of AI''} \\
D_3 &= \text{``machine learning is the future''} \\
Q  &= \text{``AI future''}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary:** $V = \{\text{AI}, \text{is}, \text{the}, \text{future}, \text{powers}, \text{of}, \text{machine}, \text{learning}\}$, so $n = 8$.

**Step 2 — TF Table (raw counts):**

| Term | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $Q$ | $\text{df}$ |
|------|-------|-------|-------|-----|-------------|
| AI | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| is | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| the | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 3 |
| future | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| powers | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| of | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| machine | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| learning | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |

$N = 3$.

**Step 3 — IDF $\log_{10}(N/\text{df})$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{idf}(\text{AI}) &= \log(3/2) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{idf}(\text{is}) &= 0.1761 \\
\text{idf}(\text{the}) &= \log(3/3) = 0 \\
\text{idf}(\text{future}) &= 0 \\
\text{idf}(\text{powers}) &= \log(3/1) \approx 0.4771 \\
\text{idf}(\text{of}) &= 0.4771 \\
\text{idf}(\text{machine}) &= 0.4771 \\
\text{idf}(\text{learning}) &= 0.4771
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — TF-IDF Vectors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{D_1} &= (0.1761,\ 0.1761,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0) \\
\vec{D_2} &= (0.3522,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.4771,\ 0.4771,\ 0,\ 0) \\
\vec{D_3} &= (0,\ 0.1761,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.4771,\ 0.4771) \\
\vec{Q}   &= (0.1761,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Cosine Similarity:**

**$\text{sim}(Q, D_1)$:**

$$
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_1} = 0.1761 \times 0.1761 = 0.0310
$$

$$
\vert\vec{Q}\vert = 0.1761, \quad \vert\vec{D_1}\vert = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.0310} = \sqrt{0.0620} \approx 0.2490
$$

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_1) = \frac{0.0310}{0.1761 \times 0.2490} = \frac{0.0310}{0.0438} \approx 0.7071
$$

**$\text{sim}(Q, D_2)$:**

$$
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_2} = 0.1761 \times 0.3522 = 0.0620
$$

$$
\vert\vec{D_2}\vert = \sqrt{0.1240 + 0.2276 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.5792} \approx 0.7610
$$

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_2) = \frac{0.0620}{0.1761 \times 0.7610} = \frac{0.0620}{0.1340} \approx 0.4626
$$

**$\text{sim}(Q, D_3)$:** No shared non-zero dimension ⇒ $\text{sim}(Q, D_3) = 0$.

**Final Ranking:**

$$
\boxed{D_1 \; (0.7071) \;\; > \;\; D_2 \; (0.4626) \;\; > \;\; D_3 \; (0.0000)}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]

#### (a) Derive the cosine similarity formula used in the Vector Space Model. State and justify its advantages over Euclidean distance for text retrieval. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Let $\vec{A}, \vec{B} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ be two non-zero vectors. By the definition of the dot product in Euclidean space:

$$
\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vert\vec{A}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{B}\vert \cdot \cos\theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between them. Rearranging:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vec{A}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{B}\vert}
$$

In VSM, $\vec{A} = \vec{Q}$ (query) and $\vec{B} = \vec{D_j}$ (document), and the dot product expands as:

$$
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq} \cdot w_{ij}
$$

Hence the **Cosine Similarity** is:

$$
\boxed{\text{sim}(Q, D_j) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq} \, w_{ij}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}^2}}}
$$

**Advantages over Euclidean distance:**

1. **Scale (Length) Invariance:** Cosine measures *angle*, not magnitude. Two documents of vastly different lengths covering the same topic will have nearly identical cosines. Euclidean distance penalizes longer documents.
2. **Bounded Range:** $\cos\theta \in [-1, 1]$, easily comparable across queries; Euclidean distance is unbounded in $[0, \infty)$.
3. **Better Semantics:** For sparse high-dimensional text vectors, *direction* carries topical meaning; magnitude reflects length, which is often irrelevant.
4. **Numerically Stable:** Cosine avoids the dominance of large-magnitude dimensions that can plague Euclidean distance.

#### (b) For the corpus given below, compute TF-IDF weights and the cosine similarity between the query and each document. Comment on the ranking. (7 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \text{``cloud computing security''} \\
D_2 &= \text{``edge computing security''} \\
D_3 &= \text{``cloud edge computing''} \\
Q  &= \text{``cloud security''}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Vocabulary:** $V = \{\text{cloud}, \text{computing}, \text{security}, \text{edge}\}$, $n = 4$, $N = 3$.

**TF Table:**

| Term | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $Q$ | $\text{df}$ |
|------|-------|-------|-------|-----|-------------|
| cloud | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| computing | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 3 |
| security | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| edge | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 |

**IDF (log base 10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{idf}(\text{cloud})      &= \log(3/2) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{idf}(\text{computing}) &= \log(3/3) = 0 \\
\text{idf}(\text{security})  &= \log(3/2) \approx 0.1761 \\
\text{idf}(\text{edge})      &= \log(3/2) \approx 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

**TF-IDF Vectors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{D_1} &= (0.1761,\ 0,\ 0.1761,\ 0) \\
\vec{D_2} &= (0,\ 0,\ 0.1761,\ 0.1761) \\
\vec{D_3} &= (0.1761,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.1761) \\
\vec{Q}   &= (0.1761,\ 0,\ 0.1761,\ 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cosine Similarity:**

**$\text{sim}(Q, D_1)$:**

$$
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_1} = 0.1761^2 + 0 + 0.1761^2 + 0 = 0.0620
$$

$$
\vert\vec{Q}\vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2} = 0.1761\sqrt{2} \approx 0.2490
$$

$$
\vert\vec{D_1}\vert = 0.2490 \text{ (by symmetry)}
$$

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_1) = \frac{0.0620}{0.2490^2} = \frac{0.0620}{0.0620} = 1.0000
$$

**$\text{sim}(Q, D_2)$:**

$$
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_2} = 0 + 0 + 0.1761^2 + 0 = 0.0310
$$

$$
\vert\vec{D_2}\vert = \sqrt{0 + 0 + 0.0310 + 0.0310} = \sqrt{0.0620} \approx 0.2490
$$

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_2) = \frac{0.0310}{0.2490 \times 0.2490} = \frac{0.0310}{0.0620} = 0.5000
$$

**$\text{sim}(Q, D_3)$:**

$$
\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_3} = 0.1761^2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0.0310
$$

$$
\vert\vec{D_3}\vert = 0.2490
$$

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_3) = 0.5000
$$

**Ranking:**

$$
\boxed{D_1 \; (1.0000) \;\; > \;\; D_2 \; (0.5000) \; = \; D_3 \; (0.5000)}
$$

**Comment:** $D_1$ is *perfectly aligned* with $Q$ (both contain "cloud" and "security" with identical IDF-weighted structure), hence cosine = 1. $D_2$ and $D_3$ each share only **one** weighted term with $Q$ (either "security" or "cloud"), giving the tie. This demonstrates the **partial matching** strength of VSM.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VSM represents both documents AND queries as vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$.**
- **TF-IDF weight** = $f_{t,d} \times \log(N / \text{df}(t))$ — always remember the formula on your fingertip.
- **Cosine similarity** is preferred over Euclidean distance because it is **length-invariant** and **bounded** in $[0, 1]$.
- **IDF = 0** for terms appearing in *every* document — this is correct, not an error.
- **VSM supports partial matching** (graded ranking), unlike Boolean IR which is set-based.
- **Vocabulary size $n$** = the number of dimensions; large corpora can produce $n \geq 10^5$, leading to **curse of dimensionality** (mention in long answers for bonus marks).
- **The orthogonality assumption** (terms are independent) is a *limitation* of VSM — write it as a drawback in Part B answers.
- **Normalization step** in cosine similarity divides by norms $\vert\vec{Q}\vert$ and $\vert\vec{D_j}\vert$ — forgetting this is the #1 mark-deduction error.
- **Always state the logarithm base** explicitly in the exam (base 10 or base $e$).
- **Ranking is descending** by similarity score: highest score = top retrieval.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"T-D-I-V-C"** → **T**F, **D**F, **I**DF, **V**ectors, **C**osine. Walk through these 5 steps in every numerical and you will not lose marks in PECST523 Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite Automata and Language Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Finite Automata and Language Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Finite Automaton (FA)

A **Finite Automaton** (also called a *finite state machine*) is a mathematical model of computation that serves as a fundamental abstract device for pattern recognition, sequence classification, and the theoretical foundation of formal languages. Formally, a deterministic finite automaton is a 5‑tuple

$$
M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)
$$

where:
- **Q** is a *finite*, *non‑empty* set of internal **states**.
- **Σ** is a *finite* set of input symbols called the **alphabet**.
- **δ : Q × Σ → Q** is the **transition function** (deterministic case).
- **q₀ ∈ Q** is the distinguished **start (initial) state**.
- **F ⊆ Q** is the set of **accepting (final) states**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Criterion (KTU Board Definition):** A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is **accepted** by the DFA $M$ if and only if $\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$, where $\delta^*$ is the extended transition function applied over the entire string. The set of all accepted strings is the **language recognized by M**, denoted $L(M)$.

## 1.2 Variants of Finite Automata

| Variant | Transition Form | Distinctive Property |
|---|---|---|
| **DFA** | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | One deterministic next state |
| **NFA** | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma_{\varepsilon} \rightarrow 2^Q$ | Multiple possible next states |
| **ε‑NFA** | $\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$ | Allows empty (silent) transitions |
| **PFA (Probabilistic FA)** | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q)$ | Transition carries probability mass |
| **WFSA (Weighted FA)** | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Weights, used in speech/language |

## 1.3 Formal Definition of a Language Model (LM)

A **Statistical Language Model** assigns a probability measure to every sequence of tokens drawn from a vocabulary $V$. It is a function

$$
P: V^+ \rightarrow [0, 1]
$$

such that for any sequence $w_1 w_2 \dots w_n \in V^+$ the chain rule of probability holds:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the *Data Analytics* context, the language model is the **probability backbone** of every NLP pipeline — spell‑checkers, autocomplete, machine translation, sentiment classifiers, and chatbots all consume $P(\text{next token} \mid \text{history})$ as their central input.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real‑World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Metro Station Turnstile**
> Think of a DFA as a metro turnstile that has a small **LED screen** displaying a state (e.g., *Locked*, *Coin‑Inserted*, *Pass‑Issued*). Each coin or token you insert is an **input symbol** that deterministically changes the screen. The turnstile **opens** (accept state) only after the *exact* sequence of coins is fed. Any other sequence leaves it locked (reject state).
>
> Similarly, a **Language Model** is like a *predictive keyboard* on your smartphone: it observes the words you have already typed (its *current state*) and assigns a probability to every possible next word.

### 1.5 Why Combine FA + LM?

Finite Automata provide the **structural skeleton** (states, transitions, acceptance) while Language Models layer a **probabilistic interpretation** on top — converting a rigid yes/no recogniser into a *soft*, ranked, generative engine. This bridge is called a **Probabilistic Finite Automaton (PFA)** and is the conceptual ancestor of modern neural LSTMs and Transformers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** PFAs power **Named‑Entity Recognition (NER)**, **POS taggers**, **speech‑to‑text decoders**, and **bio‑sequence analysis** (DNA/Protein motif detection) — all of which KTU Data Analytics graduates encounter in industry 4.0 pipelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High‑Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a DFA Processes a String

The DFA executes the following algorithm on input $w = a_1 a_2 \dots a_n$:

1. Initialise the current state $s \leftarrow q_0$.
2. **For each symbol** $a_i$ in $w$, apply the transition: $s \leftarrow \delta(s, a_i)$.
3. After the last symbol is consumed, **check acceptance**: if $s \in F$, accept; else reject.
4. The *trace* of states $(q_0, q_1, \dots, q_n)$ is the **run** of the DFA on $w$.

### 2.1.1 Why Determinism Matters

Determinism guarantees:
- **Linear time complexity** $O(\vert w \vert)$ for membership testing.
- **Unique run** for every input — useful for **lexical analysis** in compilers.
- **Closure** under complement and intersection (via *product construction*).

## 2.2 NFA — Non‑Determinism and the Subset Construction

An NFA may explore **multiple computational paths in parallel**. Acceptance requires *at least one* path ending in $F$. The classical **Rabin–Scott subset (powerset) construction** converts any NFA $N$ into an equivalent DFA $D$:

$$
Q_D = 2^{Q_N}, \quad q_{0,D} = E(q_{0,N}), \quad \delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E(\delta_N(q, a))
$$

where $E(q)$ is the **ε‑closure** (set of states reachable from $q$ using only ε‑moves).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why"** — Non‑determinism lets us write *concise* regular expressions; the *exponential* state blow‑up ($2^{\vert Q_N \vert}$) is the price we pay to convert back to a DFA for efficient execution.

## 2.3 N‑Gram Language Model — The Markov Approximation

A pure chain‑rule model is **intractable** because $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ requires conditioning on unbounded history. The **N‑gram assumption** (Markov order $N-1$) truncates the history:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})
$$

| N‑gram | Order | Example Probability |
|---|---|---|
| **Unigram** | 0 | $P(w_i)$ |
| **Bigram** | 1 | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$ |
| **Trigram** | 2 | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$ |

### 2.3.1 Maximum‑Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

Parameters are estimated by **relative frequency** from a training corpus $C$:

$$
P_{\text{MLE}}(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_{i-N+1}^{i-1} w_i)}{\text{count}(w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})}
$$

### 2.3.2 The Zero‑Probability Problem & Smoothing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High‑Yield Topic:** Smoothing redistributes probability mass from observed events to unseen events.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DFA acceptance | $\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$ | $\delta^*$ = extended transition |
| 2 | Extended transition (DFA) | $\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = q,\ \delta^*(q, xa) = \delta(\delta^*(q, x), a)$ | $x \in \Sigma^*, a \in \Sigma$ |
| 3 | NFA acceptance | $\delta_N(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ | *Existential* over paths |
| 4 | ε‑closure | $E(q) = \{p \in Q \mid q \Rightarrow^* p\}$ | $\Rightarrow^*$ = reflexive‑transitive closure |
| 5 | Subset construction | $\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E(\delta_N(q, a))$ | $S \in 2^{Q_N}$ |
| 6 | Chain rule | $P(w_{1:n}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1})$ | $w_{1:i-1}$ = history |
| 7 | N‑gram assumption | $P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$ | $N$ = context size |
| 8 | MLE n‑gram | $P = C(w_{i-N+1}^{i}) \,/\, C(w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})$ | $C(\cdot)$ = corpus count |
| 9 | Laplace (add‑1) smoothing | $P_L = (C(w) + 1)\,/\,(N + V)$ | $V = \vert V \vert$ vocabulary size |
| 10 | Add‑α smoothing | $P_\alpha = (C(w) + \alpha)\,/\,(N + \alpha V)$ | $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ |
| 11 | Good–Turing | $C^* = (C + 1) \cdot N_{C+1} \,/\, N_C$ | $N_C$ = frequency of frequency $C$ |
| 12 | Perplexity | $PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}$ | Lower $\Rightarrow$ better LM |
| 13 | Cross‑entropy | $H(W) = -\tfrac{1}{N} \log_2 P(w_1 \dots w_N)$ | In **bits/word** |
| 14 | Entropy (Shannon) | $H(X) = -\sum_{x} p(x) \log_2 p(x)$ | In **bits** |
| 15 | KL divergence | $D_{KL}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \sum_x P(x) \log_2 \tfrac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ | Asymmetry: $D_{KL} \neq D_{KL}^{-1}$ |
| 16 | State count bound | $\vert Q_D \vert \leq 2^{\vert Q_N \vert}$ | Worst‑case subset construction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Perplexity is the *single metric* used by virtually every KTU Data Analytics capstone project that builds a chatbot or autocomplete engine. Industry‑standard bigram models achieve $PP \approx 50\text{–}100$; modern Transformers reach $PP < 20$.

## 2.5 Perplexity — Intuitive Meaning

Perplexity is the **average branching factor** of the language model — i.e., how "confused" it is on average. If a model assigns probability $1/k$ to the correct next word, its perplexity is exactly $k$.

$$
PP(W) = 2^{H(W)} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1})}^{1/N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Data Analytics:** Perplexity is *language‑independent* and *model‑independent* — the same metric compares an n‑gram on Malayalam, a Transformer on English, and a PFA on genomic sequences.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step‑by‑Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chain Rule to Perplexity (Full Algebraic Detail)

We start with the **chain rule of probability** for a sequence of length $N$:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_N \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{N-1})
$$

**Step 1 — Compact the product:**
Using $\prod$ notation to compress the long product into a single expression:

$$
P(w_{1:N}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1})
$$

This is just shorthand — the right‑hand side expands back to the full chain rule shown above.

**Step 2 — Take the negative log‑base‑2 (this gives us bits, the standard information unit):**

$$
-\log_2 P(w_{1:N}) = -\log_2 \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the logarithm product rule $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$:**

$$
-\log_2 P(w_{1:N}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1})
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by N to obtain the *average* log‑loss per word (this is the cross‑entropy in bits/word):**

$$
H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1})
$$

This quantity $H(W)$ is called the **cross‑entropy** of the model on the test sequence.

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of perplexity as the exponential of the cross‑entropy:**

$$
PP(W) = 2^{H(W)} = 2^{-\frac{1}{N} \log_2 P(w_{1:N})}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the exponent using the identity $2^{\log_2 x} = x$:**

$$
PP(W) = \left(2^{\log_2 P(w_{1:N})}\right)^{-1/N} = P(w_{1:N})^{-1/N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $PP(W) = \big[P(w_{1:N})\big]^{-1/N}$. The derivation shows that perplexity is simply a *normalised geometric mean inverse* probability — making it a length‑independent measure of model quality.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Laplace (Add‑One) Smoothing

**Problem:** A trigram *"the cat __"* never occurred with the word *"squirrels"* in training, giving MLE probability $0$, which kills perplexity.

**Step 1 — Start from the MLE estimate:**

$$
P_{\text{MLE}}(w \mid h) = \frac{C(hw)}{C(h)}
$$

**Step 2 — Add 1 to every numerator count and $V$ to every denominator count:**

$$
P_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid h) = \frac{C(hw) + 1}{C(h) + V}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the probability axioms sum to 1 over the vocabulary $V$:**

$$
\sum_{w \in V} P_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid h) = \sum_{w \in V} \frac{C(hw) + 1}{C(h) + V} = \frac{\sum_w C(hw) + V \cdot 1}{C(h) + V} = \frac{C(h) + V}{C(h) + V} = 1 \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Generalise to Add‑$\alpha$ smoothing (avoids over‑discounting for large $N$):**

$$
P_{\alpha}(w \mid h) = \frac{C(hw) + \alpha}{C(h) + \alpha V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU‑Examiner Tip:** The proof that probabilities sum to 1 is *exactly* the kind of 1‑mark sub‑step examiners love to test. Memorise Step 3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Subset Construction (NFA → DFA)

Given NFA $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$, construct DFA $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, q_{0,D}, F_D)$.

**Step 1 — States are subsets of $Q_N$:**

$$
Q_D = 2^{Q_N}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial state is the ε‑closure of the NFA's start state:**

$$
q_{0,D} = E(q_0) = \{q_0\} \cup \{p \mid q_0 \Rightarrow^* p\}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the DFA transition on a subset $S$ and symbol $a$:**

$$
\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E(\delta_N(q, a))
$$

**Step 4 — Accepting states are subsets that contain at least one NFA accepting state:**

$$
F_D = \{S \in Q_D \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example.** Consider the NFA with $Q_N = \{p, q\}$, $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, start $p$, accepting $\{q\}$, and transitions $\delta(p, a) = \{p, q\}$, $\delta(p, b) = \{p\}$, $\delta(q, b) = \{q\}$.

The reachable subsets are $\{p\}, \{p, q\}, \{q\}$. State $\{p, q\}$ is accepting. Transitions: $\delta_D(\{p\}, a) = \{p, q\}$, $\delta_D(\{p\}, b) = \{p\}$, $\delta_D(\{p,q\}, a) = \{p,q\}$, $\delta_D(\{p,q\}, b) = \{p, q\}$ (since $p \xrightarrow{b} p$ and $q \xrightarrow{b} q$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The DFA has 3 states, confirming $\vert Q_D \vert \leq 2^{\vert Q_N \vert} = 4$ in this case.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Good–Turing Discounting

The Good–Turing estimator revises the count of an event observed $C$ times to:

$$
C^* = (C + 1) \cdot \frac{N_{C+1}}{N_C}
$$

where $N_k$ = number of n‑grams that occur exactly $k$ times in the training corpus.

**Step 1 — Re‑estimate probability of any n‑gram with original count $C$:**

$$
P_{\text{GT}}(w) = \frac{C^*}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — For n‑grams seen zero times, use $C=0$:**

$$
C^* = 1 \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Renormalise to ensure $\sum P = 1$ (leave‑mass for unseen n‑grams):**

$$
P_{\text{GT}}^{*}(w) = (1 - \lambda) \cdot P_{\text{GT}}(w), \quad \lambda = 1 - \sum_{C > 0} \frac{C^*}{N}
$$

This $\lambda$ is the **back‑off mass** redistributed to a lower‑order n‑gram (e.g., bigram falls back to unigram).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Strength vs Weakness of N‑Gram Models

| Aspect | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| **Interpretability** | Transparent counts | None |
| **Training speed** | $O(\vert C \vert)$ single pass | None |
| **Memory** | Compact hash table | Sparsity for large $N$ |
| **Generalisation** | Poor without smoothing | Suffers from *curse of dimensionality* |
| **Long‑range deps** | None (Markov‑truncated) | Misses long‑range semantics |
| **OOV handling** | Needs <unk> token | Vocab limitation |
| **Perplexity** | Competitive for small $N$ | Plateaus beyond $N = 5$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFA — Accepts All Strings Ending with the Substring "ab"

> [!NOTE]
> **Description.** This DFA has three states: $q_0$ (start, "no useful suffix"), $q_1$ ("just saw an *a*"), $q_2$ (accept, "just saw *ab*"). Transitions on $\{a, b\}$ are labelled on the edges. The DFA accepts *iff* it ends in $q_2$.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> state0
    state0 --> state0 : b
    state0 --> state1 : a
    state1 --> state0 : b
    state1 --> state2 : a
    state2 --> state0 : b
    state2 --> state2 : a
    state2 --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each circle is a **state** (named `state0`, `state1`, `state2` to satisfy the alphanumeric‑only node‑ID rule).
- The `[*]` symbol denotes the **start arrow** and the **accepting halo** on `state2`.
- Edge labels (`a`, `b`) are the **input symbols** that drive the transition.

## 4.2 NFA — Non‑Deterministic Version of the Same Language

This NFA *guesses* when an "ab" begins. The double‑circle on `stateAccept` marks acceptance.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> nfaStart
    nfaStart --> nfaStart : a
    nfaStart --> nfaStart : b
    nfaStart --> nfaMidA : a
    nfaMidA --> nfaAccept : b
    nfaAccept --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Block‑Level Functional Architecture — End‑to‑End Statistical Language Model Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText[Raw Text Corpus] --> tok[Tokenizer Module]
    tok --> vocab[Vocabulary Builder]
    vocab --> counts[N gram Counter Hash Table]
    counts --> mle[MLE Estimator]
    mle --> smooth[Smoothing Layer Laplace or Good Turing]
    smooth --> lm[Language Model Object]
    lm --> eval[Evaluator Perplexity or Cross Entropy]
    lm --> deploy[Deployment Engine Autocomplete or TTS]
    eval --> report[Performance Report]
```

**Block‑by‑block explanation:**
1. **Raw Text Corpus** — the unprocessed training documents.
2. **Tokenizer Module** — splits text into words/sub‑words.
3. **Vocabulary Builder** — compiles the unique token set $V$.
4. **N‑gram Counter (Hash Table)** — tallies $C(hw)$ for every n‑gram.
5. **MLE Estimator** — converts raw counts to probabilities $C(hw)/C(h)$.
6. **Smoothing Layer** — applies Laplace / Good‑Turing / Kneser‑Ney to handle zero‑counts.
7. **Language Model Object** — packaged model ready for queries.
8. **Evaluator** — computes perplexity on a held‑out test set.
9. **Deployment Engine** — the consumer (autocomplete, speech decoder, etc.).

## 4.4 Probabilistic Finite Automaton (PFA) — Augmenting DFA with Transition Probabilities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State S0 Start] -- "a : 0.6" --> B[State S1]
    A -- "b : 0.4" --> C[State S2]
    B -- "a : 0.5" --> B
    B -- "b : 0.5" --> D[State S3 Accept]
    C -- "a : 0.3" --> C
    C -- "b : 0.7" --> D
    D -- "a : 0.0" --> D
    D -- "b : 1.0" --> D
```

**Reading the PFA:** Each edge now carries a **transition probability** (e.g., "a : 0.6" means 60% of the time, on seeing *a*, the automaton moves from $S_0$ to $S_1$). For the model to be *valid*, for every state, the sum of outgoing probabilities on each input symbol must equal **1.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Data Analytics:** A PFA is precisely the **forward algorithm** of a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) restricted to a single observation per step — a foundation for **POS tagging**, **gene finding**, and **sequence labelling** in KTU‑level analytics projects.

## 4.5 Sequence‑to‑Probability Flow Inside a Bigram LM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    w0[Start Token] --> w1[Word 1]
    w1 -- "P w1 given start" --> w2[Word 2]
    w2 -- "P w2 given w1" --> w3[Word 3]
    w3 -- "P w3 given w2" --> w4[Word N]
    w4 -- "P wN given wN minus 1" --> out[Total Sequence Probability]
```

Each arrow is a conditional probability $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$; multiplying them all gives the joint probability of the entire sentence.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA). With the help of a suitable diagram, explain how it accepts or rejects a string.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DFA is a 5‑tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $Q$ is a finite set of states, $\Sigma$ is the input alphabet, $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ is the transition function, $q_0$ is the start state, and $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of accepting states. The DFA processes an input string symbol‑by‑symbol, starting in $q_0$. For each symbol read, it consults $\delta$ to move to a unique next state. After consuming the entire string, the DFA **accepts** the input if its current state belongs to $F$; otherwise it **rejects**. *(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — processing logic; 1 mark — acceptance criterion with example diagram).*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is perplexity in the context of a language model? Why is it preferred over raw probability as an evaluation metric?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Perplexity $PP(W)$ of a language model on a test sequence $W = w_1 w_2 \dots w_N$ is defined as $PP(W) = \big[P(w_1 w_2 \dots w_N)\big]^{-1/N}$. Geometrically, it is the **average branching factor** of the model — i.e., the effective number of choices the model is uncertain about at each step. It is preferred over raw probability because (i) raw probability is extremely small and length‑dependent, while perplexity is normalised to a *length‑independent, comparable* number; (ii) lower perplexity always means a *better* model, regardless of vocabulary; and (iii) it is **interpretable** as a count of equally likely alternatives. *(1 mark — definition with formula; 1 mark — two reasons; 1 mark — example, e.g., $PP=50$ means model is as confused as picking uniformly from 50 words).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub‑parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Construct a DFA that accepts all strings over the alphabet $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that contain the substring **"011"** as a suffix. Draw the state diagram, formally define the 5‑tuple, and trace the DFA's run on the input string **"10011011"**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram and the formal definition, explain the architecture of a **statistical n‑gram language model**. How is its quality quantified using **perplexity**? Derive the relationship between perplexity and cross‑entropy.

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Identify states by what we have just seen:**
- $q_0$: nothing useful yet (start state).
- $q_1$: last symbol was *0* (we are *one step* into "011").
- $q_2$: last two symbols were *"01"* (two steps in).
- $q_3$: accept state — last three symbols were *"011"*.

**Step 2 — Construct the state transition table:**

| Current State | Input 0 | Input 1 |
|---|---|---|
| → $q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| $q_2$ | $q_1$ | $q_3$ |
| $\ast q_3$ | $q_1$ | $q_3$ |

**Step 3 — Formal 5‑tuple definition (1 mark):**
$$
M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\},\ \{0, 1\},\ \delta,\ q_0,\ \{q_3\})
$$

**Step 4 — Trace on $w = \text{"10011011"}$:**

| Step | Symbol Read | Current State | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | $q_0$ | $q_0$ |
| 2 | 0 | $q_0$ | $q_1$ |
| 3 | 0 | $q_1$ | $q_1$ |
| 4 | 1 | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| 5 | 1 | $q_2$ | $q_3$ |
| 6 | 0 | $q_3$ | $q_1$ |
| 7 | 1 | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| 8 | 1 | $q_2$ | $q_3$ |

The DFA halts in $q_3 \in F$, hence the string is **accepted**. (1 mark — diagram, 2 marks — formal definition, 2 marks — full transition table, 2 marks — trace.)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Diagram:** The n‑gram LM architecture is the block diagram shown in §4.5 above, where each arrow is a conditional probability.

**Formal Definition (1 mark):**
A statistical n‑gram language model is a probability distribution $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ computed as

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}^{i-1})
$$

using the **Markov assumption** (1 mark) and the **MLE estimator** $P(w_i \mid h) = C(hw_i)/C(h)$ (1 mark).

**Perplexity Quantification (2 marks):**
Perplexity is the geometric mean of the inverse probabilities:

$$
PP(W) = P(w_1 \dots w_N)^{-1/N}
$$

A *lower* perplexity indicates a *better* model.

**Derivation of Cross‑Entropy Link (2 marks):**
Start from $PP(W) = P(w_{1:N})^{-1/N}$. Taking $\log_2$:

$$
\log_2 PP(W) = -\tfrac{1}{N} \log_2 P(w_{1:N}) = -\tfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{1:i-1}) = H(W)
$$

Hence $PP(W) = 2^{H(W)}$, establishing perplexity as the **exponential of the cross‑entropy in bits/word**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **n‑gram language model** in detail. Discuss the **zero‑probability problem** and derive the **Laplace (add‑one) smoothing** formula, showing that the resulting distribution is valid.

**(b) [7 marks]** What is a **Probabilistic Finite Automaton (PFA)**? Differentiate it from a deterministic finite automaton. Show how a PFA can be used to compute the probability of an input string with a suitable example.

#### Model Solution — (a)

**N‑gram LM Explanation (3 marks):**
An n‑gram model approximates the joint probability of a word sequence by assuming each word depends only on the previous $N-1$ words (Markov property of order $N-1$). The unigram ($N=1$), bigram ($N=2$), and trigram ($N=3$) cases are most common. Parameters are estimated by Maximum‑Likelihood Estimation: $P(w_i \mid h) = C(hw_i)/C(h)$. The model is simple, fast, and interpretable, but suffers from data sparsity — a serious problem for large $N$.

**Zero‑Probability Problem (2 marks):**
If any n‑gram in the test set was *not* observed in the training set, $C(hw_i) = 0$, making the joint probability of the *entire sequence* collapse to 0. This makes perplexity undefined ($1/0 = \infty$) and renders the model useless on any novel sentence.

**Laplace Smoothing Derivation (2 marks):**
Add 1 to every count and $V$ to every denominator:

$$
P_L(w_i \mid h) = \frac{C(hw_i) + 1}{C(h) + V}
$$

Validation:

$$
\sum_{w \in V} P_L(w \mid h) = \frac{\sum_w C(hw) + V}{C(h) + V} = \frac{C(h) + V}{C(h) + V} = 1 \ \checkmark
$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

**PFA Definition (2 marks):**
A Probabilistic Finite Automaton is a 6‑tuple $P = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F, p)$ where $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to \mathcal{P}(Q)$ and $p((q, a) \to q')$ assigns a probability to each transition such that, for every state $q$ and symbol $a$, $\sum_{q'} p((q,a) \to q') = 1$.

**Differences from DFA (2 marks):**

| Aspect | DFA | PFA |
|---|---|---|
| Transition | Single deterministic | Probabilistic (multiple with weights) |
| Acceptance | Boolean (yes/no) | Probability mass over accepted paths |
| Purpose | Recognition | Generation + ranking |

**Worked Example (3 marks):**
Consider a PFA with states $S_0, S_1, S_2$ where $S_0$ is the start, $S_2$ is the accepting state, $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$.
- $S_0 \xrightarrow{a: 0.7} S_1,\ \ S_0 \xrightarrow{b: 0.3} S_1$
- $S_1 \xrightarrow{a: 0.4} S_1,\ \ S_1 \xrightarrow{b: 0.6} S_2$
- $S_2$ is absorbing.

Probability of the string "ab" is the product along the unique path $S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2$:

$$
P(\text{"ab"}) = 0.7 \times 0.6 = 0.42
$$

Probability of "aab" (paths: $S_0 \xrightarrow{a} S_1 \xrightarrow{a} S_1 \xrightarrow{b} S_2$):

$$
P(\text{"aab"}) = 0.7 \times 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.168
$$

This illustrates how the PFA assigns a *numerical likelihood* to each string — the foundation of every probabilistic language model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the ε‑closure** in the subset construction → wrong DFA states → cascading 3–4 mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing acceptance criteria** between DFA ($\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$) and NFA ($\delta_N(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$). Examiners allot a dedicated 2‑mark sub‑part to test this.
> 3. **Missing the proof of validity** when deriving smoothing — students often write the formula but *omit* the $\sum P = 1$ verification, forfeiting 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up perplexity and entropy** — remember $PP = 2^H$, *not* $PP = H$.
> 5. **No diagram** in long answers → automatic **2 mark deduction** as per KTU valuation key. Always draw the state diagram, even if informal.
> 6. **Forgetting base‑2 vs natural log** in entropy derivations. KTU standard is **bits** ($\log_2$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid‑Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **DFA** is a 5‑tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ and processes input deterministically in $O(\vert w \vert)$ time.
- ✅ A **string is accepted** iff its computation ends in an accepting state.
- ✅ An **NFA** allows multiple transitions per input symbol; conversion to DFA via **subset construction** can blow up to $2^{\vert Q_N \vert}$ states.
- ✅ A **Language Model** assigns probabilities to word sequences; the **chain rule** is the foundation.
- ✅ The **N‑gram assumption** truncates history to $N-1$ previous words; **MLE** gives $\frac{C(hw)}{C(h)}$.
- ✅ **Smoothing** is mandatory: **Laplace (add‑1)**, **add‑$\alpha$**, **Good–Turing**, **Kneser–Ney** are the four KTU‑tested techniques.
- ✅ **Perplexity** $PP(W) = P(W)^{-1/N}$ is the standard evaluation metric — lower is better.
- ✅ **Perplexity ↔ Cross‑Entropy**: $PP = 2^H$ where $H$ is in bits/word.
- ✅ A **Probabilistic Finite Automaton (PFA)** = DFA + transition probabilities summing to 1 per state‑symbol pair.
- ✅ PFAs are the **theoretical bridge** between classical automata and modern statistical / neural LMs.
- ✅ Real‑world apps: spell‑checking, autocomplete, machine translation, speech recognition, bioinformatics, POS tagging.
- ✅ **Always verify** $\sum P = 1$ for any proposed probability distribution in your answer.
- ✅ **Always include a diagram** for any 7‑mark sub‑question — KTU examiners allot partial marks specifically for visual clarity.
- ✅ **Syllabus cross‑links**: Module 4's FA + LM directly feeds into Module 5 (HMMs, CRFs) and Module 6 (neural sequence models — RNN, LSTM, Transformer).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Query Likelihood Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Query Likelihood Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Query Likelihood Model** is a probabilistic Information Retrieval (IR) framework that ranks documents in response to a user query by estimating the likelihood that the query text could be observed as a random sample drawn from a probabilistic language model estimated from each document. Formally, given a query $Q = q_1 q_2 \ldots q_n$ and a document $D$, the document is scored and ranked by the conditional probability $P(Q \mid D)$. Documents for which the query is a more probable outcome under their underlying language model are deemed more relevant and placed higher in the result list.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Data Analytics syllabus, the model sits within the *Probabilistic Retrieval* cluster of Module 4 and is typically taught as a bridge between classical Boolean/Vector Space retrieval and modern statistical/NLP-based retrieval systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PECST523 / Module 4**
> The Query Likelihood Model is a **statistical language modeling approach** in which each document $D$ is treated as the source of its own language model $M_D$. Retrieval reduces to a generative inference problem: $P(Q \mid M_D)$ — the probability that $M_D$ generated $Q$.

## 1.2 Intuition Through a Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a library containing a million books, but the librarian is on leave. You have only a fragment of a sentence from a novel you once read, and you want to find which book it most likely came from.

- You pick up a book, and your brain subconsciously asks: *"If this book's author had a particular vocabulary and style, how likely would they have written this exact fragment?"*
- The more a book's word usage, style, and topic distribution matches the fragment, the higher its score.
- You repeat this mental scoring for every book and rank them.

The **Query Likelihood Model** formalises this exact mental process mathematically:

| Mental Analogy Element | Mathematical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Each book in the library | A document $D$ in the collection |
| Author's vocabulary and style | Document language model $M_D$ |
| The query fragment | Query $Q = q_1 q_2 \ldots q_n$ |
| Mental scoring | Likelihood computation $P(Q \mid M_D)$ |
| Ranking the books | Sorting documents by decreasing $P(Q \mid M_D)$ |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions (KTU Frequently Asked)

- **Language Model $M_D$**: A probability distribution over the vocabulary $V$ that characterises the word generation process believed to underlie document $D$.
- **Document Length $\vert D \vert$**: Total number of tokens (terms) in document $D$.
- **Collection $C$**: The entire corpus of all documents.
- **Collection Language Model $M_C$**: A language model estimated from the entire collection, used for smoothing.
- **Term Frequency $\text{tf}(t, D)$**: Number of times term $t$ appears in document $D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Query Likelihood Model matters in engineering practice**
> Search engines (Lucene/Elasticsearch BM25 cousins, Indri, Terrier), enterprise document retrieval, legal e-discovery, biomedical literature search (PubMed pipelines), and recommendation systems all leverage probabilistic language model ideas. Understanding this model is foundational for grasping modern dense retrieval and transformer-based re-rankers used in contemporary Generative AI search pipelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Six Logical Steps

The Query Likelihood Model executes retrieval in the following sequence:

1. **Vocabulary Assumption**: Assume a fixed vocabulary $V$ of all unique words appearing in the collection $C$.
2. **Document-as-Model Postulate**: Hypothesise that each document $D \in C$ possesses its own language model $M_D$ governing word generation.
3. **Query Independence Assumption**: Assume query terms $q_i$ are generated independently — i.e., the unigram assumption. This converts the joint probability into a product of marginals.
4. **Probability Estimation (MLE)**: Estimate the parameters of $M_D$ using Maximum Likelihood Estimation on the observed term counts in $D$.
5. **Scoring**: For each document $D$, compute $P(Q \mid M_D)$ under the estimated model.
6. **Ranking & Return**: Sort documents in descending order of $P(Q \mid M_D)$ and return the top-$k$.

## 2.2 The Core Mathematical 'Why'

Under the **query independence (unigram) assumption**:

$$
P(Q \mid M_D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid M_D)
$$

Because floating-point multiplication of many small probabilities underflows to zero, we apply a **monotonic log-transformation** to convert the product into a sum. Ranking by $P(Q \mid M_D)$ is identical to ranking by $\log P(Q \mid M_D)$ since $\log$ is monotonically increasing:

$$
\log P(Q \mid M_D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(q_i \mid M_D)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MLE (Maximum Likelihood Estimation) for a unigram multinomial model**
> The probability of a term $t$ under the document's raw maximum-likelihood model is simply its relative frequency in that document:
> $$P_{\text{MLE}}(t \mid M_D) = \frac{\text{tf}(t, D)}{\vert D \vert}$$
> where $\vert D \vert = \sum_{t \in V} \text{tf}(t, D)$ is the document length.

## 2.3 The Zero-Probability Problem & Smoothing

If a query contains even a single term that does **not** occur in a document, raw MLE assigns probability zero to the entire query, eliminating that document from consideration — even if the document is otherwise highly relevant.

**Solution — Smoothing**: Mix the document's MLE probability with a background (collection) probability to ensure no term ever gets zero probability.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside table cells instead of the pipe symbol `|`.**

| # | Formula | Name | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(Q \mid M_D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid M_D)$ | Query Likelihood (Unigram) | Document scoring |
| 2 | $\log P(Q \mid M_D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(q_i \mid M_D)$ | Log-Likelihood Score | Numerical stability |
| 3 | $P_{\text{MLE}}(t \mid M_D) = \dfrac{\text{tf}(t, D)}{\vert D \vert}$ | MLE Estimator | Parameter estimation |
| 4 | $P(t \mid D) = \lambda \, P_{\text{MLE}}(t \mid D) + (1 - \lambda) \, P(t \mid C)$ | Jelinek–Mercer Smoothing | Linear interpolation |
| 5 | $P(t \mid D) = \dfrac{\text{tf}(t, D) + \mu \, P(t \mid C)}{\vert D \vert + \mu}$ | Dirichlet Prior (Bayesian) Smoothing | Length-sensitive smoothing |
| 6 | $P(t \mid D) = \dfrac{\max(\text{tf}(t, D) - \delta, 0)}{\vert D \vert} + \dfrac{\delta \, \vert D \vert_u}{\vert D \vert} \, P(t \mid C)$ | Absolute Discounting | Subtract-$\delta$ smoothing |
| 7 | $P(t \mid C) = \dfrac{\sum_{D \in C} \text{tf}(t, D)}{\sum_{D \in C} \vert D \vert}$ | Collection Model MLE | Background probability |

Where:
- $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ — Jelinek–Mercer mixing weight.
- $\mu > 0$ — Dirichlet prior pseudo-count.
- $\delta \in (0, 1]$ — Absolute discount value.
- $\vert D \vert_u$ — number of unique terms in $D$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Search Engines**: Form the statistical backbone of modern ranking functions like BM25 (which can be derived as a smoothed query-likelihood model with a specific $tf$-transform).
- **Spelling Correction & Autocomplete**: Same generative model used to score candidate word completions.
- **Speech Recognition & Machine Translation**: Language models assign probability to candidate output strings.
- **Enterprise Search & Compliance**: Used in legal and biomedical retrieval for relevance ranking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Query Likelihood Ranking Function

We begin with the formal definition and arrive at the operational ranking formula used by search engines.

**Step 1 — Start with Bayes' Theorem perspective.**

We want to estimate which document is most likely relevant given the query. Using Bayes' rule:

$$
P(D \mid Q) = \frac{P(Q \mid D) \, P(D)}{P(Q)}
$$

Since $P(Q)$ is identical for all documents and $P(D)$ is often assumed uniform (a *prior* over documents), ranking by $P(D \mid Q)$ reduces to ranking by $P(Q \mid D)$ — the **query likelihood**.

**Step 2 — Apply the unigram (term-independence) assumption.**

The probability of the query $Q = q_1 q_2 \ldots q_n$ under the document's language model $M_D$ is the product of the probabilities of its individual terms:

$$
P(Q \mid M_D) = P(q_1 \mid M_D) \cdot P(q_2 \mid M_D) \cdots P(q_n \mid M_D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid M_D)
$$

*Conversion logic*: We assume that the occurrence of one term does not influence the probability of the next term appearing — a simplifying but effective modelling choice in IR.

**Step 3 — Apply the monotonic log-transformation.**

Because the product of many small numbers rapidly underflows to zero in floating-point arithmetic, we take the natural logarithm (which preserves ordering):

$$
\log P(Q \mid M_D) = \log \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid M_D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(q_i \mid M_D)
$$

*Conversion logic*: $\log(a \cdot b) = \log a + \log b$, and because $\log$ is monotonically increasing, the ranking order is preserved.

**Step 4 — Estimate probabilities via MLE on document $D$.**

For a unigram multinomial language model, MLE gives each term's probability as its relative frequency in $D$:

$$
P_{\text{MLE}}(t \mid M_D) = \frac{\text{tf}(t, D)}{\vert D \vert}
$$

*Conversion logic*: The MLE of a categorical distribution's parameters equals the empirical relative frequencies; this maximises the likelihood of observing the document's actual word sequence.

**Step 5 — Identify the zero-probability problem.**

If any query term $q_i$ does not appear in $D$ (i.e., $\text{tf}(q_i, D) = 0$), then $P_{\text{MLE}}(q_i \mid M_D) = 0$, causing the entire product — and hence the score — to collapse to zero. This unhelpfully eliminates potentially relevant documents.

**Step 6 — Apply Dirichlet Prior smoothing.**

The Bayesian solution assumes a Dirichlet prior over document language models with pseudo-count parameter $\mu$. The resulting posterior-mean estimate is:

$$
P(t \mid D) = \frac{\text{tf}(t, D) + \mu \, P(t \mid C)}{\vert D \vert + \mu}
$$

*Conversion logic*: This is the posterior expectation of a multinomial likelihood with Dirichlet prior; it guarantees $P(t \mid D) > 0$ for every term $t \in V$, while heavily weighting $P(t \mid C)$ when $\text{tf}(t, D)$ is small.

**Step 7 — Assemble the final ranking formula.**

Substituting the smoothed estimator back into the log-likelihood:

$$
\log P(Q \mid D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \left( \frac{\text{tf}(q_i, D) + \mu \, P(q_i \mid C)}{\vert D \vert + \mu} \right)
$$

Documents are ranked by descending order of this value.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a collection $C$ contains documents $D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$, and the query is $Q = \text{"data mining"}$ — i.e., two terms: $q_1 = \text{"data"}$, $q_2 = \text{"mining"}$.

Given values:

| Document | $\text{tf}(\text{data}, D)$ | $\text{tf}(\text{mining}, D)$ | $\vert D \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | 4 | 1 | 50 |
| $D_2$ | 1 | 0 | 10 |
| $D_3$ | 0 | 1 | 8 |

Let $P(\text{data} \mid C) = 0.30$, $P(\text{mining} \mid C) = 0.05$, and choose $\mu = 5$ (Dirichlet smoothing).

**Score for $D_1$:**

$$
P(\text{data} \mid D_1) = \frac{4 + 5 \cdot 0.30}{50 + 5} = \frac{5.5}{55} = 0.1000
$$

$$
P(\text{mining} \mid D_1) = \frac{1 + 5 \cdot 0.05}{50 + 5} = \frac{1.25}{55} \approx 0.0227
$$

$$
\log P(Q \mid D_1) = \log(0.1000) + \log(0.0227) \approx -2.303 + -3.785 = -6.088
$$

**Score for $D_2$:**

$$
P(\text{data} \mid D_2) = \frac{1 + 5 \cdot 0.30}{10 + 5} = \frac{2.5}{15} \approx 0.1667
$$

$$
P(\text{mining} \mid D_2) = \frac{0 + 5 \cdot 0.05}{10 + 5} = \frac{0.25}{15} \approx 0.0167
$$

$$
\log P(Q \mid D_2) \approx \log(0.1667) + \log(0.0167) \approx -1.792 + -4.094 = -5.886
$$

**Score for $D_3$:**

$$
P(\text{data} \mid D_3) = \frac{0 + 5 \cdot 0.30}{8 + 5} = \frac{1.5}{13} \approx 0.1154
$$

$$
P(\text{mining} \mid D_3) = \frac{1 + 5 \cdot 0.05}{8 + 5} = \frac{1.25}{13} \approx 0.0962
$$

$$
\log P(Q \mid D_3) \approx \log(0.1154) + \log(0.0962) \approx -2.159 + -2.341 = -4.500
$$

**Final ranking (descending score):**

$$
D_3 \; (\,-4.500\,) \; \succ \; D_2 \; (\,-5.886\,) \; \succ \; D_1 \; (\,-6.088\,)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation**: Notice that $D_2$ and $D_3$ each contain *only one* of the two query terms, yet both still receive non-zero scores thanks to Dirichlet smoothing. This demonstrates the practical effect of smoothing in handling vocabulary mismatch — a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix of Smoothing Techniques

| Smoothing | Formula Skeleton | Strength | Weakness | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MLE (No smoothing)** | $\dfrac{\text{tf}(t,D)}{\vert D \vert}$ | Simple, intuitive | Zero-probability problem | Toy examples only |
| **Jelinek–Mercer** | $\lambda P_{\text{MLE}} + (1-\lambda) P(t\mid C)$ | Easy to tune | Doesn't account for doc length | Short queries |
| **Dirichlet Prior** | $\dfrac{\text{tf}(t,D) + \mu P(t\mid C)}{\vert D \vert + \mu}$ | Length-aware, Bayesian | Sensitive to $\mu$ choice | Variable-length documents |
| **Absolute Discounting** | $\dfrac{\max(\text{tf}-\delta,0)}{\vert D \vert} + \delta \frac{\vert D \vert_u}{\vert D \vert} P(t\mid C)$ | Preserves probability mass | Requires unique-term count | Highly sparse corpora |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Query Likelihood Model — End-to-End Functional Flow

The diagram below illustrates how a query traverses the Query Likelihood Model, from raw input through document scoring to ranked output. Node identifiers are alphanumeric only to comply with Mermaid's parsing rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Query Q q1 q2 ... qn] --> B[Tokenization and Lowercasing]
    B --> C[Query Term List q1 to qn]
    C --> D{For Each Document D in Collection C}
    D --> E[Estimate Language Model M sub D]
    E --> F[Apply Dirichlet Prior Smoothing]
    F --> G[Compute P of q sub i given D for each term]
    G --> H[Aggregate Log Likelihood Sum of log P]
    H --> I[Store Score Document Pair]
    I --> J{All Documents Scored?}
    J -->|No| D
    J -->|Yes| K[Sort Documents by Descending Score]
    K --> L[Return Top K Ranked Documents]
    M[Collection Language Model M sub C] --> F
    N[Background Probability Table] --> F
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Scoring Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputStage[INPUT STAGE]
        A1[Raw Query String]
        A2[Document Corpus C]
    end
    subgraph preprocessStage[PREPROCESSING STAGE]
        B1[Tokenizer]
        B2[Stopword Filter]
        B3[Stemmer or Lemmatizer]
    end
    subgraph indexStage[INVERTED INDEX STAGE]
        C1[Term Frequency Table]
        C2[Document Frequency Table]
        C3[Document Lengths]
    end
    subgraph modelStage[LANGUAGE MODEL STAGE]
        D1[Collection Model M sub C Estimator]
        D2[Document Model M sub D Estimator]
        D3[Dirichlet Smoother]
    end
    subgraph scoringStage[SCORING STAGE]
        E1[Log Likelihood Computer]
        E2[Heap based Top K Selector]
    end
    subgraph outputStage[OUTPUT STAGE]
        F1[Ranked Document List]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> E1
    A2 --> B1
    C1 --> D2
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2 --> F1
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Choosing a Smoothing Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need to Smooth Document Model] --> B{Documents Highly Variable in Length?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Choose Dirichlet Prior Smoothing]
    B -->|No| D{Query Length Predominantly Short?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Choose Jelinek Mercer with lambda near 0.1 to 0.3]
    D -->|No| F{Corpus Has Many Unique Terms per Document?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Choose Absolute Discounting with delta near 0.7]
    F -->|No| H[Choose Jelinek Mercer with lambda near 0.5 to 0.7]
    C --> I[Output Smoothed P of t given D]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```
