# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the Query Likelihood Model in Information Retrieval.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Query Likelihood Model is a probabilistic retrieval framework that ranks documents $D$ by estimating the probability $P(Q \mid D)$ that the query $Q$ could be generated by a probabilistic language model associated with $D$. Each document is treated as a sample drawn from its own language model $M_D$. Under the unigram (term-independence) assumption:

$$
P(Q \mid M_D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid M_D)
$$

Documents with higher query likelihood are deemed more relevant and ranked higher. To handle the zero-probability issue, smoothing techniques such as Jelinek–Mercer or Dirichlet Prior are used.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the generative view, 1 mark for smoothing mention.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is smoothing necessary in the Query Likelihood Model? List any two smoothing techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Smoothing is necessary because raw Maximum Likelihood Estimation assigns zero probability to any query term not present in a document. Even a single unseen term collapses the entire query likelihood to zero, eliminating potentially relevant documents — this is known as the **zero-probability problem**.

Two smoothing techniques:

1. **Jelinek–Mercer (Linear Interpolation)**: $P(t \mid D) = \lambda \, P_{\text{MLE}}(t \mid D) + (1 - \lambda) \, P(t \mid C)$.
2. **Dirichlet Prior Smoothing**: $P(t \mid D) = \dfrac{\text{tf}(t, D) + \mu \, P(t \mid C)}{\vert D \vert + \mu}$.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for problem statement, 2 marks for the two techniques.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully**
> Students commonly lose 2–4 marks on Query Likelihood questions because they:
> 1. Forget the **log-transformation** step and write raw products (numerical stability is essential in implementation).
> 2. Confuse the **document model** $M_D$ with the **collection model** $M_C$ in smoothing formulas.
> 3. Skip mentioning the **query independence (unigram) assumption** explicitly.
> 4. Forget that $\vert D \vert$ in Dirichlet smoothing is in the *denominator* added with $\mu$.
> 5. Do not convert log-space products to log-space sums when writing the final ranking formula.

---

### Question A `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the ranking formula for the Query Likelihood Model under Dirichlet Prior smoothing, clearly stating each assumption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a collection with documents $D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$ and query $Q = \text{"machine learning"}$. Given:
- $D_1$: $\text{tf}(\text{machine}) = 3$, $\text{tf}(\text{learning}) = 2$, $\vert D_1 \vert = 40$.
- $D_2$: $\text{tf}(\text{machine}) = 1$, $\text{tf}(\text{learning}) = 0$, $\vert D_2 \vert = 12$.
- $D_3$: $\text{tf}(\text{machine}) = 0$, $\text{tf}(\text{learning}) = 1$, $\vert D_3 \vert = 8$.
- $P(\text{machine} \mid C) = 0.25$, $P(\text{learning} \mid C) = 0.10$.
- $\mu = 4$.

Compute the Dirichlet-smoothed query likelihood scores and rank the documents. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption 1 — Unigram (term independence):**

$$
P(Q \mid M_D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid M_D)
$$

**Assumption 2 — Monotonic log-transformation:**

$$
\log P(Q \mid M_D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(q_i \mid M_D)
$$

**Assumption 3 — Dirichlet prior over document language models:**

$$
P(t \mid D) = \frac{\text{tf}(t, D) + \mu \, P(t \mid C)}{\vert D \vert + \mu}
$$

**Final ranking formula:**

$$
\text{Score}(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \left( \frac{\text{tf}(q_i, D) + \mu \, P(q_i \mid C)}{\vert D \vert + \mu} \right)
$$

---

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**For $D_1$:**

$$
P(\text{machine} \mid D_1) = \frac{3 + 4 \cdot 0.25}{40 + 4} = \frac{4.0}{44} \approx 0.0909
$$

$$
P(\text{learning} \mid D_1) = \frac{2 + 4 \cdot 0.10}{40 + 4} = \frac{2.4}{44} \approx 0.0545
$$

$$
\log P(Q \mid D_1) = \log(0.0909) + \log(0.0545) \approx -2.398 + -2.910 = -5.308
$$

**For $D_2$:**

$$
P(\text{machine} \mid D_2) = \frac{1 + 4 \cdot 0.25}{12 + 4} = \frac{2.0}{16} = 0.1250
$$

$$
P(\text{learning} \mid D_2) = \frac{0 + 4 \cdot 0.10}{12 + 4} = \frac{0.4}{16} = 0.0250
$$

$$
\log P(Q \mid D_2) = \log(0.1250) + \log(0.0250) \approx -2.079 + -3.689 = -5.768
$$

**For $D_3$:**

$$
P(\text{machine} \mid D_3) = \frac{0 + 4 \cdot 0.25}{8 + 4} = \frac{1.0}{12} \approx 0.0833
$$

$$
P(\text{learning} \mid D_3) = \frac{1 + 4 \cdot 0.10}{8 + 4} = \frac{1.4}{12} \approx 0.1167
$$

$$
\log P(Q \mid D_3) = \log(0.0833) + \log(0.1167) \approx -2.485 + -2.148 = -4.633
$$

**Final ranking (descending score):**

$$
D_3 \; (-4.633) \; \succ \; D_1 \; (-5.308) \; \succ \; D_2 \; (-5.768)
$$

---

### Question B `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast Jelinek–Mercer smoothing and Dirichlet Prior smoothing in the Query Likelihood Model. Discuss when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A collection contains 3 documents with lengths $\vert D_1 \vert = 100$, $\vert D_2 \vert = 50$, $\vert D_3 \vert = 200$, and term frequencies of "analytics" are $\text{tf}(\text{analytics}, D_1) = 5$, $\text{tf}(\text{analytics}, D_2) = 2$, $\text{tf}(\text{analytics}, D_3) = 8$. The collection probability is $P(\text{analytics} \mid C) = 0.08$. Compute the smoothed probability of "analytics" using Jelinek–Mercer with $\lambda = 0.7$ for each document. Comment on the effect of document length. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Jelinek–Mercer | Dirichlet Prior |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Linear interpolation | Bayesian posterior mean |
| Document length | Not directly modelled | Naturally accounted for via $\mu$ |
| Tunable parameter | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | $\mu > 0$ |
| Behaviour on long docs | Trust $P_{\text{MLE}}$ with weight $\lambda$ | Trust $P_{\text{MLE}}$ when $\vert D \vert \gg \mu$ |
| Behaviour on short docs | Still trust MLE proportionally | Trust collection more (pseudo-count effect) |
| Preferred when | Query is short, doc lengths similar | Document lengths vary widely |

**When to prefer:**
- **Jelinek–Mercer**: When document lengths are roughly uniform and the corpus is not too sparse; classical choice for short web queries.
- **Dirichlet Prior**: When document lengths vary significantly (as in web corpora), since $\mu$ acts as a virtual document length controlling how much smoothing is applied.

---

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Jelinek–Mercer formula: $P(t \mid D) = \lambda \, P_{\text{MLE}}(t \mid D) + (1 - \lambda) \, P(t \mid C)$ with $\lambda = 0.7$.

**For $D_1$:**

$$
P_{\text{MLE}}(\text{analytics} \mid D_1) = \frac{5}{100} = 0.05
$$

$$
P(\text{analytics} \mid D_1) = 0.7 \cdot 0.05 + 0.3 \cdot 0.08 = 0.035 + 0.024 = 0.059
$$

**For $D_2$:**

$$
P_{\text{MLE}}(\text{analytics} \mid D_2) = \frac{2}{50} = 0.04
$$

$$
P(\text{analytics} \mid D_2) = 0.7 \cdot 0.04 + 0.3 \cdot 0.08 = 0.028 + 0.024 = 0.052
$$

**For $D_3$:**

$$
P_{\text{MLE}}(\text{analytics} \mid D_3) = \frac{8}{200} = 0.04
$$

$$
P(\text{analytics} \mid D_3) = 0.7 \cdot 0.04 + 0.3 \cdot 0.08 = 0.028 + 0.024 = 0.052
$$

**Comment on length effect:**

Jelinek–Mercer treats all documents uniformly — the same interpolation weight is applied regardless of document length. Even though $D_1$ is twice as long as $D_2$ and $D_3$, the smoothing contribution from the collection remains fixed at $0.3 \cdot 0.08 = 0.024$. This means short documents are not given extra smoothing protection — a key disadvantage compared to Dirichlet Prior smoothing, which adapts $\mu$ relative to $\vert D \vert$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Query Likelihood Model**

- **Core Idea**: Documents are ranked by $P(Q \mid D)$ — the probability that the document's language model generated the query.
- **Generative View**: Each document $D$ is the source of its own language model $M_D$.
- **Unigram Assumption**: Query terms are conditionally independent — products convert to log-sums for numerical stability.
- **MLE Estimator**: $P_{\text{MLE}}(t \mid D) = \text{tf}(t, D) \, / \, \vert D \vert$.
- **Zero-Probability Problem**: A single unseen query term kills the score — solved by **smoothing**.
- **Jelinek–Mercer**: Linear interpolation with collection; parameter $\lambda$ controls weight on MLE.
- **Dirichlet Prior**: Bayesian smoothing; parameter $\mu$ is a virtual document length and naturally handles length variation.
- **Absolute Discounting**: Subtract a constant $\delta$ from observed counts and redistribute the freed mass via the collection model.
- **Log-Likelihood Ranking**: Always convert products to log-sums to avoid floating-point underflow.
- **Score Aggregation**: $\text{Score}(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(q_i \mid D)$ — documents with higher scores are ranked higher.
- **Final Order of Operations**: Tokenise $\rightarrow$ Estimate $M_D$ $\rightarrow$ Smooth $\rightarrow$ Compute log-likelihood $\rightarrow$ Sort $\rightarrow$ Return Top-$k$.
- **Engineering Relevance**: Foundation of BM25, Indri, Lucene's classical scorer, and precursor to neural dense retrievers (DPR, ColBERT).
- **Common Exam Pitfall**: Do NOT confuse $P(t \mid C)$ (collection model) with $P(t \mid D)$ (document model) inside smoothing formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naïve Bayes Text Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST523 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Naïve Bayes Text Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Naïve Bayes Text Classification

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Naïve Bayes Classifier** is a probabilistic supervised machine learning algorithm based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a strong (naïve) assumption of **conditional independence** between every pair of features given the class label. In the context of **text classification**, it is used to assign a predefined category (class) $C_k$ to a document $\mathbf{d}$ based on the joint probability of the words (features) appearing in that document.

Mathematically, the classifier selects the class that maximizes the posterior probability:

$$\hat{C} = \underset{C_k}{\arg\max} \; P(C_k \vert \mathbf{d})$$

For a document $\mathbf{d}$ containing words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$, Bayes' Theorem expands to:

$$P(C_k \vert w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert C_k)}{P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayes' Theorem (Core Principle):**
> The posterior probability of a hypothesis $H$ given evidence $E$ is proportional to the prior probability of $H$ multiplied by the likelihood of observing $E$ given $H$.
> 
> $$P(H \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Suspicious Email Filter"

Imagine you are a postmaster in the 1990s, sorting letters into two boxes: **SPAM** and **LEGIT**. You don't have time to read every letter. Instead, you glance at a few keywords:

- If a letter contains the words *“free,” “offer,” “win,” “prize”* → it likely goes to **SPAM**.
- If it contains *“meeting,” “project,” “schedule”* → it likely goes to **LEGIT**.

You aren't checking every word — you're making a quick, independent judgment about each keyword, then combining them into an overall verdict. This is exactly what **Naïve Bayes** does: it treats each word in a document as an **independent clue** and combines all clues using probability to pick the most likely class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Naïve" Assumption:**
> The classifier assumes that the presence of one word is **independent** of another, given the class. For example, the word *"free"* appearing in an email does not affect the probability of the word *"prize"* appearing — they are treated as independent evidence. This is "naïve" because, in reality, words often co-occur, but the assumption makes computation tractable and surprisingly effective in practice.

### 1.3 Why Naïve Bayes is a KTU High-Yield Topic

- **Time-tested**: One of the oldest formal classification algorithms (1960s).
- **Computationally cheap**: Linear in the number of features.
- **Works well in high-dimensional spaces**: Ideal for text data where feature vectors can have 10,000+ dimensions (one per word in the vocabulary).
- **Foundation for spam filters, sentiment analysis, and document categorization** — all common KTU viva/ESE questions.

---

## 2. Components & Terminology of Bayes' Theorem

| Component | Symbol | Meaning in Text Classification |
|---|---|---|
| **Posterior Probability** | $P(C_k \vert \mathbf{d})$ | Probability that document $\mathbf{d}$ belongs to class $C_k$ — *what we want to compute*. |
| **Prior Probability** | $P(C_k)$ | Probability of class $C_k$ before seeing any document — *based on training data distribution*. |
| **Likelihood** | $P(\mathbf{d} \vert C_k)$ | Probability of observing document $\mathbf{d}$ given the class is $C_k$. |
| **Evidence / Marginal** | $P(\mathbf{d})$ | Probability of the document regardless of class — *constant across classes, often ignored during $\arg\max$*. |
| **Class-Conditional Probability** | $P(w_i \vert C_k)$ | Probability of word $w_i$ appearing in documents of class $C_k$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Since $P(\mathbf{d})$ is the same for all classes, we can drop it during classification and use the proportionality:
> 
> $$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{d}) \propto P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert C_k)$$

---

## 3. The Three Variants of Naïve Bayes for Text

| Variant | Assumption on Word Counts | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Multinomial Naïve Bayes** | Word frequency matters (counts) | Standard text classification, spam filtering, topic classification. |
| **Bernoulli Naïve Bayes** | Only word **presence/absence** matters (binary) | Short documents, sentiment with boolean features. |
| **Gaussian Naïve Bayes** | Continuous features follow a normal distribution | Not typically used for raw text, more for numeric features. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus:** For text classification problems in the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Multinomial Naïve Bayes** is the most commonly tested variant, as it incorporates word counts through the *Bag-of-Words* representation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 4. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Operational Pipeline

### 4.1 Step-by-Step Working of a Naïve Bayes Text Classifier

**Step 1 — Vocabulary Construction (Training Phase):**
- Tokenize every training document into words.
- Remove stopwords (optional), apply stemming/lemmatization.
- Build a vocabulary $V = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{\vert V \vert}\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute Class Priors $P(C_k)$:**
$$P(C_k) = \frac{\text{Number of training documents in class } C_k}{\text{Total number of training documents}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Likelihoods $P(w_i \vert C_k)$:**
For a class $C_k$, count occurrences of $w_i$ in all documents of that class, then normalize:
$$P(w_i \vert C_k) = \frac{\text{Count of } w_i \text{ in all } C_k \text{ documents}}{\text{Total word count in all } C_k \text{ documents}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace (Additive) Smoothing:**
To handle unseen words in test data, add a smoothing constant $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 1$):
$$P_{\text{smoothed}}(w_i \vert C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}_{\text{total}}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Laplace Smoothing?**
> If a word never appeared in class $C_k$ during training, $P(w_i \vert C_k) = 0$. Since Naïve Bayes multiplies all probabilities, a single zero wipes out the entire product. Smoothing assigns a small non-zero probability to unseen words.

**Step 5 — Classify a New Document:**
For test document $\mathbf{d} = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$:
$$\hat{C} = \underset{C_k}{\arg\max} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \vert C_k) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use $\log$?**
> The product of many small probabilities can underflow to zero in floating-point arithmetic. Taking the **logarithm** converts the product into a sum, which is numerically stable and faster to compute.

**Step 6 — Use the Bag-of-Words (BoW) Representation:**
- Each document becomes a vector of word counts: $\mathbf{d} = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_{\vert V \vert})$.
- Word order is discarded — only **multiset** of words is considered.

### 4.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | LaTeX Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | Foundation of the algorithm | $P(C \vert \mathbf{d}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{d} \vert C) P(C)}{P(\mathbf{d})}$ |
| 2 | Naïve Bayes Decision Rule | Final classification rule | $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_{i} P(w_i \vert C_k)$ |
| 3 | Class Prior | Probability of class $C_k$ | $P(C_k) = \dfrac{N_{C_k}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ |
| 4 | Likelihood (Multinomial) | Word probability in class | $P(w_i \vert C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C_k)}$ |
| 5 | Laplace Smoothing | Handle unseen words | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \vert C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}_{\text{tot}}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$ |
| 6 | Log-Form Decision Rule | Numerically stable form | $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i} c_i \log P(w_i \vert C_k) \right]$ |
| 7 | Vocabulary Size | Total unique words | $\vert V \vert$ |
| 8 | Smoothing Parameter | Default value | $\alpha = 1$ |
| 9 | Posterior (un-normalized) | Proportional score | $\text{score}(C_k) = P(C_k) \prod_{i} P(w_i \vert C_k)^{c_i}$ |
| 10 | Total Probability (denominator) | Constant across classes | $P(\mathbf{d}) = \sum_{k} P(C_k) P(\mathbf{d} \vert C_k)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In Formula 4 and 5, the sums are over the **entire vocabulary $V$**, not just the words in a single document. This is the **denominator** in the multinomial distribution and represents the total number of word tokens in the class.

### 4.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Email Spam Filtering** (Gmail, Outlook): Categorize incoming mail as spam/ham.
- **Sentiment Analysis**: Classify product reviews as positive/negative.
- **News Article Classification**: Tag articles into politics, sports, tech, etc.
- **Medical Diagnosis Support**: Predict disease class from symptom keywords.
- **Document Routing in Legal/Compliance**: Auto-sort legal documents.
- **Recommendation Systems**: Predict user interest categories from text profiles.

### 4.4 Why Naïve Bayes Works Despite the "Naïve" Assumption

The independence assumption is almost always **violated** in real text (e.g., "New York" is highly correlated). Yet Naïve Bayes still performs competitively because:
1. **Classification depends only on the *ranking* of probabilities, not their absolute values.**
2. The errors in independence assumption often cancel out across many features.
3. It requires **very little training data** to estimate parameters reliably.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 5. Step-by-Step Numerical Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 5.1 Worked Example 1 — Spam vs. Ham Classification (Multinomial NB)

#### Training Data

| Document | Class | Content (Bag of Words) |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | Spam | `free offer win prize` |
| $D_2$ | Spam | `free ticket win` |
| $D_3$ | Ham | `meeting project schedule` |
| $D_4$ | Ham | `meeting tomorrow` |

**Task:** Classify the new document: `D_test = "free meeting"` using **Multinomial Naïve Bayes with Laplace smoothing** ($\alpha = 1$).

#### Step 1 — Build the Vocabulary

$$V = \{\text{free, offer, win, prize, ticket, meeting, project, schedule, tomorrow}\}$$

Therefore, $\vert V \vert = 9$.

#### Step 2 — Compute Class Priors

Total documents $N = 4$.

$$P(\text{Spam}) = \frac{N_{\text{Spam}}}{N} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$P(\text{Ham}) = \frac{N_{\text{Ham}}}{N} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

#### Step 3 — Count Words Per Class

**Spam corpus:** $D_1 + D_2$ = "free offer win prize free ticket win"
- Total word tokens in Spam: $8$
- Word counts: free=2, offer=1, win=2, prize=1, ticket=1, meeting=0, project=0, schedule=0, tomorrow=0

**Ham corpus:** $D_3 + D_4$ = "meeting project schedule meeting tomorrow"
- Total word tokens in Ham: $5$
- Word counts: free=0, offer=0, win=0, prize=0, ticket=0, meeting=2, project=1, schedule=1, tomorrow=1

#### Step 4 — Apply Laplace Smoothing to Compute Likelihoods

Using Formula 5 with $\alpha = 1$:

$$P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \vert \text{Spam}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Spam}) + 1}{8 + 1 \cdot 9} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Spam}) + 1}{17}$$

$$P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \vert \text{Ham}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Ham}) + 1}{5 + 1 \cdot 9} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Ham}) + 1}{14}$$

| Word $w_i$ | $\text{count}(w_i, \text{Spam})$ | $P(w_i \vert \text{Spam})$ | $\text{count}(w_i, \text{Ham})$ | $P(w_i \vert \text{Ham})$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| free | 2 | $\frac{2+1}{17} = \frac{3}{17} \approx 0.1765$ | 0 | $\frac{0+1}{14} = \frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$ |
| offer | 1 | $\frac{1+1}{17} = \frac{2}{17} \approx 0.1176$ | 0 | $\frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$ |
| win | 2 | $\frac{3}{17} \approx 0.1765$ | 0 | $\frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$ |
| prize | 1 | $\frac{2}{17} \approx 0.1176$ | 0 | $\frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$ |
| ticket | 1 | $\frac{2}{17} \approx 0.1176$ | 0 | $\frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$ |
| meeting | 0 | $\frac{0+1}{17} = \frac{1}{17} \approx 0.0588$ | 2 | $\frac{2+1}{14} = \frac{3}{14} \approx 0.2143$ |
| project | 0 | $\frac{1}{17} \approx 0.0588$ | 1 | $\frac{2}{14} \approx 0.1429$ |
| schedule | 0 | $\frac{1}{17} \approx 0.0588$ | 1 | $\frac{2}{14} \approx 0.1429$ |
| tomorrow | 0 | $\frac{1}{17} \approx 0.0588$ | 1 | $\frac{2}{14} \approx 0.1429$ |

#### Step 5 — Compute the Posterior Scores for the Test Document

Test document: $D_{\text{test}} = \{\text{free, meeting}\}$

**For class Spam:**

$$\text{score}(\text{Spam}) = P(\text{Spam}) \cdot P(\text{free} \vert \text{Spam}) \cdot P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Spam})$$

$$\text{score}(\text{Spam}) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{3}{17} \cdot \frac{1}{17}$$

$$\text{score}(\text{Spam}) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{3}{289} = \frac{1.5}{289} \approx 0.00519$$

**For class Ham:**

$$\text{score}(\text{Ham}) = P(\text{Ham}) \cdot P(\text{free} \vert \text{Ham}) \cdot P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Ham})$$

$$\text{score}(\text{Ham}) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{14} \cdot \frac{3}{14}$$

$$\text{score}(\text{Ham}) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{3}{196} = \frac{1.5}{196} \approx 0.00765$$

#### Step 6 — Compare Scores and Normalize

Since $P(\mathbf{d})$ is the same for both, we compare the un-normalized scores:

$$\text{score}(\text{Ham}) = \frac{1.5}{196} \approx 0.00765 \;\; > \;\; \text{score}(\text{Spam}) = \frac{1.5}{289} \approx 0.00519$$

Therefore, $\hat{C} = \text{Ham}$.

For full posterior probabilities, we can normalize:

$$P(\text{Spam} \vert \mathbf{d}) = \frac{0.00519}{0.00519 + 0.00765} \approx 0.404$$

$$P(\text{Ham} \vert \mathbf{d}) = \frac{0.00765}{0.00519 + 0.00765} \approx 0.596$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The new document "free meeting" is classified as **HAM (legitimate email)** with posterior probability **0.596**, because the strong evidence from the word "meeting" (which appears only in Ham training data) outweighs the "free" keyword.

#### Step 7 — Verification Using Log-Form (Numerically Stable)

$$\log P(\text{Spam}) + \log P(\text{free} \vert \text{Spam}) + \log P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Spam})$$

$$= \log(0.5) + \log\left(\frac{3}{17}\right) + \log\left(\frac{1}{17}\right)$$

$$= -0.6931 + (-1.7346) + (-2.8332) = -5.2609$$

$$\log P(\text{Ham}) + \log P(\text{free} \vert \text{Ham}) + \log P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Ham})$$

$$= \log(0.5) + \log\left(\frac{1}{14}\right) + \log\left(\frac{3}{14}\right)$$

$$= -0.6931 + (-2.6391) + (-1.5404) = -4.8726$$

Since $-4.8726 > -5.2609$, classification is again **HAM** ✓

---

### 5.2 Worked Example 2 — Bernoulli Naïve Bayes (Word Presence/Absence)

For the same training data, using **binary** indicators (only whether a word appears in a document, not its count).

| Document | Class | Words Present |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | Spam | free, offer, win, prize |
| $D_2$ | Spam | free, ticket, win |
| $D_3$ | Ham | meeting, project, schedule |
| $D_4$ | Ham | meeting, tomorrow |

Test document: "free meeting"

For Bernoulli NB, the probability that a document $\mathbf{d}$ is in class $C_k$:

$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{d}) \propto P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{w_i \in V} \left[ P(w_i \vert C_k)^{d_i} \cdot (1 - P(w_i \vert C_k))^{(1 - d_i)} \right]$$

where $d_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is 1 if word $w_i$ is present, 0 otherwise.

**Calculate $P(\text{word} \vert \text{class})$ as document-frequency:**

$P(\text{free} \vert \text{Spam}) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$ (free appears in both spam docs)

$P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Spam}) = \frac{0}{2} = 0.0$ → use Laplace: $\frac{0+1}{2+2} = 0.25$

$P(\text{free} \vert \text{Ham}) = \frac{0}{2} = 0.0$ → with Laplace: $\frac{0+1}{2+2} = 0.25$

$P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Ham}) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$

For the test document, we evaluate only the present words (and their complements for absent words). The test document contains: **free** (present), **meeting** (present), and all other words are absent.

The classification would similarly favor the class that maximizes the score based on presence/absence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Multinomial vs Bernoulli — Key Difference:**
> - **Multinomial** uses word **counts** — repeated words increase evidence strength.
> - **Bernoulli** uses word **presence/absence** — repeated words count once.
> For long documents, Multinomial generally outperforms Bernoulli.

---

### 5.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages & Limitations

| Aspect | Multinomial NB | Bernoulli NB |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Type** | Word counts (integer) | Binary (0/1) presence |
| **Best for** | Long documents | Short documents |
| **Handles "free free free"** | Stronger signal for "free" | Treats same as single "free" |
| **Computational cost** | Slightly higher | Slightly lower |
| **Test data unseen word** | Uses Laplace smoothing | Uses Laplace smoothing |

### 5.4 Confusion Matrix Setup for Naïve Bayes Evaluation

|  | Predicted Spam | Predicted Ham |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Spam** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Ham** | FP | TN |

**Key Metrics:**
- Accuracy $= \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$
- Precision $= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- Recall $= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- F1-Score $= \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$

# 7. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. State Bayes' Theorem and explain its significance in text classification.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Bayes' Theorem:**
$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{d}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{d} \vert C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{d})}$$

**Significance in text classification (any 2 points, 1 mark each):**
1. It provides a principled probabilistic framework to compute the **posterior probability** of a class given the document's words.
2. It allows combining **prior knowledge** ($P(C_k)$) with **observed evidence** (word likelihoods) for decision-making.
3. It enables the **$\arg\max$ decision rule**, which is the basis of the Naïve Bayes classifier.

---

### **Q2. What is the "naïve" assumption in Naïve Bayes? Why is it called naïve?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The "naïve" assumption is that **all features (words) are conditionally independent of each other given the class label**. That is, for a document with words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n \vert C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \vert C_k)$$

**Why "naïve"?**
This assumption is unrealistic because in real text, words often co-occur (e.g., "New York", "machine learning"). It is called "naïve" because the algorithm makes this simplifying assumption regardless of whether it holds true. Despite being mathematically flawed, it works surprisingly well in practice and drastically reduces computational complexity.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture and working of the Multinomial Naïve Bayes classifier for text classification. Include the formulas for prior, likelihood, and posterior probabilities. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
The Multinomial Naïve Bayes classifier treats each document as a **multiset (bag) of word counts**. The architecture consists of:
- **Input layer:** Document represented as a vector of word counts $\mathbf{d} = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_{\vert V \vert})$ over vocabulary $V$.
- **Probability computation layer:** Computes $P(C_k)$ and $P(w_i \vert C_k)$ from training data.
- **Decision layer:** Uses the $\arg\max$ rule to select the most probable class.

**Working — Step by Step (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Training:**
- Tokenize training documents and build vocabulary $V$.
- Compute class priors:
$$P(C_k) = \frac{N_{C_k}}{N}$$
- Compute word likelihoods using counts:
$$P(w_i \vert C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C_k)}$$

**Step 2 — Laplace Smoothing (if asked):**
$$P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \vert C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + 1}{\text{count}_{\text{tot}}(C_k) + \vert V \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**
For test document $\mathbf{d}$, compute:
$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{d}) \propto P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert} P(w_i \vert C_k)^{c_i}$$

Pick the class with the highest posterior:
$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_{i} P(w_i \vert C_k)^{c_i}$$

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** Given the following training data for a sentiment classification task, classify the review "great product amazing" using Multinomial Naïve Bayes with Laplace smoothing. Show all calculations. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

| Review | Class |
|---|---|
| "great product amazing quality" | Positive |
| "amazing product worth it" | Positive |
| "bad quality terrible" | Negative |
| "terrible product waste" | Negative |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary and Counts:**
$V = \{$ great, product, amazing, quality, worth, it, bad, terrible, waste $\}$, so $\vert V \vert = 9$.

Positive corpus: "great product amazing quality amazing product worth it"
- Total words: 8
- Counts: great=1, product=2, amazing=2, quality=1, worth=1, it=1, bad=0, terrible=0, waste=0

Negative corpus: "bad quality terrible terrible product waste"
- Total words: 6
- Counts: great=0, product=1, amazing=0, quality=1, worth=0, it=0, bad=1, terrible=2, waste=1

**Step 2 — Priors:**
$$P(\text{Pos}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \quad ; \quad P(\text{Neg}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Smoothed Likelihoods (α = 1):**

$$P(w_i \vert \text{Pos}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Pos}) + 1}{8 + 9} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Pos}) + 1}{17}$$

$$P(w_i \vert \text{Neg}) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Neg}) + 1}{6 + 9} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, \text{Neg}) + 1}{15}$$

| Word | $P(w \vert \text{Pos})$ | $P(w \vert \text{Neg})$ |
|---|---|---|
| great | $\frac{1+1}{17} = \frac{2}{17}$ | $\frac{0+1}{15} = \frac{1}{15}$ |
| product | $\frac{2+1}{17} = \frac{3}{17}$ | $\frac{1+1}{15} = \frac{2}{15}$ |
| amazing | $\frac{2+1}{17} = \frac{3}{17}$ | $\frac{0+1}{15} = \frac{1}{15}$ |

**Step 4 — Compute Scores for "great product amazing":**

$$\text{score}(\text{Pos}) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{2}{17} \cdot \frac{3}{17} \cdot \frac{3}{17} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{18}{4913} = \frac{9}{4913} \approx 0.00183$$

$$\text{score}(\text{Neg}) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{15} \cdot \frac{2}{15} \cdot \frac{1}{15} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{2}{3375} = \frac{1}{3375} \approx 0.00030$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**
$\text{score}(\text{Pos}) = 0.00183 > \text{score}(\text{Neg}) = 0.00030$

**Predicted Class: POSITIVE ✓**

Normalized posteriors:
$$P(\text{Pos} \vert d) = \frac{0.00183}{0.00183 + 0.00030} \approx 0.859$$

$$P(\text{Neg} \vert d) = \frac{0.00030}{0.00183 + 0.00030} \approx 0.141$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** Compare Multinomial, Bernoulli, and Gaussian Naïve Bayes classifiers. State one real-world application for each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Multinomial NB | Bernoulli NB | Gaussian NB |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feature Type** | Word counts (non-negative integers) | Binary word presence/absence | Continuous real values |
| **Data Distribution** | Multinomial | Bernoulli (multivariate) | Gaussian (Normal) |
| **Vocabulary Role** | Counts weighted in product | Presence/absence in product | Mean & variance per feature |
| **Training Time** | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Best for Text?** | Yes (long documents) | Yes (short documents) | Not ideal for raw text |
| **Real-World App** | Spam filtering (Gmail), news classification | Short tweet sentiment, document categorization | Sensor data classification, medical diagnosis from continuous features |
| **Likelihood Formula** | $P(w \vert C) = \frac{\text{count}(w,C) + \alpha}{N_C + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | $P(w \vert C) = \frac{\text{docs with } w \text{ in } C + \alpha}{N_C + 2\alpha}$ | $P(x \vert C) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_C^2}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu_C)^2}{2\sigma_C^2}}$ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For text classification in KTU syllabus, **Multinomial NB is the default choice** because it captures word frequency information which is critical for distinguishing topics and sentiments.

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **zero-frequency problem** in Naïve Bayes. How does Laplace smoothing solve it? Demonstrate with an example. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Zero-Frequency Problem (3 marks):**
In Naïve Bayes, the posterior is computed as a product of probabilities:
$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{d}) \propto P(C_k) \cdot \prod_i P(w_i \vert C_k)$$

If **any word $w_j$ in the test document was never seen in class $C_k$ during training**, then $P(w_j \vert C_k) = 0$. The product becomes zero regardless of how strong the other evidence is — the model completely ignores the class, even if it is otherwise the best candidate.

**Example:** In our spam/ham example, the word "meeting" never appeared in any Spam training document, so $P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Spam}) = 0$. For the test document "free meeting":
$$P(\text{Spam} \vert \mathbf{d}) \propto P(\text{Spam}) \cdot P(\text{free} \vert \text{Spam}) \cdot P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Spam}) = 0.5 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Laplace Smoothing Solution (2 marks):**
Add a small constant $\alpha$ (typically 1) to every count:

$$P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \vert C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}_{\text{tot}}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

This ensures **no probability is ever exactly zero**, and the model can still classify documents containing previously-unseen words.

**Demonstration (2 marks):**
With $\alpha = 1$ and $\vert V \vert = 9$, $P(\text{meeting} \vert \text{Spam}) = \frac{0+1}{8+9} = \frac{1}{17} \approx 0.0588 > 0$.

Now the test document can be classified (in our example, as Ham) using non-zero evidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to **smooth the denominator** — you must add $\alpha \cdot \vert V \vert$ to the total count, not just add $\alpha$ to the numerator. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using the wrong base for Laplace — for **Bernoulli NB**, the denominator is $N_C + 2\alpha$, not $N_C + \alpha \vert V \vert$, because each word has two outcomes (present/absent).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to use the **log-form** for documents with many words — products of small probabilities underflow to zero, leading to incorrect classification. KTU expects either the log-form or explicit normalized posteriors.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **multinomial** (word counts) with **Bernoulli** (word presence) — they use different denominators. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Dropping the **prior $P(C_k)$** in the calculation. Even when classes appear balanced, include it for full marks.

---

## 8. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU Module 4**

- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(C \vert \mathbf{d}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{d} \vert C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{d})}$ — the foundation.
- ✅ **Naïve Assumption:** Words are conditionally independent given the class — unrealistic but computationally efficient.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_i P(w_i \vert C_k)$ — pick the class with the highest posterior.
- ✅ **Class Prior:** $P(C_k) = \dfrac{N_{C_k}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ — fraction of training docs in class.
- ✅ **Multinomial Likelihood:** $P(w_i \vert C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k)}{\text{total word count in } C_k}$.
- ✅ **Laplace Smoothing:** $P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \vert C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}_{\text{tot}}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$ — fixes the zero-frequency problem.
- ✅ **Log-Form Trick:** $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_i c_i \log P(w_i \vert C_k) \right]$ — prevents underflow.
- ✅ **Three Variants:** Multinomial (counts), Bernoulli (binary), Gaussian (continuous) — Multinomial is the KTU default for text.
- ✅ **Bag-of-Words (BoW):** Discards word order, retains counts/presence — the standard document representation.
- ✅ **Why Naïve Bayes Works:** Classification depends on **ranking**, not absolute probabilities; independence errors often cancel.
- ✅ **Real-World Apps:** Spam filtering, sentiment analysis, news categorization, medical diagnosis, document routing.
- ✅ **Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score from the confusion matrix.
- ✅ **Vocabulary Size $\vert V \vert$:** Always required in the Laplace smoothing denominator — a frequent KTU trap.
- ✅ **Don't forget the prior $P(C_k)$** — it is part of the posterior calculation even when classes are balanced.
